This  is  a  digital  copy  of  a  book  that  was  preserved  for  generations  on  library  shelves  before  it  was  carefully  scanned  by  Google  as  part  of  a  project 
to  make  the  world's  books  discoverable  online. 

It  has  survived  long  enough  for  the  copyright  to  expire  and  the  book  to  enter  the  public  domain.  A  public  domain  book  is  one  that  was  never  subject 
to  copyright  or  whose  legal  copyright  term  has  expired.  Whether  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  may  vary  country  to  country.  Public  domain  books 
are  our  gateways  to  the  past,  representing  a  wealth  of  history,  culture  and  knowledge  that's  often  difficult  to  discover. 

Marks,  notations  and  other  marginalia  present  in  the  original  volume  will  appear  in  this  file  -  a  reminder  of  this  book's  long  journey  from  the 
publisher  to  a  library  and  finally  to  you. 

Usage  guidelines 

Google  is  proud  to  partner  with  libraries  to  digitize  public  domain  materials  and  make  them  widely  accessible.  Public  domain  books  belong  to  the 
public  and  we  are  merely  their  custodians.  Nevertheless,  this  work  is  expensive,  so  in  order  to  keep  providing  this  resource,  we  have  taken  steps  to 
prevent  abuse  by  commercial  parties,  including  placing  technical  restrictions  on  automated  querying. 

We  also  ask  that  you: 

+  Make  non-commercial  use  of  the  files  We  designed  Google  Book  Search  for  use  by  individuals,  and  we  request  that  you  use  these  files  for 
personal,  non-commercial  purposes. 

+  Refrain  from  automated  querying  Do  not  send  automated  queries  of  any  sort  to  Google's  system:  If  you  are  conducting  research  on  machine 
translation,  optical  character  recognition  or  other  areas  where  access  to  a  large  amount  of  text  is  helpful,  please  contact  us.  We  encourage  the 
use  of  public  domain  materials  for  these  purposes  and  may  be  able  to  help. 

+  Maintain  attribution  The  Google  "watermark"  you  see  on  each  file  is  essential  for  informing  people  about  this  project  and  helping  them  find 
additional  materials  through  Google  Book  Search.  Please  do  not  remove  it. 

+  Keep  it  legal  Whatever  your  use,  remember  that  you  are  responsible  for  ensuring  that  what  you  are  doing  is  legal.  Do  not  assume  that  just 
because  we  believe  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  the  United  States,  that  the  work  is  also  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  other 
countries.  Whether  a  book  is  still  in  copyright  varies  from  country  to  country,  and  we  can't  offer  guidance  on  whether  any  specific  use  of 
any  specific  book  is  allowed.  Please  do  not  assume  that  a  book's  appearance  in  Google  Book  Search  means  it  can  be  used  in  any  manner 
anywhere  in  the  world.  Copyright  infringement  liability  can  be  quite  severe. 

About  Google  Book  Search 

Google's  mission  is  to  organize  the  world's  information  and  to  make  it  universally  accessible  and  useful.  Google  Book  Search  helps  readers 
discover  the  world's  books  while  helping  authors  and  publishers  reach  new  audiences.  You  can  search  through  the  full  text  of  this  book  on  the  web 


atjhttp  :  //books  .  qooqle  .  com/

PREFACE 

The  following  alterations  and  additions  have  been  made  in 
the  fifteenth  annual  volume  of  the  Literary  Year-Book — 
features  which  the  Editor  hopes  will  improve  its  usefulness 
as  a  book  of  reference  : 

The  Obituary  Notices  have  been  transferred  to  Part  II., 
and  are  placed  alphabetically  after  the  Nobel  Prizes.  This 
allows  of  notices  being  inserted  up  to  a  later  date  than  was 
possible  before.  Part  I.  is  therefore  now  wholly  given  up 
to  Authors  and  Authors'  Assistants. 

A  new  section  of  Training  Offices  and  Institutions  (some 
of  which  formerly  appeared  under  Agents — a  somewhat 
inaccurate  classification)  has  been  added  at  the  end  of 
Part  I. 

Immediately  following  the  Directory  of  Authors  will  be 
found  a  list  of  Pen-Barnes  and  Pseudonyms  of  living  authors, 
compiled  from  the  preceding  directory. 

Considerable  additions  have  also  been  made  to  the  list 
of  Societies  by  the  inclusion  of  literary  and  dramatic  clubs 
and  other  institutions  whose  aims  are  of  a  similar  nature. 

As  promised  last  year,  the  list  of  Indian  Periodicals  has 
been  largely  increased,  and,  in  addition,  a  separate  list  of 
Canadian  Publications  is  given. 

Suggestions  have  been  made  that  Agents,  Literary 
Searchers,  Typists,  etc.,  should  be  arranged  alphabeti- 
cally in   Part   II.,  as  was  done  in   some  of  the  earlier 

7 

Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


8  PREFACE 

issues.  In  some  instances,  however,  this  would  mean  a 
repetition  of  the  same  name  under  different  headings,  and 
the  Editor  considers  it  simpler  to  continue  to  group  these 
under  subheadings  in  the  single  section  of  Authors' 
Assistants  than  to  scatter  this  information  throughout 
the  volume.  This  has  been  the  arrangement  for  the  past 
six  issues,  to  which  readers  will  have  now  become  accus- 
tomed. 

Comment  has  been  made  in  certain  quarters  upon  -the 
various  forms  of  publishing  agreements  set  forth  in  the 
Law  and  Letters  section,  on  the  ground  that,  in  a  book 
designed  to  assist  authors,  these  agreements  err  in  being 
too  favourable  to  the  publisher*  who  is  well  able  to  look 
after  his  own  interests.  On  this  point  .we  can  but  suggest 
that  our  critics  have  not  read  carefully  the  explanations 
given  of  the  various  clauses,  but  only  the  agreements  them- 
selves ;  otherwise  they  would  notice  that  the  why  and  the 
wherefore  of  any  clauses  which  might  be  prejudicial  to  the 
author  are  made  clear  to  him.  Also,  we  have  seen  agree- 
ments submitted  to  authors  by  well-known  firms  which 
were  far  less  favourable  to  the  former  than  any  which 
appears  in  the  Literary  Year-Book,  and  which  contained 
clauses  we  should  never  recommend  an  author  to  accept. 

The  agreements  set  forth  in  our  pages  are  in  the  form 
which  the  majority  of  publishing  houses  of  repute  submit 
to  authors,  and,  if  our  own  experience  counts  for  anything, 
are  found  to  work,  on  the  whole,  satisfactorily.  The 
Editor's  object  in  this  volume  is  to  deal  with  facts  as  they 
are.  The  Authors'  Society  is  always  at  hand  to  try  (and 
often  to  succeed)  in  moulding  facts  as  they  should  be  ;  but 
it  seems  to  us  more  useful  and  more  practical  to  take  the 
normal  publisher's  agreement  and  to  make  it  clear  to  the  in- 
tending signatory  than  to  hamper  his  desire  for  publication 
by  advising  him  to  demand  exceptional  or  unlikely  terms. 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


PREFACE  9 

In  view  of  certain  correspondence  which  the  Editor  has 
received  during  the  past  year,  he  thinks  it  advisable  to   j 
state  that,  while  every  effort  is  made  to  obtain  all  infor- 
mation at  first  hand — or,  failing  that,  from  as  authoritative   I 
a  source  as  possible — he  is  not  responsible  for  uninten-   | 
tional  errors  of  commission  or  omission.     In  an  annual 
publication  covering  so  wide  a  variety  of  topics  as  the 
Literary  Year-Book  it  is  almost  inevitable  that,  even  with    I 
the  closest  supervision,  slight  errors  will  creep  in.  | 

At  the  same  time  the  Editor  wishes  to  thank  those 
numerous  correspondents  and  critics  who  have  so  gener- 
ously expressed  their  kind  appreciation  of  his  efforts,  and 
have  been  good  enough  to  point  out  errors  and  make  sug- 
gestions for  improvement.  Some  of  these  suggestions  are 
good,  and  others,  in  his  opinion,  are  not  good ;  but  even 
amongst  good  suggestions  there  are  many  which  are  pro- 
hibited by  their  cost ;  and  as  the  Year-Book  has  increased 
in  a  few  years  from  three  or  four  hundred  pages  to  very 
nearly  a  thousand,  readers  will  readily  admit  that  we  have 
not  been  backward  in  consulting  their  interests  and  wishes. 

Our  thanks  are  due  to  Mr.  C.  T.  Jacobi  and  io  Mr.  G.  H. 
Thring  for  their  assistance  in  connection  with  the  typo- 
graphical section  and  with  the  information  as  to  the  Nobel 
Prizes  respectively.  With  regard  to  the  typographical 
section,  we  would  draw  the  reader's  attention  to  the  new 
examples  of  proof  correction  (pp.  494-496),  kindly  supplied 
by  Mr.  Jacobi,  and  from  which,  we  think,  authors  will  be 
able  to  correct  their  proofs  more  easily  than  from  those 
formerly  appearing  in  our  pages. 

In  conclusion,  the  Editor  invites  the  co-operation  of 
authors,  editors,  and  others  to  assist  him  in  compiling  the 
various  directories,  and  is  always  glad  to  receive  par- 
ticulars for  this  purpose,  which  should  reach  him  not  later 
than  October  1  for  insertion  in  the  next  issue  of  the  Year- 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


io  PREFACE 

Book.  The  Editor  addresses  this  invitation  more  par- 
ticularly to  authors,  as  there  seems  to  be  a  misconception 
amongst  some  as  to  the  methods  employed  in  collecting  our 
information. 

REVIEW  OF  1910 

The  death  of  King  Edward  on  May  6,  though  it  did  not 
produce  so  large  a  crop  of  literary  memorials  as  the  death 
of  Queen  Victoria  in  1901,  was  the  subject  of  extensive 
obituary  notices,  marked  in  some  instances  by  the  ex- 
aggeration characteristic  of  modern  journalism,  but  in 
others,  which  it  would,  perhaps,  be  invidious  to  select,  by 
a  high  level  of  dignity  and  reserve.  The  Quarterly  article 
on  the  late  King's  youthful  years  was  a  particularly 
interesting  sidelight  on  Victorian  ideals. 

At  the  close  of  1909,  and  after  the  issue  of  the  last 
volume  of  the  Literary  Year-Book,  there  were  ominous 
signs  of  a  new  "  book-war  "  breaking  out,  the  matter  in 
dispute  being  the  right  of  the  six  libraries  forming  the 
Circulating  Libraries  Association  to  pass  judgment  on  the 
contents  of  books  offered  them  for  circulation,  to  demand 
delay  in  publication  in  order  to  enable  them  to  make  their 
selection,  and  to  refuse  to  take  books  which  they  considered 
objectionable. 

The  Committee  of  the  Society  of  Authors  passed  a  reso- 
lution to  the  effect  that  they  considered  such  interference, 
requiring  delay  in  publication,  as  unwarrantable — an 
opinion  with  which  we  heartily  agree. 

The  intentions  of  the  Circulating  Libraries  were,  no 
doubt,  excellent,  but  we  think  they  could  have  attained 
their  object  without  so  much  pother  and  fuss,  and  without 
appointing  themselves  the  literary  censors  of  public  morals. 


Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


PREFACE  ii 

Opinions  as  to  what  is,  or  is  not>  objectionable  in  literature  j 
can    vary   very    considerably.    Books   really   obnoxious  j 
could  be  left  to  be  dealt  with  by  the  police,  and  in  other 
respects  the  reading  public,  and  not  the  libraries  who ! 
circulate  books,  are  the  best  judges  of  what  they  should 
read.     In  this  opinion  we  are  glad  to  find  that  the  Public  j 
Morals  Conference  held  at  the  Caxton  Hall,  Westminster, 
in  July  last  concurred. 

On  February  24  was  announced  a  gift  of  £500  a  year  to 
the  British  Academy,  through  Professor  Gollancz,  by  an 
anonymous  donor,  for  the  benefit  of  English  literature. 
The  money  is  to  form  the  nucleus  of  a  fund  for  the  further- 
ance of  research  and  criticism — historical,  philological,  and 
philosophical — in  the  various  branches  of  English  literature, 
and  to  establish  two  annual  lectures — one  dealing  with  a 
Shakespearean  theme,  to  be  given  publicly  on  or  about 
April  23 ;  the  other  on  English  poetryy  to  be  called  the 
"  Warton  Lecture "  in  memory  of  Thomas  Warton, 
Dr.  Courthope's  great  predecessor  and  the  first  historian 
of  English  poetry. 

Dr.  Mason,  Master  of  Pembroke,  in  the  coarse  of  his 
valedictory  address  to  the  Senate  at  Cambridge  on  the 
occasion  of  his  vacating  the  office  of  Vice-chancellor  at  the 
beginning  of  the  new  academic  year,  mentioned  that  Sir 
Harold  Harmsworth  had  offered  £20,000  to  found  a  pro- 
fessorship of  English  Language  and  Literature. 

During  the  months  of  May  and  June  an  Imperial  Copy- 
right Conference  was  held  in  London,  to  consider  the  Berne 
Copyright  Convention,  as  revised  at  the  Berlin  Conference 
in  November,  1908,  and  the  attitude  of  the  Empire  towards 
the  subject  of  copyright.  Particulars  of  this  Conference 
and  the  results  arising  from  its  deliberations  will  be  found 
in  the  Law  and  Letters  section. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


12  PREFACE 

The  "  Peeters-Baertsoen  "  prize,  which  is  awarded  every 
•ten  years  by  the  "  Grand  Orient  Lodge  "  of  Belgium  for 
the  best  work  on  Freemasonry  written  within  that  period, 
has,  for  the  period  1899-1909,  been  awarded  to  Mr.  Robert 
Freke  Gould  for  his  Concise  History  of  Freemasonry,  pub- 
lished in  1904.    The  value  of  the  award  is  4,000  francs. 

The  price  of  novels — a  topic  which  may  almost  be 
described  as  a  "  hardy  annual " — was  much  to  the  fore 
last  year,  largely  due  to  certain  publishing  houses — notably 
Messrs.  Nelson  and  Sons — issuing  fiction  by  well-known 
authors  at  the  price  of  two  shillings. 

A  report  by  a  sub-committee  of  the  Society  of  Authors 
on  the  price  of  novels  was  issued  in  May  last.  The  con- 
clusions of  the  committee  were  to  the  effect  that  it  was  not 
advisable,  as  a  rule,-  to  lower  the  price  from  six  shillings, 
there  being  no  evidence  that  such  lowering  of  price  led  to 
any  proportionate  increase  in  circulation.  Only  in  special 
cases  should  the  standard  price  of  six  shillings  be  departed 
from  as  the  price  of  new  fiction.  At  the  same  time  the 
committee  suggested  that  a  certain  deterioration  in  the 
outward  aspect  of  novels,  the  cheap  paper,  and  catch- 
penny cover>  was  to  some  extent  responsible  for  the  cry 
for  cheaper  books. 

This  question  was  also  discussed  at  the  conference  of  the 
Associated  Booksellers  held  in  Birmingham  the  last  week 
in  June,  when  similar  conclusions  were  arrived  aty  and  the 
suggestion  made  that  every  good  book  should  be  published 
at  a  price  yielding  a  profit,  and  then  to  lower  its  price  in 
two  years'  time.  The  practice,  however,  of  issuing  novels 
at  six  shillings,  and  afterwards,  if  successful,  bringing  out 
a  cheap  edition  later,  is  one  which  is  already  followed  to  a 
considerable;extent . 

Another  factor,  we  think,  which  gives  rise  to  the  demand 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


PREFACE  13 

for  cheaper  fiction  is  the  enormous  output  of  non-copyright 
reprints  of  standard  authors  at  prices  ranging  in  some 
cases  as  low  as  sixpence  a  volume — a  state  of  affairs  which 
can  only  be  possible  by  correspondingly  large  sales. 

An  instructive  addendum  to  the  above  report  of  the  sub- 
committee of  the  Society  of  Authors  was  to  the  effect  that, 
since  the  market  in  France  has  been  flooded  with  new  novels 
at  one  franc,  the  result  has  been  most  disastrous  to  French 
literature  as  well  as  to  French  authors,  as  it  has  meant  that 
the  great  mass  of  writers  have  now  to  produce  novels  which 
are  short  and  sensational,  and  which  depend  for  a  large 
circulation  upon  their  violent  appeal. 

The  honour  of  a  baronetcy  was  conferred,  on  the  King's 
birthday  in  June  last,  upon  Sir  Adolph  Tuck,  head  of 
the  firm  of  Raphael  Tuck  and  Sons,  Ltd.,  publishers;  and 
knighthood  was  bestowed  on  Sir  A.  T.  Quiller-Couch 
("  Q-")>  *^e  well-known  author  ;  and  upon  Sir  J.  Edward 
Parrott>  LL.D.,  head  of  the  educational  department  of 
Messrs.  T.  Nelson  and  Sons,  publishers. 

Pensions  were  granted  under  the  provisions  of  the  Civil 
List  Act  to  the  following  for  their  services  to  literature  and 
science : 

Dr.  Thomas  Rice  Holmes,  Litt.D.,  for  his  historical 
writings  on  the  Indian  Mutiny  and  the  campaigns  of  Julius 
Caesar  (£200). 

Mr.  Richard  Whiteing,  in  consideration  of  the  literary 
merits  of  his  writings  (£100). 

Mr.  Arthur  Granville  Bradley,  for  his  writings  on 
Canadian  history  (£80). 

Mr.  Edward  Dwelly,  for  his  labours  in  the  compilation 
of  a  Gaelic  dictionary  (£50). 

Dr.  James  Sully,  LL.D.,  in  recognition  of  his  services  to 
psychology,  in  addition  to  his  existing  pension  (£95).

i 


14  PREFACE 

Mrs.  Constance  Garnett,  in  consideration  of  the  merits  of 
her  translations  from  the  Russian  (£75). 

In  July  two  new  appointments  were  announced  to  the 
Order  of  Merit,  the  recipients  being  Sir  William  Crookes, 
one  of  our  leading  men  of  science,  and  Mr.  Thomas  Hardy, 
our  leading  man  of  letters,  and  the  obviously  right  successor 
to  Mr.  Meredith  as  the  sole  representative  of  pure  letters 
in  that  distinguished  Order. 

A  notice  of  the  recently-formed  Academic  Committee  in  the 
Royal  Society  of  Literature  will  be  found  in  its  proper  place 
under  Societies.  September  17  last,  the  anniversary  of 
Dr.  Johnson's  birth,  was  made  the  occasion  of  inaugurating 
the  Johnson  Society  at  Lichfield.  Particulars  of  this 
society  also  appear  under  the  same  heading. 

Trade  Changes  during  1910. — Both  Messrs.  Methuen 
and  Co.  and  George  Bell  and  Sons  have  formed  their 
businesses  into  private  limited  companies. 

The  publications  of  Messrs.  Dawbarn  and  Ward,  Limited, 
formerly  of  Farringdon  Avenue,  E.C>  publishers  of  the 
Photographic  Monthly,  etc.,  are  now  issued  by  Messrs. 
George  Rcutledge  and  Sons,  Limited. 

Amongst  new  publications  which  have  made  their  appear- 
ance during  the  past  year  are  :  The  English  Church  Review 
(Longmans)  ;  The  Jewish  Review  (Routledge),  bi-monthly  ; 
The  Railway  and  Travel  Monthly  y  edited  by  Mr.  Sekon  ; 
T.P.'s  Magazine  (monthly)  ;  Mrs.  Bull  (weekly) ;  The 
Evening  Times  (daily,  halfpenny);  The  Green  Book  of 
London  Society  (Whitaker),  the  first  new  annual  book  of 
reference  to  be  published  in  the  new  reign,  edited  by  Mr. 
Douglas  Sladen  and  Mr.  W.  Wigmore. 

Other  new  publications  will  be  found  in  the  Periodical 
Publications  Section, 


Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


PREFACE  15 

The  Times  has  added  a  Woman's  Supplement  (Saturdays) 
and  an  Educational  Supplement  (Tuesdays,  occasionally) 
to  its  valued  "  extras." 

A  new  edition  (the  eleventh)  of  the  Encyclopedia  Brit- 
annica  has  been  in  preparation,  and  is  to  be  issued  complete 
in  twenty-eight  volumes  at  the  close  of  the  year  (1910)  by 
the  Cambridge  University  Press. 

The  Classical  Quarterly  and  The  Classical  Review  have 
been  acquired  by  the  Classical  Association,  and  constituted 
the  official  organs  of  that  Association  and  of  the  Philo- 
logical Societies  of  Oxford  and  Cambridge.  They  are  now 
published  by  Mr.  Murray.  The  editors  are  Professor  Arnold 
and  Mr.  F.  W.  Hall,  of  St.  John's  College,  Oxford. 

The  Academy  changed  hands  in  June  last,  and  is  now 
edited  by  Mr.  Cecil  Cowper. 

Three  important  books  of  biography  recently  published 
are  the  first  volume  of  the  Life  of  Benjamin  Disraeli, 
(two  volumes,  Murray),  by  William  F.  Monypenny ; 
Chatham :  his  Early  Life  and  Connections  (Humphreys), 
by  Lord  Rosebery,  based  to  a  large  extent  on  material 
hitherto  unpublished  ;  The  Life  of  the  Right  Hon.  Cecil  John 
Rhodes  (Arnold,  two  volumes),  by  the  Hon.  Sir  Lewis 
Michell — a  work  which  took  its  author  five  years  to  com- 
plete. Other  important  books  of  the  autumn  publishing 
season  are  Commander  Peary's  The  North  Pole  (Hodder  and 
Stoughton),  and  In  the  Heart  of  Africa  (Cassell),  by  the 
Duke  of  Mecklenburg,  the  latter  being  an  account  of  a 
hunting  and  scientific  expedition  into  the  heart  of  un- 
known territory  in  Central  Africa.  Mr.  Heinemann  has 
just  issued  a  popular  edition  in  one  volume  at  six  shillings 
net  of  Sir  Ernest  Shackleton's  The  Heart  of  the  Antarctic, 
originally  published  in  the  autumn  of  1909  in  two  volumes, 
at  thirty-six  shillings  net. 

The  centenary  of  the  birth  of  Mrs.  Gaskell  was  noted  in

the  Press  in  September,  and  produced  one  or  two  creditable

The  year  191 1  will  be  the  centenary  of  the  birth  of 
Thackeray,  and  it  is  also,  it  is  interesting  to  note,  the  ter- 
centenary of  the  publication  of  the  Authorized  Version  of 
the  Bible  in  the  reign  of  James  I.,  and  the  importance  of 
this  event  and  its  epoch-making  character  will  b  duly 
celebrated  in  the  course  of  the  year.

First  Lessons  in  Story  Writing. 

By  BARRY  PAIN 
2S.  60.  net.      Post  free  2s.  6d. 

Of  this  work  the  Westminster  Gazette  write* :  "  The  beginner  who  takes  these  lessons 
to  heart  may  be  quite  assured  of  an  advantage  over  his  competitors." 

How  to  Become  an  Author. 

By  ARNOLD   BBNNETT 

A    Practical    Guide    full    of    Useful    Hints. 

5s.  net.      Post  free  5s.  4d. 


LITERATURE  and  JOURNALISM  .-A  judicious  training  will  help  the  author 
to  avoid  the  pitfalls  that  lie  in  the  way  of  success.  There  is  a  handsome  income  in 
literary  work— articles,  short  stories,  serials,  etc. — but  to  the  inexperienced  the  way  is 
paved  with  rejected  manuscripts. 

OUR  LITERARY  COURSE  will  teach  you  the  essentials  which  are  necessary  to 
your  success,  and  may  save  you  years  of  fruitless  striving.  It  consists  of  twelve  prac- 
tical lessons,  and  enables  the  student  to  serve  his  apprenticeship  to  literature.  The 
student's  work  in  connection  with  each  lesson  is  corrected  and  criticised  by  one  of  our 
literary  experts,  who  are  men  of  experience,  and  well  qualified  to  instruct  the  aspirant. 
Lessons  are  also  given  on  Journalism  and  Play-writing,  and  the  College  has  an  Agency 
Department  which  places  the  manuscripts  of  its  students,  and  also  the  work  of  those  to 
whom  training  is  unnecessary. 

For  full  particulars,  write  for  Pamphlet  K, 

THE    LITERARY   CORRESPONDENCE  COLLEGE, 

9,  ARUNDEL  STREET,  STRAND,  W.C. 

THE  WIENER  AGENCY,  Limited. 

ADVANTAGES. 

Clients  are  entitled  to  the  following-  advantages  : — 

To  have  all  MSS.  read  and,  where  approved,  sub- 
mitted to  publishers  and  editors. 

When  an  author's  work  cannot  be  accepted  for 
negotiation,  the  Agency  will  be  pleased  to  give  advice 
as  to  its  improvement,  and  any  necessary  revision  can 
be  arranged. 

All  MSS.  are  kept  constantly  "on  the  move,"  and 
reports  as  to  their  whereabouts  will  be  sent  to  authors 
when  desired. 

A  Selective  Principle  is  applied  to  all  Manuscripts  ne  accept  for  negotia- 
tion.   Knowledge  ana  discrimination  guide  our  methods  in  regard  to  them. 

Attention  is  directed  to  our  arrangements  for  disposing  of  literary  pro- 
ductions in  the  United  States,  as  our  Resident  Manager  in  New  York  is 
exceptionally  well  acquainted  with  the  field. 

New  authors  are  invited,  not  only  to  send  us  their  work,  but  to^  get  into 
personal  touch  with  the  head  of  the  Department.  The  initial  difficulties  of  the 
literary  calling  are  great  enough  without  the  additional  drawbacks  incident  to 
inexperience.     Frank  criticism  and  candid  advice  for  those  who  desire  it. 

TERMS    ON    APPLICATION. 


The  Literary  Department:  Wiener  Agency,  Ltd.,  64,  Strand,  London.

ot  the  Literary  Year-Book. 

qlN  order  that  every  reader  of  the  "  Liter  \RY  Year-Book"  may  have 
the  opportunity  of  judging-  if  the 

PUBLISHERS'  CIRCULAR  &  BOOKSELLERS'  RECORD 

is  likely  to  be  of  use  to  them,  we  will  send  free  of all  charge  for  six  weeks, 
to  any  address,  a  copy  of  this  well-known  paper. 

qA  few  of  its  Special  Features  are :  The  best  weekly  and  monthly 
Lists  of  New  Books  published  in  Great  Britain  and  Ireland,  with  titles, 
sizes,  prices,  and  publishers.  Information  about  New  and  Forthcoming- 
Books.  Special  Articles  on  Book  and  Book-Trade  Subjects.  Impartial 
Notices  of  New  Books.  Thousands  of  "Books  Wanted"  and  "For 
Sale  "  are  advertised  for  every  week.  Etc.

Do  you  Write  Plays  ? 

THE  INTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT  BUREAU,  LTD., 
can  be  of  service  to  you. 

We  act  as  Agents  for  placing  Plays  of  all  kinds,  Operas,  Operettas, 
etc. ,  in  England  and  abroad  on  the  best  possible  terms. 

We  have  placed  Plays  with  almost  all  leading  Managers. 

We  copyright  dramatic  property  and  collect  authors'  fees. 

We  arrange  Invitation  Performances. 

We  review*  advise  upon,  and  remodel  Plays  and  Musical  Compo- 
sitions. 

We  undertake  translations  and  adaptations  of  Plays  from  and  into 
all  European  languages. 

All  English  Plays  submitted  to  the  Bureau  are  read  by  Mr.  Anthony 
L.  Ellis,  so  well  known  as  a  dramatic  critic  and  now  joint  director 
of  the  International  Copyright  Bureau.  Mr.  Ellis's  advice  and 
assistance  are  placed  at  our  clients'  disposal. 


THE  INTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT  BUREAU,  LIMITED, 

DEWAR  HOUSE,  HAYMARKET,  8.W. 


Ernest  Mayer

M  AUTHORS 

:"B      NOTE.— The  entries  in  this  Direetory  have  been  revised,  in  nearly 
.        all  eases,  by  the  authors  themselves,  and  the  Editor  desires  to 
DM   ^     thank  the  very  large  number  of  correspondents  who  have  assisted 
M  in  this  manner.    Owing  to  considerations  of  spaee,  it  has  not 

rn .  always  been  possible  to  give  an  exhaustive  list  of  each  writer's 

work ;  but  the  titles  included  having  been  selected  by  the  authors, 
,  the  information  in  such  cases  will  be  found  representative.  Where 

11  ~  no  returns  have  been  made,  information  has  been  gathered  to  a 

large  extent  from  other  sources ;  but  in  doubtful  cases — i.e.,  where 

no  definite  information  has  been  forthcoming — names  have  been 
£•'  necessarily   though    reluctantly   omitted.     Songs,    pamphlets, 

serials,  and  sub-titles  have  not,  as  a  rule,  been  inserted. 
A  (j)  prefixed   means  that  the  name  is  a  pseudonym;   a 

(})  implies  Membership  of  the  French  Academy. 
The  abbreviations  used  in  the  Directory  will,  it  is  hoped, 
.  explain   themselves.      C= Contributor.      E  =  Elected.      Where 

•  the  titles  of  two  or  more  consecutive  books  have  been  given, 

issued  by  the  same  firm,  the  publisher's  name  has  not  been 

repeated.    Foreign  books  are  inserted  with  the  date  of  publica- 
~  tion  without  brackets;  but  if  also  published  in  England,  these 

,  i  particulars  are  bracketed.

ree,  Sir  John  Hare,  Arthur  Bourchier,  &c.  Has  visited  the

art  periodicals.  Palazzo  Buondelmonti,  6  Piazza  S.  Trinita, 
Florence,  Italy.

AUTHORS'  ASSISTANTS 
AGENTS 

(i.)   BRITISH 

This  list  consists  of  the  names  of  Press  and  Literary  Agents,  whose 
work  includes  the  arrangement  of  books  and  articles  for  publica- 
tion and  all  commercial  negotiations  concerning  them,  together 
with  the  general  management  of  literary  property. 

ANGLO-IRISH  PRESS  AGENCY,  25  Belgrave  Road,  Rathmines, 
Dublin.  Undertakes  research  and  press  work  in  Ireland. 
Literary,  genealogical,  statistical,  journalistic,  and  pictorial. 
(See  Advertisement,  p.  lvii.) 

ARUNDEL  LITERARY  AGENCY,  THE,  9  Arundel  Street, 
Strand,  w.c.  Undertakes  all  negotiations  connected  with 
literary  work,  and  relieves  authors  of  the  unnecessary  anxiety 
associated  with  the  business  side  of  their  profession.  Novels, 
short  stories,  articles,  and  poems  are  carefully  read  and  criti- 
cized. Accounts  are  promptly  collected,  and  the  money  re- 
mitted to  authors  immediately  on  receipt.  (See  Advertisement, 
p.  18.)      . 

AUTHORS'  ALLIANCE,  THE,  2  Clement's  Inn,  w.c.  Manager  ; 
Hatherley  Clarke.  The  Authors'  Alliance  is  an  association 
of  authors,  journalists,  editors,  and  publishers'  readers,  who 
place  their  services  at  the  disposal  of  authors,  dramatists,  and 
literary  workers  and  aspirants  generally.  Its  managers  possess 
nearly  twenty  years'  practical  experience  in  every  department 
of  literary  work,  and  are  able  to  offer  expert  advice  and 
assistance,  while  their  facilities  for  placing  MSS.  with  publishers 
are  unrivalled.  The  Alliance  works  in  the  interests  of  the  author, 
its  aim  being  in  all  cases  to  protect  the  author's  interests  and 
to  promote  his  literary  success. 

AUTHORS'  AND  PLAYWRIGHTS'  AGENCY,  iio-m  Strand, 
London,  w.c.  Managers  :  A.  Wilson  Barrett  and  Oscar 
Parker,  M.A.  The  managers  have  had  a  long  experience  in 
dramatic,  literary,  and  journalistic  work,  and  undertake  to 
devote  themselves  to  furthering  the  best  interests  of  their 
clients.  They  advise  authors  upon  MSS.  submitted,  when 
desired  to  do  so,  and  employ  all  the  facilities  at  their  disposal 
to  bring  their  clients'  work:  to  the  attention  of  managers  of 
theatres,  editors,  and  publishers,  conducting  negotiations  for 
the  production  of  plays  and  for  the  sale  of  fiction  and  general 
MSS.  for  publication.  Authors  of  plays  may,  for  a  fee  of 
£2  2s.,  have  the  advantage  of  a  signed,  critical  opinion  on  their 
work  by  the  well-known  dramatist,  Mr.  F.  C.  Philips. 

AUTHORS*  SYNDICATE,  3-7  Southampton  Street,  Strand,  w.c. 
Founded  1890.  This  organization  acts  as  advisers  and  agents 
for  the  valuation,  sale,  purchase,  registration,  and  management 
427 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


428  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  i. 

of  literary  and  dramatic  copyright  property  in  England,  the 
Continent  of  Europe,  and  the  United  States  of  America. 
Mr.  W.  Morris  Colles,  the  founder  and  manager,  is  part  author  of 
Playright  and  Copyright  in  all  Countries  (Macmillan). 

BROWN  (CURTIS)  &  MASSIE,  5  Henrietta  Street,  Covent  Garden, 
w.c,  and  i  Union  Square,  New  York.  Business  management 
for  authors  and  dramatists.  British,  American,  and  Conti- 
nental book,  dramatic,  and  serial  rights  placed.  Representa- 
tives in  Paris,  Berlin,  Florence,  Vienna,  Copenhagen,  St. 
Petersburg,  Madrid,  Helsinborg  (Sweden),  and  Tokio.  This 
firm  is  now  handling  the  business  of  a  large  proportion  of  the 
total  number  of  distinguished  contemporary  authors,  and  has 
placed  some  of  the  most  successful  plays  of  the  year. 

BURGHES,  Mr.  A.  M.,  34  Paternoster  Row,  e.c.  Authors'  agent. 
Established  1879.  Mr.  Burghes  has  carried  on  the  business 
of  an  "  authors'  agent "  with  great  success.  He  has  acted 
on  behalf  of  "  Allen  Raine,"  Mrs.  Henry  de  la  Pasture,  the 
Rev.  T.  B.  Dover,  Mr.  C.  F.  Moberley  Bell,  the  Rev.  Anthony 
Deane,  Mr.  Clive  Holland,  Mrs.  Emma  Marshall,  Mr.  F.  E. 
Weatherley,  Mr.  Joseph  Whitaker,  "  John  Oliver  Hobbes," 
"Ouida,"  Dr.  W.  Roberston  Nicoll,  Mr.  Basil  Stewart,  Mr. 
Morley  Roberts,  Hon.  and  Rev.  James  Granville  Adderley,  Miss 
Evelyn  Everett-Green,  Mr.  Eustace  Miles,  Dr.  Emil  Reich, 
Dr.  Ryle  (late  Bishop  of  Liverpool),  Mrs.  Mona  Caird,  Mr.  Harry 
Tighe,  Mrs.  Hamilton  Synge,  Mrs.  Thomas  Sadler,  Mr.  Thomas 
MacKean,  and  many  hundreds  of  others.  Mr.  Burghes  reads 
MSS.  and  places  them  with  suitable  publishers,  negotiates 
terms  on  authors'  behalf,  and  adequately  represents  their 
interests  in  dealing  with  publishing  houses  and  editors. 

CAMBRIDGE  LITERARY  AGENCY,  Savoy  House,  115  Strand, 
London,  w.c.  Managing  Director :  George  G.  Magnus. 
Secretary :  Claude  Scudamore  .  Representative  in  New  York  : 
F.  Priestman.  Business  :  Placing  novels,  short  stories, 
articles,  and  poems.  This  Agency  places  literary  matter  with 
over  150  publishers  and  periodicals.  Numerous  unique  testi- 
monials received  from  authors  whose  work  has  passed  through 
the  Agency.  During  the  first  week  of  1908  (January  1  to 
January  7),  the  C.JL.A.  placed  over  twenty  novels,  serials, 
and  short  stories  by  unknown  writers.  This  record  was  beaten 
March  13  to  19,  1909,  during  which  week  the  Agency  sold  one 
novel,  twenty-four  short  stories,  and  four  articles.  The  policy 
of  the  C.L.A.  is  to  deal  with  only  suitable  matter,  and  it  is 
forced  to  refuse  over  one  thousand  MSS.  annually  that  do  not 
reach  the  Agency's  standard.  This  careful  selection  is  the 
secret  of  the*  C.L.A.'s  phenomenal  success.  The  obtaining  of 
the  best  possible  terms  and  the  highest  prices  is  the  aim  of  this 
firm,  and  it  has  numerous  testimonials  in  proof  of  its  good 
offices  in  this  important  direction.  Telephone  :  1648  Gerrard. 
(See  Advertisement,  p.  lvii.) 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  I.]  AGENTS  429 

CASSELL'S  GENERAL  PRESS.  Established  to  supply  the  require- 
ments of  London,  Provincial,  and  Colonial  newspapers,  serial 
and  short  stories  by  all  the  best  authors,  and  articles  of  general 
and  topical  interest  by  leading  writers  of  the  day.  Weekly 
features  a  speciality.  Address  :  CasselTs  General  Press,  La 
Belle  Sauvage,  e.c. 

CENTRAL  PRESS.  Established  1863.  The  oldest  press  agency 
in  the  United  Kingdom.  Leading  articles,  London  letters, 
reviews  of  books,  reviews  of  Parliament,  reviews  of  the  year, 
tales,  etc.,  supplied.  The  reporting  of  Parliamentary  Com- 
mittees a  special  feature.  Managers  :  Guy  L'Estrange  and 
W.  N.  Shansfield,  127-8  Palace  Chambers,  Westminster,  s.w. 

EMPIRE  LITERARY  AGENCY,  THE.  Established  in  1905  as 
Foster's  Agency.  Incorporated  in  1908  with  the  Fleet  Street 
Bureau.  Principal :  David  Foster,  late  editor  of  The  Empire 
Gazette.  Head  Office  :  18  Bouverie  Street,  Fleet  Street,  London, 
e.c.  The  Agency  makes  a  special  point  of  giving  advice  and 
practical  assistance  to  authors  just  commencing  journalism, 
as  well  as  encouraging  those  living  in  the  Colonies  to  find  a 
market  for  their  work  in  this  country.  MSS.  of  all  kinds  are 
read,  if  needed  typed,  and,  if  approved,  placed  with  editors  and 
publishers.  Advertisements  of  all  kinds  are  received  by  the 
Agency  and  inserted  in  all  papers  at  lowest  rates.  r(See 
Advertisement,  p.  Iviii.) 

GALLICHAN  AND  GASQUOINE,  188  Strand,  w.c.  Literary 
Agency  under  the  direction  of  Walter  M.  Gallichan  and  C. 
Gasquoine  Hartley,  who  are  the  authors  of  many  volumes, 
and  well-known  contributors  to  the  periodical  press.  Gallichan 
and  Gasquoine  place  MSS.  of  all  kinds,  plays,  songs,  and  music, 
and  line  and  wash  drawings.  They  give  advice  and  literary 
instruction  to  beginners,  and  revise  MSS.  for  publication. 
Testimonials  from  prominent  authors  shown  to  prospective 
clients.  Typewriting  and  translation  at  moderate  rates.  The 
llustration  Department  supplies  black  and  white  drawings  and 
photographs  of  all  kinds  to  illustrate  articles  and  books.  We 
make  a  speciality  of  taking  exclusive  photographs  for  journalists, 
authors,  and  publishers.  Photographs  of  topical  interest  sold 
on  commission.     Telephone  :  13291  Central. 

GARDEN  CITY  LITERARY  AGENCY  AND  TYPEWRITING 
AND  TRANSLATION  BUREAU,  Letchworth,  Herts.  Chief 
Critic  and  Reviser  :  G.  F.  Leatherdale,  M.A.  (Oxon).  All  com- 
munications should  be  addressed  to  the  Secretary.  MSS. 
criticized,  revised,  typewritten,  and  placed.  Translations. 
(See  Advertisement,  p.  Iviii.) 

HINKINS,  F.  R.,  &  SON,  Lyndhurst,  New  Forest,  Hants.  Press 
Artists,  Art  Photographers,  and  Nature  Study  Illustrators.  This 
firm  occupies  a  unique  position  for  its  specialities  in  the  centre 
of  the  finest  woodland  scenery  in  England.  Telegrams  : 
Hinkins,  Lyndhurst.

INTERNATIONAL  ART  SUPPLY  AND  GENERAL  INFORMA- 
TION AGENCY,  The  Studios,  Linden  Gardens,  London,  w. 
Established  in  1890  by  Mr.  Augustin  Rischgitz.  Under  Royal 
Patronage.  This  agency  specially  appeals  to  editors,  publishers, 
authors,  collectors,  and  supplies  pictures,  engravings,  and 
photographs  of  paintings,  objects,  and  documents  in  museum 
galleries  and  institutions  for  purposes  of  special  illustrations, 
also  arranging  for  rights  of  reproduction  when  required.  Tele- 
grams :  Chercheur,  London.  Telephone :  4413  Paddington. 
ABC  and  Lieber's  code  used. 

INTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT  BUREAU,  LIMITED.  Founded 
to  give  information  in  copyright  matters,  to  act  as  intermedi- 
aries between  authors  and  publishers,  playwrights  and  managers, 
for  the  purpose  of  placing  their  books,  plays,  operas,  &c,  both 
here  and  abroad.  Agents  in  the  chief  capitals.  Managing 
Directors :  Ernest  Mayer  and  Anthony  L.  Ellis,  Dewar 
House,  Haymarket,  s.w.  (See  Advertisement,  p.  32.) 

JOHNSTON,  Mr.  R.   KEITH,  Sparrows'  Heme,  Bushey,  Herts. 

Mr.  Keith  Johnston  has  had  extensive  experience  in  revising 
0*    and  editing  manuscripts  of  all  kinds,  and  undertakes  this  work 

when  desired.     Every  manuscript  submitted  is  carefully  read 

with  a  view  to  its  being  offered  in  the  most  suitable  quarter. 

Terms  on  application. 

LECTURE  AGENCY  (THE),  LTD.,  The  Outer  Temple,  Strand, 
w.c.  Founded  1879.  Acts  as  Agent  for  all  the  famous 
lecturers  of  the  day,  and  supplies  popular  lecturers  to  literary 
societies,  institutes,  schools,  etc.,  throughout  the  country. 
Sole  agents  for  Sir  Ernest  Shackleton.  Managing  Director  : 
Gerald  Christy 

LITERARY  AGENCY  OF  LONDON  (THE),  5  Henrietta  Street, 
Covent  Garden,  w.c.  This  literary  agency  was  founded  in 
1899  ;  but  its  directors,  Messrs.  G.  H.  Perris  and  C.  F.  Cazenove, 
have  long  been  engaged  in  literary  and  kindred  work.  The 
agency  deals  with  all  kinds  of  literary  work,  and  arranges  for 
the  sale  and  publication  of  short  or  long  MSS.  The  agency  is 
strongly  represented  in  Paris  and  New  York  and  other  foreign 
capitals,  and  also  in  the  Colonies.  MSS.  by  the  following, 
among  other  authors,  have  been  placed  by  the  agency  :  Hilliard 
Atteridge,  H.  H.  Bashford,  Miss  Betham-Edwards,  Frank 
Bullen,  Desmond  Coke,  Alphonse  Courlander,  Richard  Davey, 
W.  H.  Davies,  F.  Martin  Duncan,  Miss  Edith  Durham,  Right 
Rev.  Abbot  Gasquet,  Prof.  P.  Geddes,  Philip  Gibbs,  Warwick 
Goble,  Maxim  Gorky,  ^ighe  Hopkins,  Baroness  von  Hutten, 
Prince  Kropotkin,  Joseph  McCabe,  W.  H.  Mallock,  J.  E. 
Masefield,  George  Meredith,  Alfred  Noyes,  B.  S.  Rowntree, 
Frank  Savile,  Sir  Ernest  Shackleton,  H.  L.  Stuart,  Upton 
Sinclair,  Netta  Syrett,  Edward  Thomas,  Mrs.  Katherine  Cecil 
Thurston,  Count  Tolstoy,  H.  G.  Wells,  Edward  Stewart  White. 
(See  Advertisement,  p.  426.)

LONDON  LITERARY  SYNDICATE  FOR  THE  SALE  OF  LITER- 
ARY AND  JOURNALISTIC  MATERIAL  (THE).  Compe- 
tent work  handled  without  preliminary  fees.  Revision,  tuition,

and  editors.  Collaboration  arranged.  Address  :  The  Manager, 
40  SaGkville  Street,  w.  Telephone  :  1848  Mayfair.  (See  Ad- 
vertisement, p.  lix.) 

LONDON  PRESS  EXCHANGE  (THE),  LIMITED,  15  Strand, 
w.c.  Founded  in  1892  for  the  purpose  of  undertaking  the 
representation  of  provincial  newspapers  in  London,  both  as 
correspondents  and  as  advertisement  and  general  business 
agents,  it  has  developed  into  a  general  advertising  and  news 
agency,  most  of  its  news  and  correspondence  being  supplied 
on  the  syndicate  plan.  London  letters,  ladies'  letters,  literary 
notes,  and  special  reports  are  supplied  to  the  clients  of  the 
Exchange,  and  it  makes  a  feature  of  early  Parliamentary  and 
political  intelligence.     Mr.  R.  J.  Sykes  is  director  and  manager. 

MEREDITH,  MARK,  240  Anfield  Road,  Liverpool.  Has  had 
wide  editorial  experience  in  various  Press  positions,  and  deals 
with  all  classes  of  MSS.  (long  or  short)  for  placing  to  the  author's 
benefit.  A  large  stock  of  literary  matter  is  always  available 
for  author's  or  editor's  use.     (See  Advertisement,  p.«lvii.) 

NATIONAL  PRESS  AGENCY,  LIMITED.  Established  1873, 
This  agency,,  situated  at  Whitefriars  House,  Carmelite  Street. 
London,  B.C.,  supplies  every  requirement  for  daily  and  weekly 
provincial  newspapers.  Leading  articles  and  London  letters  ; 
special  columns,  serial  and  short  stories  by  well-known  writers. 
Branches  at  New  York  and  Sydney.  Manager  :  Mr.  John 
Reburn. 

NORTHERN  NEWSPAPER  SYNDICATE  (THE),  Kendal,  Eng- 
land. This  agency  supplies  the  Press  with  fiction  (serial  and 
short  stories)  and  with  articles  (signed)  on  the  foremost  subjects 
of  the  day.  Proposals  and  MSS.  from  authors  are  promptly 
considered  and  decided  upon.  Manager  :  Mr.  James  E.  Lyons. 
Telegraphic  Address  ;  Syndicate,  Kendal. 

PINKER,  Mr.  J.  B.,  Talbot  House,  Arundel  Street,  London,  w.c, 
and  1 56  Fifth  Avenue,  New  York.  Literary  agent.  Mr.  Pinker 
has  always  made  a  special  point  of  helping  young  authors  in 
the  early  stages  of  their  career,  when  they  need  most  the  aid 
of  an  adviser  with  a  thorough  knowledge  of  the  literary  world 
and  the  publishing  trade.  (See  Advertisement,  p.  4.) 
PRESS  ASSOCIATION,  LIMITED,  14  New  Bridge  Street,  e.c. 
Manager  :  Mr.  E.  Robbins.  Incorporated  in  1868  for  supply- 
ing telegraphic,  telephonic,  and  other  news  to  newspapers,  news- 
rooms, and  clubs.  It  has  the  sole  right  of  supplying  Reuter's 
telegrams  in  the  provinces  ;  it  supplies  all  kinds  of  home  news, 
and  it  undertakes  the  duties  of  London  reporters  for  its  sub- 
scribers. Telegrams  :  Press  Association,  London.  Telephones  : 
National,  2740  Holborn  (ten  lines)  ;  Post-Office,  6130  Central.

QUILL  CLUB.  This  organization  gives  its  members  free  advice 
as  to  the  disposal  of  their  MSS.f  but  does  not  submit  matter  to 
editors  or  publishers.  Though  the  Club  does  not  undertake 
to  act  as  an  author's  agent,  this  notice  is  here  inserted  in  its 
capacity  as  an  "  author's  assistant."  (For  fuller  particulars, 
see  "  Societies.") 

SPRIGG,  PEDRICK  &  CO.  (MESSRS.),  LIMITED,  1 10,  St.  Martin's 
Lane,  London,  w.c,  the  well-known  Literary  Agents,  have  for 
many  years  been  associated  with  leading  authors,  and  have 
advantageously  placed  a  very  large  number  of  their  MSS.  An 
efficient  staff  of  expert  readers  and  advisers  places  them  in  a 
position  second  to  none  as  "  middlemen  "  between  authors  and 
publishers.  Amongst  their  many  clients  are  the  following  well- 
known  names :  Alice  and  Claude  Askew,  Arthur  Applin, 
J.  Bloundelle  Burton,  Harold  Bindloss,  Winifred  Graham, 
R.  Murray  Gilchrist,  Fergus  Hume,  Mrs.  Coulson  Kernahan, 
Bart  Kennedy,  Mrs.  L.  T.  Meade,  "  Rita,"  Mrs.  Fred  Reynolds, 
H.  de  Vere  Stacpoole,  Guy  Thorne,  and  many  others. 

It  is  the  avowed  object  of  this  firm  to  lay  before  the  public 
the  work  of  new  authors,  who  have  perhaps  experienced  some 
difficulty  in  getting  into  touch  with  publishing  firms,  and  to 
this  end  Messrs.  Sprigg,  Pedrick  &  Co.  have  carefully  organized 
their  advisory  staff.  In  consequence  of  the  large  demand  for 
and  increasingly  difficult  supply  of  plays  suitable  for  production, 
Messrs.  Sprigg,  Pedrick  &  Co.  have  made  special  arrangements 
in  this  department,  and  can  offer  exceptional  facilities  to  those 
who  are  desirous  of  having  their  work  placed  before  leading 
actor- managers. 

THOMSON'S  LITERARY  BUREAU,  for  dealing  with  fiction  and 
other  literary  work  suitable  for  newspapers  and  periodicals. 
Authors  are  invited  to  submit  serial  stones  (60,000  to  80,000 
words),  novelettes  of  10,000  words,  short  stories  and  articles. 
All  MSS.  receive  prompt  and  careful  consideration.  Payment 
on  acceptance.  Address  :  D.  C.  Thomson  &  Co.,  Limited, 
170  Fleet  Street,  London,  B.C. 

UNDERWOOD  &  UNDERWOOD,  104  High  Holborn,  London, 
w.c.  Literary  and  illustration  agency.  Branches  in  New 
York,  Toronto,  San  Francisco,  Bombay,  Shanghai,  Manila, 
St.  Petersburg.  Agents  and  correspondents  in  all  the  prin- 
cipal cities  of  the  world.  This  firm's  speciality  is  the  supply  of 
illustrations  for  publication  in  books,  papers,  and  magazines. 
They  possess  an  immense  stock  of  photographs,  comprising 
some  300,000  subjects,  covering  almost  every  locality  in  the  world, 
as  well  as  photographs  of  national  and  international  events 
and  prominent  people,  including  an  endless  variety  of  special 
features.  Their  photographs  are  made  by  their  own  staff  of 
specially  trained  operators,  who  are  well  known  to  produce  life 
and  artistic  effects,  which  make  the  work  of  this  firm  of  dis- 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  i.]  AGENTS  433 

languished  merit.  The  firm  also  acts  as  agents,  both  in  Europe 
and  America,  for  many  leading  photographers  in  both  hemi- 
spheres. Telegraph  and  Cable  Address :  Views,  London. 
Telephone  :  1805  Central. 

WATT,  Messrs.  A.  P.,  &  SON,  Hastings  House,  Norfolk  Street, 
Strand,  w.c.  To  Mr.  A.  P.  Watt,  the  senior  member  of  the  firm, 
is  due  the  rise  of  the  literary  agent.  He  was  the  first  to  con- 
ceive and  put  into  practice  the  idea  of  the  literary  agent,  acting 
as  an  intermediary  between  authors  and  publishers  and  editors. 
Messrs.  A.  P.  Watt  &  Son  are  at  all  times  glad  to  hear  from 
authors,  whether  at  the  beginning  of  their  career,  or  of  established 
reputation,  with  a  view  to  the  disposal  of  their  work.  A  good 
idea  of  the  value  to  authors  of  Messrs.  A.  P.  Watt  &  Son's 
Literary  Agency  is  given  in  the  following  extract  from  the 
Autobiography  of  the  late  Sir  Walter  Besant :  "  Let  me  here 
express  my  great  and  lasting  gratitude  to  my  agents,  Mr.  A.  P. 
Watt  and  his  son,  by  whose  watch  and  ward  my  interests  have 
been  so  carefully  guarded  for  eighteen  years.  During  that 
time  I  have  always  been  engaged  for  three  years  in  advance  ; 
I  have  been  relieved  from  every  kind  of  pecuniary  anxiety ; 
my  income  has  been  multiplied  by  three  at  least ;  and  I  have 
had,  through  them,  the  offer  of  a  great  deal  more  work  than  I 
could  undertake.  I  cannot  speak  too  strongly  of  the  services 
rendered  to  me  by  my  literary  agents.  Of  course,  there  are 
different  kinds  of  agents.  There  is  the  agent,  for  example, 
who  knows  nothing  about  his  business.  But  the  agent  who 
does  know  his  business,  who  knows  also  editors,  publishers, 
and  their  arrangements,  may  be  of  immense  use  to  the  novelist, 
the  essayist,  the  traveller — in  short,  to  the  author  of  any  book 
that  can  command  a  circulation  and  a  public  demand.  And 
such  an  agent  is  Mr.  A.  P.  Watt."  Amongst  the  many  clients 
of  the  firm  are  :  The  Rt.  Hon.  A.  J.  Balfour,  M.P.,  Mr.  Egerton 
Castle,  the  Rt.  Hon.  Winston  Spencer  Churchill,  M.P.,  Miss 
Marie  Corelli,  Mr.  S.  R.  Crockett,  Sir  Arthur  Conan  Doyle,  Miss 
Fowler,  Mr.  Charles  Garvice,  Mr.  H.  Rider  Haggard,  Miss 
Harraden,  Mr.  Robert  S.  Hichens,  Mr.  Anthony  Hope,  the 
Baroness  von  Hutten,  Mr.  Rudyard  Kipling,  "  Lucas  Malet," 
Mr.  A.  E.  W.  Mason,  the  Baroness  Orczy,  "  Q,"  Mr.  H.  A. 
Vachell,  Mr.  Stanley  J.  Weyman,  &c. 

WIENER  AGENCY  (THE),  LTD.,  64  Strand,  London,  w.c,  and 
Tribune  Building,  New  York,  U.S.A.  Every  facility  for  the 
placing  of  literary  work  in  England  and  in  America,  and  for 
securing  copyright  in  the  latter  country.  Advice  on  and 
revision  of  MSS.  is  undertaken  for  authors.  Ideas  for  new 
publications  submitted  to  editors  and  publishers.  All  work 
is  scrutinized  by  experienced  readers  in  order  that  MSS.  may 
be  submitted  only  in  suitable  quarters.  Work  unsuited  for 
publication  is  returned  with  criticism  and  suggestions  for 
amendment,  if  desired.  General  advice  given  to  inexperienced 
Writers.    Application  for  prospectus,  sent  post  free,  is_invited.

WRITERS'  BUREAU  (THE),  10  Niton  Street,  Fulham,  S.W. 
(Removed  from  Craven  House,  Kingsway).  Founded  in  1909 
for  the  purpose  of  assisting  authors  to  find  suitable  markets 
for  their  work.  On  the  principle  of  "  helping  an  author  to  help 
himself."  Unknown  writers  are  guided  by  criticism  and 
literary  advice.  The  Bureau  has  at  its  call  experts  in  every 
department  of  literature,  and  is,  therefore,  able  to  provide 
technical  assistance  when  such  is  required  in  the  interest  of 
accuracy.  Literary  business  management  undertaken  for  a 
few  authors  by  special  arrangement.  Director :  William  J. 
Corley,  for  several  years  Honorary  Secretary  to  the  Quill  Club.

Page  as  corrected. 

Do  not  try  to  correct  tho  faults  of  harried  making-ready 
by  a  weak  impression,  and  by  carrying  an  excess  of  ink  to 
hide  the  weakness.  Excess  of  ink  fouls  the  rollers,  clogs  the 
type,  and  makes  the  printed  work  smear  or  set  off.  A  good 
print  cannot  be  had  when  the  impression  is  so  weak  that  the 
paper  barely  touches  tho  ink  on  the  types  and  is  not  pressed 
against  the  types.  There  must  be  force  enough  to  transfer 
the  ink  not  only  on  to  the  paper,  but  into  the  paper.  A  firm 
impression  should  be  had,  even  if  the  paper  be  indented. 
The  amount  of  impression  required  will  largely  depend  on 
the  making-ready.  With  careful  making-ready,  impression 
may  be  light ;  roughly  and  hurriedly  done,  it  must  be  hard ; 
indentation  is  evidence  of  wear  of  type.  The  spring  and 
resulting  friction  of  an  elastic  impression  surface  is  most  felt 
where  there  is  least  resistance — at  the  upper  and  lower  ends 
of  lines  of  Type,  where  they  begin  to  round  off.  It  follows 
that  the  saying  of  time  that  may  be  gained  by  hurried  and 
rough  making-ready  must  be  offset  by  an  increased  wear  of 
type. 

That  impression  is  the  best  for  preventing  wear  of  type 
which  is  confined  to  its  surface  and  never  laps  over  its 
edges.  But  this  perfect  surface  impression  is  possible  only 
on  a  large  forme  with  new  type,  sound,  hard  packing,  and 
ample  time  for  "  making-ready."  If  types  are  worn,  the  in- 
dentation of  the  paper  by  impression  cannot  be  entirely 
prevented :  good  presswork  does  not  depend  entirely  upon 
the  press  or  machine,  neither  on  the  workman,  nor  on  the 
materials.  Nor  will  superiority  in  any  point  compensate 
for  deficiency  in  another :  new  type  will  suffer  from  a  poor 
roller,  and  careful  making-ready  is  thrown  away  if  poor  ink 
be  used!  It  is  necessary  that  all  the  materials  shall  be 
good,  that  they  should  be  adapted  to  each  other  and  fitly 
used.  A  good  workman  can  do  much  with  poor  materials, 
but  a  neglect  to  comply  with  one  condition  often  produces 
as  bad  a  result  as  the  neglect  of  all.     If  the  foregoing  facta

Synopsis  of  Readers'  Marks. 

CJ  This  indicates  that  the  line  has  to  be  indented  one  em 
of  its  own  body. 

©  A  full-stop  or  full-point  has  to  be  inserted. 

Trs.  A  transposition  of  a  word  or  words. 

Ital.  Change  roman  into  italic.  Also  indicated  by  under- 
lining the  word  or  words  to  be  in  italic. 

L  o.  A  capital  or  small  capital  to  be  changed  to  a  lower- 
case letter. 

Bom.  Change  italic  into  roman. 

Cap.  A  lower-case  or  small  capital  letter  to  be  changed  to 
a  capital. 

8m.  Oap.  A  lower-case  or  capital  letter  to  be  changed  to 
a  small  capital. 

X  A  bad  or  battered  letter. 

Btet.  Matter  wrongly  altered  shall  remain  as  it  was.  Dots 
are  usually  placed  under  the  matter  in  question. 

f  A  space  has  to  be  inserted. 

L  Space  to  be  reduced, 
c*  A  turned  letter. 

New  par.  or  n.  p.  or  [  Commence  a  fresh  line. 

Bun  on.  Sentence  not  to  commence  a  new  line,  but  to 
follow  on  previous  matter. 

8  Delete  or  expunge. 

JL  A  space  or  quadrat  standing  high  to  be  p ashed  down. 

w.f.  Denotes  a  wrong-fount  letter. 

Equal.  Equalize  spacing  throughout  the  line. 

J  The  matter  has  something  foreign  bet  ween  the  lines,  or 
a  wrong-fount  space  in  the  line,  causing  the  types  to 
get  crooked. 

j  When  a  superior  letter  or  inverted  comma  is  required  to  be 
inserted  in  the  matter,  it  is  usually  written  over  this  sign. 

3  The  words  or  letters  over  which  this  is  marked  to  bo 
joined.

A  FEW  OF  THE 

BIBLIOGRAPHICAL  AND  TYPOGRAPHICAL  TERMS 

IN  MORE  GENERAL  USE 

Books  are  defined  respectively  as  folio,  quarto  (4to),  octavo 
(8vo),  duodecimp  (i2mo),  sextodecimo  (i6mo),  octodecimo 
(i8mo),  vigesimo-quarto  (24mo),  trigesimo-secundo  (32mo), 
etc.  These  definitions  are  arrived  at  by  the  number  of 
times  a  sheet  is  folded  ;  for  instance,  a  folio  would  be  two 
leaves  to  the  sheet,  a  quarto  four  leaves,  and  so  on.  Some- 
times it  is  difficult  to  distinguish,  say,  an  octavo  from  a 
quarto  ;  but  if  it  is  printed  on  a  laid  paper,  the  water-lines 
that  run  at  intervals  through  the  sheet  (not  to  be  confused 
with  the  smaller  and  closer  wire-marks  of  the  sheet)  will 
determine  this.  The  water-lines  on  a  folio,  octavo,  and 
octodecimo  would  be  perpendicular  ;  on  a  quarto,  duo- 
decimo, and  sextodecimo,  horizontal.  The  signatures  or 
letters  placed  at  the  foot  of  the  first,  and  sometimes  the 
third,  page  also  serve  as  a  guide  to  identification. 

Advance  sheets  or  copies.  Printed  in  advance  for  simul- 
taneous publication  elsewhere,  or  for  preliminary 
notices. 

Ampersand.  The  abbreviation  or  sign  for  the  word 
"  and  "  :  thus,  &  (roman),  &  (italic),  &  (black-letter). 

Antiqua.    A  German  expression  for  Roman  types. 

Bastard  title.  The  fly-  or  half-title  before  the  full  title  of  the 
work. 

Black-letter.  A  general  expression  used  to  indicate  old 
English  text  or  church  type. 

Block.  A  general  term  used  as  embracing  woodcut,  electro, 
or  zinco. 

Digitized  by  V3OOQLC 


500  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

Body  of  the  work.    The  text  or  subject-matter  is  thus 

described   to   distinguish  it   from    the    preliminary, 

appendix,  or  notes. 
Broadside,    A  sheet  printed  on  one  side  only. 
Calendered  paper.     Paper  very  highly  rolled  or  glazed,  much 

used  for  printing  illustrated  books  or  magazines. 
Cast  off.    To  calculate  or  estimate  length  of  copy  to  be 

printed — a   troublesome   task   in   uneven   or   badly- 
written  MS. 
Clarendon.    A  bold   or  fat-faced  type    is  generally  thus 

described.    The  older  founts  were  called  "  Egyptian." 
Clerical  errors.     Mistakes  in  copying  MS. 
Clichi.     French  term  for  a  cast,  usually  applied  to  stereo 

or  electro  duplicates. 
Collate.    To  run  through  the  sheets  of  a  book  to  see  if  the 

signatures  are  in  sequence. 
Colophon.    An  inscription  or  tail-piece,  usually  a  printer's 

imprint,  at  the  end  of  a  book. 
Cut  edges.    A  book  which  has  been  cut  on  the  three  sides 

is  said  to  have  cut  edges. 
Cut-in  notes.    Side-notes  which  are  inserted  within  the 

text  at  the  side  instead  of  in  the  margin. 
Deckle.    The  raw  rough  edge  of  handmade  paper  is  thus 

termed. 
Dummy  copy.    A  copy  generally  made  up  of  blank  leaves 

to  represent  the  actual  bulk  of  a  work  not  quite  com- 
plete. 
End  leaves  or  papers.    The  blank  fly-leaves  at  either  end 

of  a  book. 
Fly-title.    The  half-title  in  front  of  the  general  title,  or 

which  divides  sections  of  a  work. 
Folio.    A  sheet  of  paper  folded  in  two  leaves  only. 
Fore-edge.    The  outer  side  of  .   jook  (distinct  from  the  head 

or  tail)  when  folded. 
Format.    The  bibliographical  term  for  size  and  shape  of  a 

book. 
Fount.    This  term  is  applied  to  the  whole  number  of  letters 

constituting  a  complete  set  of  types  of-  any  particular 

class  of  face  or  body. 
Fractur.    A  German  expression  for  their  text  or  black-letter 

characters. 

Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


TYPOGRAPHICAL  AND  OTHER  TERMS   501 

Half -bound,    Books  partly  bound  in  leather,  with  cloth  or 

paper  sides. 
Half -title.    The  sub-title  in  front  of  the  full  title. 
Heads.    A  term  applied  to  the  margins  at  the  tops  of  the 

pages. 
Indent.    To  set  a  line  some  little  distance  back,  as  in  the 

case  of  a  fresh  paragraph. 
Inferior  figures  and  letters.    Made  to  range  at  the  bottom 

of  letters,  thus  :   u8aeio 
Leaded  matter.    Types  with  leads  between  the  lines,  in 

contradistinction  to  "  solid  "  matter. 
Letterpress.     Printed  matter  from  type,  as  distinct  from 

lithographic  or  plate  printing. 
Lower-case  letters.     The  small  letters  as  used  here,  a,  b,  c, 

d,  etc. 
Moulds.     Generally  understood  as  the  preliminary  stage  in 

stereotyping  by  paper  process. 
Octavo.    A  sheet  of  paper  folded  into  eight,  shortly  written 

thus  :  8vo. 
Offset.    The  set-off  of  ink  from  one  sheet  to  another,  the 

result  of  insufficient  drying  or  bad  ink. 
Preliminary.    Any  matter  coming  before  the  text  of  a 

work  :  title,  preface,  contents,  etc. 
Proof.     A  trial  print  of  any  forme  of  type,  plates,  or  blocks. 
Pulls.    A  term  generally  applied  to  proofs. 
Quarto.    A  sheet  folded  into  four  leaves  ;  shortly  :  4to. 
Quire.    Twenty-four  sheets.     Books  in  sheets,  that  is,  not 

bound,  are  said  to  be  in  quires. 
Quotes.    A  printer's  term  for  turned  commas  (")  and  apos- 
trophes ("),  placed  respectively  at  the  beginning  and 

end  of  quoted  matter. 
Ream.    Twenty  quires.     A  printer's  ream  is  516  sheets ; 

handmade  and  drawing  papers  sometimes  472,  480,  or 

500  sheets. 
Recto.    The  right-hand  pages  of  any  work. 
Rubricated.    Printed  in  red  ink. 
Serif.    The  fine  line  on  the  top  and  bottom  of  a  letter,  as 

in  H.     Sanserif  thus  :  H. 
Set-off.     Where  the  ink  sets  off  from  one  sheet  to  another. 
Shoulder  notes.    Marginal  notes  placed  at  the  top  corner  of 

the  page. 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


502  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  11. 

Signature.    The  letter  or  figure  at  the  foot  of  the  lust  page 

of  a  sheet  to  guide  the  binder  in  folding  ;  also  used  by 

printers  to  identify  any  particular  sheet. 
Size  copy.  A  thickness  or  dummy  copy  [q.v.]. 
Slips.    Applied  to  matter  not  made  up  into  pages,  but 

pulled  as  proofs  in  long  slips. 
Small  capitals     The  smaller  capitals  (as  distinct  from  the 

full  capitals),  thus  :  Standing.     Indicated  in  MS.  by 

two  strokes  (=)  underneath. 
Standing.    Type  not  distributed  after  printing  is  said  to 

be  "  standing/' 
Stereotypes.     Casts  of  pages  of  types,  etc.,  in  metal,  either 

by  "  plaster  "  or  "  paper  "  process. 
Superior  letters  and  figures.    Small  letters  cast  at  the  top  of 

the  shoulder  of  type,  used  for  references  or  abbrevia- 
tions, thus  : 1  *  8  Mr  N°. 
Swash  letters.     Seventeenth-century   italic   capitals,   with 

tails  and  flourishes,  thus  :  j£  (M  C^9  etc. 
Tails.    The  bottoms  or  tail  ends  of  pages. 
Turned  commas.    Used  at  the  beginning  of  quoted  matter, 

thus,  " — and  followed  at  the  end  by".    Sometimes 

called  "  inverted  commas." 
Typography.    The  art  or  style  of  printing  from  movable 

letters. 
Uncut  edges.     Books  not  cut  down,  but  not  necessarily 

"  unopened." 
Unopened  edges.    Applied  to  books  the  edges  of  which  have 

not  been  opened. 
Verso.    The  obverse  or  back  of  a  leaf,   the  reverse  of 

"  recto  "  [q.v.]. 
Wrong  fount.    Letters  of  a  different  character  or  series 

mixed  with  another  fount. 

[From  Some  Notes  on  Books  and  Printing,  by  C.  T.  Jacobi.]

A  LIST  OF 

TECHNICAL  TERMS  IN  THE  VARIOUS 

PROCESSES   OF    REPRODUCTION 

*  Processes  or  engraved  plates  which  must  be  printed  separately. 

*  Aquatint.     Etching  on  copper  or  steel  through  grounds 

of  finely-powdered  resinous  substances. 
*Autogravure.    A  copper-plate  photo-etched  from  a  bichro- 
mated  gelatine  basis. 

*  Autotype.    Prints  reproduced  by  means  of  certain  bichro- 

mated  gelatine  processes  (carbon  or  collotype). 
*Chromograph.    A  reproduction  of  a  coloured  original  by 

litho,  photo-litho,  or  photo-etched  blocks. 
^Collotype.    A  photo  reproduction  printed  in  ink  from  a 

surface  of  sensitized  and  exposed  gelatine. 
Cuts.    Any  impression  from  a  type  block. 
Electrotype.    A  copper-faced  facsimile  of  a  block  or  plate 
produced  from  a  wax  mould  in  a  depositing  battery. 

*  Etching.     An  impression  from  a  copper  or  steel  plate,  the 

lines  of  which  have  been  drawn  by  hand  and  etched 
with  or  without  an  acid. 
*Ferro-print.      Ferro-gallic    and    ferro-prussiate.      Prints 
made  from  salts  of  iron  sensitizers,  principally  used 
for  plans  and  copying  tracings  in  blue  or  black  lines. 
*Glyptotype.    A  print  produced  by  the  Woodbury-type 
process  transferred  to  plate  paper. 
Half-tint  blocks.    See  Tint  blocks. 
Half-tone  blocks.    See  Tint  blocks. 
*Heliotype.    A  process  of  collotype  printing. 
India  proofs.    An  impression  on  China,  Japan,  or  India 
paper,  from  an  engraved  plate. 
Hntaglio,  sunk  or  hollowed.    An  etched  plate  having  sunk 
lines  or  picture. 

503 


■ 


504  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

*Lichtdruck.    Collotype. 
Line  process.    Usually  applied  to  any  photo  process  for 

producing  type  blocks  in  relief. 
^Lithography.      Printing  from   stone  with  ink   specially 

prepared. 
*Lithotint.    An  etching  executed  on  stone. 
*  Mezzotint,  mixed  (as  in  Turner's  Liber  Studiorum).     The 
subject  first  etched  on  steel  or  copper  to  secure  the 
forms,  then  rocked  over  as  in  pure  mezzotint,  and  the 
subject  "  scraped  out." 
*Mezzotint9  pure.    The  plate,  steel  or  copper,  first  roughened 
by  a  rocking-tool  till  the  surface  partakes  more  or  less 
of  the  nature  of  a  very  fine  file ;  then  the  subject 
"  scraped  out "  to  the  various  degrees  of  light  and 
shade  required,  and  burnished  where  very  high  lights 
are  wanted. 
Monotint.    Tint  printing  in  a  single  colour. 
Nickelface.    The  coating  of  an  engraved  plate  or  block 
with  a  battery-deposited  surface  of  nickel. 
*Photocrome.    A  process  of  reproducing  photographs  in 

colours. 
*Photo-chromotypy.    Photo-etched  type  blocks  for  printing 

in  colours. 
^Photogravure.    A  photo-engraved  plate  in  lines  or  tones 

for  copper-plate  printing,  or  impressions  therefrom. 
^Photo-lithography.    The  process  of  printing  from  stone 

by  means  of  a  photographic  transfer. 
Photo-zinco.     A  photo-etched  type  block. 
Process   blocks.     Blocks    produced  by  a  photo  -  etching 

process. 
Process    work.      Work   produced   by    a    photo  -  etching 

process. 
Relief  printing,    (a)  Printing  from  type-blocks,  (b)  printing 

letters  or  designs  in  relievo. 
Remarque  proofs.  Original  impressions  from  steel  or  copper 
engraved  plates,  and  which  usually  have  a  device  on 
the  margin  instead  of  a  title. 
*Silverprints.    Usually  defines  a  chloride  of  silver  print — 

an  ordinary  photograph. 
Stereotypes.  Casts  in  stereotype  metal  (lead  and  zinc)  from 
type  or  type  blocks. 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  TECHNICAL  TERMS  505 

^Three-colour  blocks.  Blocks  chemically  produced  from 
nature  or  pictures  in  colours  by  means  of  a  photo  pro- 
cess, and  which  render  all  the  colours  of  the  original. 

Tint  blocks.   Type  blocks  of  wash  drawings  or  photographs 
produced  by  means  of  ruled  dots  or  tints. 

Vignettes.  Usually  applied  to  illustrations  which  are  shaded 

or  softened  off  at  the  edges. 
*Woodbury  Prints.     Prints  which  closely  resemble  ordinary 
photographs,  produced  by  a  process  invented  by  Mr. 
W.  B.  Woodbury. 

Woodcuts.    Wood  blocks  or  impressions  from  the  same. 

Xylography.     Generally  applied   to   the  printing  of  old 
block-books.
