LAW   AND    LETTERS 

We  begin  with  a  sketch  of  the  law  of  copyright,  including 
the  right  of  public  representation  of  dramatic  and  musical 
pieces.  After  dealing  with  the  law  as  it  is,  with  due  regard 
to  the  cases  decided  since  the  last  publication  of  the  Year- 
Book,  we  shall  discuss  the  relations  of  authors  and 
publishers,  and  shall  conclude  with  a  brief  consideration  of 
the  usual  clauses  in  agreements  ordinarily  entered  into 
between  author  and  publisher,  explaining  where  necessary 
their  use  and  application. 

I.  LAW  OF  COPYRIGHT 

It  is  convenient  to  deal  separately  with  the  law  ex- 
clusively regarding  the  United  Kingdom  or  Home  copy- 
right, and  with  Colonial  and  International  copyrights. 

HOME   COPYRIGHT 

The  heads  under  which  the  law  treats  the  subject-matter 
are  as  follows  : 

i.  Books. 

2.  Dramatic  pieces. 

3.  Musical  compositions. 

4.  Lectures. 

5.  Engravings  and  other  works  of  the  same  kind. 

6.  Fine  arts,  including  paintings,  drawings,  and  photo- 
graphs. 

7.  Sculpture. 

The  law  consists  partly  of  common  law  principles,  and 
partly  of  statute  law,  which  is  contained  in  the  provisions 

507 

Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


) 


508  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

of  the  several  Acts  of  Parliament,  which  are  next  stated  in 
order  of  date,  with  their  titles,  namely  : 

8  Geo.  II.,  c.  13 — Engraving  Copyright  Act,  1734. 

7  Geo.  III.,  c.  38 — Engraving  Copyright  Act,  1766. 

15  Geo.  III.,  c.  53— Copyright  Act,  1775. 

17  Geo.  III.,  c.  57 — Prints  Copyright  Act,  1777. 

54  Geo.  III.,  c.  56 — Sculpture  Copyright  Act,  1814. 

3  &  4  Wm.  IV.,  c.  15 — Dramatic  Copyright  Act,  1833. 

5  &  6  Wm.  IV.,  c.  65— Lectures  Copyright  Act,  1835. 

6  &  7  Wm.  IV.,  c.  59 — Prints  and  Engravings  Copy- 
right (Ireland)  Act,  1836. 

5  &  6  Vict.,  c.  45 — Copyright  Act,  1842  (Books,  &c). 

15  Vict.,  c.  12 — International  Copyright  Act,  1852  (ex- 
planatory of  the  Prints  and  Engravings  Copyright  Acts 
above). 

25  &  26  Vict.,  c.  68 — Fine  Arts  Copyright  Act,  1862. 

45  &  46  Vict.,  c.  40 — Copyright  (Musical  Compositions) 
Act,  1882. 

51  &  52  Vict.,  c.  17 — Copyright  (Musical  Compositions) 
Act,  1888. 

2  Edward  VII.,  c.  15 — Musical  (Summary  Proceed- 
ings) Copyright  Act,  1902. 

6  Edward  VII.,  c.  30 — Musical  Copyright  Act,  1906. 

Unpublished  Matter 

As  regards  any  of  the  above  subject-matter  which  has 
not  been  published,  the  author  or  owner  has  by  the  common 
law  an  absolute  right,  so  long  as  it  remains  unpublished, 
to  prevent  the  publication  of  any  copy  of  it. 

The  communication  to  others  in  a  limited  way  or  on 
the  faith  that  there  will  not  be  publication  (what  may  be 
termed  "  private  publication  ")  does  not  destroy  such  right. 
For  example,  communication  by  sending  a  letter,  or  by 
delivering  an  address  to  a  limited  audience,  or  communica- 
tion made  to  an  employe  in  the  course  of  his  employment, 
is  not  publication  in  the  legal  sense. 

I.  Copyright  in  Books 
Generally 

This  copyright  endures  for  the  life  of  the  author  and 
seven  years  after  his  death,  or  for  forty-two  years  from 


Digitized  by  C3OOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  509 

the  date  of  publication,  whichever  is  the  longer,  and  is 
by  the  Copyright  Act,  1842,  declared  to  be  the  property 
of  the  author  and  his  assigns,  but  in  the  case  of  a  book 
published  after  the  author's  death,  the  copyright  endures 
for  forty-two  years  from  the  date  of  first  publication,  and 
is  by  the  same  Act  declared  to  be  the  property  of  the 

Proprietor  of  the  author's  manuscript  from  which  such 
00k  shall  be  first  published,  and  his  assigns.  The  term 
"  book  "  includes  every  volume,  part  or  division  of  a 
volume,  pamphlet,  sheet  of  letterpress,  sheet  of  music, 
map,  chart,  or  plan,  separately  published  ;  and  under  one 
or  other  of  these  heads  protection  has  been  extended  to — 
J^Maps, 

Newspapers, 

Compilations  oi  various  kinds — e.g.,  directories  and 
catalogues,  annotations  of  standard  works,  new  editions 
with  substantial  alterations  and  additions, 

And  even  verbatim  reports  of  speeches  (see  Walter  v. 
Lane  [1900,  A.C.  539],  the  case  01  the  Times  reports  of 
Lord  Rosebefy's  speeches). 

Before  dealing  with  recent  legal  decisions  that  are  of 
most  interest  to  publishers  and  authors,  it  is  necessary  to 
refer  shortly  to  the  Imperial  Copyright  Conference.  This 
important  Conference  was  held  under  the  chairmanship 
of  the  Right  Honourable  Sydney  Buxton,  M.P.,  President 
of  the  Board  of  Trade,  in  May,  1910,  and  was  attended  by 
representatives  from  the  Colonies,  as  well  as  our  own 
Government  departments.  The  Colonial  representatives 
were :  The  Hon.  Sydney  Fisher  (Canada),  the  Right  Hon. 
Lord  Tennyson  (G.C.M.G.  (Australia),  the.  Hon.  Sir  W. 
Hall  Jones,  K.C.M.G.  (New  Zealand),  the  Hon.  Sir  Richard 
Solomon,  K.C.B.  (South  Africa),  the  Hon.  Sir  Edward 
Morris,  K.C.  (Newfoundland).  The  representatives  met  on 
seven  occasions,  and  deliberated  as  to  how  the  existing 
uniformity  of  law  on  copyright  could  best  be  maintained, 
and  in  what  respect  the  existing  law  should  be  altered, 
the  basis  for  discussion  being  the  revised  International 
Copyright  Convention.  This  Convention,  it  will  be  remem- 
bered, was  the  outcome  of  the  International  Conference  on 
Copyright,  held  at  Berlin  in  the  autumn  of  1908,  and  was 
provisionally  signed  by  the  British  representatives  of  His 

Digitized  by  CjOOQlC 


5io  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  it. 

Majesty's  Government.  The  Convention*  however,  con- 
tained a  number  of  alterations  in  the  existing  law,  upon 
which  it  was  thought  desirable  to  ascertain  the  views  of 
our  Colonial  subjects,  even  though  a  strong  Departmental 
Committee,  presided  over  by  Lord  Gorell,  reported  in 
December,  1909,  that  in  their  opinion  the  convention 
should  be  substantially  ratified. 

The  Conference  have  now  issued  the  result  of  their  de- 
liberations in  the  form  of  twelve  resolutions  and  a  draft 
Bill  of  thirty-eight  sections  for  the  consolidation  and 
amendment  of  the  law  of  copyright.  Until  this  Bill,  or 
something  similar,  has  passed  into  law*  any  criticism  of  its 
provisions  would  be  superfluous;  but  its  motive  is  un- 
doubtedly to  bring  the  law  into  uniformity  with  the  Berlin 
Convention,  and  to  widen  the  definition  of  copyright  and 
give  additional  protection  to  copyright  holders. 

The  decision  of  the  House  of  Lords  in  Hulion  and  Co.  v. 
Jones  [1910],  A.C.  20,  is  by  far  the  most  important  decision 
of  the  year  to  the  literary  world.  In  that  case  the  appel- 
lants, who  were  owners  and  publishers  of  a  newspaper 
called  the  Sunday  Chronicle,  upon  the  staff  of  which  the 
respondent  had  formerly  been,  published  in  an  article 
defamatory  statements  of  a  named  person  believed  by  the 
author  of  the  article  and  the  editor  to  be  fictitious.  The 
name  used  was  that  of  the  respondent.  At  the  trial,  before 
Mr.  Justice  Channell  and  a  jury,  friends  of  the  respondent 
gave  evidence  that  they  had  read  the  libel,  and  believed  it 
to  refer  to  the  respondent.  It  was  admitted  that  neither  the 
writer,  the  editor*  nor  the  appellants  intended  to  libel  the 
respondent ;  nevertheless*  the  House  of  Lords  affirmed  the 
decision  of  the  Court  of  Appeal,  and  held  that  in  an  action 
for  libel  it  is  no  defence  to  prove  that  the  defendant  did 
not  intend  to  defame  the  plaintiff,  if  reasonable  people 
would  think  the  language  used  defamatory  to  the  plaintiff. 
This  being  the  law*  it  behoves  all  authors  to  be  careful  by 
what  names  they  designate  characters  in  their  books,  plays, 
etc.*  lest  they  find  that  they  unwittingly  have  libelled  some 
living  person. 

Two  more  cases  are  worthy  of  notice  :  Weatherby  and  Sons 
v.  The  International  Horse  Agency  and  Exchange,  Limited 
[i9io]y  2  Ch.  297 ;  and  Crotch  v.  Arnold  (54  S.J.  49).    In 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  511 

the  first  the  plaintiffs  brought  an  action  to  restrain  the 
defendants  from  printing,  publishing,  or  selling  any  copy 
of  a  book  called  Bruce-Lowe  Figures  to  Stud-Book,  vol.  xxi., 
which  contained  practically  the  whole  of  the  list  of  brood 
mares  published  in  vol.  xxi.  of  the  General  Stud  Book. 
This  General  Stud  Book  (a  fresh  volume  of  which  appears 
every  four  years)  contains  lists  of  all  the  thoroughbred 
mares  at  the  stud  in  this  country,  and  is  entered  and 
registered  at  Stationers'  Hall.  The  defendants  contended 
that  they  had  the  right  to  make  use  of  the  list  in  question, 
that  there  can  be  no  copyright  in  a  mere  list  of  names, 
and  that  the  plaintiffs  had  suffered  no  damage,  as  their 
book  would  be  likely  to  increase  the  sales  of  the  Stud  Book. 
Mr.  Justice  Parker,  after  reserving  judgment,  decided  that 
the  lists  were  not  such  mere  lists  of  names  as  to  be  incapable 
of  copyright ;  that  an  action  to  restrain  infringement  of 
copyright  could  be  brought,  though  no  damage  could  be 
proved ;  and  that  the  defendants,  in  spite  of  their  having 
acted  throughout  with  entire  bona  fides,  must  be  restrained 
from  selling  the  infringing  matter.  In  the  second  case 
Mr.  Justice  Swinfen  Eady  decided  that  an  injunction  will 
not  be  granted  to  restrain  the  sale  of  a  book  on  the  ground 
that  it  bears  the  same  title  as  a  book  written  by  the  plaintiff. 
This  view  of  the  law  follows  the  opinions  of  the  judges  who 
in  1881  decided  Dicks  v.  Yates  (18  Ch.  Div.  77). 

The  case  of  Macmillan  v.  Dent  (1907,  1  Ch.  107)  raised 
some  interesting  questions  as  to  the  right  to  publish  post- 
humous letters.  The  case  was  decided  by  Mr.  Justcie 
Kekewich  in  December,  1905,  and  his  decision  was  upheld 
in  the  Court  of  Appeal  by  Lord  Justices  Vaughan  Williams, 
Moulton,  and  Buckley  in  November,  1906.  The  letters  in 
question  were  written  by  Charles  Lamb,  and  had  not  been 
published  in,  or  prior  to,  1895,  when  the  copyright  therein 
was  sold  and  the  actual  letters  lent  by  A.  B.,  in  whose  pos- 
session they  then  were,  to  Messrs.  Smith*  Elder  and  Co.,  by 
whom  a  licence  to  print  them  was  granted  to  the  plaintiffs. 
The  letters  were  subsequently  published  by  the  plaintiffs 
from  copies  taken  by  them.  Some  years  later  the  defendant 
bought  the  actual  letters  from  A.  B.,  having  been  previously 
informed  by  A.  B.  of  the  said  assignment  by  him  to  the 
plaintiffs  of  the  copyright  therein,  and  published  the  letters 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


512  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  11. 

from  the  originals  in  an  edition  of  Lamb's  letters.  The 
plaintiffs,  having  registered  themselves  as  proprietors  of 
the  copyright,  sued  the  defendant  to  restrain  publication 
of  any  book  infringing  their  riehts  in  the  letters,  and  it  was 
decided  and  upheld  on  appeal  that  they  were  the  assigns 
(under  Section  3  of  the  Copyright  Act,  1842)  of  the  author's 
manuscript — that  is,  of  the  actual  paper  on  which  the 
writing  was  and  the  writing  upon  it — and  therefore  that 
the  copyright  in  the  letters  was  the  property  of  the  plaintiffs 
as  such  assigns,  and  that  the  right  of  publication  was 
vested  in  them.  A.  B.,  having  the  letters  rightfully  in  his 
possession,  was  entitled  to  publish  them  himself,  or  to  hand 
them  over  to  the  plaintiffs  for  publication,  and  to  give  them 
the  right  of  publication*  and  having  done  the  last,  A.  B. 
had  nothing  remaining  in  him  to  pass  to  anyone,  except 
the  right  to  the  letters  themselves,  which  he  had  retained* 
and  could  part  with — e.g.,  to  a  collector  of  autographs, 
but  without  any  right  of  publication. 

In  the  case  of  Mansell  v.  The  Valley  Printing  Company 
and  Rankine  (1908,  2  Ch.  441),  the  exclusive  right  of  an 
author  of  any  unpublished  literary  production  or  work  of 
art  to  publish  it  or  not,  as  he  may  think  fit,  and  to  restrain 
others  from  publishing  it,  or  obtain  damages  for  any  such 
publication,  was  conclusively  confirmed.  The  Printing 
Company  had  bona  fide  purchased  and  published  designs 
reproduced  from  photographs  of  the  originals  by  the  de- 
fendant Rankine  without  the  knowledge  of  the  owner  of  the 
copyright ;  but  they  were  held  liable  in  damages  to  the 
plaintiff,  and  were  ordered  to  deliver  up  all  copies  of  the 
designs. 

EncyclopcediaSy  Periodicals^  and  other  Proprietary  Works 

As  regards  literary  work  composed  for  publication  in 
or  as  part  of  any  encyclopaedia,  review,  magazine,  periodical 
work,  or  serial  publication,  by  any  persons  employed  to 
compose  the  same  on  the  terms  that  the  copyright  should 
belong  to  the  proprietor  or  publisher,  after  the  work  has 
been  paid  for  by  such  proprietor  or  publisher,  the  copy- 
right in  all  such  work  becomes  the  property  of  the  pro- 
prietor or  publisher,  subject,  however,  to  the  following 
qualification  in  the  case  of  contributions  to  periodicals  : 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  513 

After  twenty-eight  years  from  first  publication  the  right 
of  separate  pubfication  reverts  to  the  author,  and  endures 
for  the  remainder  of  the  full  term  of  copyright,  and  during 
the  twenty-eight  years  the  publisher  or  proprietor  may  not 
publish  them  separately  without  the  consent,  previously 
obtained,  of  the  author  or  his  assigns  (see  Section  18  of 
the  Copyright  Act,  1842). 

The  contract  between  the  contributing  author  and  the 
publisher  or  proprietor  of  the  periodical  work  need  not 
be  in  writing,  nor  is  an  express  agreement  that  the  copy- 
right shall  belong  to  the  latter  necessary. 

It  was  laid  down  many  years  ago  that,  where  the  pro- 
prietors of  a  periodical  employ  a  person  to  write  an  article, 
or  a  series  of  articles  or  reports,  expressly  for  publication 
therein,  it  is  necessarily  implied  that  the  copyright  of  the 
articles  so  written  for  such  periodical,  and  paid  for  by  the 
proprietors  and  publishers  thereof,  is  the  property  of  such 
proprietors  and  publishers.  (By  the  Court  of  Common 
Pleas  in  Sweet  v.  Benning  (1855,  *6  C.B.y  459)*  where  the 
periodical  was  a  weekly  paper  called  the  Jurist  which  prin- 
cipally consisted  of  law  reports.)  The  principle  of  that 
case  was  recently  approved  by  the  House  of  Lords  and 
applied  to  articles  contributed  to  an  encyclopaedia  of 
sport  in  Lawrence  and  BuUen  v.  Aflalo  and  Cook  (1904, 
A.C.  17),  and  it  is  conceived  that  if  an  author  who  has 
been  paid  for  his  work  claims  separate  copyright  in  con- 
tributions to  any  of  the  different  kinds  of  publications 
mentioned  above,  he  will,  in  the  absence  of  an  express 
agreement  reserving  his  right,  have  great  difficulty  in 
negativing  an  implied  agreement  that  the  copyright  should 
belong  to  the  proprietor  or  publisher.  But  the  question  is 
one  01  fact  as  well  as  law,  and  will  always  depend  upon  the 
particular  circumstances  of  each  case. 

If  an  author  specially  reserve  the  right  of  separate 
publication  of  any  contributed  work,  he  will  then  be 
entitled  to  the  copyright  in  it  when  published  separately 
at  any  time,  but  without  prejudice  to  the  right  of  the  pro- 
prietor or  publisher  to  copyright  in  the  production  of 
which  it  formed  part. 

Whether  a  proprietary  work  is  published  in  serial  form 
or  otherwise,  employment  of  the  author  by  the  proprietor 

Digitized  by  LjOOQLC 


514  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

to  compose  the  work,  the  composition  of  the  work  by  the 
author  under  such  employment  on  the  terms  that  the 
copyright  shall  belong  to  the  proprietor,  and  payment  for 
the  work  by  the  proprietor,  are  conditions  precedent  to 
the  vesting  of  the  copyright  therein  in  the  proprietor.  In 
the  case  of  Ward,  Lock  and  Co.,  Limited,  v.  John  Long 
(1906,  2  Ch.,  550)  these  requisites  were  much  discussed  in 
rather  peculiar  circumstances. 

It  appeared  that  the  author  submitted  through  his 
agent  a  synopsis  of  a  story  to  the  plaintiffs,  and  an 
agreement  by  letters  in  December,  1004,  was  come  to 
by  which  the  plaintiffs  agreed  to  purchase  the  copyright 
of  the  story,  which  was  to  consist  of  80,000  words,  for 
the  sum  of  £200,  of  which  £160  was  subsequently  paid. 
But  when  the  words  of  the  story  came  to  be  counted  it 
was  found  to  consist  of  not  more  than  about  70,000.  That 
being  so,  thtie  plaintiffs  refused  to  pay  the  remaining  £40, 
the  balance  of  the  purchase-money.  Whereupon  the 
author  treated  the  contract  as  void,  and  sold  the  rights 
of  the  story  to  the  defendants,  who  knew  nothing  about 
the  above-mentioned  agreement  with  the  plaintiffs.  The 
plaintiffs,  who  were  duly  registered  as  proprietors  of  copy- 
right>  claimed  to  be  the  owners  of  the  complete  copyright 
m  the  book.  The  main  defence  was  that  the  plaintiffs,  as 
publishers,  employed  the  author  to  compress  the  story  and 
that  the  purchase  price  of  £200  had  not  been  paid. 

The  Judge  was  so  impressed  with  the  importance  and 
difficulty  of  the  case  that  he  adjourned  it  for  further 
argument.  He  thought  that  an  agreement  to  assign 
copyright,  if  in  writing,  might  be  an  assignment  of  copy- 
right. The  difficulty  was  to  say  whether  it  was  in  itself 
necessarily  an  assignment.  Section  18  of  the  Act  of  1842 
contemplated  the  employment  of  some  author.  What 
was  the  meaning  of  "  employment "?  There  was  no 
authority  on  the  point.  Some  meaning  must  be  given 
to  it,  and  it  must  be  distinguished  from  assignment.  It 
seemed  quite  possible  to  regard  the  contract  in  the  case  as 
an  employment.  Could  an  agreement  to  assign  something 
not  in  existence  be  an  "  employment "  ?  That  was  the  great 
difficulty  of  the  section  as  regarded  this  ca^e.  But  the 
judge  subsequently  decided  that  the  agree#6ent  was  a  good 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  515 

assignment  of  copyright,  and  the  fact  that  the  plaintiffs 
had  employed  the  author  to  compose  the  book  did  not 
bring  the  case  within  Section  18.  He  pointed  out,  however, 
that  Section  18,  though  it  seemed  at  first  sight  only  to 
apply  to  periodicals,  said  "  any  book/'  and  might  apply 
to  a  single  volume  where  the  parties  so  intended. 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

The  book  must  be  published — 

1.  In  the  United  Kingdom. 

2.  The  book  must  not  have  been  previously  published. 

It  may  be  published  contemporaneously — e.g.,  in 
the  United  States. 

Territorial  Extent 

The  right  extends  to  the  United  Kingdom  and  to  every 
part  of  the  British  dominions. 

Presentation  to  Libraries 

One  complete  copy  of  every  edition  of  every  book 
published  (unless  a  new  edition  is  a  mere  reissue  without 
change)  must  be  delivered  gratuitously  by  the  publisher 
(1)  to  the  British  Museum  without  demand ;  and  on  de- 
mand in  writing  to  (2)  the  Bodleian  Library,  Oxford ; 
(3)  the  Cambridge  University  Library ;  (4)  the  Advocates' 
Library,  Edinburgh  ;  and  (5)  the  Library  of  Trinity  College, 
Dublin.    Default  exposes  the  publisher  to  a  £5  penalty. 

Registration  of  Books 

The  law  provides  for  the  keeping  at  Stationers'  Hall  of 
a  register  wherein  may  be  registered  the  proprietorship  in 
the  copyright  of  books  and  assignments  thereof  and 
licences  affecting  such  copyright,  and  for  inspection  by 
the  public,  and  the  issue  of  stamped  and  certified  copies 
of  entries  which  can  be  given  in  evidence,  and  will  be 
primd  facie  proof  of  the  proprietorship  or  assignment  of 

17—2 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


f 


516  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

copyright  or  licence;  and,  in  order  to  protect  his  right, 
the  owner  of  the  copyright  must  register  in  the  prescribed 
form  (fee  five  shillings). 

No  proceedings  can  be  taken  before  registration,  but 
after  registration  all  infringements  may  be  sued  for,  whether 
they  took  place  before  or  after  registration,  unless  the 
action  is  barred  by  time. 

As  regards  encyclopaedias  and  periodicals  or  serial  pro- 
ductions (including  newspapers),  the  date  of  publication 
of  the  first  volume,  number,  or  part  must  be  registered. 

Assignment  of  Right 

A  registered  owner  may  assign  his  interest,  or  any  portion 
of  his  interest,  by  entry  on  the  register.  An  assignment 
made  otherwise  must  be  in  writing. 

A  recent  case  of  Jude  v.  Reid  (Jade's  Musical  Com- 
positions [1907,  1  Ch.  651])  may  be  mentioned  as  a  warning 
to  parties  to  have  it  clear  on  the  face  of  the  document 
whether  an  assignment  is  intended  or  not.  Under  the 
agreement  there  the  author,  Mr.  Jude,  gave  Mr.  Newsam, 
managing  director  of  the  defendants,  who  were  publishers, 
'  the  sole  and  exclusive  right  1'  to  print  and  publish  the 
work  on  three  conditions.  The  first  was  not  inconsistent 
with  an  assignment  of  the  copyright ;  the  second  was  also 
consistent  with  an  assignment,  but  was  equally  consistent 
with  there  being  no  assignment ;  and  the  third  was  to 
supply  Jude  with  such  copies  as  he  might  require.  Now, 
was  that  a  mere  publisher  and  author  agreement,  or  was 
it  an  assignment  ?  The  conclusion  was  that  the  words 
pointed  to  an  author  and  publisher  agreement. 

Infringements  of  Copyright 

These  are  committed — 

i.  By  publication  in  the  United  Kingdom  of  an  un- 
authorized edition,  or  importation  for  sale  of  foreign 
reprints,  of  a  copyright  work — i.e.,  literary  piracy. 

2.  By  the  illegitimate  appropriation  of  another's  literary 
labour — i.e.,  literary  larceny. 


Digitized  by  C3OOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  517 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

The  statute  law  gives  an  action  for  damages  for — 

1.  Unlawful  printing. 

2.  Importing  unlawfully  printed  books  for  sale  or  hire. 

3.  Knowingly  selling,  or  publishing,  or  exposing  for  sale 
or  hire,  or  possessing  for  sale  or  hire,  unlawfully  printed  or 
imported  books. 

It  also  provides  for  the  seizure  of  unlawfully  imported 
books  by  the  Customs,  and  for  offenders  being  fined  upon 
conviction. 

It  gives  the  property  in  pirated  copies  to  the  registered 
proprietor  of  the  copyright. 

He  may  also  sue  for  an  injunction  to  restrain  infringe- 
ment of  his  copyright. 

Limitations  of  Actions 

Any  such  actions  as  above  must  be  brought  within 
twelve  months  after  the  offence. 

Other  Remedies 

The  common  law  treats  sale  of  a  work  under  the  name 
and  title  of  another  man  or  another  man's  work,  so  as  to 
deceive  the  public,  as  a  fraud,  and  the  offender  is  liable 
to  an  action,  and  may  be  restrained  by  injunction.  There 
is  no  special  period  of  limitation  of  such  action. 

Infringement  and  Fair  Use 

Fair  use  may  be  made  even  of  copyright  works  of  other 
authors  in  the  composition  or  compilation  of  new  books, 
but  the  new  work  must  be  substantially  the  result  of 
independent  labour  and  research.  Thus  the  text  for  a 
new  edition  of  old  MSS.  in  the  British  Museum  must  not 
be  copied  from  an  existing  copyright  edition,  but  from  the 
MSS.  (Parry  v.  Moringi  an  unreported  decision  of  Justice 
Farwell  in  1903,  the  case  of  the  Letters  of  Dorothy  Osborne). 
Dramatization  of  novels  is  not  an  infringement  of  copy- 
right>  but  copies  of  the  words  of  the  play  may  infringe  the 


Digitized  by  C3OOQIC 


5i8  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

copyright  in  the  novel  (Warne  v.  Seebohm  [39  Ch.  Div.  73]* 
the  case  of "  Little  Lord  Fauntleroy  "),  and  the  law  requires 
a  copy  of  every  play  produced  to  be  sent  to  the  Lord  Cham- 
berlain. An  abridgement  may  or  may  not  be  an  infringe- 
ment of  copyright*  according  to  the  use  made  of  the  original 
work. 

Immoral  Books 

There  is  no  copyright  in  matter  which  is — 

1.  Immoral  or  illegal. 

2.  Irreligious. 

3.  Seditious  or  libellous. 

4.  Fraudulent — i.e.,  professing  to  be  what  it  is  not  in 
such  a  manner  as  to  be  a  fraud  upon  purchasers. 


II.  Copyright  in  Dramatic  Pieces 

This  copyright  comprises  two  separate  rights,  the  first 
dealing  with  copyright  in  the  author's  work  as  written 
matter,  and  the  second  with  the  copyright  in  such  work 
as  represented  on  the  stage  or  otherwise  to  the  public. 

(a)  Right  of  Publication  in  Print 
The  law  is  the  same  as  that  of  copyright  in  books  (ante, 

(b)  Right  of  Public  Performance 

The  exclusive  right  of  public  performance  of  dramatic 
pieces,  sometimes  called  playright,  endures  for  forty-two 
years  from  the  first  public  representation,  or  for  the  life 
of  the  author  and  seven  years  after  his  death,  whichever 
is  the  longer.  Such  representation  must  be  given  in  the 
United  Kingdom. 

Registration 

Proprietorship  may  be  registered  at  Stationers'  Hall,  but 
registration  is  not  necessary  in  order  to  take  proceedings 
for  infringement,  this  registration  being  permissive  only, 
and  not  compulsory. 

It  seems  that  the  effect  of  registering  a  statement  in- 


Digitized  by  VjOOQlC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  519 

correct  in  material  particulars  is  merely  to  vitiate  the 
registration,  and  prevent  its  being  accepted  as  primA 
facie  evidence  of  title  (Hardacre  v.  Armstrong  [1905, 
21  T.L.R.  189]). 

Assignment  or  License 

Writing  of  the  author  or  his  agent  is  necessary.  An 
assignment  of  copyright  in  the  printed  book  does  not 
transfer  the  right  of  performance,  unless  such  assignment 
is  registered  with  a  statement  of  the  intention  to  do  so, 
or  unless  the  right  is  clearly  conveyed  by  an  unregistered 
assignment,  and  a  sole  license  to  produce  does  not  amount 
in  law  to  an  assignment  of  the  acting  rights  (Neilson  v. 
Horniman,  1909,  25  T.L.R.  684). 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Every  unauthorized  representation  of  a  dramatic  piece 
exposes  the  offender  to  a  statutory  penalty  of  forty  shillings 
or  full  damages,  and  to  an  action  for  an  injunction  to 
restrain  the  infringement  of  right  (Section  2  of  the  Copy- 
right [Drama]  Act,  1833). 

Limitation  of  Actions 

All  actions  or  proceedings  must  be  brought  within  twelve 
months  after  the  offence. 


III.  Copyright  in  Musical  Compositions 

This  copyright  is  analogous  to  the  copyright  in  dramatic 
pieces,  and  in  like  manner  comprises — 

1.  Right  of  publication  in  print. 

2.  Right  of  public  performance. 

1.  Right  of  Publication  in  Print 

The  law  is  the  same  as  that  of  copyright  in  books 
(ante,  q.v.),  except  in  the  following  particulars:  It  has 
been  decided  that  perforated  music  sheets  for  use  in  a 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


520  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

mechanical  piano  or  organ  are  not  copies  of  music,  and 
copyright  gives  no  protection  against  the  reproduction 
of  music  in  this  manner  (Booseyv.  Whigkt,  [1900]  1  Ch.  122, 
music  for  an  iEolian  organ). 

In  addition  to  the  same  remedies  for  infringement  of 
copyright  as  in  the  case  of  books,  in  the  case  of  music 
under  the  statute  2  Edward  VII.,  c.  15,  a  constable  was 
empowered,  by  order  of  a  magistrate  or  on  the  written 
request  of  the  owner  of  the  copyright,  to  seize  pirated 
copies,  and  the  magistrate  may  order  their  destruction  or 
delivery  up,  but  not  a  perforated  music  roll  for  a  piano- 
player  (Mabe  v.  Connor,  1909,  1  K.B.>  515).  Technical 
difficulties  as  to  applying  this  statute  arose — mainly  owing 
to  the  decision  of  the  Divisional  Court  that  a  summons 
must  be  first  served  on  the  hawker  or  other  offender  (ex 
parte  Francis  [1903,  1  K.B.,  275]).  An  Act  with  more 
stringent  provisions  received  the  Royal  Assent  in  August, 
1906,  by  which  "  every  person  who  prints,  reproduces,  or 
sells,  or  exposes,  offers,  or  has  in  his  possession  for  sale  any 
pirated  copies  of  music,  or  has  in  his  possession  plates  for 
reproducing  pirated  copies  of  music,  shall  (unless  he  proves 
that  he  acted  innocently)  be  guilty  of  an  offence  "  punish- 
able summarily,  and  he  will  be  liable  to  be  fined  £5,  and 
on  a  second  or  subsequent  conviction  to  be  imprisoned  or 
fined  £10.  It  is  also  enacted  that  any  constable  may  take 
into  custody  without  warrant  any  person  who  sells,  exposes, 
offers,  or  has  in  his  possession  for  sale  any  pirated  copies 
of  any  such  music  as  may  be  specified  in  a  writing  addressed 
to  the  chief  of  police,  and  signed  by  the  apparent  owner 
of  the  copyright  or  his  agent>  requesting  the  arrest,  at  the 
risk  of  such  owner,  of  all  offenders  against  the  Act.  And 
a  search  warrant  may  be  granted  in  the  case  of  suspected 
premises. 

2.  Right  of  Public  Performance 

The  law  is  the  same  as  that  of  playright  (see  ante), 
except  in  the  following  particulars :  A  note  of  the  reser- 
vation of  the  right  of  public  performance  must  be  printed 
on  the  title-page  of  every  copy  of  a  musical  compo- 
sition, in  order  to  preserve  the  exclusive  right  of  public 
performance. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  521 

Instead  of  the  penalty  of  forty  shillings  for  every  un- 
authorized performance,  the  penalty  and  the  costs  are  in 
the  discretion  of  the  court. 

IV.  Copyright  in  Lectures 

(a)  By  the  Lectures  Copyright  Act,  1835,  the  author  or 
his  assign  has  the  sole  right  of  printing  and  publishing  a 
lecture,  notwithstanding  delivery.  This  copyright  endures 
for  the  longer  of  the  two  periods  of  twenty-eight  years 
and  the  life  of  the  author. 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

Notice  in  writing  of  the  intended  lecture  must  be  given 
to  two  justices  two  days  before  delivering  it.  The  statute 
does  not  apply  to  a  lecture  delivered  in  a  University  or 
public  school  or  college,  or  on  any  public  foundation,  or 
under  any  gift  or  endowment. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Action  under  the  statute  for  penalties  and  forfeiture  for 
unlawful  printing,  and  for  knowingly  selling  or  publishing, 
or  exposing  for  sale,  unlawfully  printed  lectures. 

(6)  By  the  Copyright  Act,  1842,  a  lecture  will  be  pro- 
tected as  a  "  book  "  (see  ante)  if  it  is  first  published  in  print. 

Publication 

Delivery  of  a  lecture  for  the  instruction  or  amusement 
of  a  particular  audience  is  not  true  publication,  so  as  to 
make  it  public  property;  it  is  only  " private  publication," 
and  the  author  retains  his  right  in  it  as  unpublished  matter. 

V.  Copyright  in  Engravings  and  the  Like 

This  copyright  endures  for  twenty-eight  years  from  the 
first  publishing.  The  subject-matter  includes  any  print 
invented  or  designed,  graved,  etched,  or  worked  in  mezzo- 
tints or  chiaroscuro,  or  lithographed  by  the  author,  or 
reproduced  at  his  instance  by  any  of  such  methods  from 
the  author's  work,  design,  or  invention,  or  from  any 
picture,  drawing,  model,  or  sculpture. 

Digitized  by  C3OOQIC 


522  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

The  print  must  be  engraved  in  Great  Britain,  but  the 
engraver  need  not  be  British. 

The  day  of  first  publication,  with  the  name  of  the  pro- 
prietor, must  be  engraved  on  each  plate,  and  printed  on 
every  print. 

No  registration  is  required. 


Licensed  Reproductions 

The  written  consent  of  the  proprietor,  attested  by  two 
witnesses,  is  necessary. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Action  for  damages  in  respect  of  copying  and  selling 
without  the  proper  consent,  or  printing,  reprinting,  or 
importing  for  sale,  any  copyright  print,  or  knowingly 
publishing  or  exposing  for  sale  unlawfully  printed  copies, 
or  causing  others  to  do  any  of  these  things. 

Action  for  Penalties. — rive  shillings  for  every  print  in 
the  offender's  possession,  and  forfeiture  of  the  plate  and 
all  prints. 

Action  for  injunction  to  restrain  infringement. 

Limitation  of  Actions 

The  penalty  must  be  sued  for  within  six  months  after 
the  offence,  and  is  recoverable  by  summary  proceedings. 


VI.  Copyright  in  Paintings,  Drawings,  and  Photo- 
graphs— Fine  Arts  Copyright 

This  copyright  endures  for  the  life  of  the  artist,  and 
seven  years  after  his  death. 

The  subject-matter  includes  original  paintings  and  draw- 
ings and  the  designs  of  such,  and  original  photographs  and 
the  negatives. 


Digitized  by  CjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  523 

Conditions  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

The  author  must  be  a  British  subject  or  resident  within 
the  British  dominions. 

If  any  such  work  is  (1)  sold,  or  (2)  executed  "  for  or  on 
behalf  of  any  other  person  for  a  good  or  a  valuable  con- 
sideration," the  author  will  not  retain  the  copyright, 
unless  it  be  expressly  reserved  to  him  by  agreement  in 
writing,  but  the  copyright  will  belong  in  the  first  case  to 
the  purchaser,  and  in  the  second  case  to  the  person  for 
whom  the  work  was  executed,  with  this  qualification  in 
the  case  of  a  purchaser — that  an  agreement  in  writing 
that  he  shall  have  the  copyright  is  necessary. 

Accordingly,  where  a  photograph  is  taken  at  the  request 
of  the  sitter  upon  the  terms  that  the  sitter  will  pay  for 
taking  it,  or  in  circumstances  which  raise  an  implied 
promise  on  his  part  to  pay,  the  copyright  in  the  photo- 
graph belongs  to  the  sitter,  though  the  negative  remains 
the  property  of  the  photographer  (Boucas  v.  Cooke  [1903, 
2  K.B^  227],  the  boy  preacher's  case). 

It  has  been  recently  held  that  photographers  who  were 
at  their  own  request  allowed  by  the  proprietor  of  a  school 
to  take  photographs  of  the  school  at  their  own  risk,  on  the 
chance  of  selling  copies  to  the  proprietor,  must  be  deemed 
to  have  taken  the  photographs  for  or  on  behalf  of  the  pro- 
prietor "  for  a  good  consideration,"  and  that  the  copy- 
right therefore  belonged  to  him  (Stackemann  v.  Paton  [1906, 
1  Ch.,  774]). 

Territorial  Extent 

This  copyright  extends  to  the  whole  of  the  United  King- 
dom, but  not  to  any  part  of  the  British  dominions  outside 
Graves  v.  Gorrte  [1903*  A.C.  496],  unlicensed  reproduction 
in  Canada  of  copies  of  an  English  copyright  picture). 

Registration 

The  Fine  Arts  Copyright  Act,  1862,  provides  for  the 
keeping  of  a  register  at  Stationers'  Hall,  and  prescribes 
registration  of  the  proprietor  of  the  copyright  and  other 
particulars.  The  provisions  as  to  inspection,  etc.,  are  the 
same  as  for  the  register  of  books  (ante,  q.v.).    No  action 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


524  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

will  lie  for  any  infringement  or  offence  committed  before 
registration. 

Assignment  of  Right 

Assignments  and  licenses  must  be  in  writing.  Assign- 
ments have  to  be  registered. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Action  for  penalties — up  to  £10  for  every  offence,  with 
forfeiture  of  piratical  copies  and  negatives — in  respect  of 
repeating  or  copying  for  sale,  hire,  or  exhibition  and 
distribution,  or  knowingly  importing,  selling,  publishing 
unlawful  copies,  or  causing  any  such  thing  to  be  done. 

Action  for  damages,  with  forfeiture  of  piratical  copies, 
in  respect  of  the  same  matters. 

Action  for  injunction  to  restrain  future  infringements. 
Penalties  and  forfeitures  may  be  enforced  by  action  or 
summarily. 

Limitation  of  Actions 

There  is  no  special  limitation. 


VII.  Copyright  in  Sculptures 

This  copyright  endures  for  fourteen  years  from  the  first 
putting  forth  or  publishing  the  sculpture,  with  a  further 
fourteen  years  if  the  sculptor  shall  be  living  at  the  end 
of  that  time.  The  subject-matter  includes  new  and 
original  sculptures,  models,  copies,  or  casts. 

Condition  for  Acquiring  Copyright 

Name  of  proprietor,  with  the  date,  must  be  put  on  before 
publication. 

Transfer  of  Right 

A  deed,  signed  by  the  proprietor  and  attested  by  two 
witnesses,  is  required. 

Remedies  for  Infringement 

Action   for   damages,  and   for   injunction  to   restrain 
'ure  infringements.
