part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  525 

Limitation  of  Actions 

Actions  must  be  brought  within  six  months  next  after 
the  discovery  of  the  offence. 


We  now  pass  to 

COLONIAL  COPYRIGHT 

The  law  is  contained  in  the  following  statutes  : 

io  &  11  Vict.,  c.  95 — The  Colonial  Copyright  Act,  1847, 
sometimes  called  the  Foreign  Reprints  Act. 
38  &  39  Vict.,  c.  53— The  Canada  Copyright  Act,  1875. 
49  &  50  Vict.,  c.  33 — International  Copyright  Act,  1886. 

Admission  of  Foreign  Reprints 

By  the  first-named  Act  the  provisions  of  the  Imperial 
legislation  against  the  importation  and  sale  of  foreign 
reprints  of  English  copyright  books  may  be  suspended  by 
Order  in  Council  in  colonies  which  make  legislative  pro- 
visions for  protecting  the  rights  of  British  authors.  It 
appears  that  nineteen  colonies  obtained  the  benefit  of  this 
Act,  but  so  far  as  the  protection  of  British  writers  is 
concerned,  the  Act  has  proved  a  failure,  the  sums  remitted 
from  the  colonies  for  their  benefit  being  insignificant.  In- 
deed, the  Government  of  Canada  and  the  Cape  of  Good 
Hope  have  ceased  to  collect  the  royalty  on  imported 
foreign  reprints,  and  are  now,  it  is  conceived,  excluded 
from  the  benefit  of  the  Act. 

Canadian  Law 

The  Canada  Copyright  Act,  1875,  gives  Imperial  sanction 
to  the  provisions  of  an  Act  of  the  Canadian  Parliament 
putting  the  law  of  copyright  in  Canada  on  an  independent 
basis,  but  prohibits  the  importation  of  Canadian  reprints 
of  English  copyright  books. 

The  Canadian  Act  enables  the  author  of  a  literary  or 
artistic  work  domiciled  in  Canada,  or  in  any  part  of  the 
British  possessions,  or  being  a  citizen  of  any  country, 
having  an  international  copyright  treaty  with  the  United 
Kingdom,  to  obtain  copyright  in  Canada  for  twenty-eight 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


526  THE   LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

years,  by  publication  or  republication  in  Canada  under 
certain  specified  conditions. 

This  copyright  is  in  addition  to,  and  concurrent  with, 
copyright  under  the  British  statute  of  1842,  the  rights 
under  which  are  not  interfered  with  by  the  Imperial  and 
Canadian  Acts  of  1875.  The  latter  Act  is  now  replaced 
and  substantially  re-enacted  by  the  revised  statutes  of 
Canada,  1886,  cap.  62,  cited  in  Canada  as  '  the  Copy- 
right Act.'  Next  came  an  abortive  Act  of  the  Canadian 
Parliament  in  1889,  requiring  publication  or  republication 
in  Canada  as  a  condition  of  copyright.  This  Act  is  a  dead- 
letter,  because  it  has  never  been  proclaimed  by  the 
Governor-General,  and  was  not  to  come  into  force  until 
so  proclaimed.  Finally,  a  more  moderate  step  in  the  same 
direction  was  taken  in  1900  by  the  Canadian  Copyright 
Act,  1900,  which  provides  that,  when  the  owner  of  the 
copyright  in  a  work  first  published  in  some  part  of  the 
Empire  other  than  Canada  has  lawfully  granted  a  license 
to  reproduce  it  in  Canada,  then,  notwithstanding  anything 
in  '  the  Copyright  Act,'  the  Minister  of  Agriculture  may 
prohibit  the  importation  into  Canada  of  any  copies  printed 
elsewhere,  and  the  Act  directs  the  seizure  and  forfeiture 
of  all  books  imported  in  contravention  of  the  Act,  and 
imposes  a  penalty  for  unlawful  importation.  The  inten- 
tion, of  course,  is  to  stop  the  importation  into  Canada  of 
copies  of  the  English  edition  of  a  book,  and  so  to  give  the 
grantee  in  Canada  of  the  author's  Canadian  rights  a 
monopoly  of  the  Canadian  market.  It  has  been  suggested 
that  the  Act  of  1900  is  in  conflict  with  the  British  law 
of  copyright,  in  that  it  derogates  from  the  right  to  sell 
copies  throughout  the  Empire,  and  its  validity  has  been 
questioned  by  text-writers.  See  Dr.  Briggs's  Law  of 
International  Copyright,  p.  615. 

This  work,  which  was  published  in  1906,  may  be  wel- 
comed as  a  valuable  addition  to  the  contributions  of 
English  text -writers  to  the  exposition  of  the  law  of  inter- 
national copyright. 

Australian  Law 

The  Commonwealth  of  Australia  passed  a  Copyright 
Act  for  Australia  in  1905.     It  deals  with  literary,  musical, 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  nj  LAW  AND  LETTERS  527 

and  dramatic  copyright  in  one  part  of  the  Act,  and  with 
artistic  copyright  separately.  Subject  to  the  conditions 
specified  in  the  Act,  protection  is  given,  whether  the 
author  is  a  British  subject  or  not,  to  books  published,  and 
musical  and  dramatic  works  publicly  performed,  in  Aus- 
tralia, before  or  simultaneously  with  first  publication  or 
performance  elsewhere,  for  the  life  of  the  author  and  seven 
years  after  his  death,  or  for  forty-two  years  from  the  date 
of  publication  or  performance  in  Australia,  whichever  is 
the  longer,  but  in  the  case  of  a  book  first  published,  or  of 
a  musical  or  dramatic  work  first  performed  after  the 
author's  death,  for  the  term  of  forty-two  years.  Similar 
protection  is  also  accorded  to  lectures  first  publicly  de- 
livered in  Australia  after  the  commencement  of  the  Act. 
The  copyright  in  an  artistic  work  attaches  to  works  made 
in  Australia  after  the  commencement  of  the  Act,  whether 
the  author  is  a  British  subject  or  not,  and  subsists  for 
forty-two  years  from  the  making  of  the  work,  or  for  the 
author's  life  and  seven  years,  whichever  shall  last  the 
longer.  It  is  expressly  provided  that  the  owner  of  any 
copyright  or  performing  right  in  any  literary,  musical,  or 
dramatic  work  or  artistic  work  entitled  to  protection  in 
Australia  by  virtue  of  any  Act  of  the  Imj)eriaJ  Parlia- 
ment, shall,  on  obtaining  the  prescribed  certificate  of  the 
registration  of  his  copyright  or  performing  right,  have  the 
same  protection  in  the  Commonwealth  as  the  owner  of 
any  copyright  or  performing  right  under  the  Act.  The 
Act  requires  a  proclamation  of  the  Governor-General  to 
bring  it  into  force,  and  it  was  announced  that  a  proclama- 
tion bringing  into  force  the  Commonwealth  Copyright  Act, 
1905,  would  be  issued  about  the  middle  of  June.  A 
telegram  was  received  from  the  Governor-General  in  July, 
stating  that  the  proclamation  had  not  yet  been  issued,  but 
that  the  date  fixed  for  the  coming  of  the  Act  into  operation 
would  be  telegraphed  as  soon  as  practicable. 

Generally 

A^number  of  other  colonies  have  local  copyright  laws 
of  their  own,  and  whenever  that  is  the  case,  '  the  con- 
ditions of  protection  which  affect  works  first  published  in 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


528  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

any  of  these  must  be  sought  in  two  bodies  of  law — in  the 
Acts  of  the  British  Parliament,  as  regards  their  protection 
throughout  all  parts  of  the  British  dominions  except  the 
colony  of  first  publication,  and  in  the  Acts  or  Ordinances 
of  the  Colonial  legislative  authority,  as  regards  their  pro- 
tection within  the  territory  of  that  colony  itself '  (Dr. 
Briggs's  Law  of  International  Copyright,  p.  594). 

But  copyright  acquired  in  respect  of  literary  works 
published  in  the  United  Kingdom,  extending  to  every 
part  of  the  British  dominions,  is,  of  course,  entitled  to 
protection  in  any  of  these  colonies. 

Right  of  Colonial  Productions  to  English 
Copyright 

The  English  Copyright  Acts  apply  to  a  literary  or  artistic 
work  first  produced  in  a  British  possession  by  virtue  of  the 
International  Copyright  Act,  1886;  but  the  provisions  as 
to  registration  of  copyright  will  not  apply  where  there  is 
a  colonial  register,  and  no  copies  of  a  colonial  book  need 
be  given  to  any  public  library. 

Right  of  Colonial  Productions  to  International 

Copyright 
See  infra. 


INTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT 

This  depends  upon  the  provisions  of  the  International 
Copyright  Acts,  1844  to  1886,  the  Berne  Convention  of 
1887,  as  modified  in  1896,  and  the  Orders  in  Council 
adopting  it. 

The  principal  Acts  are  the  International  Copyright  Acts 
of  1844  and  1886,  7  &  8  Vict.,  c.  12,  and  49  &  50  Vict., 
c  33. 

The  Copyright  Union 

The  Berne  Convention  aims  at  securing  reciprocity  of 
copyright  on  uniform  lines.  It  applies  to  most  of  the 
great  countries  of  Europe  and  to  Japan,  but  not  to  Austria 
and  Hungary,  or  to  Russia  or  Turkey,  and  the  contracting 


Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


part  iij  LAW  AND  LETTERS  529 

States  are  called  the  Copyright  Union.    It  is  provided  that 
other  countries  may  accede  to  the  Convention. 

Term  of  Copyright 

The  author  of  any  literary  or  artistic  work  first  pro- 
duced in  a  country  of  the  Copyright  Union  will  have 
throughout  the  King's  dominions  the  same  copyright  as 
if  the  work  had  been  first  produced  in  the  United  Kingdom, 
and  during  the  same  period,  but  not  any  greater  right  or 
longer  term  of  copyright  than  that  enjoyed  in  the  country 
where  it  is  produced. 

This  applies  also  to  works  already  enjoying  copyright 
in  a  foreign  country  when  the  Berne  Convention  was 
adopted,  with  a  saving  clause  as  to  valuable  rights  or 
interests  arising  from  the  prior  production  of  any  work 
in  the  United  Kingdom. 

'  Rights  '  means  strictly  legal  rights ;  '  interest,'  such 
an  interest  as  arises  from  any  investment  of  capital  or 
skill. 

Territorial  Extent  of  Right 

The  right  extends  throughout  the  countries  which  are 
parties  to  the  Copyright  Union,  and  their  colonies  or 
foreign  possessions,  if  a  country  has  declared  itself  a  party 
in  respect  of  its  colonies. 

Remedy  for  Infringement  Abroad 

An  English  author  desiring  to  stop  infringement  of  his 
copyright  in  a  foreign  country  must  pursue  his  remedy 
in  the  foreign  court. 

Registration 

Neither  registration  of  the  work  nor  the  deposit  of  any 
copy  will  be  required  of  a  work  produced  in  a  foreign 
country  in  the  Copyright  Union. 

Translations 

The  exclusive  right  of  translating  or  authorizing  trans- 
lations is  given  to  authors  belonging  to  one  of  the  countries 
of  the  Union ;  but  if  after  ten  years  an  authorized  English 
translation  of  a  foreign  work  has  not  been  produced,  the 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


530  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

right  ceases  in  the  United  Kingdom;  Similarly,  if  after 
ten  years  a  translation  of  the  work  of  an  English  author 
has  not  been  produced  in  any  country  in  the  Copyright 
Union,  his  right  of  translation  ceases. 

International  Copyright  in  the  Colonies 

Works  produced  in  India  or  the  colonies  enjoy  copyright 
in  the  countries  in  the  Copyright  Union. 

Countries  Outside  the  Copyright  Union 

There  is  a  separate  convention  between  Austria  and 
Hungary  and  the  United  Kingdom  on  the  same  lines  as 
the  Berne  Convention. 

Copyright  in  the  United  States 

English  authors  can  now  obtain  copyright  for  their 
literary  and  artistic  productions  in  the  United  States 
under  the  Chace  Act  of  1891  and  the  President's 
proclamation;  but  as  regards  books  and  photographs, 
chromos  and  lithographs,  two  copies,  which  are  required 
to  be  sent  to  the  Librarian  of  Congress,  have  to  be  '  printed 
from  type  set  within  the  limits  of  the  United  States,  or 
from  plates  made  therefrom,  or  from  negatives  or  draw- 
ings on  stone  made  within  the  limits  of  the  United  States, 
or  from  transfers  made  therefrom/  A  further  Act  of 
Congress  of  March  4,  1909,  has  been  passed  to  amend  and 
consolidate  the  Acts  respecting  copyright*  and  the  benefits 
of  that  Act  are  expressly  limited  by  Section  8  to  :  (a)  When 
an  alien  author  or  proprietor  shall  be  domiciled  within  the 
United  States  at  the  time  of  the  first  publication  of  his 
work ;  or  (b)  when  the  foreign  6tate  or  nation*  of  which 
such  author  or  proprietor  is  a  citizen  or  subject*;  grants, 
either  by  treaty*  convention,  agreement,  or  law  to  citizens 
of  the  United  States  the  benefit  of  copyright  on  substan- 
tially the  same  basis  as  to  its  own  citizens,  or  copyright 
protection  substantially  equal  to  the  protection  secured 
to  such  foreign  author  under  this  Act*  or  by  treaty,  or  when 
such  foreign  State  or  nation  is  a  party  to  an  international 
agreement  which  provides  for  reciprocity  in  the  granting  of 


Digitized  by  C3OOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  531 

copyright,  by  the  term6  of  which  agreement  the  United 
States  may  at  its  pleasure  become  a  party.  The  existence 
of  such  reciprocal  conditions  shall  be  determined  by 
proclamation  of  the  President.  On  April  9,  1910,  Presi- 
dent Taft,  by  proclamation  of  that  date,  declared  the 
benefits  of  the  Act  of  1909  available  to  Great  Britain  and 
her  possessions  in  consequence  of  this  country  having 
allowed  to  Americans  the  benefits  of  copyright  here  on 
substantially  the  same  basis  as  Englishmen.  Similar 
privileges  by  this  proclamation  were  granted  to  other 
countries,  the  only  limitation  on  which  inquiry  is  still 
pending  being  in  respect  of  musical  performing  right  and 
the  mechanical  reproduction  of  musical  work. 

An  American  author,  in  order  to  obtain  copyright  in 
England,  must  publish  in  England  and  the  United  States 
simultaneously. 

An  important  decision  was  reached  by  the  Board  of 
United  States  General  Appraisers  in  March,  1908,  with 
reference  to  the  duty  on  imported  sheets,  reversing  a 
previous  ruling.  The  new  ruling  is  as  follows :  Books  of 
foreign  authors  published  in  the  United  States  are  subject 
to  no  duty  upon  royalty.  Books  printed  and  bound  abroad 
are  subject  to  duty  upon  the  entire  royalty  paid  thereon. 
Sheets  printed  abroad  and  bound  and  finished  into  books 
in  the  United  States  are  subject  only  to  duty  upon  the 
percentage  of  royalty  applicable  to  the  parts  involved. 


II.  AUTHOR,  PUBLISHER,  AND  AGENT 

In  the  foregoing  paragraphs  a  brief  exposition  has  been 
given  of  the  law  which  governs  the  barter  of  literary 
property.  Author  and  publisher  should  know  the  essential 
facts  of  copyright  law,  if  for  no  other  reason  than  because 
a  lawyer  is  seldom  or  never  called  in  to  draft  an  agreement 
between  these  parties.  The  assistance  of  the  law  is  in- 
voked in  almost  every  similar  relation.  In  buying  or 
leasing  a  house  a  man  would  be  extremely  ill-advised 
if  he  failed  to  consult  his  solicitor,  but  in  buying  or  leasing 
a  book  the  author  is  content  to  rely  on  the  printed  form 

Digitized  by  VjOOQLC 


532  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

of  agreement  (varying  between  different  publishers  but 
very  slightly  in  wording),  which  he  exchanges  with  the 
publisher,  and  which  is  subject  to  stamp  duty. 

General  Considerations 

:r  We  propose  now  to  consider  what  kinds  of  agreement 
these  are,  but  before  we  discuss  their  clauses  in  detail  a 
few  general  observations  may  be  made.  Between  the 
writing  of  a  book  and  its  publication  a  large  number  of 
processes  intervene,  many  of  which  have  been  obscured 
rather  than  illuminated  by  recent  controversial  discussions. 
Let  us  try  to  unravel  them  a  little  : 

i.  A  publisher  must  be  found,  either  directly  or  by 
means  of  an  agent. 

2.  Terms  of  publication  must  be  discussed  and  agreed 
between  the  interested  parties. 

At  this  point  the  publisher's  task  may  be  said  properly 
to  begin,  and,  in  view  of  recent  statements  and  conjectures 
on  the  matter,  it  may  fairly  be  urged  that  authors  are  apt 
to  under-estimate  the  share  due  to  a  publisher  in  the  suc- 
cessful production  of  their  books.  It  is  not  merely  that 
he  employs  the  printers  and  the  binders,  thus  taking  the 
responsibility  of  format  and  of  taste  ;  his  generic  function 
consists  in  the  distribution  of  the  book,  when  published,  by 
means  of  travellers  and  advertisement  and  other  agencies 
of  sale.  Above  all,  in  the  vast  majority  of  instances,  he 
pays  the  initial  cost.  An  author  may  write  another  book, 
and  either  recover  his  lost,  or  establish  his  unmade 
reputation,  but  when  a  publisher  has  lost  his  money  on  a 
book,  he  has  been — stupid  and  over-confident,  perhaps, 
but  at  least — unfortunate  in  the  business  on  which  he 
depends  for  his  livelihood.  And  ill  speculation  of  this  kind 
is  bound  to  fall  to  the  lot  even  of  a  Daniel  among  book 
judges.  Hence,  in  his  trade,  as  in  others,  the  profits  on 
successful  trading  must  go  towards  the  recoupment  of 
losses  and  bad  debts,  else  there  would  be  no  margin  for 
enterprise. 

The  author  should  remember,  too,  that  all  the  business 
side  of  the  transaction  falls  on  the  publisher ;  he  is  the 
intermediary  between  his  own  bookshelves  and  the  public. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  533 

The  complicated  questions  of  trade  discounts,  travellers' 
commissions,  booksellers'  terms,  and  so  forth,  have  all 
to  be  dealt  with  by  the  publisher,  who  must  at  the  same 
time  be  a  man  of  some  literary  attainments,  inasmuch  as 
his  operations  extend  from  the  manuscript  of  the  author — 
or,  rather,  as  it  should  always  be,  from  the  '  copy '  fresh 
from  the  typewriter — to  the  last  ramifications  of  selling 
the  published  book.  Apart  from  works  of  pure  scholarship, 
for  which  ex  hypothesi  not  large  sales  are  anticipated,  the 
most  '  successful '  books,  in  the  sense  of  remunerative 
commercial  ventures,  often  owe  as  big  a  debt  to  the 
publisher's  expert  knowledge  and  suggestions,  and  to  his 
business  abilities  as  to  the  author  himself. 

Till  a  few  years  ago  there  was  no  recognized  intermediary 
between  the  author  and  the  publisher,  and  in  those  more 
spacious  days  of  less  specialized  functions  the  publisher  was 
often  his  own  printer  and  his  own  bookbinder  as  well. 
The  tendency  of  the  present  day,  however,  is  to  narrow 
the  publisher's  sphere  more  and  more  to  the  business  side. 
A  class  of  agents  has  sprung  up  who  take  the  literary  side 
of  publishers'  work  more  and  more  out  of  the  hands  of  the 
publishers.  Under  these  conditions  the  modern  author 
rarely  brings  his  book  direct  to  the  publisher's  office. 
He  tends  to  commit  it  to  an  agent,  whose  business  it 
becomes  to  find  a  suitable  publisher,  and  to  arrange  on 
the  author's  behalf  fair  terms  of  publication.  In  this 
tendency  of  things  two  obvious  facts  must  be  noted  : 
first,  the  publishers  lose  touch  with  the  authors  whose 
works  they  bring  out,  and,  secondly,  a  great  responsibility 
rests  on  the  literary  agent.  The  author,  accordingly,  has 
to  deal  with  two  intervening  '  middlemen '  between  his 
MS.  and  his  readers,  and  the  protection  of  the  Authors' 
Society  is  almost  as  frequently  sought  against  agents  as 
against  publishers.  The  attempt  in  these  paragraphs  to 
put  the  case  for  the  publishers  as  prominently  before  the 
writing  public  as  is  compatible  with  fairness  has  been 
the  subject  of  sundry  animadversions — to  employ  a  term 
which  contains  the  appropriate  animus— on  the  part  of  some 
members  of  the  agents'  profession.  The  late  Mr.  Gale 
Pedrick,  for  example,  has  been  quoted  as  stating  "  that  the 
very  fact  that  the  publishers  are  squealing  against  the 

Digitized  by  C3OOQIC 


534  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

literary  agent  proves  that  the  agent  has  uses  which,  from 
the  author's  point  of  view,  must  be  of  the  most  valuable 
kind."  The  uses  "  have  not  been  called  in  question  :  it  is 
the  question  of  abuses  which  has  been  raised  ;  and  when  Mr. 
Pedrick  proceeds  to  say  that,  "  apart  from  his  usefulness,  or 
want  of  usefulness,  whether  to  author  or  publisher,  the 
agent  has  so  established  himself  .  .  .  that  it  is  absolutely 
impossible  to  turn  him  out/'  he  goes,  we  venture  to  think, 
too  far.  Worse  barnacles  than  agents  have  been  dislodged 
from  time  to  time,  and  if  Mr.  Pedrick's  hypothesis  were 
raised  to  the  dignity  of  a  proposition  to  the  effect  that 
agents  are  useless  to  authors  and  publishers  alike,  these 
remarks  would  be  unnecessary.  It  is  because  their  ser- 
vices are  useful,  but  require  in  some  respects  to  be  regulated 
and  organized,  now  that  they  have  developed  to  so  con- 
siderable an  extent,  that  it  is  worth  while  to  submit  the 
general  lines  of  our  present  argument. 

The  Agency  Clause 

The  author  should  be  warned  against  a  clause,  some- 
times introduced  into  agents'  agreements,  which  requires 
him  to  pay  the  agent  a  commission,  not  merely  on  the 
book  under  discussion,  but  on  future  books  which  the 
author  may  write  during  a  stated  term  of  years.  Cases 
have  not  been  unknown  in  which,  when  one  book  has 
been  launched  with  more  of  promise  than  of  profit  in  its 
record,  the  publisher  has  suggested  to  the  author  the 
exact  lines  and  scope  on  which  a  second  book  should  be 
written  in  order  to  insure  a  genuine  success.  And  when 
all  the  preliminaries  have  been  settled,  and  the  author 
and  publisher  together  have  arranged  for  the  production 
of  a  book  for  then:  joint  and  mutual  advantage,  the  agent 
has  then  intervened  under  a  clause  in  his  first  agreement, 
and  has  claimed  a  commission  on  the  proceeds  of  the 
book  in  which  from  start  to  finish  he  had  no  part  nor 
parcel.  This  strikes  us  as  an  excessive  price  to  pay  for 
the  agent's  first  service,  and  as  distinctly  unfair  to  author 
and  publisher  alike.  Indeed,  we  would  go  further  than 
this,  and  urge  that  an  agent's  work  stands  for  all  practical 
purposes  on  the  same  level  as  a  solicitor's.     He  is  called 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  535 

in  by  the  author  to  carry  through  certain  negotiations, 
and  at  the  close  of  these  negotiations  he  should  be  required 
to  send  in  his  bill.  There  should  be  a  scale  of  fees  for  his 
work,  beginning  with  a  low  fee  for  the  work,  which  he 
most  commonly  has  to  perform,  of  tying  up  the  parcels 
of  manuscript  with  fresh  paper  and  string  as  they  return 
to  him  from  successive  publishers.  He  should  in  no  case, 
or  in  only  the  most  exceptional  cases,  be  entitled  to  deduct 
a  percentage  from  the  author's  profits  in  the  form  of  a 
commission  on  the  agent's  preliminary  work  in  finding  a 
publisher  for  the  book  and  in  arranging  the  terms  of  publica- 
tion. The  '  agency  clause '  in  particular,  which  entitles 
the  agent  to  '  collect  and  receive  all  moneys  due  under 
the  terms  of  this  agreement,'  and  which  acknowledges 
the  agent's  receipt  as  a  good  and  valid  discharge,  should 
be  signed  only  with  the  utmost  caution.  It  ties  the 
author  to  the  agent  through  the  whole  career  of  the  book  ; 
it  effectually  prevents  that  rapprochement  between  the 
author  and  the  publisher,  and  keeps  the  publisher  at 
arm's  length,  to  the  detriment  of  a  relationship  which  might 
be  useful  to  both.  Many  reputable  publishers  argue  that 
the  '  agency  clause '  acts  as  a  deterrent  to  that  whole- 
hearted co-operation  with  the  author  and  that  mutual 
confidence  and  harmony  which  are  at  the  bottom  of  com- 
mercial success ;  and  though  some  go  too  far  in  the  opposite 
direction,  and  commit  all  the  paraphernalia  of  a  publishing 
agreement  to  a  simple  exchange  of  letters — an  extremely 
unsatisfactory  '  old '  fashion — we  believe  in  a  moderate 
conservatism  in  these  matters,  with  the  Authors'  Society 
in  the  background,  and  without,  as  we  trust,  too  keen  a 
competition  for  success. 

Four  Kinds  of  Agreement 

Whether  effected  directly  or  by  means  of  an  agent,  the 
agreement  between  author  and  publisher  may  take  one 
of  four  forms.  It  may  be  (1)  a  royalty  agreement,  in 
which  the  publisher  takes  all  the  costs  and  risk  of  produc- 
tion, and  pays  the  author  a  certain  percentage  on  every 
copy  of  the  book  sold ;  (2)  a  profit-sharing  agreement,  in 
which  the  author  and  publisher  divide  the  net  profits  of 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


536  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK         [part  n. 

the  book ;  (3)  a  sale  outright  of  the  copyright  by  the 
author  to  the  publisher ;  or  (4)  a  commission  agreement, 
which  is  more  or  less  exactly  the  reverse  of  a  royalty 
agreement — the  author,  that  is  to  say,  pays  all  the  costs 
of  production,  and  pays  the  publisher  a  commission  in 
the  form  of  a  percentage  on  the  sales  effected. 

Commissioning  the  Publisher 

This  last  form  of  agreement  is  perhaps  the  simplest, 
though  for  more  than  one  reason  it  is  the  most  unpopular 
with  publishers.  We  shall  not  be  stating  these  reasons 
unfairly  if  we  say  that  some  of  them  are  disinterested 
and  others,  perhaps,  a  little  interested.  The  commission 
book,  if  it  is  successful,  certainly  does  not  pay  the  publisher 
as  well  as  a  royalty  book.  But  apart  from  this,  the 
publisher  naturally  feels  considerably  less  interest  in 
undertaking  it,  inasmuch  as  the  whole  initiative  is  taken 
out  of  his  hands,  and  he  has  to  receive  the  author's  sanction 
for  every  pennyworth  of  expenditure  upon  the  book,  and 
further,  to  justify  such  expenditure  to  the  author,  who 
may  be  difficult  to  convince  or  even  hard  of  understanding, 
with  the  consequence  that  commission  books,  especially 
when  only  a  small  sale  is  expected,  are  often  regarded  by 
publishers  as  involving  more  trouble  than  they  are  worth, 
and  are,  indeed,  usually  declined,  except  in  rare  cases,  by 
the  best  firms. 

In  entering  on  a  memorandum  of  agreement  for  the 
publication  of  a  book  on  commission,  to  take  this  fourth 
and  simplest  form  first,  such  memorandum  should  be  sent 
by  the  publisher  accompanied,  in  every  instance,  by  an 
estimate  for  producing  the  requisite  number  of  copies  of 
the  book  under  discussion,  to  form  one  volume  or  more 
of  approximately  so  many  sheets  of  sixteen  pages  in  such 
and  such  a  size  of  paper.  This  estimate  should  state  the 
rateand  amount  of  the  following  items  : 

1.  Composition  per  sheet. 

2.  Paper  per  ream. 

3.  Machining  per  ream. 

4.  Moulding  or  stereotyping,  per  sheet,  if  required. 

5.  Binding,  per  copy  or  per  100  copies. 


Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  537 

Allowances  should  also  be  made  for  : 

6.  Illustrations,  if  required. 

7.  The  brass  blocks  or  letterings  required  by  the  binder. 

8.  The  reader's  time  spent  on  proofs  and  revises. 

9.  Corrections,  per  hour. 

10.  Insurance,  per  £100. 

11.  Advertisements,  which  should  be  charged  to  the 
author  at  scale  prices  as  ordered  by  him  for  insertion  in 
papers  of  his  selection. 

Based  on  this  estimate,  the  memorandum  will  witness 
that  *  in  consideration  of  the  payment  by  the  author  to 
the  publisher  of  the  amount  of  the  estimate  hereinafter 
appearing,  the  receipt  of  which  sum  is  hereby  acknowledged, 
the  publishers  hereby  agree  to  print,  publish,  advertise, 
and  sell  a  first  edition  of  a  number  to  be  agreed  upon  of 
the  work  written  by  the  author,  and  entitled  .  :  .  and 
to  pay  to  the  author  the  sale  profits  thereof  as  herein- 
after provided  and  calculated,  and  according  to  the  terms 
hereinafter  contained.' 

The  first  clause  of  the  agreement  will  then  provide  that 
the  publishers  shall  produce  the  work  in  one  volume  or 
more,  and  shall  warehouse  the  bound  and  quire  stock, 
and  shall  deliver  the  usual  press  copies,  and  shall  account 
to  the  author  for  every  copy  produced,  annually  or  semi- 
annually, as  may  be  agreed. 

The  second  clause  will  deal  with  the  method  of  calcu- 
lating the  sale  proceeds.  Copies  sold  in  the  United 
Kingdom  at  the  regular  trade  prices  are  most  commonly 
accounted  for  to  the  author  at  two-thirds  of  the  full  pub- 
lished price,  thirteen  copies  being  reckoned  as  twelve, 
less  a  discount  of  10  per  cent.  This  calculation  is  best 
reached  by  taking  the  average  prices  charged  by  the 
publishers  to  wholesale  booksellers  and  exporters.  We  may 
remark  here,  in  reference  to  this  form  of  agreement,  as  well 
as  to  the  three  others,  that  the  words  '  thirteen  copies  being 
reckoned  as  twelve  '  frequently  prove  a  stumbling-block  to 
authors  and  to  the  partisans  of  authors'  rights.  It  is  equiva- 
lent, of  course,  to  a  loss  to  the  author  of  about  eight  and 
a  quarter  copies  of  his  book  in  every  hundred  copies  sold, 
and  in  making  a  commission-agreement  especially,  the 
author  may  feel  disposed  to  resent  this  apparent  extor- 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


538  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

tion  on  the  part  of  the  publishers.  On  the  other  hand,  the 
publisher  says  that,  in  a  very  large  number  of  cases,  he  has 
to  supply  the  trade — ».«.,  booksellers  and  exporters — on 
these  terms.  It  would  be  especially  unfair  to  him  in  a 
commission-agreement  that  he  should  account  to  the  author 
for  copies  as  sold  which  he  has  actually  supplied  as  odd  or 
thirteenth  copies ;  in  a  royalty-agreement,  where  large  sales 
are  anticipated,  he  might  consent  to  waive  the  point  in  the 
author's  favour. 

A  second  section  of  Clause  i  will  provide  that  stereo- 
type plates  and  copies  sold  for  the  American,  Indian, 
and  Colonial  markets  at  reduced  prices  will  be  calcu- 
lated at  the  amounts  actually  received  for  them,  and 
the  same  method  of  calculation  will  apply  to  copies 
sold  in  the  United  Kingdom  or  elsewhere  by  auction 
or  privately  to  a  dealer  at  reduced  prices,  or  by  way 
of  remainder.  On  the  other  hand,  something  depends 
on  the  class  of  book  to  be  sold ;  single  copies,  or  less  than 
half  a  dozen,  may  be  most  in  demand,  and  probably  no 
publisher  would  object  to  modify  the  clause  if  the  circum- 
stances of  the  case  seemed  to  require  it. 

The  further  clauses  necessary  for  such  an  agreement 
commonly  run  as  follows.  Our  comments,  where  neces- 
sary, are  made  in  smaller  type  : 

'  3.  That  the  Publishers  shall  not  make  any  charges 
for  their  expenses  of  bookings  packings  telegrams  and 
postages  or  any  other  ordinary  publishing  expenses  in 
connection  with  the  sale  of  the  said  book  and  that  only 
the  actual  postages  on  Provincial  and  Foreign  Press 
Copies  (where  paid)  shall  be  charged. 

*  4.  That  the  Copyright  and  Plant  of  the  book  shall  be 
the  property  of  the  Author. 

'  5.  That  the  Author  shall  not  be  deprived  of  his  share 
of  the  sale-proceeds  of  any  copies  sold  the  purchase  money 
whereof  shall  not  be  paid  to  the  Publishers. 

'  6.  That  the  manner  of  Production  Publication  Sale 
and  Advertisement  of  the  said  work  shall  in  the  absence 
of  express  agreement  to  the  contrary  he  wholly  within  the 
discretion  of  the  Publishers. 

This  description  covers  the  choice  of  type,  selection  of  binding, 
arrangements  with  the  trade,  and  suggestions  as  to  methods  of  pushing 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  II.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  539 

the  sale.    The  author  should  make  up  his  mind  very  carefully  before- 
hand how  far  he  wishes  to  have  a  voice  in  these  matters. 

'  7.  That  the  Author  shall  give  the  Publishers  six 
calendar  months'  notice  in  writing  before  he  remove  the 
publication  of  the  book  from  them. 

'  8.  That  the  Publishers  shall  be  entitled  to  a  commission 
of  ten  per  centum  on  all  sale  proceeds  and  to  a  commission 
of  five  per  centum  on  all  advertisements  of  the  book  put 
out  by  them  but  that  such  advertisements  shall  invariably 
be  charged  at  the  scale  prices  of  the  respective  papers  and 
journals. 

'  9.  That  the  Publishers  shall  annually  make  up  as  at 
the  thirtieth  of  June  the  accounts  of  each  year  and  shall 
render  the  same  to  the  Author  within  three  months  there- 
from and  shall  at  the  same  time  pay  over  any  balance 
which  may  be  found  due  to  the  Author. 

It  is  better  that  these  accounts  should  be  rendered  semi-annually, 
but  we  print  the  clause  in  the  form  in  which  it  is  commonly  found. 

'  10.  That  the  Publishers'  Day-books  and  Ledgers 
shall  be  open  to  the  inspection  of  the  Author  either  per- 
sonally or  by  agent  at  any  reasonable  time  after  notice 
given  in  writing. 

'  11.  That  the  Author  shall  be  responsible  for  any 
action  of  Libel  or  of  Infringement  of  Copyright  arising  out 
of  the  publication  of  the  said  work  anci  shall  indemnify 
and  reimburse  the  Publishers  for  any  damages  cost  or 
charges  which  they  may  reasonably  incur  or  be  called 
upon  to  pay  in  respect  thereof. 

'  12.  That  the  Author  shall  be  entitled  on  publication 
of  the  book  to  twelve  copies  without  any  charge  on  the 
part  of  the  Publishers  and  to  any  further  copies  required 
at  two-thirds  the  published  price  (thirteen  copies  being 
reckoned  as  twelve)  subject  to  a  further  discount  of  ten 
per  centum  (if  cash  accompany  the  order)  such  further 
copies  to  appear  as  sales  in  the  Publishers'  accounts.' 

The  author  is  obviously  entitled  to  buy  his  books  at  the  lowest  trade 
terms. 

Sale  of  Copyright 

Next  in  order  we  may  take  the  sale-of-copyright  agree- 
ment, in  which  the  author  is  paid  for  his  work  on  the  book 


Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


540  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  n. 

by  a  lump  sum  for  the  copyright.  Such  an  agreement 
conveys  the  copyright  of  the  book,  and  vests  it  in  the 
publisher  on  publication.  There  is  usually  a  time-clause 
as  to  the  date  at  which  the  complete  manuscript  is  to  be 
delivered  to  the  publisher,  and  there  should  always  be  a 
clause  fixing  (under  penalty)  the  date  of  publication. 
Further,  there  is  a  clause  prohibiting  the  author  from 
writing  or  publishing  any  similar  book  on  the  same  subject. 
This  clause  must  be  taken  for  what  it  is  worth.  It  will 
readily  be  seen  that  it  is  difficult  to  state  precisely  what 
degree  of  similarity  constitutes  an  infringement  by  one  book 
of  the  copyright  of  another.  If  author  and  publisher  sign- 
ing this  clause  have  reason  to  mistrust  each  other,  the 
points  of  likeness  covered  by  the  prohibition  should  be 
expressly  specified,  a  condition  which  can  readily  be  ful- 
filled in  some  instances,  such  as  those  of  books  of  a  highly 
technical  nature,  where,  indeed,  the  clause  acquires  real 
significance. 

The  most  important  clause  in  such  an  agreement  from 
the  author's  point  of  view,  and  to  a  less  extent  from  the 
publisher's,  is  the  sum  to  be  paid  in  purchase  of  the  book 
and  rights  therein.     It  is  quite  impossible  for  us  to  give 
information  of  any  value  whatsoever  as  a  guide  to  authors 
in  this  matter.    The  opinion  may  be  hazarded,  however, 
that  the  multiplication  of  would-be  authors  during  the 
last  fifteen  or  twenty  years  has  created  a  kind  of  '  slump  ' 
in  certain  markets.     Thus,  the  prizes  recently  offered  for 
novels  by  new  writers  may  tend  further  to  depress  the  fiction 
market,  and  aspirants  will  find  this  depression  reigning  in 
what  publishers  call  juveniles.    Stories  of  adventure  and 
stories  of  a  Sunday-school  or  goody-goody  type  for  girls  or 
children  command,  at  this  moment,  we  believe,  very  low 
prices,  especially  from  new  authors.    We  are  probably  not 
far  from  the  mark  in  conjecturing  that  a  first  offer  for  such 
a  work  would  vary  from  £15  to  £25  or  £30.     Established 
writers  are,  of  course,  better  paid.     But  in  no  case  is  it 
possible  to  give  anything  like  even  anjapproximate  scale  of 
prices  for  literary  wares,  though  an  offer  below  the  rate 
of  £1  per  1,000  words  should  be  regarded  in  the  light  of  a 
[  sporting  bid.'    In  agreements  of  this  kind  the  author,  if 
inexperienced,  would  probably  do  well  to  invoke  the  opinion 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  541 

of  a  literary  agent  before  consenting  to  part  with  his  copy- 
right for  a  definite  sum  of  money. 


Division  of  Profits 

Thirdly,  we  come  to  the  agreement  on  a  profit-sharing 
basis.  In  this  case  the  publisher  takes  all  the  risks  and 
cost  of  publication,  either  for  a  single  edition  of  a  stated 
number  of  copies  or  for  a  stated  number  of  editions  and 
for  a  stated  time.  If  these  statements  are  specified  in 
the  agreement,  it  may  be  renewed  at  option  at  the  time 
of  its  natural  termination.  The  essential  clause  of  such 
an  agreement  is,  of  course,  that  which  deals  with  the 
division  of  the  net  profits.  The  amount  of  these  is  reached 
after  deducting  all  the  costs  of  production  and  publication, 
including  trade  discounts,  advertisements,  and  an  allow- 
ance for  establishment  expenses — i.e.,  clerks'  time  and 
other  office  conveniences.  It  is  obvious  that  an  author 
is  very  much  at  the  mercy  of  his  publisher  in  entering 
upon  such  an  agreement,  for  the  establishment  expenses, 
proportionate  costs  of  travelling,  rent,  warehousing,  and  so 
forth,  as  well  as  the  calculation  of  trade  discounts,  are 
extremely  difficult  in  many  instances  and  impossible  in 
others  to  check.  Yet,  from  the  publisher's  point  of  view, 
these  calculations  form  a  considerable  item.  A  simpler 
way  of  dealing  with  them  would  be  for  the  publisher  to 
state  from  his  own  knowledge  the  proportion  borne  by 
establishment  charges  in  his  gross  profits,  and  to  apply 
this  scale  to  the  particular  book  ;  but  this  method  presses 
hardly  on  the  publisher,  as  the  costs  of  travelling,  etc., 
in  the  case  of  a  new  book  are  higher  than  the  average 
based  on  the  whole  of  his  catalogue.  Authors  should 
only  enter  upon  a  profit-sharing  agreement  with  the  utmost 
caution  and  with  a  most  reputable  firm,  though  it  forms  a 
convenient  arrangement  in  the  case  of  a  book  by  more  than 
one  author.  If  the  publisher  retains,  say,  one-third  or  one- 
half  of  the  net  profits,  the  division  of  the  remaining  two- 
thirds  or  one-half  between  the  several  authors  is  a  matter 
of  simple  arithmetic. 


Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


542  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  n. 

Royalty  Agreement 

Fourthly,  we  come  to  the  royalty  agreement,  which  is 
the  commonest  and  in  most  instances  the  fairest  method  of 
publication.  The  essence  of  a  royalty  agreement  is  that 
the  author  shall  receive  a  certain  sum  in  respect  of  every 
copy  of  the  book  sold,  and,  plainly,  the  most  important 
part  of  such  an  agreement  is  to  determine  the  amount  of 
the  royalty  to  be  paid.  Some  general  considerations 
suggest  themselves  at  this  point.  First:  It  should  be 
fairly  obvious  that  the  author  may  expect  to  receive  a 
larger  royalty  as  the  price  of  the  book  increases.  Taking 
the  commonest  range  of  prices  from  6d.  to  ios.  6d.,  it  is 
clear  that  a  10  per  cent,  royalty  on  a  sixpenny  book  is  an 
almost  impossible  condition,  whereas  it  would  be  an  ex- 
tremely small  charge  on  a  book  published  at  ios.  6d. 
Three-fifths  of  a  penny  deducted  from  a  book  which  may 
be  bought  by  the  public  at  4jd.,  and  which  is  sold  by  the 
publisher  at  any  price  between  3d.  and  4d.,  is  a  far  heavier 
deduction  than  is.  ofd.  deducted  from  a  book  which 
may  be  bought  by  the  public  at  7s.  iojd.,  and  for  which 
the  publisher  receives  something  over  6s.  In  the  first 
instance  the  publisher  would  have  about  2jd.  out  of 
which  to  pay  for  the  production  of  the  book  and  his  own 
profits ;  m  the  latter  case  he  would  have  about  5s. 
Secondly :  Royalties  are  paid  to  the  author  on  the  full 
published  price  of  the  b<x>k.  A  10  per  cent,  royalty  on 
a  2s.  6d.  Book  is  3d.  But  the  publisher  is  paid  only  a 
trade-price.  On  a  2s.  6d.  net  book  he  will  receive,  at  the 
best,  2s.  id.,  less  certain  discounts,  and  for  a  2s.  6d.  non-net 
or  '  ordinary '  book  he  will  receive  on  an  average  about 
is.  6d.  Accordingly,  in  paying  3d.  to  the  author,  and 
calling  it  a  10  per  cent,  royalty,  the  publisher  is  really 
paying  about  one-eighth  of  what  he  receives  on  the  net 
price,  and  about  one-sixth  of  what  he  receives  on  the 
ordinary  price,  or  a  X2\  and  i6f  per  cent,  royalty  respec- 
tively. In  justice  to  publishers,  it  may  be  urged  that  the 
author  who  is  inclined  to  cavil  at  the  small  figure  10  in 
a  royalty  proposal  is  too  frequently  ignorant  or  oblivious 
of  the  real  facts  of  the  case.  Thirdly  :  In  a  royalty  agree- 
ment the  publisher  bears,  as  has  been  said,  all  the  costs 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  543 

of  production.  He  is  accordingly  considerably  out  of 
pocket  by  producing  the  book,  and  even  in  favourable 
circumstances  he  does  not  recoup  his  outlay  till  a  thousand 
copies  of  the  book  have  been  sold.  Hence  it  follows 
that  perhaps  the  fairest  form  of  royalty  agreement  is  one 
which  provides  for  a  graduated  royalty.  If  the  author 
receives  10  per  cent,  on  the  published  price  of  the  first 
thousand  copies,  he  may  fairly  ask  for  124  per  cent,  on 
the  published  price  of  the  1,001st  to  the  2,000th  copy, 
by  which  time  the  publisher  should  also  be  making 
his  first  profits,  if  the  selling  price  of  the  book  has  been 
fairly  adjusted  to  its  production  expenses.  And  if  the 
published  price  can  bear  so  heavy  a  deduction,  the  royalty 
may  then  rise  by  z\  per  cent,  per  thousand  copies  to 
20  per  cent.,  which  is  equivalent,  as  has  been  shown 
above,  to  a  royalty  of  about  33  per  cent.,  or  a  little  more, 
on  the  actual  amount  received  by  the  publisher  for  each 
copy. 

Advance  on  Royalties 

The  last  consideration  to  which  we  would  direct  the 
particular  attention  of  authors  and  publishers,  in  dealing 
with  a  royalty-agreement,  is  that  of  a  stipulated  sum 
to  be  handed  over  in  advance  of  royalties.  Such  a  clause 
providing  that  a  sum,  usually  from  £20  upwards,  shall 
be  paid  to  the  author  by  the  publisher,  either  on  delivery 
of  manuscript,  or  on  the  return  of  the  last  proof  for  press, 
or  on  publication,  has  become  quite  a  common  demand 
in  recent  times  at  the  instance  of  literary  agents  or  of  the 
authors  whose  interests  they  represent.  At  bottom  it 
amounts  to  nothing  more  than  the  author's  borrowing 
money  without  interest  on  a  security  in  which  the  money- 
lender has  already  invested  capital,  and,  looked  at  from 
this  point  of  view,  there  is  certainly  some  force,  at  least,  in 
the  objections  made  by  many  reputable  publishers  to 
such  a  transaction.  The  author's  argument  is  that  he 
is  always  considerably  out  of  time  by  his  labour  on  his 
book,  and  that  he  is  sometimes  even  out  of  pocket  for  the 
expenses  of  typewriting  and  so  forth,  and  that  he  may 
accordingly  not  unreasonably  ask  that  a  part  of  his  future 
profits  shall  be  paid  to  him  in  advance.     This  plea  is 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


544  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK        [part  ii. 

tenable  enough  in  the  case  of  authors  of  established  reputa- 
tion, and  of  books  the  profits  of  which  are  certain  to 
accrue,  and  in  such  cases  a  moderate  request  of  the 
kind  would  probably  meet  with  no  serious  opposition  from 
the  publisher.  But  in  the  case  of  the  large  majority  of 
books,  the  total  profits  on  which  are  not  likely  to  exceed 
a  sum  of  between  £50  and  £100,  and  in  the  case  of  authors 
whose  names  do  not  carry  much  weight  with  the  reading 
public,  and  the  publication  of  whose  work,  therefore,  is 
speculative  on  the  publisher's  part,  such  a  demand  for  a 
loan  without  interest  can  only  be  supported  by  the  fact 
that  publishers  can  be  found  to  comply  with  it.  If  the 
author  has  put  his  time  into  the  book,  the  publisher  is 
prepared  to  put  his  money  and  the  organized  machinery 
of  his  business,  on  which  he  depends  for  a  livelihood. 
Both  legitimately  expect  to  reap  the  reward  of  their 
investment  in  the  profits  that  accrue  in  the  future. 

The  above  facts  and  figures  should  go  far  to  place  the 
normal  facts — we  are  not  dealing  with  exceptional  circum- 
stances— before  the  prospective  author.  He  will  be  dealing 
with  a  business  man,  and  must  be  prepared  to  meet  a  busi- 
ness point  of  view.  In  every  particular,  a  mean  must  be 
found  between  the  author's  faith  in  his  own  book  and  the 
publisher's  willingness  to  back  it.  Thus,  the  price  of  a  book 
will  be  partly  determined  by  the  bargain  struck  between 
the  author  and  the  publisher,  which  it  is  the  agent's  business 
to  make  as  favourable  to  the  author  as  he  can.  If  the 
author  is  content  to  back  his  own  faith  in  his  own  book, 
in  the  sense  of  accepting  a  fair  royalty  on  a  low  price, 
without  power  of  anticipation,  a  publisher  who  likewise 
believes  in  the  book — and  no  other  is  likely  to  publish  it — 
will  similarly  be  willing  to  fix  the  price  within  the  reach  of 
moderate  purses,  and  thus  to  recoup  himself  slowly  for  his 
outlay  on  plant,  advertisement,  travelling,  establishment, 
and  on  stock.  On  the  other  hand,  if,  as  mostly  happens, 
the  author's  estimate  of  sales  has  to  be  divided  by  a  half, 
and  if,  further,  he  insists  on  a  large  payment  in  advance  of 
royalties,  the  publisher  must  either  decline  the  book  or 
must  publish  it  at  a  price  which  will  bring  him  back  his 
money  within  a  reasonable  period — i.e.,  at  a  high  price, 
after    which — as    always    happens — a    cheap    '  popular ' 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  545 

edition  becomes  a  wise  speculation.    This  is  the  normal 
course  of  the  successful  launching  of  new  authors. 
We  now  come  to  the  memorandum  itself : 

III.  MEMORANDUM  OF  AGREEMENT 

After  what  has  been  said  above,  the  common  form^of 
memorandum  does  not  require  any  lengthy  consideration. 
The  order  and  wording  of  the  clauses  will  differ  from  house 
to  house,  but  must  all  alike  carry  out  the  principles  agreed 
above. 

The  preamble  states  that  '  This  Agreement  made '  on 
such-and-such  a  date  '  between '  so-and-so  '  Publishers 
[and  Exporters]  of  for  themselves  their 

successors  and  assignees*  hereinafter  called  the  Publishers 
of  the  one  part  and '  so-and-so  '  of  ■  for  him 

(her)  self  his  (her)  heirs  executors  administrators  and 
assignees  hereinafter  styled  the  Author  of  the  other  part 
Witnesseth: 

The  preamble  gives  us  the  essential  parties — viz.,  pub- 
lisher and  author — without  further  circumlocution.  These 
parties  agree  as  to  a  sale  or  lease  of  the  author's  property 
to  the  publisher  on  certain  terms  and  conditions.  The 
essence  of  the  agreement  follows  the  technical  word  wit- 
nesseth, and  a  royalty  agreement  proceeds  thus  or  to  this 
effect :  '  That  in  consideration  that  the  copyright  and 
plant  including*  therein '  such  foreign  and  Colonial  rights 
of  translation  and  reproduction  as  the  author  may  arrange 
to  part  with  '  of  the  work  to  be  written  by  the  Author  and 

*  The  opinion  has  been  expressed  that  the  contract  should  be 
personal  to  the  publisher,  ana  not  also  with  his  heirs,  executors, 
administrators,  and  assigns.  It  wiU  be  sufficient  to  say  that 
no  publisher  of  repute  (except  under  very  special  circumstances) 
would,  if  only  in  justice  to  his  own  creditors,  make  it  a  practice  to 
enter  into  purely  personal  contracts,  and  thus  destroy  the  whole 
conception  of  a  trading  concern,  converting  a  genuine  commercial 
enterprise  into  mere  speculations  on  his  own  longevity.  Even  were 
he  to  do  so,  the  value  of  the  article  to  be  transferred  by  the  author 
would  be  correspondingly  reduced.  The  word  "  assigns,"  however, 
might  advantageously  be  excluded,  as  an  author  may  reasonably 
insist  on  being  made  a  party  to  an  assignment,  seeing  that  his 
pecuniary  interests  are  involved  and  that  the  assignee  may  be  a 
less  solvent  person  than  the  assignor,  or  even  insolvent. 

18 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


546  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

entitled  provisionally '  so-and-so  '  shall  forthwith  belong 
to  and  become  the  property  of  the  Publishers,  but  subject 
always  to  a  lien  thereon  for  any  sums  due  to  the  Author 
by  the  Publishers  for  Royalties,  the  Publishers  hereby 
agree  '  [this  verb  is  governed  in  legal  grammar  by  the  first 
conjunction  that  after  witnesseth]  to  print  publish  adver- 
tise and  sell  a  first  edition  of '  so  many  '  copies  of  the  said 
work  and  as  many  subsequent  editions  thereof  as  they  may 
think  desirable  and  to  pay  to  the  Author  a  Royalty  on  the 
sales  as  hereinafter  provided.' 

A  few  commas  would  improve  the  appearance  of  this 
statement,  but  punctuation  is  taboo,  as  a  rule,  in  such 
documents.    Otherwise,  the  points  worthy  of  notice  are  : 

1.  The  stamp  duty  is  reduced  to  6d.  by  describing  the 
book  as  '  to  be  written,'  and  its  title  as  '  provisional.' 

2.  The  transfer  of  copyright.  This  clause^appears  in 
practically  all  agreements  of  this  nature,  and  our  advice'to 
authors  is  never  to  part  with  their  copyright,  but  to  alter 
this  to  '  exclusive  right  of  printing,'  an  alteration  to  which 
no  firm  of  repute  should  object.  The  object,  of  course,  is 
to  protect  the  publisher  against  the  negligent  author  who 
brings  out  another  or  very  similar  edition  with  a  rival  firm, 
and  so  kills  the  market  for  the  original  edition. 

3.  '  Their  successors^ and  assignees.'  We  leave  ^these 
words  as  they  stand  in  ordinary  agreements,  but  it  is 
extremely  desirable  (see  after  Clause  10,  below)  to  provide 
for  the  determination  of  the  agreement  in  the  event  of 
certain  contingencies,  such  as  a  sale  to  creditors  or  to  a 
house  of  inferior  standing  of  the  publisher's  assets.  Much 
depends  upon  the  status  of  the  firm  with  which  the  author 
is 'negotiating,  and  it  is  in  matters  of  this  kind  that  an  agent's 
advice  would  be  useful,  if  he  would  consent^  to  be  consulted 
like  a  solicitor,  and  to  receive  a  fee  for  his  services. 

4.  Foreign  and  colonial  rights  vary  in  value  with  the 
nature  of  the  book,  and  in  some  cases  are  nil.  When  con- 
siderable sales  out  of  England  are  expected,  the  arrange- 
ments should  be  carefully  discussed  beforehand,  and  a 
publisher  selected  who  has  facilities  for  distribution  in  other 
countries.  The  import  duty  in  the  U.S.A.  makes  a  profit- 
able sale  increasingly  difficult,  but,  as  it  is  cheaper  to  print 
a  large  edition  than  a  small,  a  part  of  the  publisher's 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.  LAW  AND  LETTERS  547 

profit  lies  in  what  he  saves  in  his  printer's  bill.  Trans- 
lation rights,  as  a  rule,  fetch  only  small  sums,  as  they 
handicap  the  book  with  a  preliminary  fee  to  the  translator 
before  the  ordinary  production  expenses  begin.  Colonial 
editions,  as  such,  are  very  fast  falling  into  disuse  ;  they 
should  carry  a  royalty  proportionate  to  the  selling  price  of 
the  book  in  that  form. 

5.  If  the  author  wishes  to  make  stipulations  as  to  ad- 
vertisements, he  must  do  so  at  this  stage.  Some  firms 
advertise  much  more  extensively  than  others,  who  rely, 
perhaps,  on  better  '  travelling '  arrangements. 

The  memorandum  now  continues  : 

'  And  it  is  hereby  further  agreed 

'  1.  That  the  Publishers  shall  :— 

'  (a)  Produce  the  book  in  volume    . 

1  (b)  Warehouse  the  bound  and  quire  stock. 
'  (c)  Deliver  the  usual  Press  copies  to  the  London 
and  Provincial  Journals. 

See  note  3  above  (under  "  Commissioning  the  Publisher,"  page  554). 

'  (d)  Account  annually  to  the  Author  for  every 
copy  produced.' 

Semi-annual  renditions  are  sometimes  granted,  if  desired,  and  are 
preferable  to  annual  accounts. 

'  2.  That  the  Author's  Royalty  shall  be  calculated  in 
manner  following  : — 
'  (a)  As  to  copies  sold  in  the  United  Kingdom  at 
the  regular  trade  price,  per 

centum  on  the  full  published  price  there- 
of, thirteen  copies  being  reckoned  as 
twelve. 

Comment  on  this  clause  is  governed  by  the  remarks  in  the  fore- 
going section  on  'Author,  Publisher,  and  Agent.'  Should  a  gradu- 
ated royalty  be  agreed,  as  recommended,  the  clause  would  run  : 
so  much  '  per  cent,  on  the  full  published  price  of  the  first  thousand.' 
(or  so  many)  '  copies  thereof/  so  much  '  per  cent,  on  that  of  the 
next '  so  many  '  copies  thereof/  so  much  '  per  cent,  on  that  of 
the  next*  so  many  'copies  thereof/  and  so  on,  '13  copies  being 
reckoned  as  12/ 

l8—2 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


C 


548  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  n. 

( (6)  As  to   stereotype  plates   and  copies  sold 
for  the  American,  Indian,  and  Colonial 
markets   at  reduced  prices, 
per   centum   on    the   amount    actually 
received  in  respect  thereof. 

Subject  to  discussion.  Ten  per  cent,  on  the  actual  receipts  is 
often  quite  fair  to  the  author,  as  books  are  sold  to  the  American 
market  at  not  much  above  cost  price  of  production.  The  condi- 
tions ruling  the  transaction  in  question  should  be  examined  on  their 
merits. 

'  (c)  As  to  copies  sold  in  the  United  Kingdom 
by  auction,  or  privately  to  a  dealer  at 
reduced  prices,  or  by  way  of  remainder, 
per  centum  on  the  amount 
actually  received  in  respect  thereof,  but 
the  Author  shall  always  have  the  option 
of  purchasing  such  remainders  from  the 
Publisher  at  their  actual  cost  of  pro- 
duction.' 

This  clause  is  not  as  tautological  as  it  sounds.  The  remaining 
copies  of  an  old  edition  may  be  sold  at  reduced  prices,  though  the 
book  itself  is  not '  remaindered/  as  special  trade  reasons  may  inter- 
vene. Five  or  ten  per  cent,  is  quite  a  fair  proportion  to  the  author, 
as  a  rule.  Note  that  the  remainder-clause  is  essential  in  an  author's 
interest.  Failing  the  provision  for  the  author's  share  in  copies 
sold  '  by  way  of  remainder/  it  has  recently  been  held  in  law  mat 
the  publisher  is  not  liable  to  pay  any  royalty  on  such  sales  {Farmer 
v.  Lane  :  Mr.  Heinemann's  evidence  for  the  defendants).  The 
injustice  of  this  decision  in  equity  is  clear,  and  the  author  should  see 
thiat  he  is  protected.  The  clause  should  further  invariably  provide 
for  the  first  offer  of  the  '  remainder '  to  be  made  to  the  author. 

The  remaining  clauses  of  the  memorandum  are  as  follows, 
and  do  not  call  for  special  comment  beyond  what  has  been 
given  in  advance  : 

1  3.  That  bad  debts  shall  be  the  charge  of  the  Publishers, 
the  Author  not  being  deprived  of  any  Royalty  on  copies 
sold  by  reason  of  the  sale-proceeds  not  having  been  paid 
to  the  Publishers. 

'  4.  That  the  manner  of  Production,  Publication,  Sale, 
and  extent  of  Advertisement  of  the  said  work  shall  in  the 
absence  of  express  agreement  to  the  contrary  lie  wholly 
within  the  discretion  of  the  Publishers. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  n.]  LAW  AND  LETTERS  549 

'  5.  That  the  Author  shall  be  liable  for  the  expenses  of 
Author's  proof  corrections  (exclusive  of  the  corrections  of 
Printers'  errors)  beyond  the  amount  of  six  shillings  per 
sheet  of  sixteen  pages  and  that  should  such  charges 
exceed  this  amount  the  Author  shall  be  debited  with 
the  excess. 

'  6.  That  the  Publishers  shall  annually  make  up  as  at 
the  thirtieth  of  June  [or  twice  a  year,  as  at  the  thirtieth 
of  June  and  the  thirty-first  of  December]  the  accounts  of 
each  year  and  shall  on  demand  render  the  same  to  the 
Author  two  months  thereafter  and  shall  at  the  same  time 
pay  over  any  balance  which  may  be  found  due  to  the 
Author. 

'  7.  That  the  Publishers'  books  shall  be  open  to  the 
inspection  of  the  Author  either  personally  or  by  agent  at 
any  reasonable  time  after  notice  given  in  writing. 

*  8.  That  the  Author  shall  be  responsible  for  any  action 
of  Libel  or  Infringement  of  Copyright  arising;  out  of  the 
publication  of  the  said  work  and  shall  indemnify  and 
reimburse  the  Publishers  for  any  damages  costs  or  charges 
which  they  may  reasonably  incur  or  be  called  upon  to 
pay  in  respect  thereof. 

9.  That  the  Author  shall  be  entitled  to  six  [or  twelve] 
gratis  copies  of  the  said  work  on  publication,  and  that  any 
further  copies  required  shall  be  supplied  at  two-thirds  of 
the  published  price  (thirteen  copies  being  reckoned  as 
twelve). 

1 10.  That,  should  the  Publishers  allow  the  book  to  be 
and  remain  out  of  print  for  a  period  of  [six]  months  or  more, 
the  copyright  shall  ipso  facto  revert  to  and  become  vested 
in  the  Author.' 

The  payment  on  account  and  agency  clauses,  if  included, 
would  follow  here,  and  likewise  any  special  clauses  agreed 
between  the  parties,  such  as,  for  example,  a  proviso  for 
the  termination  of  the  Agreement  in  the  event  of  the 
Publisher's  bankruptcy.

PUBLIC  LIBRARIES 


PREFACE 

As  was  the  case  last  year,  the  returns  in  the  Library 
Section  of  the  191 1  issue  of  the  Literary  Y ear-Book  have 
been  made  by  requesting  the  various  public  and  other 
libraries,  supplying  fairly  full  information,  to  correct  the 
previous  year  s  entries,  and  the  Editor  desires  to  thank  all 
Librarians  who  have  assisted  in  this  respect.  This  course 
was  adopted  for  various  reasons,  two  of  which  will  suffice 
— namely,  (a)  some  librarians  complained  that  the  filling 
up,  year  after  year,  of  the  same  extensive  circular  of 
questions — the  replies  to  many  of  which  do  not  change — 
was  irksome  ;  and  (b)  the  considerable  extra  expense  and 
time  involved  in  preparing  the  returns  from  these  circulars 
were  not  justified. 

The  alphabetical  list  of  libraries  which  have  museums 
or  art  galleries  in  connection  has  been  retained,  and  the 
editor  hopes  this  will  be  found  useful  to  librarians  and 
others. 

The  schedule  of  questions  upon  which  the  returns  are 
based  are  as  follows  : 

Name  of  Library  (Town,  Address,  etc.).  Administrative  area 
(County,  Borough,  Parish,  etc.).  Population.  Year  of  adoption 
of  the  Acts  (or  of  the  foundation,  if  not  rate-supported).  Librarian's 
name.  What  rate  in  the  £  is  levied  ?  Does  the  Library  pay 
(a)  Local  or  district  rates ;  (b)  Income  tax ;  (c)  Inhabited  House 
duty  ?  Total  income  from  all  sources  (including  product  of  rate 
if  rate-supported).  Annual  expenditure  (including  branches)  on 
(a)  Books  ;  (6)  Binding ;  (c)  Periodicals ;  (d)  Salaries  and  wages, 
(e)  Loans  (including  interest).  Number  of  assistants  (including 
those  at  branches) :  (a)  Male ;  (b)  Female ;  (c)  Caretakers,  attendants 
and  cleaners.  Number  of  volumes  in  stock  (including  branches) — 
in  Lending  Library,  in  Reference  Library.      Number  of  borrowers, 

55i 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


( 


552  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  ii. 

tickets  in  force  (including  branches).  Annual  issues  (including 
branches) — in  Lending  Library,  in  Reference  Library.  System 
of  classification  in  use— in  landing  Library,  in  Reference  Library  : 

(a)  Dewey ;  (6)  Cutter ;  (c)  Adjustable ;  (d)  Brown's  subject  ; 
(e)  Numerical  in  main  classes  ;  (/)  Any  other.  Form  of  catalogue— 
in  Lending  Library,  in  Reference  Library  :  (a)  Dictionary,  printed  ; 

(b)  Dictionary,  MS. ;  (c)  Dictionary,  MS.,  on  cards  or  sheaves ; 
{d)  Classified,  printed  (Sect,  or  com.) ;  (e)  Classified,  MS.,  or  (/)  Classi- 
fied, MS.,  on  cards  or  sheaves.  Do  you  obliterate  betting  news  ? 
Do  you  open  on  Sundays  or  public  holidays  ?  Do  you  publish  a 
periodical  or  bulletin  ?  Have  you  a  children's  reading-room  and 
separate  lending  department  ?  Have  you  lectures  ?  Have  you 
school  libraries  ?  Have  you  any  other  special  features  (Museums 
and  Art  Gallery,  School)  ?  Method  of  issuing  and  charging  books — 
in  Lending  Library,  in  Reference  Library:  (a)  Indicator  (i.  AH 
stock ;  2.  Fiction  only)  ;  (b)  Card  charging ;  (c)  Ledger  charging ; 
(d)  Application  forms ;  {e)  Signature  book.  Do  you  allow  access 
to  the  shelves — in  Lending  Library,  in  Reference  Library  ?  (a)  Safe- 
guarded access  ;  (b)  Non-safeguarded  access.  Has  your  Committee 
to  submit  proceedings  to  the  Council  for  (a)  Confirmation*  ;  (6)  Per- 
mission to  act* ;  (h)  Report  ?  Number  of  branches.  Number  of 
delivery  stations.  Hours  of  opening.  Is  the  public  admitted  to 
the  Library  ?  *  Has  special  permission  to  be  obtained  for  admis- 
sion ?  If  by  special  permission,  what  steps  are  necessary  to  obtain 
it  ?  -   Date  of  catalogue,  if  not  brought  up  to  date. 


*  (a)  and  (6)  are  practically  the  same  under  ordinary  circum- 
stances.

PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  ASSESSED  FOR  LOCAL  OR 
DISTRICT  RATES 

In  the  cases  where  it  is  stated  that  inhabited  portions 
of  the  library  buildings  only  are  assessed,  or  where  the  rate 
is  paid  as  rent  to  the  local  authority,  the  names  of  those 
institutions  are  not  included  in  this  list,  but  nominal 
assessments  are  included.

THE  LIBRARY  ASSOCIATION 

The  Library  Association  was  founded  in  1877  as  the 
Library  Association  of  the  United  Kingdom,  and  incor- 
porated as  '  The  Library  Association  '  by  royal  charter 
m  1898. 

Amongst  other  work  for  the  good  of  the  library  move- 
ment, the  objects  of  the  Association  include  the  promotion 
of  the  better  administration  of  libraries  ;  of  the  adoption 
of  the  Public  Libraries  Acts  ;  of  the  union  of  all  persons 
engaged  or  interested  in  library  work  by  holding  con- 
ferences and  meetings  for  the  discussion  of  bibliographical 
questions  and  matters  affecting  libraries  or  their  regulation 
or  management,  or  otherwise ;  the  oversight  and  promotion 
of  legislation ;  and  examinations  in  librarianship,  and  the 
issue  of  certificates.  In  connection  with  the  last  the 
Association  has  instituted  classes  and  examinations,  and 
the  efforts  of  the  Education  Committee  are  meeting  with 
the  greatest  success. 

Monthly  meetings  are  held  throughout  the  session  from 
November  to  June,  and  a  General  Meeting  takes  place 
annually,  usually  at  the  end  of  August  or  at  the  beginning 
of  September. 

The  Library  Association  Record,  a  monthly  magazine,  is 
the  official  organ  of  the  Association,  and  among  many  useful 
and  important  publications  issued  by  the  Association  is  the 
Class  List  of  Best  Books. 

The  Association  is  watching  over  the  whole  question 
of  the  improvement  of  book  production,  and  the  Sound 
Leather  Committee  published  a  valuable  report  on  leather 
for  bookbinding  in  1905.  Already  many  leather  manu- 
facturers have  risen  to  the  occasion  and  produced  leathers 
of  the  necessary  standard.  President,  1910-11 :  Mr.  F.  G. 
Ken  yon,  M.A.,  D.Litt.,  Principal  Librarian  British  Museum. 
Hon.  Treasurer:  Mr.   Henry  R.   Tedder,   F.S.A.,  The 

566 

Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


part  n.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  567 

Athenaeum,  Pall  Mall,  s.w.  Hon.  Solicitor:  Mr.  H.  W. 
Fovargue,  Town  Hall,  Eastbourne.  Hon.  Secretary: 
Mr.  L.  Stanley  Jast,  Public  Library,  Town  Hall,  Croydon. 
Offices :  24,  Whitcomb  Street,  London,  w.c. 

Library  Association  Branches. 

There  are  several  district  Library  Associations  more  or 
less  closely  connected  with  the  Library  Association.  The 
following  is  a  list  of  these  branch  and  district  associations, 
with  their  principal  officers  : 

North-Western  Branch  of  the  Library  Association.— 
President:  Mr.  C.  W.  Sutton,  M.A.,  Librarian  Public 
Libraries,  Manchester.  Hon.  Secretary:  Mr.  Edward 
McKnight  (Chorley). 

Birmingham  and  District  Library  Association. — President  : 
Mr.  G.  O.  Hodges  (West  Bromwich).  Vice-President: 
Mr.  W.  Salt  Brassington,  F.S.A.  (Stratford-on-Avon). 
Hon.  Secretary :  Mr.  Robert  K.  Dent  (Aston  Manor). 

North  Midland  \  Library  Association.— President :  Mr. 
W.  J.  Willcock  (Peterborough).  Vice-President:  Mr.  A. 
Lineker  (Nottingham).  Hon.  Secretary:  Mr.  J.  Potter 
Briscoe,  F.R.S.L.  (Nottingham). 

Northern  Counties  Library  Association.  —  President  : 
Mr.  G.  W.  Byers  (Harrogate).  Vice-Presidents :  Messrs. 
B.  Anderton,  B.A.  (Newcastle-upon-Tyne)  ;  A.  H.  Fur- 
nish (York)  ;  T.  W.  Hand,  F.R.Hist.S.  (Leeds) ;  B.  R. 
Hill  (Newcastle-upon-Tyne)  ;  D.  S.  Ward  (Harrogate) ; 
Butler  Wood  (Bradford).  Hon.  Secretary:  Mr.  H.  E. 
Johnston  (Gateshead).

THEJLIBRARY  ASSISTANTS'  ASSOCIATION 

The  Library  Assistants'  Association  was  founded  in 
1895  by  a  few  prominent  London  library  assistants,  who 
realized  that,  in  order  to  organize  the  growing  profession 
of  librarianship,  and  to  procure  adequate  professional 
education,  some  such  society  was  necessary.  It  differs 
from  the  Library  Association  in  being  a  purely  professional 
body,  admitting  as  members  only  persons  under  the  rank 
of  chief  librarian  who  are  actually  engaged  in  the  adminis- 
tration of  libraries.  It  works  in  consonance  with  all 
existing  library  societies,  but  is  not  affiliated  to  any  of 
them.  Its  membership  consists  of  Hon.  Fellows,  Fellows 
(former  members,  now  Chief  librarians),  Members,  and 
Associates.  The  Association  is  controlled  by  a  president, 
vice-president,  hon.  treasurer,  and  hon.  secretary,  and  a 
council  of  two  Fellows  and  ten  London  and  ten  non- 
London  Members  and  Associates,  who  are  elected  annually 
in  June.  It  holds  meetings  monthly  from  October  to  June 
at  various  libraries  and  institutions  in  and  around  London, 
at  which  papers  are  read  and  discussed.  The  majority  of 
these  papers  are  written  by  the  members,  but  nearly  every 
prominent  chief  librarian  in  London  has  contributed  to  its 
proceedings.  It  endeavours  to  represent  every  shade  of 
library  opinion,  and  rigorously  represses  any  leaning 
towards  individual  systems  of  method  and  training.  Its 
inaugural  addresses  have  been  delivered  by  Messrs.  Sidney 
Lee,  Sidney  Webb,  Edmund  Gosse,  Israel  Gollancz,  T.  J. 
Macnamara,  and  the  Hon.  Pember  Reeves  in  recent  years. 
Its  objects,  as  stated  in  its  constitution,  are  to  promote 
the  professional,  educational,  and  social  interests  of  its 
members  by  the  reading  of  original  papers,  by  discussions, 
meetings  of  a  social  character,  and  in  other  ways.  In 
addition  to  the  monthly  meetings,  excursions,  football, 
cricket,  and  other  sports  are  engaged  in. 

568 

Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  PUBLIC  LIBRARIES  569 

It  publishes  a  journal,  the  Library  Assistant,  monthly, 
which  is  a  record  of  the  proceedings  of  the  Association, 
and  a  means  of  intercommunication  between  library 
assistants  ;  this  is  issued  free  to  members.  The  officers  of 
the  Association  are  :  President :  Mr.  W.  C.  Berwick  Sayers, 
Central  Library,  Town  Hall,  Croydon.  Vice-President: 
Mr.  G.  Strother,  Central  Library,  Leeds.  Hon.  Treasurer: 
Mr.  W.  Geo.  Chambers,  Public  Library,  Plumstead.  Hon. 
Editor:  Mr.  H.  Rutherford  Purnell,  Central  Library, 
Croydon.  Hon.  Secretary :  Mr.  Henry  T.  Coutts,  North 
Library,  Manor  Gardens,  Holloway,  London,  N. 

The  Association  has  five  vigorous  branches  The 
Yorkshire  branch  has  its  centre  at  Leeds,  and  holds  bi- 
monthly meetings.  President:  Mr.  G  W.  Strother, 
Leeds ;  Hon.  Secretary :  Mr.  J.  B.  Ellison,  2,  Edinburgh 
Grove,  Armley,  Leeds. 

The  Association  of  Assistant  Librarians  is  the  Irish 
branch.  This  has  its  centre  at  Belfast,  and  holds  monthly 
meetings.  Chairman:  Mr.  R.  J.  Gourley,  Belfast;  Hon. 
Secretary :  Mr.  W.  Moore,  Central  Library,  Belfast. 

The  North-Eastern  Branch  was  founded  in  1908,  and 
embraces  the  assistant  librarians  in  Northumberland, 
Durham,  and  Cumberland,  and  holds  quarterly  meetings. 
Chairman:  Mr.  J.  Walton,  Newcastle;  Hon.  Secretary: 
Mr.  W.  H.  Gibson,  Central  Public  Library,  Newcastle. 

The  South  Wales  Branch  was  also  formed  in  1908.  It 
embraces  all  South  Wales  and  the  South- Western  counties, 
and  holds  monthly  meetings.  Chairman :  Mr.  Wyndham 
Morgan,  Cardiff ;  Hon.  Secretary :  Mr.  C.  Sexton,  Central 
Public  Library,  Cardiff. 

The  Midland  Branch  was  constituted  in  December, 
1909,  and  has  its  centre  at  Birmingham.  Chairman: 
Mr.  H.  W.  Checketts,  Birmingham  University;  Hon. 
Secretary :  Mr.  W.  Ewart  Owen,  Public  Library,  Coventry. 


THE  MEDICAL  LIBRARY  ASSOCIATION  OF 
GREAT  BRITAIN  AND  IRELAND 

For  particulars  of  this  Association  see  under  SOCIETIES.

AMERICAN  LIBRARY  ASSOCIATION 

The  American  Library  Association,  founded  in  1876, 
a  year  before  the  formation  of  the  Library  Association,  has 
similar  objects — viz.,  to  promote  the  welfare  of  libraries 
in  America  by  interchange  of  opinion  and  experience,  and 
by  co-operative  publication  of  useful  library  and  biblio- 
graphical aids.  These  publications  include  the  A.L.A. 
Bulletin  ;  the  A.L.A.  Book  List;  the  Proceedings  of  the 
Annual  Meeting  ;  and  the  Annual  Handbook,  besides  other 
special  titles,  found  in  the  Annual  Handbook. 

The  membership  of  the  American  Library  Association 
now  numbers  2,000.  The  official  organ  of  the  American 
Library  Association  is  the  Bulletin.  The  executive  offices 
are  situated  at  34,  Newbury  Street,  Boston,  Mass. 
Secretary:  Mr.  J.  I.  Wyer,  jun.,  New  York  State  Library, 
Albany,  N.Y. 

(See  also  under  SOCIETIES:  FOREIGN.)

library,  and  books  are  sent  from  one  library  to  another  at 
the  reader's  request.  The  Mitchell  Library  is  for  reference 
only,  and  details  marked  *  refer  to  the  branch  libraries.)
