LONDON. 

The  London  Government  Act,  in  some  cases,  formed  Boroughs 
of  several  existing  Vestries.  The  Libraries  so  amalgamated  are 
sometimes  administered  by  sub-committees,  and  an  endeavour 
has  been  made  to  give  the  fullest  details  of  all  the  Boroughs. 

British  Museum  Reading  Room,  Great  Russell  Street,  Blooms* 
bury,  w.c.  (1753).  The  number  of  books  cannot  be  stated 
exactly,  probably  two  millions. 

Admission. — This  is  granted  (to  applicants  over  twenty- 
one  years  of  age)  on  receipt  of  a  recommendation  by  a  house- 
holder who  is  personally  acquainted  with  the  applicant. 
The  particular  purpose  for  which  admission  is  sought  must 
also  be  indicated  in  the  letter  of  application.  This  must  be 
sent  by  post  two  days  at  least  before  admission  is  required. 
The  ticket  is  usually  for  three  or  six  months.  When  asking 
for  renewal,  it  is  necessary  to  return  the  old  ticket.  Tickets 
must  be  returned  when  no  longer  required.  Applicants  under 
twenty-one  years  of  age  require  the  Trustees'  special  permission . 
All  tickets  are  strictly  non-transferable,  and  must  be  shown, 
if  required,  before  entering  the  room.  ,  For  thejStudents* 
Room  of  the  Department  of  MSS.,  the  Print;  Room,  etc. 


Digitized  by  C3OOQIC 


618  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ji. 

LONDON  {continued)— 

a  separate  ticket  is  required,  subject  to  the  same  conditions 
as  to  its  granting,  etc.,  as  for  the  Reading  Room. 

No  sticks  or  umbrellas  are  permitted  in  the  rooms  ;  over- 
coats should  also  be  left  with  the  cloak-room  officials. 

Rooms. — Besides  the  Newspaper  Room  (on  the  right  of 

the  main  entrance),  which  also  contains  the  London  Gazette, 

Journals  of  the  House  of  Commons,  and  other  Parliamentary 

papers,  and  the  Print  Room  (open  from  10  a.m.  till  5  p.m.), 

/*  there  is  the  Rotunda.      The  latter   is  the  Reading   Room 

s^£>  |  0  (for  Hours,  see  p.  6fft. 

\  Application    for    Books. — Round    the    central    desk, 

occupied  by  the  Superintendent  and  his  attendants,  will  be 
seen  boxes  of  printed  slips.  These  are  filled  in,  as  indicated, 
from  the  Catalogues,  the  chief  items  to  note  being  the  correct 
press-mark  of  the  book,  and  the  number  of  the  reader's  seat. 
The  other  particulars — i.e.,  date  of  publication,  title  and 
author  of  the  book — are  also,  however,  of  importance,  and 
not  to  be  omitted  by  those  desiring  to  be  served  without 
delay.  The  sHps  are  placed  in  baskets  at  the  centre  counter, 
the  reader  returning  to  his  desk  to  await  the  books,  a  matter 
usually  of  between  ten  and  thirty  minutes,  according  to  the 
pressure  of  work  and  the  portion  of  the  library  in  which  the 
books  are  stored.  No  applications  are  received  after 
3.30  p.m.  in  January,  February,  November,  and  December ; 
4.30  p.m.  in  March  and  October;  5.30  p.m.  in  April  and 
September;  and  6.30  p.m.  in  the  summer  months.  When 
reading  is  over  for  the  day  the  reader  restores  the  books  to 
the  centre  desk,  receiving  in  exchange  the  slips,  which,  unless 
he  intends  using  the  same  book  or  books  again  within  the  next 
few  days,  are  best  destroyed.  Readers  are  held  responsible 
for  books  until  the  slips  have  been  re-delivered  to  them. 

Kept  Books. — Books  are  kept,  by  request,  subject  to  no 
other  reader  putting  in  a  ticket  for  them  during  the  absence 
of  the  reader  for  whom  they  are  put  aside.  In  each  book 
a  slip  of  paper  must  be  placed,  across  the  top  of  which  (so 
as  to  be  legible  without  opening  the  book)  are  written  the 
date  and  reader's  name.  There  are  special  baskets  at  Bar  A 
and  Bar  T  for  slips  of  "  kept "  books,  and  the  latter  are 
usually  brought  round  within  a  few  minutes. 

Thb  Bar. — It  is  sometimes  impossible  for  a  reader  to 
spend  any  time  at  the  Reading  Room  until  after  the  latest 
hour  for  putting  in  application  slips  in  the  ordinary  way. 
In  such  cases  it  is  permitted  to  send  the  slips,  filled  in  before- 
hand, by  post,  to  be  delivered  at  the  Museum,  some  hours 
before  the  books  are  actually  wanted.  Instead  of  filling  in  any 
desk-number  the  applicant  marks  Bar  A  or  Bar  T,  and  the 
books  are  kept  for  him  in  the  cages  on  either  side  of  the 
central  desk  until  called  for  later  in  the  day.  "Kept"  books, 
if  not  called  for  within  a  day  or  two,  are  returned  to  the 
shelves. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  II.]  LIBRARIES  619 

LONDON  (continued)— 

Reference  Shelves. — In  addition  to  the  bulk  of  the 
collection,  which  is  only  available  as  indicated  in  the  fore- 
going remarks,  there  are  on  the  shelves  on  the  ground  floor 
of  the  Rotunda  itself  some  20,000  reference  volumes,  ranging 
through  history,  geography  and  travel,  and  every  science, 
down  to  such  works  of  reference  as  encyclopaedias,  lexicons, 
directories,  and  the  like.  These  the  reader  is  permitted  to 
take  down  for  himself,  but  must  replace  on  the  proper  shelf 
when  he  has  finished  using  them.  With  regard  to  the 
encyclopaedias,  dictionaries,  etc.,  these  must  be  returned  to 
the  shelves  as  soon  as  done  with,  and  in  some  cases  must  be 
consulted  at  the  shelf.  In  Continental  libraries  readers  are 
not  permitted  to  carry  off  to  their  own  desks  reference  works 
of  this  description. 

Catalogues. — The  Author  Catalogue,  stretching  in 
double  row  around  the  central  desk,  is  now,  in  its  printed 
form,  condensed  to  about  970  volumes.  In  the  way  of 
Class  Catalogues,  which  for  the  most  part  exist  in  the 
unrealized  dreams  of  successive  generations  of  librarians,  we 
have  Fortescue's  exceedingly  useful  Subject  Index,  and 
Poole's  likewise  valuable  Index  to  Periodicals.  A  new 
edition  of  Fortescue's  Subject  Index,  i 881  -1900,  has  now 
been  printed,  as  also  an  additional  volume  for  the  years 
1 90 1 -1905.  These  aids  to  reference  are,  with  their  respec- 
tive supplements,  to  be  found  next  the  central  desk.  There 
are,  in  addition  to  these,  special  catalogues  of  manuscripts, 
music,  and  maps,  and  useful  bibliographies  on  every  subject 
are  to  be  found  in  the  cases  at  the  end  of  the  readers'  desks. 

Tracing. — Application  must  be  made  to  the  Superinten- 
dent for  permission. 

The  Reading  Room  is  open  on  week-days  (except  the  first 
four  days  in  March  and  September,  Good  Friday,  Christmas 
Day,  and  days  appointed  by  authority),  from  9  a.m.  to  7  p.m., 
throughout  the  year. 

The  Newspaper  Room,  which  is  under  the  same  regula- 
tions as  the  Reading  Room,  is  open  January,  February, 
10  a.m.  to  3.50  p.m. ;  March,  10  a.m.  to  4.50  p.m. ;  April  to 
August,  10  a.m.  to  5.50  p.m. ;  September,  October,  10  a.m. 
to  4.50  p.m. ;  November,  December,  10  a.m.  to  3.50  p.m. 
The  Room  is  closed  on  Sundays,  Good  Friday,  Christmas 
Day,  and  the  first  four  week-days  of  March  and  September. 

The  Students'  Room.  Department  of  MSS.,  is  open 
from  10  a.m.  to  5  p.m.,  it  being  required  that  aU  MSS.  be 
given  up  at  4.45  p.m.  The  Room  is  closed  at  certain  times, 
as  in  the  case  of  the  Reading  and  Newspaper  Rooms. 

The  Print  Room  and  the  Oriental  Students'  Room  are 
open  from  10  a.m.  to  5  p.m.

632  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

LONDON  (continued) — 

South  Kensington  Museum  (Victoria  and  Albert  Museum) 
Science  Library  ;  Mr.  L.  W.  Fulcher,  B.Sc.  Founded 
in  1857  as  a  general  educational  library,  it  has  been  chiefly 
developed  by  the  acquisition  of  books  on  natural  and  applied 
science.  It  contains  also  a  complete  set  of  the  specifications 
and  other  publications  of  the  Patent  Office,  and  recently  the 
bulk  of  the  library  of  the  Museum  of  Practical  Geology  at 
Jermyn  Street  has  been  incorporated  with  it.  Admission 
free  to  students,  etc.,  and  by  payment.  Application  to  the 
Secretary,  Board  of  Education,  S.  Kensington.  Library 
open  same  time  as  museum.     Closed  on  Sunday. 

National  Art  Library  ;  Mr.  G.  H.  Palmer,  B.A.  Founded 
for  the  use  of  students  in  the  Government  Schools  of  Design 
at  Somerset  House  in  1841  ;  transferred  to  Marlborough 
House  in  1852,  and  there  opened  to  the  public  on  payment 
of  a  small  fee  ;  moved  to  South  Kensington  in  1856.  Vs. 
R.  115,000  books,  with  about  160,000  prints  and  drawings, 
and  about  175,000  photographs ;  Iss.  L.  57,684  volumes, 
2,964  boxes,  portfolios,  or  volumes  of  prints  and  drawings ; 
^2,149  boxes  or  portfolios  of  photographs;  Cat.  Author 
catalogue,  till  1890  in  volumes,  after  1800  on  cards.  Type- 
written subject  index  to  current  books,  and  to  important 
articles  in  serial  publications  commenced  in  1904.  Printed 
class  lists  for  most  subjects ;  Works  are  lent  to  Schools  of 
Art  throughout  the  country  working  in  connexion  with  the 
Board  of  Education.  The  Dyce  and  Forster  Libraries, 
administered  by  the  same  staff,  contain  about  33,000  volumes, 
and  collections  of  prints  and  drawings.

COPENHAGEN— Royal  Library

FRANCE 

BORDEAUX— University  Library  (Bibliotheque  Universitaire) 

Founded  in   144 1.     Income    (ca.),    40,000    fr.     Contains   about 
100,000  volumes. 

BORDEAUX— Public  Library  (Bibliotheque  Pnbliqne) 

Founded  in  1738.    Contains  about  200,000  volumes. 

LYONS— University  Library  (Bibliotheque  Universltaire) 

Founded    in   1808.     Income   (ca.),   60,000  fr.     Contains  about 
200,000  volumes. 

MARSEILLES— Public  Library  (Bibliotheque  Municipal*) 

Income  (ca.),  30,000  fr.     Contains  about  120,000  volumes. 


PARIS— Blbllotheque  Nationale  (58  Rue  Richelieu) 

This  Library,  founded  in  1367,  contains  nearly  3,000,000  volumes, 
250,000  maps,  and  102,000  manuscripts. 

Admission  is  sought  in  a  letter  to  the  Administrate™  General, 
setting  forth  the  nature  of  the  applicant's  work,  his  name,  pro- 
fession, and  abode.  Applications  from  foreigners  should  be 
accompanied  by  a  recommendation  from  their  Embassy,  or  from 
some  responsible  person  known  to  the  Administrator.  Such 
ticket  of  admission,  when  granted,  is  signed  by  the  recipient,  and 
is  strictly  non-transferable. 

Application  for  Books. — On  entering  the  Salle  de  Travail,  each 
reader  is  handed  a  bulletin  personnel,  which,  having  filled  in 
accurately  his  name  and  address,  and  the  number  of  ms  seat,  he 
hands  to  the  Superintendent.  On  this  one  of  the  officials  enters 
the  names  of  all  books  taken  out  by  him,  and  he  is  not  allowed  to 
leave  the  room  until  he  has  handed  the  doorkeeper  this  bulletin 
with  the  official  rendu  stamped  against  every  book. 

Books  Kept. — Works  are  kept  by  request  for  twenty-four  hours, 
after  which  they  are  returned  to  the  shelves.  Should  any  other 
reader,  however,  apply  for  any  of  these  "  kept  "  books,  they  are  at 
his  disposal. 

There  is  a  Lending-out  branch  of  the  Bibliotheque,  applying  only 
to  books  of  which  there  are  more  than  one  copy. 

All  the  rooms  are  closed  January  1  and  2,  Shrove  Tuesday,  Palm 
Sunday  to  Easter  Monday  (inclusive),  Ascension  Day,  Whit- 
Sunday  and  Monday,  July  14,  August  15,  November  1,  and  Christ- 
mas Day. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


frAfct  it.]  LIBRARIES  6^5

to  the  various  faculties  :

Faculty  de  M6decine,  1 50,000  volumes. 
Ecole  sup.  de  Pharmacie,  40,000  volumes. 
Faculty  de  Theologie,  18,000  volumes.

GERMANY 

BERLIN— KSnigliehe  Bibiiothek  (Platz  ah  Opernhaus) 

Founded  in  1661,  this  Library  has  about  1,228,000  volumes  and 
33,600  manuscripts.     Maps  not  counted. 

Admission. — This  is  freely  granted  on  application,  though  the 
Director  has  to  be  satisfied  as  to  the  applicant's  credentials.  Cards 
for  the  Reading-Room  only  do  not  include  the  use  of  the  Lending 
Library  ;  but  a  card  for  the  latter  gives  access  to  every  department. 

Application  for  Books. — Order-forms  are  sold  at  the  Library 
at  the  rate  of  twenty-five  for  10  pfennig.  They  are  in  the  nature  of 
a  receipt  for  the  book  required,  of  which  the  applicant  has  to  fill  in 
all  the  usual  particulars  as  to  title,  date,  size,  &&,  which  are  to  be 
got  from  the  Alphabetic  Catalogue. 

A  book  ( Vonmerkbuch)  is  kept  in  the  Lending  library  for  the  use 
of  those  desiring  to  secure  any  book  as  soon  as  returned  by  another 
borrower. 

The  Reading-Room  (Lesesaal). — The  reader  can  take  down  all 

22 — 2 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


676  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK        [part  II. 

books  available  on  the  shelves,  but  must  return  them,  when  done 
with,  to  the  shelves.  The  same  application-forms  (see  above)  are 
available,  but  are  marked  "  Lesesaal." 

Books  Kept. — Books  can  be  kept  from  day  to  day  for  not  longer 
than  three  weeks.  Should  the  reader  stay  away  for  three  consecutive 
days,  the  book  is  returned  to  the  shell.  Those  desiring  to  keep 
books  in  this  way  place  in  them  a  slip  on  which  is  written  their 
name  and  the  date. 

The  Library  is  open  on  week-days  (with  the  exception  of  the 
customary  public  nolidays,  the  Saturday  before  Easter,  the 
Saturday  before  Whitsun,  and  December  24)  from  (1)  Lending 
Branch,  9  a.m.  to  6  p.m. ;  (2)  Reading-Room,  9  a.m.  to  9  p.m. 

Staff. — 71  officials,  42  assistants  and  39  subordinate  officials. 

Expenditure. — 649,474  marks  per  annum,  of  which  409,700 
marks  are  for  establishment  and  146,400  marks  for  increasing  and 
maintaining  the  collection  of  books.

volumes.

HOLLAND. 

AMSTERDAM— Library  ol  tbe  Municipal  University 

Founded  1578.  Number  of  volumes,  500,000,  including  pamphlets. 
An  increase  of  12,000  volumes  is  made  annually.  Study  and 
Reading-Rooms  open  every  week-day  from  9.30  a.m.  to  5  p.m.,  and 
from  7  p.m.  to  10  p.m.  Among  the  special  collections  in  this 
library  may  be  mentioned  :  (1)  Bibliotheca  Rosenthaliana  (Hebraica 
and  Judaica) ;  (2)  the  Vondel  Museum,  consisting  of  manuscripts, 
editions  and  translations  of  VondeTs  works,  paintings,  engravings, 
&c,  relating  to  or  illustrating  VondeFs  works,  his  history,  relk- 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  n.]  LIBRARIES  677 

tions,  friends,  or  to  feasts  celebrated  in  his  honour ;  (3)  the  Hilman 
Library,  a  rare  collection  of  old  and  modern  Dutch  plays,  drawings, 
plates  and  designs  of  costumes,  theatrical  performances  and  the 
like,  and  of  manuscripts  relative  to  the  history  of  the  Dutch  drama 
and  dramatic  art. 
Librarian,  Dr.  C.  P.  Burger. 

GRONINGEN— Library  of  the  Royal  University 

This  Library,  founded  in  1614,  has  141,600  volumes  ;  its  visitors 
number  15,774  per  annum,  and  the  number  of  volumes  lent  out  are 
about  13,865.  The  catalogue,  Diet.  MS.  on  cards,  is  brought  up  to 
date.  The  library  has  six  male  assistants  and  one  caretaker.  The 
annual  income  is  £12,000,  apportioned  as  follows  :  Books  115,500, 
Bind.  5700,  Period.  £13,650,  Salaries,  etc.,  fl6,50O.  The  Study  and 
Reading-Rooms  are  open  every  weekday  from  10  to  4,  and  in  the 
evening  from  7  to  10.  Books  are  lent  on  personal  application,  or 
sent  by  book-post  to  persons  introduced  or  well  known. 

Librarian,  Dr.  A.  G.  Roos.  Official  title  :  Director  of  the  Library 
of  the  Groningen  University. 

LETDEN— Library  ol  the  Royal  University 

Founded  in  1575.  Number  of  volumes,  210,000.  The  manu- 
scripts number  6,400,  of  which  3,400  are  Oriental. 

Librarian,  Dr.  S.  G.  de  Veres  (Director  of  the  Library  of  the 
Leyden  University). 

Conservator  of  the  Manuscripts  :  Louis  D.  Petit. 

To  this  Library  is  annexed  the  famous  Library  of  the  Maat- 
schappij  roor  Nederlandsche  Letter kunde  (founded  in  1757),  con- 
taining an  immense  number  of  German  and  Dutch  books  and 
manuscripts  dating  from  the  remotest  times,  together  with  manu- 
scripts of  some  of  the  famous  authors  of  the  nineteenth  century. 
Study  and  Reading-Rooms  open  from  10  to  4.  Books  are  lent  or 
forwarded  by  book-post  to  persons  introduced  or  well  known. 

Librarian,  Louis  D.  Petit. 

THE  HAGUE— Royal  Library  (KonlnkUjke  Bibliotheek) 

This  Library,  founded  in  1798,  has  a  large  number  of  incunabula, 
codices  and  illuminated  medieval  manuscripts.  Religion,  History, 
Literature,  Art,  Law,  and  Politics  are  well  represented.  The  Library 
also  contains  30,000  pamphlets  illustrating  the  history  of  the 
Netherlands,  700  volumes  of  publications  by  Elsevier  and  works 
by  Aldine,  Estienne,  and  Plantyn  in  great  number.  Of  the  6,000 
manuscripts,  600  relate  to  theology  and  the  history  of  the  Church. 
There  is  a  "  Book  of  Hours  "  which  belonged  to  Philip  the  Good 
(fifteenth  century),  containing  178  miniatures.  The  oldest  illumi- 
nated manuscript,  a  copy  of  the  Gospels,  dates  from  the  ninth 
century.  The  Library  also  possesses  a  copy  of  the  third  Shake- 
speare folio.    Nearly  all  recent  works   published  in   Holland  are 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


6^8  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK        [part  n. 

received  by  the  Library,  as  well  as  important  foreign  books.  The 
annual  budget  for  the  acquisition  of  books,  etc.,  is  nearly  ,£3,000. 
The  Reading-Room  is  open  from  10  to  4.  Books  are  lent  on  per- 
sonal application,  or  sent  by  post  to  applicants  who  are  well  known 
or  recommended. 

Librarian,  Dr.  W.  G.  C.  Byvanck.

Founded  in  1800.  Income,  $800,000.  The  method  of  calculating

that  adopted  at  the  British  Museum.  The  number  is  given  at  about 
two  millions.  The  Library  of  Congress  issues  a  large  number  of 
publications,  the  best  known  of  which  are  the  A.  L.  A.  Catalog  and  • 
the  A .  L.  A .  Catalog  Rules.

THE- NOBEL  FOUNDATION   AND 
THE   NOBEL   PRIZES 

(Reprinted  from  The  Author  with  the  kind  permission 
of  the  Incorporated  Society  of  Authors.) 

Alfred  Bernhard  Nobel,  the  inventor  of  dynamite,  died  in  1896, 
leaving  the  residue  of  his  great  fortune,  estimated  at  about 
£2,000,000  sterling,  for  the  foundation  of  five  annual  prizes,  to  be 
awarded  for  the  most  important  discoveries  in  physics,  chemistry, 
and  physiology  or  medicine,  for  the  most  remarkable  work  of  an 
idealist  tendency,  and  for  the  greatest  service  rendered  to  the  cause 
of  peace  during  the  year.  The  value  of  each  prize  is  about  £8,000. 
An  official  French  translation  of  the  statutes  and  regulations 
relating  to  the  Nobel  Foundation  has  been  issued.  The  original 
documents  received  the  sanction  of  the  King  of  Sweden  on  June  29, 
1900. 

It  will  be  seen  that  the  text  of  the  will  has  received  a  liberal 
interpretation.  In  addition  to  the  prizes,  provision  is  made  for  the 
establishment  of  Nobel  institutes  where  researches  may  be  carried 
on,  and  of  special  funds  from  which  grants  may  be  given  to  promote 
the  objects  which  the  founder  had  at  heart ;  and  both  institutes 
and  special  funds  are  likely,  to  say  the  least,  to  be  quite  as  productive 
of  good  as  the  great  prizes. 

It  will  be  noticed  that  the  benefits  of  the  foundation  are  to  be 
open  to  all  nationalities  without  distinction.  No  restriction  of  sex 
is  mentioned  in  the  statutes. 

The  Nobel  Foundation  is  based  on  the  will  of  Dr.  Alfred  Bernhard 
Nobel,  dated  November  27,  1895,  °*  which  the  following  passage 
contains  the  stipulations  with  regard  to  the  foundation  :  "  The 
residue  of  my  realizable  property  left  after  my  death  shall  be  disposed 
of  as  follows:  The  capital,  invested  in  safe  investments  by  the 
executors  of  my  will,  shall  constitute  a  fund  of  which  the  interest 
shall  be  distributed  annually  as  a  reward  to  such  persons  as  have 
rendered  to  humanity  the  greatest  services  during  the  preceding 
year.  The  total  amount  shall  be  divided  into  five  equal  parts, 
and  allotted,  one  [part]  to  the  person  who  has  made  the  most 
important  discovery  or  invention  in  the  physical  sciences ;  another 
[part]  to  the  person  who  has  made  the  most  important  discovery 
or  the  greatest  improvement  in  chemistry  ;  the  third  to  the  author 
of  the  most  important  discovery  in  the  domain  of  physiology  or 
medicine  ;  the  fourth  to  the  person  who  has  produced  the  most 
remarkable  literary  work  of  an  idealist  tendency  ;  and,  finally,  the 
fifth  to  the  person  who  has  done  most,  or  laboured  best,  for  the 
cause  of  fraternity  among  different  peoples,  for  the  suppression  or 
reduction  of  standing  armies,  or  for  the  formation  and  promotion  of 
peace  congresses. 

"  The  prizes  shall  be  awarded  as  follows :  For  physics  and 
chemistry  by  the  Swedish  Academy  of  Sciences  ;  for  work  in  physi- 

680 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  THE  NOBEL  PRIZES  681 

ology  and  medicine  by  the  Caroline  Institute  of  Stockholm ;  for 
literature  by  the  Stockholm  Academy  ;  and,  finally,  for  the  cause  of 
peace,  by  a  commission  of  five  members  elected  by  the  Norwegian 
Storthing.  It  is  my  express  desire  that  in  the  award  of  the  prizes 
no  account  shall  be  taken  of  nationality,  so  that  each  prize  may  go 
to  the  most  worthy,  whether  he  be  Scandinavian  or  not." 

An  agreement  was  made  with  certain  heirs  to  a  smaller  portion 
of  Dr.  Nobel's  property,  by  which  they  renounced  the  inheritance 
for  themselves  and  their  heirs,  on  the  express  condition  that — 

(a)  The  general  statutes  regulating  the  award  of  the  prizes  by  the 
competent  authorities  should  be  drawn  up  in  concert  with  a 
delegate  representing  the  Robert  Nobel  family,  and  sub- 
mitted for  approval  to  the  King  of  Sweden. 

(6)  That  certain  permanent  regulations,  stated  below,  should  be 
passed  with  regard  to  the  award  of  the  prizes. 

I. — The  Statutes. 
(i)  The  General  Fund. 

1.  The  General  Fund  is  to  be  controlled  by  a  council  of  administra- 
tion, composed  of  five  members,  of  Swedish  nationality,  sitting  at 
Stockholm.  The  president  is  to  be  nominated  by  the  King  of 
Sweden.     The  four  other  members  are  to  be  elected  by  a  body  of 

.  fifteen  representatives,  chosen  by  the  corporate  bodies  named  in  the 
will.     (See  2,  below.) 

A  deputy  is  also  to  be  elected  to  act  in  the  absence  of  the  president, 
and  two  deputies  are  to  be  elected  for  each  of  the  other  members  of 
the  council. 

The  members  and  deputies  will  be  elected  in  future  for  a  period  of 
two  years,  the  term  of  office  beginning  on  May  1.  But  two  of  the 
members  of  the  first  council,  selected  by  lot,  will  retire,  after  a  single 
year  of  office,  so  that  in  future  two  may  retire  annually. 

The  council  will  elect  one  of  its  members  as  "  director  and  adminis- 
trator "  (directeur-gtrant). 

2.  Of  the  fifteen  representatives,  six  are  to  be  chosen  by  the 
Academy  of  Sciences  (for  the  two  sections  of  physics  and  chemistry), 
and  three  are  to  be  chosen  by  each  of  the  other  corporate  bodies. 
In  addition,  the  Academy  of  Sciences  will  nominate  four  deputy- 
representatives,  and  each  of  the  other  corporate  bodies  two,  to  act 
for  representatives  prevented  from  so  doing. 

The  representatives  will  hold  office  for  two  years.  They  will  elect 
a  president  from  among  their  number.  Nine  representatives  will 
form  a  quorum. 

In  certain  cases  the  representatives  may  be  called  on  to  control 
the  management  of  the  foundation  by  the  council. 

3.  The  functions  of  the  council  include  the  payment  of  the  money 
value  of  prizes  to  the  prize-winners  and  the  payment  on  requisition 
of  all  expenses  incurred  in  the  award  of  prizes,  the  payment  of  sums 
due  to  the  Nobel  Institutes,  and  of  sums  required  for  all  other 
purposes. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


682  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  H. 

4.  One-tenth  of  the  net  annual  revenue  from  the  General  Fund 
is  to  be  added  to  the  capital ;  and  the  interest  on  sums  fox  prizes 
not  awarded  is  also  to  be  added  to  the  capital  till  such  sums  have 
been  returned  to  the  General  Fund  or  transferred  to  a  special  fund. 

(2)  The  Nobel  Prizes. 

1 .  The  prizes  are  to  be  awarded  as  a  rule  annually. 

2.  For  a  work  to  be  admitted  to  compete  for  a  prize  it  must  have 
been  printed  and  published. 

3.  In  order  to  be  admitted  to  the  competition,  each  work  must  be 
proposed  in  writing  by  some  person  or  body  of  persons  qualified  to 
do  so.  Regulations  defining  the  categories  of  persons  qualified  to 
propose  works  for  the  competitions  are  given  below  under  the 
special  regulations  for  the  various  sections.  No  person  may  propose 
his  own  work. 

4.  Each  proposal  must  be  accompanied  by  the  written  works  and 
documents  on  which  the  proposal  is  based,  and  by  a  written  state- 
ment in  support  of  the  proposal.  The  corporate  body  with  whom 
the  award  of  a  prize  lies  shall  be  dispensed  from  proceeding  to  a 
detailed  examination  of  any  proposal  in  the  following  cases  :  (1)  If 
the  proposal  or  works  presented  are  not  written  either  in  one  of  the 
Scandinavian  languages,  or  in  English,  French,  German,  or  Latin  ; 
(2)  if  the  majority  of  the  corporate  body  in  question  would  be 
required,  in  order  to  appreciate  the  proposed  work,  to  acquaint 
themselves  with  a  memoir  written  in  a  language  of  which  the 
interpretation  would  entail  special  difficulties  or  considerable 
expense. 

5.  The  proposals  received  from  the  previous  1st  of  February  to 
the  1st  of  FeDruary  in  each  year  shall  be  considered  together.  The 
will  prescribes  that  prizes  shall  be  awarded  for  work  done  "  in  the 
preceding  year,"  but  this  expression  is  to  be  taken  to  mean  "  that 
the  object  of  the  awards  shall  be  [as  a  rule]  the  most  recent  fruit  of 
efforts  in  the  fields  designated  in  the  will,  but  [that  it  may  be]  older 
work  in  [exceptional]  cases  where  its  importance  has  only  recently 
been  demonstrated." 

6.  If  no  work  be  regarded  as  deserving  of  a  prize  in  any  section 
the  amount  of  the  prize  is  to  be  held  over  until  the  next  year  ;  if 
the  prize  be  not  awarded  then,  the  money  may  be  either  returned  to 
the  General  Fund  by  the  corporate  body  concerned  or  invested  to 
form  a  special  fund  for  the  section,  the  income  from  which  is  to  be 
used  to  promote  the  chief  objects  of  the  founder,  otherwise  than  by 
the  award  of  a  prize.  (See  also  under  special  funds,  below.)  In 
accordance  with  an  agreement  made  with  certain  heirs  of  Nobel, 
referred  to  above,  it  is  stipulated,  for  all  future  time,  that  in  each 
section  a  prize  shall  be  awarded  at  least  once  in  each  consecutive 

Eeriod  of  five  years  ;  and  that  the  value  of  the  prize  given  shall  not 
e  less  than  sixty  per  cent.  (60  %)  of  the  total  amount  available  of 
the  prize,  and  that  it  shall  not  be  divided  into  more  than  three 
prizes.  The  present  statutes  provide  that  the  amount  of  the  prize 
may  be  equally  divided  between  two  works,  both  being  judged 
worthy  of  the  prize. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  THE  NOBEL  PRIZES  683 

A  prize  may  be  awarded  to  the  joint  work  of  two  or  more  collabo- 
rators. 

The  corporate  bodies  are  allowed  to  decide  if  prizes  within  their 
respective  jurisdictions  may  or  may  not  be  awarded  to  an  institution 
or  a  society. 

7.  The  four  prizes  for  physics,  chemistry,  literature,  and  medicine 
will  be  awarded  by  the  corporate  bodies  named  in  the  will,  after 
receiving  a  report  in  each  case  from  a  special  committee,  to  be  called 
a  Nobel  committee,  and  consisting  of  three  or  five  members  elected 
by  the  corporate  body  concerned.  The  "  Peace "  prize  will  be 
awarded  by  a  commissoin  of  the  Norwegian  Storthing  in  accordance 
with  the  terms  of  the  will.  Members  of  the  Nobel  committees  and 
of  the  commission  of  the  Storthing  may  be  of  foreign  nationality. 
In  special  cases  the  corporate  bodies  concerned  may  add  to  the 
Nobel  committees  additional  members  possessing  special  competence. 

Members  of  the  Nobel  committees  may  receive  a  suitable  re- 
muneration for  their  work,  to  be  fixed  by  the  corporate  body 
concerned. 

8.  Against  the  decisions  with  regard  to  the  award  of  prizes  no 
appeal  can  be  made. 

If  in  the  deliberations  with  regard  to  the  award  any  difference  of 
opinion  should  arise,  it  shall  neither  be  mentioned  in  the  minutes 
of  the  proceedings  nor  otherwise  revealed  in  any  way. 

9.  A  solemn  assembly  will  be  held  on  the  10th  of  December  in  each 
year,  the  anniversary  of  the  death  of  the  founder,  at  which  the 
corporate  bodies  charged  with  the  award  of  the  prizes  will  publicly 
announce  their  decisions,  and  will  present  to  each  successful  candi- 
date an  order  for  the  amount  of  his  prize,  a  diploma,  and  a  gold 
medal  bearing  the  effigy  of  the  donor,  and  an  appropriate  inscription. 

10.  Each  prizeman  is  bound,  unless  he  is  prevented  from  doing  so 
(a  moins  d'emptchement),  to  deliver  a  public  lecture  on  the  subject  of 
the  work  for  which  the  prize  was  awarded.  The  lecture  in  the  case 
of  the  first  four  prizes  is  to  be  delivered  at  Stockholm,  in  the  case  of 
the  "  Peace  "  prize  at  Chris tiania. 

II. — Special  Regulations  for  the  Section  of  Literature. 

1.  The  corporate  body  for  the  section  is  the  Swedish  Academy. 
According  to  the  statutes,  the  term  "  literature  "  in  the  will  is 

intended  to  apply  not  only  to  purely  literary  works,  but  to  all  other 
works  possessing  from  their  form  and  style  literary  value. 

The  Nobel  Prize  in  Literature, 

2.  The  following  institutions  and  persons  are  to  be  entitled  to 
nominate  competitors  for  the  prizes,  in  accordance  with  the  statutes  : 

(i.)  Members  of  the  Swedish  Academy  (referred  to  hereafter  as 
"  the  Academy  ")  and  members  of  the  French  Academy 
and  the  Spanish  Academy,  which  resemble  this  Academy 
in  their  organization  and  aims. 

(ii.)  Members  of  the  literary  sections  of  other  Academies  and 
members  of  literary  societies  similar  to  Academies. 

(iii.)  University  professors  of  aesthetics,  literature,  and  history. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


684  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  n. 

* 

This  regulation  is  to  be  published  at  least  once  every  five  years  in 
an  ofl&cial  journal  or  in  one  of  the  newspapers  most  widely  read  in 
the  three  Scandinavian  countries  and  the  principal  countries  of  the 
civilized  world. 

The  Nobel  Literary  Institute. 

3.  The  institute  will  include  an  important  library  devoted  chiefly 
to  modern  literature.  The  Academy  will  nominate  a  librarian,  with 
one  or  more  assistant  librarians,  and  also  a  number  of  qualified 
literary  assistants,  titular  and  supernumerary,  whose  duty  it  will  be 
to  draw  up  reports  on  questions  relating  to  the  prize  and  on  recent 
foreign  literature,  and  to  execute  such  translations  of  foreign  works 
as  may  be  required. 

The  institute  will  be  placed  under  the  supervision  of  an  inspector 
nominated  by  the  King  of  Sweden,  and  under  the  immediate  direc- 
tion of  one  of  the  members  of  the  Academy  nominated  specially  by 
this  body. 

The  Special  Fund  for  Literature. 

4.  The  Academy  has  power  to  use  the  special  fund  to  encourage, 
in  accordance  with  the  chief  objects  of  the  founder,  all  literary 
activity,  in  Sweden  and  abroad,  of  such  kind  as  may  be  regarded 
as  of  importance  to  civilization,  especially  in  the  intellectual  domain 
to  which  it  is  the  duty  of  the  Academy  to  devote  its  attention  and 
care.     (See  below.) 

III. — The  Corporate  Bodies  Mentioned  in  the  Will  of  the 

Founder. 

The  Royal  Academy  of  Sciences  (Kongl.  Vetenskaps  Akademien)  of 
Stockholm  was  founded  in  1739.  Its  present  statutes  bear  the  date 
July  13,  1850.  Its  object  is  to  encourage  the  sciences,  to  aid  in  their 
development,  and  to  spread  scientific  knowledge  by  means  of 
printed  publications. 

The  King  of  Sweden  is  the  patron  of  the  Academy,  which  includes 
a  hundred  Swedish  and  Norwegian  and  seventy-five  foreign 
members.  The  national  members  are  divided  into  nine  sections,  as 
follows : 

I.  Pure  Mathematics. 
II.  Applied  Mathematics. 

III.  Applied  Mechanics. 

IV.  Physical  Sciences. 

V.  Chemistry,  Geology,  and  Mineralogy. 
VI.  Botany  and  Zoology.**" 
VII.  The  Medical  Sciences. 
VIII.  Technology,  Economics,  and  Statistics. 
IX.  The  Sciences  and  Scientific  Professions  generally. 

The  president  of  the  Academy  is  elected  annually,  and  there  are 
several  other  officers,  including  a  perpetual  secretary,  whose  especial 
business  it  is  to  manage  the  affairs  of  the  Academy. 


Digitized  by  CjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  THE  NOBEL  PRIZES  685 

The  Swedish  Academy  (Svenska  Akademien)  of  Stockholm  was 
founded  on  March  20,  1786,  by  Gustavus  III.,  and  at  the  same  time 
received  its  statutes,  which  are  still  in  force.  It  is  devoted  to  litera- 
ture, including  both  prose  and  poetry ;  and  the  chief  object  of  the 
Academy  is  to  labour  for  the  purity,  the  vigour,  and  the  elevation 
of  the  Swedish  language,  in  scientific  works,  and  especially  in  the 
various  branches  of  poetry  and  prose,  including  those  which  serve 
for  the  interpretation  of  religious  truths.  It  is  the  duty  of  the 
Academy  to  compile  a  dictionary  and  a  grammar  of  the  Swedish 
language,  and  to  publish  treatises  tending  to  strengthen  and  develop 
literary  taste  (le  oon  gout).  The  Academy  awards  annual  prizes  for 
competitions  in  prose  and  poetry.  The  King  is  the  patron  of  the 
Academy.  The  Academy  includes  eighteen  members,  who  must  be 
of  Swedish  nationality.  The  officers  include  a  director,  a  chancellor, 
and  a  perpetual  secretary. 

The  Royal  Caroline  Institute  of  Medicine  and  Surgery  {Kongl. 
Karolinska  Medico-Kirurgiska  Instituet)  of  Stockholm  dates  from 
the  year  1 8 1 5 .  The  statutes  at  present  in  force  received  the  sanction 
of  the  King  of  Sweden  on  April  29, 1886.  The  Academy  corresponds 
to  a  Faculty  of  Medicine,  and  has  the  same  powers  as  the  Faculties 
of  Medicine  in  the  Universities  of  Upsala  and  Lund.  The  institute 
carries  on  the  theoretical  and  practical  teaching  of  the  medical 
sciences,  and  awards  diplomas  to  medical  students  by  examination. 

The  direction  and  administration  of  the  institute  are  in  the  hands 
of  a  Rector,  elected  by  the  College  of  Professors  from  among  the 
members.    The  present  number  of  professors  is  thirty-three. 

The  Norwegian  Storthing  is  the  Legislative  Assembly  of  Norway. 
It  is  elected  indirectly  by  universal  suffrage  triennially.  It  consists 
of  114  members. 

The  first  prize  awarded  for  Literature  under  the  above  conditions 
was  given,  in  1901,  to  M.  Sully  Prudhomme.  It  was  not  until 
January,  1902,  that  steps  were  taken,  on  the  invitation  of  the  Swedish 
Academy,  for  the  due  representation  of  English  letters.  A  Com- 
mittee was  then  appointed  by  the  Incorporated  Society  of  Authors, 
and  is  composed  of  the  following  gentlemen  :  Chairman  :  The  Right 
Hon.  The  Lord  Avebury,  P.C.,  D.C.L.,  LLD.,  F.R.S.  Members  : 
Sir  William  Anson,  Bart.,  M.P.,  D.C.L. ;  A.  C.  Benson,  M.A. ; 
S.  H.  Butcher,  M.P.,  LL.D.,  D.Litt. ;  Austin  Dobson,  LL.D.  ;  Sir 
Robert  Giffen,  K.C.B.,  F.R.S.  ;  Edmund  Gosse,  M.A.,  LL.D. ; 
Mrs.  John  Richard  Green ;  The  Right  Hon.  R.  B.  Haldane,  M.P., 
LL.D.,  K.C. ;  Thomas  Hardy ;  Anthony  Hope  Hawkins ;  George 
Meredith,  O.M. ;  Count  G.  N.  Plunkett ;  and  Sir  Donald  Mackenzie 
Wallace,  K.C.I.E.,  K.C.V.O.  Secretary :  G.  Herbert  Thring.  The 
Committee,  acting  according  to  the  laws  that  govern  the  trust, 
meets  annually,  and  settles  the  circular  which  is  sent  round  to  all 
those  persons  m  Great  Britain  and  Ireland  who  are  entitled  to  vote. 
The  votes  are  duly  collected  by  the  Committee  and  sent  to  the 
proper  authorities  in  Sweden,  with  a  covering  letter  signed  by  the 
Chairman. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


686  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 

The  authorities  in  Sweden  are  not  in  any  way  bound  by  the  votes 
sent  in.    The  ultimate  decision  Hes  with  them  absolutely. 

It  should  be  added  that  the  English  Committee  and  those  to  whom 
the  circular  is  sent  do  not  vote  as  a  whole,  but  each  person  is  entitled 
to  record  a  separate  vote,  and  each  vote  so  recorded  is  forwarded  to 
Sweden. 

The  prize  in  1902  was  awarded  to  Theodor  Mommsen,  for  his 
great  work  in  connection  with  Roman  History.  The  award  in 
respect  of  the  year  1903  was  made  to  Bjornsterne  Bjornson  ;  and 
in  1904  was  divided  between  Fred6rique  Mistral,  the  Provencal 
poet,  and  Jose  Echegaray,  the  Spanish  dramatist.  The  award  for 
1905  was  made  to  Henry  Sienkiewiez.  The  award  for  1906  went  to 
Signor  Carducci,  for  1907  to  Mr.  Kipling,  and  for  1908  to  Professor 
Rudolph  Eucken.  The  award  for  1909  was  made  to  Selma  Lagerldf, 
the  Swedish  authoress;  that  for  1910  was  not  announced  at  the 
time  of  going  to  press.

OBITUARY 

MOTE.— -Motes  or  paragraphs  intended  for  this  section  should  be 
addressed— To  the  Editor,  "Literary  Tear-Book,"  e/o  George 
Routledge  and  Sons,  Ltd.,  Carter  Lane,  Ludgate  Hill,  E.O.,  and 
will  receive  earelnl  attention. 

ADAMSON,  Rev.  Dr.  William. — Biographer  of  the  late  Dr. 
Parker  ;  of  the  late  Rev.  Principal  James  Morison,  D.D. ;  and 
of  the  Rev.  Dr.  Fergus  Ferguson.  Author  of  several  books  on 
theology  and  philosophy.  At  one  time  edited  the  Evangelical 
monthly  Forward,  and  for  over  thirty  years  was  editor  of  the 
Christian  News,  a  weekly  journal  founded  in  1845.  (Born, 
August  29,  1830 ;  died,  May  7,  1910.) 

ARCHER-HIND,  Richard  Dacrb,  M.A. — A  classical  scholar  of 
considerable  repute.  His  publications  include  Plato's  Phado 
(1883);  Timaus  (1888);  Translations  into  Greek  (1905);  and 
various  classical  papers.     (Born,  1849  ;  died,  April  6,  19 10.) 

BENHAM,  Rev.  Canon  William,  D.D.— Rector  of  St.  Edmund, 
Lombard  Street,  and  Honorary  Canon  of  Canterbury.  Best 
known  as  an  author  for  his  weekly  contributions  to  the  Church 
Times,  extending  over  a  period  of  twenty  years,  over  the 
pseudonym  of  "  Peter  Lombard."  He  also  wrote  numerous 
commentaries  and  miscellaneous  volumes,  including  The  Dic- 
tionary of  Religion  (1887),  and  the  Episcopal  Church  in  America 
(1884).     (Born,  1831  ;  died,  July  30,  1910.) 

BJORNSON,  Bjornstjerne. — The  famous  Norwegian  poet, 
novelist,  and  dramatist,  born  ia  1832.  Started  life  as  a  journa- 
list and  editor  in  Christiania.  First  novel,  Synnbve  Solbakken, 
was  written  in  1857.  Arne,  his  best-known  work  (which  has 
been  published  in  Sweden,  Germany,  and  England,  apart  from 
the  Norwegian  edition),  was  produced  early  in  1859.  Received 
the  Nobel  Prize  for  Literature  in  1903.     (Died,  April  26,  1910.) 

BOWRING,  Lewin  Bentham,  C.S.I. — Late  Bengal  Civil  Service, 
and  author  of  Eastern  Experiences  (1871) ;  Autobiographical 
Recollections  of  Sir  John  Bowring  (1877) ;  Haidar  AH  and  Tipu 
Sultan  in  the  Rulers  of  India  Series  (O.U.P.)  (1893).  (Born, 
July  15,  1824;  died,  January  14,  1910.) 

BUTLER,  Arthur  John,  M.A. — The  well-known  Dante  scholar, 
and  Professor  of  the  Italian  Language  and  Literature  at 
University  College,  London.  Educated  at  Bradfield,  Eton, 
and  Trinity  College,  Cambridge.  (Born,  June  21,  1844  ;  died, 
February  26,  1910.) 

687 


Digitized  by  CjOOQlC 


688  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  ii. 

BUTLER,  Sir  William  Francis,  G.C.B. — A  distinguished  soldier, 
and  an  author  of  some  repute,  his  best-known  work  being  The 
Great  Lone  Land  (1872).  Other  publications  of  his  include 
Charles  George  Gordon  (1889) ;  Sir  Charles  Napier  (1890) ;  and 
Life  of  Sir  George  Colley  (1899).  (Born,  October  31,  1838; 
died,  June  7,  1 910.) 
CLEMENS,  Samuel  L. — In  "  Mark  Twain,"  there  passed  away  one 
of  the  earliest  and  beyond  question  one  of  the  most  amusing  of 
American  humorists.  The  works  of  Mark  Twain  are  so  well 
known  that  it  is  hardly  necessary  to  here  enumerate  them. 
His  first  book,  The  Innocents  Abroad,  was  the  outcome  of  a 
trip  to  the  Holy  Land  in  1857,  and,  it  is  interesting  to  note, 
was  at  first  rejected  by  all  the  chief  publishers  in  America  ;  but 
at  length  it  was  printed  by  the  American  Publishing  Company, 
of  Hartford,  Conn.,  and  Mark  Twain  became  famous.  (Born, 
November  30,  1835  ;  died,  April  21,  1 910.) 

CONDER,  Colonel  Claude  Reignier. — The  well-known  explorer 
of,  and  writer  on,  Palestine,  and  for  many  years  connected  with 
the  Palestine  Exploration  Fund.  His  best-known  work,  which 
may  be  said  to  have  become  a  classic,  is  Tent  Work  in  Palestine, 
written  in  1878  from  materials  collected  during  his  survey  of 
Palestine  in  1 872-1 875.  Other  works  of  his  are  Heth  and  Moab 
(1883  ;  The  Bible  and  the  East  (1896) ;  Latin  Kingdom  of  Jeru- 
salem (1897)  '*  Hittites  and  their  Language  (1898)  ;  Rise  of  Man 
(1908),  etc.  Colonel  Conder  was  an  Hon.  LL.D.  (Edinb.). 
(Born  December  29,  1848  ;  died,  February  16,  1910J 

COPINGER,  Walter  Arthur,  LL.D.— Professor  and  Dean  of  the 
Faculty  of  Law  in  the  University  of  Manchester.  Had  a  wide 
knowledge  of  property  law  and  manorial  history,  as  was  evinced 
by  the  numerous  books  he  had  written  on  these  and  kindred 
subjects.  Mr.  Copinger  was  founder  and  first  President  of  the 
Bibliographical  Society  in  1892,  was  President  of  the  Man- 
chester Law  Library  Society,  and  an  F.S.A.  (Born,  1847  I 
died,  March  13,  1910.) 

DOUTHWAITE,  William  Ralph. — Librarian  of  Gray's  Inn  from 
1867  to  1905.  One  of  the  founders  of  the  Library  Association. 
Author  of  Gray's  Inn,  its  History  and  Associations.  (Died, 
April  12,  1 9 10.) 

DOWDEN,  Dr.  John. — Bishop  of  Edinburgh,  a  theological  scholar, 
and  writer  on  the  Prayer-Book  and  on  ecclesiastical  archaeology. 
Hon.  LL.D.  (Edinb.}.  Author  of  Workmanship  of  the  Prayer- 
Booh  (1899J  ;  second  ed.,  1902,  Further  Studies  in  the  Prayer- 
Booh  (1908).  Educated  at  Queen's  College,  Cork,  and  Trinity 
College,  Dublin.  (Born,  June  29,  1840 ;  died,  January  30, 
1910.) 

FURNIVALL,  Dr.  Frederick  James. — A  famous  scholar  and 
philologist.  Founded  the  following  societies  :  Early  English 
Text  (1864),  Chaucer  (1868),  Ballad  (1868),  New  Shakespeare 
(1873),  Wyclif  (1882),  and  Shelley  (1885).  Joint  founder  (with 
Miss  Hickey)  of  the  Browning  Society  (1881).  He  was  Hon. 
Sec.  of  the  Philological  Society,  and  for  many  years  joint  and

part  ii.]  OBITUARY  689 

afterwards  sole  editor  of  its  English  (now  the  Oxford)  Dic- 
tionary, while  material  for  it  was  being  collected.  (Born, 
February  4,  1825  ;  died,  July  2,  1910.) 

GUINNESS,  Rev.  H.  Grattan,  D.D.,  F.R.A.S.— Author  of  several 
works  on  prophecy,  the  chief  one  being  The  ApproachingEnd 
of  the  A ge  f  1 878) ,  which  has  gone  into  fourteen  editions.  (Born, 
1835  ;  died,  June  22,  1910.) 

HILL,  Frank  Harrison. — A  journalist  of  much  distinction. 
Editor  of  the  Daily  News  from  1869  to  1886.  (Died,  30  June. 
1910.) 

HINGESTON-RANDOLPH,  Rev.  Prebendary  Francis  C— 
Rector  of  Ringmore,  Devon.  Chief  work :  The  Episcopal 
Registers  of  the  Diocese  of  Exeter ',  nine  volumes  (1886- 1909),  and 
other  historical  studies.     (Born,  1833  ;  died,  August,  1910.) 

HUGGINS,  Sir  William,  K.C.B.,  O.M.,  D.C.L.— Distinguished 
astronomer.  Publications  :  Atlas  of  Stellar  Spectra  (jointly  with 
his  wife)  (1000) ;  The  Royal  Society,  or  Science  in  the  State  (1906) ; 
Scientific  Papers  (collected)  (1909).  President  of  the  Royal 
Astronomical  Society,  1 876-1 878  ;  President  of  the  British 
Association,  1891  ;  and  President  of  the  Royal  Society,  1900- 
1905.     (Born,  1824  ;  died,  May  12,  1910.) 

HUGHENET,  Professor  A.  P.  —  Founder  and  editor  of  La 
Chronique,  the  organ  of  the  French  colony  in  England.  (Died, 
August  12,  1 910,  aged  seventy-five.) 

HUME,  Major  Martin. — The  well-known  Spanish  historian. 
Editor  of  the  Spanish  State  papers  at  Public  Record  Office. 
His  studies  in  Spanish  history  and  literature  won  for  him  many 
academic  distinctions  in  Spain.  He  had  been  Lecturer  in 
Spanish  History  at  Pembroke  College,  Cambridge  ;  examiner 
and  lecturer  in  University  of  London  ;  and  examiner  in  Univer- 
sity of  Birmingham.  For  d etailed  list  of  Major  Hume's  writings, 
see -Literary  Year-Book  for  1910.  (Born,  December  8,  1847; 
died,  July  1,  19 10.) 

IWAN-MULLER,  E.  B.— Journalist.  Formerly  editor  of  the 
Manchester  Courier,  assistant  editor  of  the  Pall  Mall  Gazette 
On  staff  of  the  Daily  Telegraph  since  1896.  Author  of  Lord 
Milner  and  South  Africa  (1902) ;  Ireland  To-day  and  To-morrow 
(1907).     (Born,  1853  ;  died,  May  7,  1910.) 

JAMES,  Professor  William. — The  philosopher  and  psychologist 
of  Harvard  University,  whose  writings  were  probably  more 
widely  read  than  those  of  any  other  contemporary  philosopher. 
(Born,  1842  ;  died,  August,  19 10.) 

JENKINS,  Edward.—- Author  of  Ginx's  Baby,  &  book  which  en- 
joyed a  wide  popularity  in  its  day,  published  in  1870.  Also 
wrote :  The  Coolie  :  his  Rights  and  wrongs  (1871) ;  Lord  Bantam 

ii872);  Little  Hodge;  and  many  other  books.  Served  on  the 
toyal  Commission  on  Copyright  in  1876-77.  Editor  of  the 
Overland  Mail  and  Homeward  Mail  from  1886  up  to  quite 
recently.     (Born,  July  28,  1838  ;  died,  June  4,  1910.)

690  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  ii. 

KEITH,  Dr.  George  Skene. — Formerly  a  prominent  Edinburgh 
physician,  now  best  remembered  by  his  Plea  for  a  Simpler  Life 
(1895).  Also  wrote  Fads  of  an  Old  Physician  (1896) ;  Plea  for  a 
Simpler  Faith  (1897) ;  On  Sanitary  and  Other  Matters  (1900). 
(Born,  March  n,  1819 ;  died,  January  12,  1910.) 

KYNASTON,  Canon  Herbert. — A  classical  scholar  and  Professor 
of  Greek  at  Durham  University.  (Born,  June  29,  1835  »  dic<*» 
August  1,  1 910.) 

McNAIR,  Major  John  F.,  C.M.G.— Late  Royal  (Madras)  Artillery. 
Author  of  Perah  and  the  Malays  (1878) ;  Prisoners  their  own 
Warders  (1899) ;  and  various  papers  on  natural  history  in 
Nature  Notes.     (Born,  1828  ;  died,  May  17,  1910.) 

MANTEGAZZA,  Professor  Paolo. — The  distinguished  Italian 
anthropologist,  a  prolific  writer,  many  of  his  works  being 
translated  into  English.     (Born,  1831  ;  died,  August  28,  1910.) 

MARSHALL,  Captain  Robert. — The  well-known  playwright. 
Plays  :  His  Excellency  the  Governor  (Court  Theatre,  1898)  ; 
A  Royal  Family  (Court,  1899) ;  Second  in  Command  (Hay- 
market,  1900);  Duke  of  Killiecranhie  (Criterion,  1904)  ;  also  a 
few  novels.     (Born,  June  21,  1863  ;  died,  July  1,  1910.) 

MASEY,  Albert. — Editor  of  The  Outlook  since  1906.  Also  assis- 
tant editor  of  the  Globe  from  1866  to  1880,  and  on  editorial  staff 
of  the  Standard  from  1881  to  1905.     (Died,  August,  1910.) 

MUNBY,  Arthur  J. — Educated  at  Trinity  College,  Cambridge, 
and  afterwards  called  to  the  Bar.  Best  known,  however,  as  a 
poet  and  litterateur.  Author  of  Benoni  (1852) ;  Verses  New  and 
Old  (1865);  Dorothy  (1880);  Vulgar  Verses  (1891);  Susan  (1893); 
Poems :  Lyric  and  Elegiac  (1901),  and  others.  (Died,  January  29, 
1910.) 

NEWNES,  Sir  George. — The  well-known  newspaper  proprietor 
and  editor.  Founder  of  George  Newnes,  Limited.  Founded 
Westminster  Gazette,  1893.  (Born,  March  13,  185 1  ;  died, 
June  9,  1910.) 

NUTT,  Alfred  TRUBNER.-The  London  publisher,  drowned  in  the 
Seine,  near  Paris,  while  endeavouring  to  save  his  son's  life.  He 
is  best  known  to  the  public  for  his  work  in  folk-lore,  and  in  the 
more  recent  Celtic  revival,  his  most  important  work  being 
Legend  of  the  Holy  Grail  (1888),  long  since  out  of  print.  He 
joined  the  Folk-Lore  Society  on  its  foundation  in  1878,  and 
was  its  President  in  1897.  Mr.  Nutt  was  also  a  joint  founder, 
as  well  as  publisher  to,  the  Irish  Texts  Society,  and  in  1868 
founded  the  English  Goethe  Society.  (Born,  November  22, 
1856  ;  died,  1910.) 

PODMORE,  Frank. — Known  by  his  writings  on  Spiritualism. 
(Died,  August,  1910.) 

ROLFE,  William  James. — A  distinguished  American  author,  who 
has  written  numerous  books  on  Shakespeare,  on  whom  he  was 
considered  an  authority.  (Born,  December  10,  1827;  died, 
July,  1 910.)

part  ii.]  OBITUARY  691 

SAMBOTJRNE,  Linley. — The  celebrated  illustrator  and  Punch 
cartoonist.  (Died,  August  3,  19 10.  He  was  born  in  London 
in  1845.) 

SCOTT,  Sir  Walter.— Founder  of  the  Walter  Scott  Publishing 
Company,  Limited.     (Died,  April  8,  1910.) 

SMITH,  Professor  Goldwin. — The  famous  historian.  Educated 
at  Eton  and  Oxford.  Called  to  the  Bar  in  1847,  but  never 
practised.  In  1868  he  migrated  to  the  United  States  to  take 
up  the  post  of  Hon.  Professor  of  English  and  Constitutional 
History  in  Cornell  University,  where  he  stayed  till  1871  ;  then 
moving  to  Canada,  where  he  lived  till  the  time  of  his  death. 
The  degree  of  D.C.L.  was  conferred  upon  him  by  the  University 
of  Oxford.  His  literary  output  was  considerable,  and  readers 
are  referred  to  the  last  issue  of  the  Literary  Year-Booh  for  a 
full  list  of  his  writings.     (Born,  1823  ;  died,  June  7,  1910.) 

STABLES,  Dr.  William  Gordon,  R.N.— The  well-known  writer 
of  boys'  books.  He  also  wrote  popular  medical  books  and 
articles  on  natural  history.  (Born,  May  21,  1840  ;  died.  May, 
1 910.) 

THOMAS,  William  Moy. — One  of  the  original  staflf  of  Household 
Words;  dramatic  critic  for  Daily  News  (1 868-1 901) ;  Academy 
(1875-1879) ;  first  editor  of  Cassell's  Magazine  ;  hon.  sec.  to  the 
Copyright  Association ;  a  member  of  the  Committee  of  the 
Society  of  Authors  ;  fellow  of  the  Institute  of  Journalists  ;  and 
author  of  some  five  books.     (Born,  1828  ;  died,  July,  19 10.) 

VANDAL,  M.  Albert. — Member  of  the  French  Academy ;  a  leading 
historian  of  diplomacy  in  France.  (Born,  1853  ;  died,  August  31 , 
1910.) 

VERNEY,  Sir  Edmund.— Educated  at  Harrow,  entered  Royal 
Navy,  185 1,  served  in  Crimea  and  Indian  Mutiny.  A  member 
of  the  Society  of  Librarians.  Author  of  The  "  Shannon's  " 
Brigade  in  India  (1862) ;  Village  Sketches  (1879) ;  Four  Years  of 
Protest  in  the  Transvaal  (1881),  and  others.  (Born,  April  6, 
1838  ;  died,  May  8,  1910.) 

VOGUE,  Vicomte  Melchior  de. — Elected  a  member  of  the  French 
Academy  in  1888.  An  authoritative  writer  on  Russian  litera- 
ture ;  also  a  novelist.  Contributed  for  many  years  to  the 
Revue  des  Deux  Mondes.  (Born,  February  24,  1848  ;  died, 
March  25,  1910.) 

WALTER,  Arthur  Fraser. — Chairman  of  The  Times  Publishing 
Company,  Limited,  and  formerly  manager  and  chief  proprietor 
of  that  journal.  The  second  and  eldest  surviving  son  of  the 
late  John  Walter,  and  is  succeeded  by  his  elder  son  John,  born 
in  1873.     (Born,  1846  ;  died,  February  22,  1910.) 

WHITMAN,  Alfred  Charles. — For  many  years  superintendent 
in  the  Students'  Room  in  the  Department  of  Prints  at  the 
British   Museum.     Author   of   several   well-known   works   of 
reference    on   engraving   and    print-collecting.     (Born,    t86o 
died,  February  4,  1910.) 


Digitized  by  CjOOQIC 


692  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK       [part  ii. 

WICKHAM,  Very  Rev.  E.  C,  D.D.— Dean  of  Lincoln.  Best 
known  fas  an  author)  for  his  very  complete  edition  of  Horace, 
followed  by  a  prose  translation  into  English.  (Born,  Decem- 
ber 7,  1834  ;  died,  August  18,  1910.) 

WICKS,  Frederick. — Inventor  of  the  Wicks  rotary  type-casting 
machine.  Began  life  as  a  journalist.  On  the  editorial  staff  of 
The  Globe  (1863),  and  later  a  member  of  the  Gallery  Staff  of 
the  Times.  Founded  Glasgow  News  (1873),  sole  proprietor 
(1878).  Author  of  several  novels,  including  The  Veiled  Hand ; 
Unfortunate  Duke  ;  Broadmoor  Patient ;  and  others.  (Born, 
February  23,  1840 ;  died,  April  1,  1910.) 

WINDUS,  William  Edward. — Of  the  publishing  firm  of  Messrs. 
Chatto  and  Windus,  from  which  he  retired  in  1909.  Author 
of  two  books  of  verse,  and  a  drama,  Illiam  Dhdne  (1886),  fol- 
lowed by  a  sequel,  Fenella  (1890).  Contributor  to  the  maga- 
zines and  daily  papers.      (Died,  June  7,  1910,  aged  eighty-two.) 


LATE    ADDITIONS. 
(To  November  8,  1910.) 

HOWE,  Mrs.  Ward. — The  well-known  American  authoress.  Her 
most  widely-known  work  is  the  Battle  Hymn  of  the  Republic, 
written  early  in  the  Civil  War.  Other  works  of  hers  are  : 
Passion  Flowers.  Sex  and  Education,  Is  Society  Polite  ?  etc. 
(Born  181 9 ;  died,  October,  19 10.) 

HUTH,  Alfred  Henry. — The  bibliophile  and  owner  of  a  very 
fine  collection  of  rare  and  early-printed  books.  Chief  works  : 
Life  of  H.  T.  Buckle  (1880)  ;  Marriage  of  Near  Kin  (1875). 
(Born,  1850  ;  died,  October  14,  19 10.) 

PEILE,  Dr.  John. — Master  of  Christ's  College,  Camb.,  a  dis- 
tinguished classical  college ;  a  fellow  of  the  British  Academy. 
(Born,  1838  ;  died,  September  9,  1910.) 

PRIOR,  Melton. — The  famous  war  artist  and  correspondent  of  the 
Illustrated  London  News.     (Died  at  Chelsea,  November  2,  1910.)

I.  BRITISH  AND  IRISH 
PERIODICAL    PUBLICATIONS 

AND    CONTRIBUTORS'   GUIDE 


NOTE .— D = Daily,  W = Weekly,  M = Monthly,  Q  =  Quarterly,  A = Annual. 
The  vast  majority  of  papers  review  books  sent  to  them.  Prices, 
Publishing  Offices,  Editors'  Names,  and  in  many  cases  the  years 
of  foundation,  are  given  in  this  list.  An  asterisk  (*)  implies 
illustration. 

NOTE.— The  particulars  added  for  the  guidance  of  would-be  contri- 
butors have  been  supplied  in  almost  all  cases  by  the  editors  of 
the  periodicals  themselves.  Periodicals  which  discourage  outside 
contributions  have  no  such  particulars  added. 

The  following  instructions  are  given  for  convenience  of  reference, 
in  the  hope  that  they  will  be  of  service  to  writers,  and  indirectly 
to  those  editors  who  are  overwhelmed  with  matter  which  is  sent 
without  reference  to  the  simple  and  necessary  rules  that  prevail  in 
all  well-conducted  offices.  A  vast  amount  of  trouble  is  caused  to 
those  who  deal  with  MSS.  by  the  want  of  consideration  that 
characterizes  the  methods  of  a  large  proportion  of  occasional 
correspondents. 

i.  All  MSS.  should  be  typewritten.  Editors,  as  a  rule,  will 
consent  to  read  written  "copy,"  but  typewritten  matter  has  a 
greater  chance  of  being  read  quickly. 

2.  MSS.  must  be  written  on  one  side  of  the  paper  only.  This 
rule  is  made  for  the  convenience  of  printers.  The  "copy"  which 
is  given  out  to  compositors  is  nearly  always  cut  into  small  sections ; 
consequently  a  MS.  which  is  written  on  both  sides  of  the  paper 
cannot  be  readily  dealt  with. 

3.  It  is  most  important  to  use  discretion  and  judgment  in  the 
matter  of  submitting  MSS.  These  should  not  be  sent  round  in- 
discriminately ;  the  character  and  tone  of  each  publication  should 
be  studied,  and  an  agent's  advice  consulted  if  necessary. 

4.  Writers  should  be  very  careful  to  ascertain  the  correct  style 
and  address  of  the  paper  to  which  they  are  submitting  their  efforts. 
Magazine  addresses  frequently  change.  It  is,  in  fact,  essential  for 
those  who  wish  to  succeed  to  be  accurate  in  every  particular,  even 
in  those  matters  which  involve  merely  technical  detail. 

5.  Letters  to  editors  are  not,  as  a  rale,  necessary.  Each  MS. 
should  have  clearly  placed  upon  the  first  page  the  author's  name 

694 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]       PERIODICAL  PUBLICATIONS  695 

and  address.    A  fully  stamped  and  addressed  envelope  for  return 
of  the  MS.  should  always  be  enclosed. 

6.  Proposals  to  submit  MSS.  to  editors  should  only  be  sent  in 
exceptional  cases.  No  interviews  must  be  expected  by  contributors, 
except  those  which  are  invited  by  the  editor.

The  principal  daily  features  are  the  full  commercial  news  of  the  cotton,

by  special  writers  every  week  on  education,  golf,  motoring,  shipping, 
the  turf,  the  army,  women's  affairs,  the  property  market ;  while  a  full

the   professors   of  Liverpool  University.      The   articles  on  Russia,

II.  AMERICAN 
PERIODICAL   PUBLICATIONS 

AND  CONTRIBUTORS'  GUIDE 

Note. — The  following  is  a  list  of  American  publications,  many 
of  which  are  open  to  receive  contributions  from  British  writers. 

Publications  issued  simultaneously  in  New  York  and  London, 
or  which  have  a  London  address  as  well  as  abroad,  will  be 
found  in  both  lists,  with  cross  reference  where  necessary. 

Many  of  the  remarks  given  at  the  head  of  the  "  British 
Periodicals'  List "  are  applicable  here,  with  the  addition  of 
the  following : 

i.  Some  American  magazines  are  apt  to  make  changes  in 
editorial  policy — *'.*.,  their  requirements  may  change  with 
change  of  editor,  but,  usually,  known  contributors  are  notified 
of  this. 

2.  A  better  idea  of  their  requirements  may  often  be  obtained 
by  perusal  of  the  current  numbers  of  the  magazines  themselves, 
rather  than  by  what  can  be  got  from  a  necessarily  short  de- 
scription of  contents. 

3.  When  selling  a  MS.  in  the  United  States  or  Canada,  care 
should  be  exercised  concerning  copyrights,  the  majority  of 
U.S.A.  periodicals  buying  outright.  Many  of  the  better  class 
of  U.S.A.  monthlies  also  obtain,  and  rigorously  adhere  to,  their 
rights  of  copyright  in  the  British  Dominions,  while  the  Canadian 
copyright  is  usually  included  in  the  sale  either  to  the  average 
British  or  American  periodical,  unless  otherwise  stipulated. 

4.  MSS.  and  informative  letter  should  not  be  sent  separately, 
a  course  against  which  the  usual  U.S.A.  editorial  department 
vigorously  protests,  as  it  entails  separate  entries  in  the  re- 
ceiving book  and  disarrangement  in  departmental  routine. 
An  envelope,  large  enough  to  take  the  MS.  folded  twice, 
should  be  enclosed  for  return  if  not  accepted.  Preferably  it 
should  be  stamped,  though  some  editorial  departments  do 
not  object  to  the  inclusion  of  a  postal  coupon  instead.  This 
coupon,  to  the  value  of  5  cents,  can  be  obtained  at  any  post- 
office  within  the  British  Isles,  Canada,  British  Columbia, 
New  Zealand,  and  the  majority  of  British  Possessions,  except- 
ing the  Commonwealth  of  Australia,  where  such  coupons  are 
neither  valid  nor  accepted. 

754 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  II.]        PERIODICAL  PUBLICATIONS  755 

5.  MSS.  can  be  sent  by  "  Commercial  Papers' "  Rate 
(2|d.  the  first  10  ounces  and  Jd.  per  2  ounces  thereafter), 
which  applies  both  to  and  from  lie  U.S.A.  and  all  other 
countries  in  the  Postal  Union,  but  such  packets  must  be  left 
with  the  ends  open  for  inspection  if  necessary.  Now  that  the 
letter  post  to  lie  U.S.A.  is  reduced  to  id.  the  ounce,  con- 
tributors may  find  it  more  convenient  to  send  their  MSS.  thus 
to  avoid  being  tampered  with  by  Customs  officials  en  route. 

An  asterisk  (*)  before  a  title  implies  illustration. 

Ainslie's  Magazine,  Af.,  15  cents*  (7|d.).   Seventh  Avenue  and  Fifteenth 
Street,  New  York.     Editor,  Mr.  A.  L.  Sessions. 
A  popular  high-class  monthly  that  accepts  stories  of  all  lengths,  based 
on  society  themes  with  a  strong  leaning  towards  American  topics ; 
also  dealing  with  love  and  adventure  involving  dramatic  situations, 
treated  with  literary  skill.     Fantastic  fiction  is  occasionally  used 
if  of  high  literary  merits,  but  tragedy  not  desired.    The  predomi- 
nant note  in  all  work,  whether  fiction  or  articles,  which  should  be 
concerned  about  prominent  people,  must  be  the  feminine  interest. 
Remuneration  is  high,  at  the  editor's  discretion,  and  usually  paid 
on  acceptance.     One  complete  novel  of  from  65,000  to  70,000 
words  is  used  in  each  issue.

setting.    No  dialect  tales  need  be  sent.     Payment  is  varied  and 
made  with  acceptance,  which  takes  from  three  to  four  weeks.

25,000  words,  not  tragical  nor  narrated  in  dialect.     Payment, 
which  varies,  is  for  sale  outright,  and  is  sent  with  acceptance. 
♦Sports  Afield,   Af.,    15   cents   (7*d.).     358   Dearborn  Street,   Chicago, 
Illinois.    Editor,  Mr.  Claude  King. 

A  sportsman's  monthly,  dealing  with  fishing,  hunting,  shooting,  and 
nature  subjects.  Desirous  of  good  matter  anent  the  home-life  of 
remote  peoples,  mining  and  farm  life  in  outlandish  places,  together 
with  good  glimpses  into  the  animal  and  natural  history  of  such 
countries.  Pays  with  prompt  acceptance  from  12s.  to  15s.  and 
upwards  per  1,000  words.  Authentic  adventure  stories,  2,500  to 
3,800  words,  are  acceptable,  and  serials,  25,000  to  35,000  words, 
dealing  with  frontier  or  farm  life,  or  adventures  and  living  in  the 
pioneer  days.  The  setting  may  be  anywhere,  so  long  as  tne  local 
colour  is  authentic — i.e.,  indisputable.  Illustrated  matter  paid 
for  at  higher  prices. 
Success  Monthly,  10  cents  (5d.).  Washington  Square,  New  York  City. 
Well-written  articles,  short  stories,  and  serial  (50,000  to  70,000 
words)  desired,  and  must  reflect  American  life  in  its  progress,  its 
problems,  home  and  national,  and  their  development.  Articles 
should  be  treated  in  an  informative  manner.  Payment  varies,  and 
is  made  after  publication. 
Sunset  Monthly,  15  cents  (7*d.).     313  Battery  Street,  San  Francisco.  | 

The  leading  magazine  of  the  United  States  Pacific  slope.     Fiction, 
long  and  short,  together  with  general  matter ;  must  be  of  a  very        I 
high  standard,  and  dealing  with  life  and  matters  west  of  the 
Mississippi.     Payment  as  arranged. 
♦System,  M.,  20  cents  (iod.).     151  Wabash  Avenue,  Chicago,  Illinois. 
Illustrated  and  non-illustrated  articles  concerning  business  and  men 
prominent  in  the  business  world,  methods  of  business,  business 
politics,  campaigns,  etc.    All  matter  should  be  instructive  and  of 
timely  interest.    Short  stories  dealing  with  fidelity,  probity,  etc., 
in  business,  most  preferably  American.    Lengths;  from  2,500  to 
4,500  words.     Payment  is  usually  arranged  by  the  editorial  de- 
partment, and  remitted  on  publication.

III.  CANADIAN   PERIODICAL  PUBLICATIONS 

Note. — The  increase  in  the  number  of  periodicals  published 
in  the  Dominion  of  Canada  during  the  past  few  years  has  been 
such  as  to  warrant  the  compilation  of  a  list  dealing  specially 
with  them.  It  might  be  noted  with  regard  to  these  Canadian 
periodicals  that  their  contents  are  made  up  chiefly  of  articles 
on  Canadian  subjects.  This  is  owing  to  the  fact  that  unless 
a  periodical  possesses  some  such  distinction,  it  cannot  success- 
fully compete  with  the  many  excellent  English  and  American 
periodicals  of  a  general  character  which  circulate  so  freely  in 
the  Dominion.  The  advice  given  to  contributors  to  American 
magazines  applies  equally  to  Canadian  magazines. 

An  asterisk  (*)  implies  illustration. 

♦Busy  Man's  Magazine  (1905),  M.,  20  cents  (iod.).  111-127,  University 
Avenue,  Toronto.  Editor,  Mr.  W.  A.  Craick. 
Uses  special  articles  dealing  exclusively  with  Canadian  subjects  and 
short  stories  of  general  interest.  It  also  devotes  a  section  to  re- 
producing in  condensed  form  leading  articles  from  other  maga- 
zines. Remuneration  averages  half  a  cent,  per  word,  and  pay- 
ment is  made  within  a  month  of  acceptance.  Illustrations  are 
always  desired. 

♦Canada  Monthly  (1906),   M„   15  cents  (7£d.).     382,   Clarence  Street, 
London,  Ontario.     Editor,  Mr.  Herbert  Vanderhoof. 

Publishes  fiction  and  illustrated  articles,  which  deal  preferably  with 
Canadian  subjects.     Circulates  principally  in  Western  Canada. 
Payment  by  arrangement. 
Canadian  Bookman  (1909),  Af.,  10   cents  (sd.),     111-127   University 
Avenue,  Toronto.     Editor,  Mr.  W.  A.  Craick. 

Deals  with  Canadian  books  and  authors,  publishing  special  inter- 
views with  authors  and  articles  on  literary  topics.     Accepts  occa- 
sional articles  of  this  character,  making  payment  by  arrangement. 
♦Canadian  Century  (1910),  W.,  5  cents  (2$d.).    38,  St.  Antoine  Street, 
Montreal.     Editor,  Mr.  A.  E.  Dean. 
The  Century  divides  its  space  between  fiction  and  articles  on  various 
phases  of  life  in  Canada.     Its  effort  is  to  publish  in  the  fiction  line 
only  the  works  of  Canadian  writers  or  stories  the  locale  of  which 
is  in  Canada.    The  fiction  is  illustrated  by  local  artists,  and  the 
articles,  in  so  far  as  possible,  are  illustrated  by  photographs. 
Articles  of  from  2,000  to  3,000  words  are  preferred.     Where  no 
price  is  set,  the  rate  of  payment  is  one-third  of  a  cent,  a  word,  with 
extra  for  photographs. 
♦Canadian  Courier  (1906),  W.,  10  cents  (5d.).     12,  Wellington  Street  East, 
Toronto.     Editor,  Mr.  John  A.  Cooper. 

Uses  illustrated  articles  of  a  news  character,  for  which  \  cent  a  word 
is  paid  on  publication.

reviews  on  political,  literary,  scientific,  and  art  subjects.

Hindu,  The  (1878),  £>.,  2  annas.     Madras. 

One  of  the  leading  Indian  journals  of  the  Southern  Residency,  and 
the  exponent  of  the  opinions  of  the  more  advanced  sections  of 
educated  Indians  in  the  southern  provinces.  Also  weekly  and

Hindustan  Review,  The  (1899),  M.  Editor,  The  Hon.Mr.  S.  Sinha,  Bar- 
rister-at-Law,  7  Elgin  Road,  Allahabad.  Annual  subscription,  10s. 
The  oldest  Indian  monthly  review  in  existence,  and  the  most 
widely  read.  Articles  on  all  subjects  of  general  interest,  specially 
those  bearing  on  current  Indian  topics — political  and  administra- 
tive, social  and  religious  ;  also  those  dealing  with  Indian  literature, 
history,  and  philosophy.  A  notable  feature  of  the  Review  is  a 
"  Literary  Supplement,"  containing  exhaustive  critical  apprecia- 
tions and  appraisements  of  new  important  publications  in  all 
branches  of  literature,  also  reviews  and  notices  of  new  editions. 
Articles  as  a  rule  signed.  Contributions  from  England,  including 
critical  reviews  of  important  books,  desired.  No  restriction  in  the 
choice  of  subjects.     Remuneration  given.    Being  the  only  Indian 

Seriodical  edited  by  one  occupying  so  high  a  position  in  public 
fe  as  that  of  an  elected  Member  of  the  Imperial  Legislative  Council, 
it  is  one  of  the  most  influential  organs  of  enlightened  Indian 
opinion,  and  its  contributed  and  editorial  articles  command 
wide  attention.

CONTRIBUTORS'  INDEX  TO  PERIODICALS 


NOTE.— The  Index  is  designed  to  show  contributors  the  most  suitable 
papers  to  whleh  to  submit  articles,  stories,  or  any  other  literary 
work.  The  periodicals  indexed  correspond  with  those  con- 
tained in  the  full  lists  preceding  this ;  but  for  obvious  reasons 
annual  directories  and  similar  compilations  without  any  opening 
for  outside  contributions  have  been  omitted.  This  category 
includes  those  periodicals  which  refuse  to  consider  matter 
submitted  to  them,  and  daily  and  weekly  newspapers  of  the 
usual  type,  except  those  taking  fiction  either  in  the  form  of 
serials  or  short  stories.  Newspapers  are  primarily  a  medium 
for  the  circulation  of  news,  and  offer  few,  if  any,  openings  for 
articles  of  the  general  sort.  Most  specials,  or  "middles,"  as 
the  day's  articles  are  sometimes  termed,  are  commissioned — 
i.e.,  given  out  to  a  specialist  on  the  particular  subject— although 
occasionally  even  so  conservative  a  paper  as  The  Times  accepts 
outside  specials.  But  such  cases  as  these  are  so  rare  that  they 
do  not  Justify  the  Inclusion  of  newspapers  pure  et  simple  in  this 
list.  Reviews,  too,  both  in  general  periodicals  and  literary 
papers,  are  invariably  commissioned.  The  same  remarks  also 
apply  to  leaders. 

The  classification  is  primarily  taken  from  the  details  given 
by  the  periodicals,  but  where  these  are  insufficient  the  informa- 
tion has  been  obtained  from  other  sources.  It  has  been  found  im- 
possible to  give  details  of  the  length  of  articles,  as  very  few  publica- 
tions have  a  hard  and  fast  rule  in  this  respect;  the  majority 
prefer  something  between  1,000  and  2,000  words,  although  the 
monthlies  frequently  publish  articles  as  long  as  6,000  words. 

An  asterisk  *  shows  that  the  periodical  is  illustrated  with 
drawings  or  photographs. 

Periodicals  marked  with  a  dagger  (f)  will  be  found  In  the 
"  American  Periodicals"  List;  those  marked  (%)  in  the  "Indian 
Periodicals"  List,  and  those  marked  (§)  in  the  "Canadian 
Periodicals  "  List

1  The  larger  number  of  periodicals  is  illustrated  at  the  present  day,  but  most  of  these 
are  commissioned — i.e.,  they  are  illustrations  drawn  to  order  for  stories  or  matter  already 
written.  Some,  however,  take  outside  drawings  and  photography.  Papers  and  maga- 
xines  taking  illustrations  on  special  subjects  wm  be  found  under  those  subjects.  Peri- 
dicale  taking  humorous  drawings  will  be  found  under  '  Humour,'  and  a  few  magazines 
occasionally  take  cover  illustrations.  Most  of  the  full  page  illustrations  in  the  weekly 
illustrated  periodicals  are  drawn  by  the  art  staff.
