PUBLISHERS:    BRITISH 

Every  effort  has  been  made  to  obtain  the  following  Information 
at  first-hand,  but  the  Proprietor  Is  not  responsible  for  unin- 
tentional errors  of  commission  or  omission. 

ALDEN  &  CO.,  LIMITED,  Bocardo  Press,  Oxford.  Pub- 
lishers and  Booksellers. 

ALDINE  PUBLISHING  CO.,  LIMITED,  Crown  Court,  Chan- 
cery Lane,  w.c.  Publishers  of  My  Queen  Novels,  Crown  Novels, 
Tales  for  Little  People,  and  adventure  books  for  boys.  Tele- 
graphic address  :  Goodship,  London.     Telephone  :  4595  Holborn. 

ALEXANDER  &  SHEPHEARD,  LIMITED,  Roll's  Buildings, 
Fetter  Lane,  e.c.  General  publishers  of  books,  magazines, 
and  newspapers.  Telegraphic  address :  Xander,  London. 
Telephone  :  12  Holborn. 

ALLEN  &  SONS,  GEORGE,  Ruskin  House,  44  and  45  Rathbone 
Place,  Oxford  Street,  w.  (Incorporating  the  publications 
of  Bbmrosb  &  Sons.) 

The  Life,  Letters,  and  Works  of  Ruskin,  now  completed 
with  the  exception  of  the  final  volume  (consisting  of  a  Biblio- 
graphy and  an  Index  with  about  100,000  references),  under 
the  editorship  of  Mr.  E.  T.  Cook  and  Mr.  Alexander  Wedder- 
burn,  is  the  only  edition  containing  all  that  author's  writings. 

This  edition,  comprising  thirty-eight  volumes,  is  the  defini- 
tive one,  and  is  strictly  limited  to  two  thousand  copies.  It  is 
hoped  to  issue  the  final  volume  early  in  191 1. 

An  interesting  feature  is  the  Editor  s  Prefaces  to  the  volumes, 
in  the  preparation  of  which  the  Author's  Diaries  and  Letters 
have  been  largely  drawn  upon. 

The  illustrations  include  about  1,800  plates  and  wood- 
cuts from  drawings  by  Ruskin,  with  numerous  plates  hitherto 
unpublished,  and  facsimiles  of  the  MSS. 

In  addition  to  the  works  of  Ruskin  in  four  different  editions, 
this  firm  publishes  books  in  all  departments  of  literature, 
and  has  recently  purchased  the  Publishing  Department  of 
Messrs.  Bemrose  &  Sons. 

The  general  list  includes  such  authors  as  Maeterlinck,  Hare, 
H.  Belloc,  G.  A.  B.  Dewar,  Edward  Carpenter,  Rose  Kingsley, 
Gilbert  Murray.  Telegraphic  address  :  Deucalion,  London. 
Telephone  :  6554  Central. 

797 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


79$  THE  LITERARY  YfcAR-BOOK     [part  m. 

AIXENSON,  H.  R.,  LIMITED,  Racquet  Court,  Fleet  Street, 
B.C.  This  company  was  formed  early  in  1906  to  take  over 
the  publishing  crasiness  of  Mr.  H.  R.  Allenson,  formerly  of 
Ivy  Lane,  Paternoster  Row.  Theological  and  general  works. 
Books  by  J.  H.  Newman,  Carlyle,  Robert  Browning,  Mrs. 
•Browning,  Principal  John  Caird,  Rev.  F.  W.  Roberston  (of 
Brighton),  Samuel  Johnson,  LL.D.,  Bishop  Phillips  Brooks, 
Dr.  Moule,  Bishop  of  Durham,  Bishop  Boyd  Carpenter, 
Rev.  R.  E.  Welsh,  Professor  Momerie,  Rev.  J.  H.  Jowett  (of 
Birmingham),  Rev.  C.  F.  Dole,  and  Rev.  B.  J.  Snell  will  be 
found  in  the  catalogue.  Publishers  of  the  popular  theological 
sixpenny  reprints,  In  Relief  of  Doubt  (R.  E.  Welsh) ;  Immor- 
tality (Momerie)  ;  Serious  Call  (Law)  ;  also  the  Heart  and  Life 
Booklets.     Telegrams  :  Allensonia,  London. 

ALLMAN  &  SON,  LIMITED,  67  New  Oxford  Street,  w.c. 
Educational  publishers.  Founded  in  1780  by  Thomas  Allman, 
in  succession  to  his  uncle,  J.  Mawman,  law  publisher.  Incor- 
porated as  a  private  limited  company  in  1888.  Publishers  of 
works  on  English  Grammar  by  W.  Davidson,  B.A.,  J.  C.  Alcock, 
and  E.  M.  Alcock,  M.A. ;  on  Botany  by  C.  L.  Laurie  and 
A.  F.  M.  Johnson,  B.Sc. ;  on  English  Literature  by  W.  J. 
Addis,  M.A.,  and  J.  Lees,  B.A. ;  on  the  Metric  System  by 
Rev.  S.  Jackson,  M.A.,  lecturer  to  the  Decimal  Association; 
and  also  of  books  on  Hygiene  "  First- Aid,"  and  Physiological 
Textbooks,  and  diagrams  for  school  students,  dec, 

AMALGAMATED  PRK3S,  LIMITED,  THE,  Bouverie  Street, 
e.c.  Capital,  £1,300,000.  Directors :  Lord  NorthclifEe 
(Chairman),  Harold  S.  Harmsworth,  Cecil  B.  Harmsworth, 
J.  Horace  Newton,  Hartley  Aspden,  St.  John  Harmsworth, 
G.  A.  Sutton,  R.  Hamilton  Edwards,  G.  H.  Cantle,  B.  W. 
Young.     Telegraphic  address  :  Mistitled,  London. 

APPLETON  &  CO.,  25  Bedford  Street,  Strand,  w.c.  Publish 
miscellaneous,  medical,  scientific,  and  educational  works, 
fiction.  Telegraphic  address  :  Hilandero,  London.  Telephone  ; 
6283  Gerrard. 

A.P.S.  CO.,  LIMITED,  no  St.  Martin's  Lane,  w.c.  Publishers 
and  proprietors  of  the  Annals  of  Psychical  Science.  Publishers 
of  works  by  Professor  Ricket,  Dr.  Maxwell,  Hereward  Carring- 
ton,  Dr.  Sienson  Hooker,  &c. 

ARNOLD,  EDWARD,  41  &  43  Maddox  Street,  w.  General  and 
educational  publisher,  and  publisher  to  the  India  Office.  Tele- 
graphic address :  Scholarly,  London.    Telephone :  1883  Mayfair. 

ARNOLD,  E.  J.,  &  SON,  LIMITED,  Leeds.     Issue  educational 
books,  appliances,  stationery,  and  furniture  for  Elementary, 
Secondary,    and    Technical    Schools.     Telegraphic    address  : 
1  Arnold,  Leeds.

AND  BOOK  CO.,  LTD.,  28  Ashley  Place,  Westminster,

CAMBRIDGE  UNIVERSITY  PRESS  (London  Warehouse), 
Fetter  Lane,  London,  e.c.  ;  and  at  100  Princes  Street. 
Edinburgh.  The  list  of  Cambridge  printers  begins  with  the 
name  of  John  Siberch,  who  printed  at  Cambridge  in  15  21 
Linacre's  translation  of  Galen,  De  Temper amentis,  and  other 
works.  It  is  said  that  about  1522  the  Stationers  Company, 
on  some  complaint  of  privilege,  seized  the  University  printing 

gress,  but  from  the  year  1584  to  the  present  time  printing 
as  been  continuously  carried  on.  The  name  Pitt  Press  strictly 
applies  only  to  a  part  of  the  present  buildings,  which  were 
erected  in  1831  with  money  subscribed  as  a  memorial  to 
William  Pitt.  Up  to  the  year  1873  the  publishing  business 
of  the  University  was  conducted  through  the  agency  of 
various  London  publishers,  but  in  that  year  the  University 
established  its  own  publishing  warehouse  at  17  Paternoster 
Row  ;  in  1884  the  business  was  removed  to  Ave  Maria  Lane, 
and  in  1905  to  Fetter  Lane.  The  publishing  business  was  in 
the  first  instance  managed  by  Mr.  C.  J.  Clay,  who  was  Uni- 
versity printer  from  1854  to  1894.  It  is  now  conducted  under 
the  management  of  his  son,  Mr.  C.  F.  Clay. 

The  University  Press,  using  its  privileges  to  the  full,  pub- 
lishes editions  of  the  Bible  and  Prayer  Book  in  every  variety 
of  size,  type  and  binding.  In  recent  years  one  of  the  great 
undertakings  of  the  Press  has  been  the  publication  of  the  Re- 
vised Version  of  the  Bible :  the  New  Testament  was  published 
in  188 1,  the  complete  Old  Testament  in  1885,  the  Apocrypha 
in  1897,  and  in  1905  the  Interlinear  Bible,  showing  the 
Authorized  and  Revised  Versions  at  a  glance. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  n.]  PUBLISHERS  803 

As  general  publishers  the  Press  issues  works  of  learning 
ranging  from  elementary  school  books  to  the  most  elaborate 
mathematical  and  scientific  treatises,  as  well  as  several 
scientific  serials.  Among  the  most  important  recent  publica- 
tions are  The  Cambridge  Modern  History,  planned  by  the  late 
Lord  Acton,  in  1 2  volumes,  8  of  which  are  now  ready,  and  the 
Cambridge  History  of  English  Literature,  in  14  volumes,  edited 
by  the  Master  of  Peterhouse  and  Mr.  A.  R.  Waller,  the  first 
two  volumes  of  which  are  now  ready. 

At  the  new  warehouse  in  Fetter  Lane  there  have  been  formed 
(1)  a  permanent  exhibition  of  Cambridge  Bibles  and  Prayer 
Books,  in  a  great  variety  of  leather  bindings,  and  (2)  a  library 
of  the  publications  of  the  Press,  where  any  book  issued  by  the 
Press  may  be  seen. 

Telegraphic  address :  Cantabrigia,  London.  Telephone :

EYRE  &  SPOTTISWOODE,  LIMITED.  H.M.  Printing  Office,  and 
Publishing  Office  for  Legal  Works,  <S»c,  East  Harding  Street, 
e.c.  Bible  Warehouse,  33  Paternoster  Row,  e.c.  Woodbury, 
&>c,  Department  and  Bible  Printing  Office,  Downs  Park  Road, 
Hackney,  n.e.  This  firm  was  founded  in  1767  by  Charles 
Eyre,  the  youngest  son  of  John  Eyre,  Esq.,  of  Landford  House, 
Wilts,  who  had  acquired  in  173 1  the  reversion  granted  by  Letters 
Patent  to  John  Baskett.  Charles  Eyre  settled  at  Clapham  and 
took  into  partnership  a  Scotch  printer,  William  Strahan,  who 
died  in  1785,  his  third  son,  Andrew,  taking  his  place.  In  1797 
George  Eyre  succeeded  his  father,  Charles  Eyre,  and  Andrew 
Strahan  being  succeeded  by  his  nephew,  Andrew  Spottiswoode, 
the  firm  of  Eyre  &  Strahan  has  since  been  known  by  the  title  of 
Eyre  &  Spottiswoode. 

The  numerous  "  King's  Printers'  "  editions  of  the  Bible  and 
Prayer-Book  include  the  Variorum  editions  of  the  Old  and  New 
Testaments  and  of  the  Apocrypha,  containing  at  the  foot  of 
each  page  a  complete  digest  of  the  most  accredited  various 
readings  and  renderings  of  the  text,  and  a  collation  of  the 
Revised  Version,  edited  by  Cheyne,  Driver,  Clarke,  Sanday, 
and  C.  J.  Ball ;  the  Variorum  and  other  editions  of  the  New 
Illustrated  Teacher's  Bible,  edited  by  the  most  eminent  specialists 
of  the  day,  and  comprising  a  representative  series  of  manu- 
scripts, antiquities,  and  sites  of  Biblical  interest,  selected  and 
described  by  C.  J.  Ball ;  The  Annexed  (or  Standard)  Book  of 
Common  Prayer,  1662,  in  type  (a  cheap  edition  of  the  large 
facsimile  edition  produced  by  this  firm),  with  three  Appendices  ; 
Bishop  Barry's  Teacher's  Prayer-Book,  with  Glossary  by  May- 
hew  ;  The  Historical  Prayer-Book,  &c. 

The  King's  Printers  have  given  special  attention  to  the  re- 
production photographically  of  the  chief  monuments  of  Baby- 
lonia, Assyria,  Syria,  and  Egypt,  and  of  the  important  sites  and 
cities  which  illustrate  the  Bible.  They  published  Light  from 
the  East,  a  complete  first  handbook  to  the  study  of  Biblical 
arcihaeology,  edited  by  the  Rev  C.  J.  Ball ;  Kenyon's  Our  Bible 
and  the  Ancient  Manuscripts,  illustrated  by  twenty-nine  fac- 
sim  les  ;  The  Young  Scholar's  Illustrated  Bible  ;  The  Landscape 
Bib'e,  with  Fifty  Coloured  Views  ;  The  Teacher's  Roll  of  Bible 
Illustrations,  They  also  publish  Lectures  to  accompany 
selections  from  their  numerous  "  Woodbury  "  lantern  slides. 

The  King's  Printers  are  the  printers  of  The  Sphere,  The 
Tatler,  The  Builders'  Journal,  and  The  Architectural  Review. 
Telegraphic  addresses :  (Printing,  Publishing,  and  Binding 
Departments)  :  Eyre,  London ;  (Bible  Warehouse)  Exaltedly, 
London ;  (Catalogue  Department)  Spotless,  London ;  (Bible 
Printing  Office)  Print  Eyre,  London.

32  Warwick  Lane,  ex.,  and  Overy  House,  100  Southwark 
Street,  s.e.  Sampson  Low,  the  founder  of  this  house,  a 
printer  and  publisher  in  Berwick  Street,  Soho,  died  in  1800. 
His  son,  Sampson,  the  "  Father  of  the  Trade,"  began  business 
as  a  publisher  and  bookseller  nineteen  years  later  in  Lamb's 
Conduit  Street.  Mr.  Edward  Marston  joined  the  firm  in  the 
fifties.  The  managing  directors  are  Mr.  W.  Tyrrell  and  Mr. 
Fred  J.  Rymer.

POWER-BOOK  CO.  This  firm  deals  only  in  books  dealing  with 
what  is  popularly  known  as  The  New  Thought  and  True 
Occultism,  and  publishes  only  those  which  are  known  to  be 
sound  in  principle  and  application.  The  proprietor  has  had 
a  long  and  varied  experience  in  teaching  and  helping  those 
who  desire  to  study,  and  has  arranged  a  graduated  course  of 
study,  which  has  proved  to  be  a  great  boon  to  those  who 
would  keep  abreast  of  modern  thinking  on  high  lines.  Any 
person  requiring  information  or  help  in  either  the  selection 
of  books,  or  advice  on  health  by  mental  development,  or  in 
connection  with  trying  circumstances,  will  be  welcome  to  make 
inquiries.

ROYALTIES 

Authors  will  find  the  following  tables  useful  whenever  they  adopt 
the  method  of  payment  by  results  known  as  royalties.  It  is  some- 
what difficult  at  times  in  publishers'  accounts  to  ascertain  the  exact 
sum  due  to  the  author  when  x  copies  of  his  book  have  been  sold  at 
the  price  of  y  shillings,  on  which  a  royalty  of  z  per  cent,  is  due  to 
the  author.  The  following  tables  are  intended  to  facilitate  this 
calculation  by  reducing  the  somewhat  laborious  test  of  complicated 
multiplication  to  a  simple  process  of  addition.  The  matter  is 
further  complicated  by  the  general  practice  of  publishers  accounting 
to  authors  for  sales  on  the  basis  of  13  as  12  (or  sometimes  25  as  24), 
which  they  generally  grant  to  the  retail  booksellers.  There  are 
accordingly  two  sets  of  tables,  one  in  order  to  reduce  13  to  12  (or 
13  to  1 2 J),  and  the  remainder  to  ascertain  the  exact  amount  due 
on  different  systems  of  royalty  and  on  price  books.  Thus,  if  936 
of  a  5s.  book  have  been  sold  on  which  a  royalty  of  10  per  cent,  is 
due,  the  first  table  will  enable  the  calculator  to  ascertain  that  936 
could  only  be  reckoned  as  864,  and  from  the  fourth  column  of  the 
table  devoted  to  5  s.  the  royalties  for  800,  for  60,  and  for  4  are 
already  given,  and  the  final  result  easily  works  out  at  £21  12s. 
Generally  speaking,  it  is  sufficient  to  check  the  results  roughly — 
e.g.,  by  finding  out  how  much  850  copies  would  bring  in. 

In  making  the  following  calculations  a  certain  difficulty  arises 
owing  to  the  deficiency  of  our  coinage,  which  reckons  nothing  lower 
than  a  farthing,  and  to  the  trade  custom  of  the  booksellers  by  which 
the  moiety  is  the  only  fractional  part  of  the  price  of  a  book  recog- 
nised. In  consequence  some  of  the  results  are  not  strictly  in  accord- 
ance with  the  Rule  of  Three. 

The  suggestion  has  been  made  that  it  is  not  entirely  reasonable 
to  define  these  royalties  within  the  limits  of  2J  and  20  per  cent.  ; 
but  the  royalty  of  2J  per  cent,  is  in  frequent  use,  especially  where 
colonial  editions  of  books  published  in  England  are  concerned,  and, 
as  has  been  shown,  in  graduated  royalties.  Royalties  of  more  than 
20  per  cent.,  on  the  other  hand,  are  not  frequent ;  and  as  these  tables 
are  intended  to  afford  assistance  to  the  majority  of  authors — to  take, 
in  fact,  the  place  of  a  working  guide — it  would  be  misleading  to 
include  figures  which  are  only  applicable  to  exceptional  cases.

THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK        [part  n. 


STANDARD  SIZES  OF  BOOKS 

The  following  are  the  measurements,  in  inches,  of  books 
before  binding.  The  cloth  binding  in  which  most  books 
are  published  adds  somewhat  to  these  dimensions.  Indi- 
vidual volumes  may  differ  slightly  from  these  measure- 
ments, according  to  the  amount  by  which  the  edges  have 
been  trimmed  : 


Folios  : 
Royal,  20  x  12J. 
Demy,  17J  x  n±. 
Foolscap,  13$  x  8$. 
Pott,  12J  x  7f. 

Quartos  (4J0.)  : 
Imperial,  15  x  11. 
Royal,  12J  x  10. 
Medium,  nfxgj. 
Demy,  n£  x  8|. 
Small,  10  x  7$. 
Foolscap,  8 J  x  6|. 
Pott,  7|  x  6£. 

Octavos  (8vo.) : 
Imperial,  11  x  7£. 
Super  Royal,  io£  x  7. 
Royal,  10  x  6£. 
Medium,  9I  x  6. 
Demy,  8|x5i. 


Octavos  (continued) — 
Square,  8J  x  6. 
Extra  Crown,  8  x  5  J. 
Crown,  7£  x  5. 
Globe,  7x41. 
Foolscap,  6|  x  4^. 
Post,  7f  x  4f . 
Pott,  6|  x  4. 

Duodecimo  (i2wo.)  : 
Foolscap,  7x4. 

Sexto-decimo  (z6mo.)  : 
Foolscap,  6J  x  4. 

Duo-tricesimo  (32tno.)  : 
Royal,  5x3. 
Imperial,  5 J  x  3f. 
Post,  4  x  2J. 
Crown,  3|  x  2J. 


Digitized  by  CjOOQLC 


SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS 

Details  are  given  as  far  as  possible  of  work  accomplished  during  the 
past  year,  but  in  some  eases  information  was  received  too  late 
for  insertion  in  this  issue. 

Aoademie  Committee.  Formed,  1910.  This  committee  has  been 
constituted  in  the  Royal  Society  of  Literature  (q.v.)  as  the  result 
of  deliberations  carried  on  by  a  joint  committee  of  the  Royal 
Society  of  Literature  and  the  Society  of  Authors,  in  order  to 
represent  pure  literature  in  the  same  way  as  the  AcadSmie 
Francaise  in  France.  Objects — (a)  To  take  all  possible  measures 
to  maintain  the  purity  of  the  English  language  and  to  hold  up 
a  standard  of  good  taste  in  style  ;  (b)  To  encourage  fellowship 
and  co-operation  among  those  who  are  disinterestedly  striving 
for  the  perfection  of  English  literature  ;  (c)  By  "  discourses  of 
reception  "  and  "  obituary  addresses  "  to  mark  the  current 
of  literary  history  in  this  country ;  (d)  To  designate  from  time 
to  time  persons  to  become  recipients  of  the  medals  of  the  society ; 
(e)  To  make  awards  of  merit  to  particular  literary  works. 
President — Lord  Morlby.  Secretary — E.  H.  Pember,  K.C., 
20  Hanover  Square,  W. 

Academies,  International  Association  of.    Founded  1899.    This  is  a 
union  of  the  principal  academies  and  learned  bodies  of  the 
civilized  world,  the  Royal  Society  and  the  British  Academy 
representing  the  British  Isles.     Objects — To  prepare  and  pro- 
mote scientific  works  of  general  interest,   and   to  facilitate 
relations  between  the  learned  bodies  of  the  different  countries. 
A  General  Assembly  is  held  every  three  years,  in  the  interval 
of  which  meetings  the  Academy  of  the  place  in  which  the  next 
Assembly  is  to  be  held  is  constituted  the  Directing  Academy. 
From  January  1,  191 1,  for  the  following  three  years  the  Im- 
perial Academy  of  Sciences  at  St.  Petersburg  is  the  Directing 
Academy  of  the  Association. 
Aeronautleal  Society  of  Great  Britain,  53  Victoria  Street,  West- 
minster, s.w.     Founded   1866,   for  the  purpose  of  giving  a 
stronger  impulse  to  the  scientific  study  of  Aerial  Navigation  ; 
to  promote  the  intercourse  of  those  interested  in  the  subject 
at  home  as  well  as  abroad  ;  and  to  aid  with  advice  and  instruc- 
tion those  studying  the  subject.     Meetings  of  the  Society  are 
frequently  held,  and  its  proceedings  are  recorded  in  the  Afro- 
nautical  Journal,  published  quarterly  by  Messrs.  King,  Sell,  & 
Olding,    27    Chancery   Lane,    w.c.     The   Hon.   Secretary   is 
Colonel  J.  D.  Fullerton,  R.E.  (ret.). 

865  28 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


866  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [i  art  ii. 

African  Society,  The.  1900.  Founded  for  the  study  of  African 
languages,  history,  ethnology,  local  laws  and  customs,  systems 
of  government,  forms  of  religion,  folk-lore,  native  wants  and 
aspirations,  trade  and  products,  etc.  Subscription — One  guinea 
per  annum.  A  Journal  is  published  quarterly.  Hon.  Secre- 
tary— H.  d'Egville.     Offices — Imperial  Institute,  s.w. 

Ambidextral  Culture  Society.  Founded  1903.  Objects— The  pro- 
motion of  a  scientific  scheme  of  ambidextral  culture  and 
teaching  throughout  the  United  Kingdom.  The  system  advo- 
cated by  the  Society  includes  (a)  Symmetrical  or  two-handed 
physical  culture,  and  (b)  the  cultivation  of  healthy  postures. 
Methods — By  lectures,  by  distribution  of  pamphlets,  etc.,  and 
by  bringing  its  objects  prominently  and  effectively  before  the 
teaching  profession.  President — W.  H.  Cummings,  Esq., 
Mus.  Doc.,  F.S.A.  Hon.  Secretary — John  Jackson,  Esq., 
F.E.I.S.,  31,  32  Paternoster  Row,  ex. 

Anoient  Buildings,  Society  for  the  Protection  of.  This  Society  is 
in  the  thirty-fourth  year  of  its  existence,  and  was  founded  to 
protect  ancient  buildings  from  ruinous  modernizings  and 
"  improvements."  The  Society  has  numerous  correspondents 
in  all  parts  of  the  country,  and  the  membership  is  about  460. 
Full  particulars  of  the  Society's  work  are  to  be  found  in  the 
Thirty-third  Annual  Report  of  the  Committee,  published  in 
June,  1910.  Secretary — Thackeray  Turner,  Esq.  F.S.A., 
20  Buckingham  Street,  Strand,  w.c. 

Anglo-Russian  Literary  Soeiety  (Imperial  Institute,  S.W.).  Founded 
by  Mr.  E.  A.  Cazalet  in  1893.  Objects — The  study  of  Russian 
language  and  literature,  and  promotion  of  friendly  Anglo- 
Russian  relations.  Lectures  are  given  on  the  first  Tuesday  of 
every  month  at  3  p.m.  A  Journal,  containing  a  variety  of  original 
Russian  news,  is  printed  three  times  in  the  year.  Communi- 
cations should  be  addressed  to  the  President,  Ed.  A.  Cazalbt, 
Anglo-Russian  L.  S.,  Imperial  Institute,  s.w. 

Antiquaries  of  Ireland,  Royal  Soeiety  of,  6  St.  Stephen's  Green, 
Dublin.  Founded  in  1849  *s  the  Kilkenny  Archaeological 
Society.  It  meets  in  Dublin  monthly,  and  quarterly  in  the 
four  provinces  seriatim.  Ptt6/tca**0»s— -Quarterly  Journal  and 
an  annual  extra  volume.  Subscriptions — Fellows,  £1  per 
annum  ;  Members,  10s.  per  annum.  Hon.  Secretary — Robert 
Cochrane,  LL.D.,  I.S.O.,  F.S.A.  Hon.  Treasurer — H.  J. 
Stokes. 
Anti-Soeialist  Union.  Founded  1908.  Objects — To  combat  So- 
cialism. Methods — Training  and  supplying  speakers  to  address 
meetings  and  take  part  in  debates ;  the  publication  of  books, 
pamphlets,  and  leaflets  dealing  with  the  various  phases  of 
Socialism ;  supplying  information  to  speakers  and  writers  ; 
contributing  articles  to  the  Press.  Publications — A  Critical 
Examination  of  Socialism,  by  W.  H.  Mallock ;  New  Australia, 
by  Stewart  Grahame ;  Red  England  :  a  Tale  of  the  Socialist 
Terror ;  The  Failures  of  Socialism ;  Liberty  (monthly,  id.)  ; 
and  numerous  pamphlets  and  leaflets.     President — The  Duke

part  ii.]  SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS  867 

of  Devonshire.  Chairman — Claude  Lowther,  Esq.  Vice- 
Chairman — W.  W.  Ashley,  Esq.,  M.P.  Secretary — Harman 
Grisewood.  Literary  Secretary — P.  S.  Bridgeford.  Offices 
— 58  and  60  Victoria  Street,  Westminster,  s.w. 

Apocrypha,  International  Society  of  the.  1005.  Object— To  make 
more  widely  known  the  theological,  ecclesiastical,  and  literary 
value  of  the  "  Books  which  the  Church  doth  read  for  example 
of  life  and  instruction  of  manners,"  and  to  promote  their 
more  general  study  among  the  clergy  and  laity.  Publication — 
The  International  Journal  of  Apocrypha,  published  quarterly, 
which  contains  varied  articles  and  notes  bearing  on  the  Apoc- 
rypha and  Pseudepigrapha  of  the  Old  and  of  the  New  Testa- 
ment, and  reviews  of  recommended  books.  Fellows  of  the 
Society  pay  an  annual  subscription  of  £1  is.  The  minimum 
subscription  for  membership,  2s.  6d.,  or  one  dollar,  per  annum. 
Patrons — All  the  Archbishops  of  the  Anglican  Church.  Presi- 
dent— The  Bishop  of  Winchester,  D.D.  Central  Warden  and 
Editor — The  Rev.  Herbert  Pentin,  M.A.,  Milton  Abbey, 
Dorset. 

Archaeological  Association  (British),  32  Sackville  Street.  Picca- 
dilly. 1843.  Objects — To  investigate,  preserve,  and  illustrate 
all  ancient  monuments  of  the  history,  manners,  customs,  and 
arts  of  our  forefathers,  in  furtherance  of  the  principles  on  which 
the  Society  of  Antiquaries  of  London  was  established  ;  and  to 
aid  the  objects  of  that  institution  by  rendering  available  re- 
sources which  had  not  been  drawn  upon,  and  which  did  not  come 
within  the  scope  of  any  antiquarian  or  literary  society.  Meetings 
—Annual  Congresses  and  evenings  of  third  Wednesday  in 
month  (Nov.  to  June).  Publications — Journal,  quarterly. 
Hon.  Secretaries — G.  Patrick,  A.R.I.B.A.,  and  J.  G.  N.  Clift, 
Hill  View,  Nightingale  Road,  Guildford,  Surrey.  Hon. 
Treasurer — R.  H.  Forster,  Esq.,  M.A. 

Archaeological  Institute  (Royal)  of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland,  20  Han- 
over Square,  w.  1843.  Incorporated  1884.  Object — The  en- 
couragement of  research  into  British  and  other  antiquities, 
and  of  care  for  their  preservation.  Membership — Life  and 
annual.  Publications — Archaeological  Journal,  a  quarterly,  free 
to  members ;  and  works.  Annual  Meeting  in  July.  General 
Monthly  Meetings — November  to  July.  Secretary  —  G.  O. 
Hardinge-Tylbr,  Esq.,  M.A. 

Architectural  and  Topographical  Society.  Founded  1908.  Objects 
— To  make  and  publish  a  survey  of  objects  of  architectural  and 
archaeological  interest  in  the  British  Islands.  Measured  draw- 
ings, sketches,  and  photographs  are  collected  and  kept  for 
reference  in  the  offices  of  the  Society,  together  with  a  map 
showing  the  position  of  places  described  or  illustrated.  Those 
who  have  control  of  ancient  buildings  are  requested  to  stipulate 
that  copies  of  any  measured  drawings  or  photographs  and  a 
record  of  any  alterations  or  restorations  shall  be  deposited  with 
the  Society.  Publications — A  quarterly  Journal,  called  The

Associated  Booksellers  of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland,  1  Bathurst 
Street,  Hyde  Park,  w.  Objects — (a)  To  place  the  book-selling 
trade  on  a  more  profitable  basis  and  to  prevent  underselling, 
and  to  endeavour  to  limit  the  discount  upon  books  issued  on  the 
old  terms  to  25  per  cent,  off  published  prices,  (b)  To  see  that 
the  agreement  dated  ist  October,  1906,  re  "net"  books,  is 
strictly  carried  out,  and  to  report  all  cases  of  underselling  such 
books  to  the  Publishers'  Association,  (c)  To  form  local 
branches.    Hon.  Secretary — Edwin  Pearce.

Athenaeum  Club,  107  Pall  Mall,  S.W.  Founded  1824  by  the  Rt. 
Hon.  J.  Wilson  Croker,  Sir  Thomas  Lawrence,  Sir.  F.  Chantrey, 
Faraday,  and  other  eminent  men  "  for  the  association  of 
individuals  known  for  their  scientific  or  literary  attainments, 
artists  of  eminence  in  any  class  of  the  fine  arts,  and  noblemen 
and  gentlemen  distinguished  as  liberal  patrons  of  science, 
literature,  or  the  arts."  Since  its  foundation  almost  every 
distinguished  name  in  literature,  art,  science,  or  public  service 
has  appeared  upon  its  roll  of  members.  The  library  contains 
about  70,000  volumes,  including  many  valuable  books  of  refer- 
ence. Objects — The  encouragement  of  literature,  science, 
and  art.  Subscription. — Entrance  30  guineas  ;  annual,  8  guineas. 
Secretary  and  Librarian — Mr.  Henry  R.  Tedder. 

Authors'  Association,  The.  1902.  Objects — To  assist  and  advise 
the  beginner  in  literature.  To  criticize  and,  if  necessary,  revise 
MSS.  ;  to  give  competent  advice  upon  the  advisability  of 
adopting  a  literary  life ;  and  to  give  legal  advice  on  questions 
within  the  scope  of  the  Association.  To  act  as  general  literary 
agents,  and  to  arrange  with  publishers  and  editors  for  the 
publication  of  the  work  of  members.  To  advise  upon  publica- 
tion of  MSS.,  and  suitable  magazines,  periodicals,  or  publishing 
houses.  To  make  contracts  with  editors  and  publishers  on 
behalf  of  members,  and  to  watch  and  protect  the  interests  of 
members.  To  advise  upon  and  (in  particular  cases)  arrange  for 
publication  of  musical  compositions,  and  book  and  magazine 
work.  And  to  use  the  funds  of  the  Society  for  the  furtherance 
of  all  or  any  of  the  above  objects,  as  maybe  deemed  desirable. 
The  Association  does  not  enter  into  competition  with  any 
existing  literary  society.  Subscription — One  guinea  for  the 
first  year ;  subsequent  years  half  a  guinea.  General  Secretary — 
Galloway  Kyle.  F.R.S.L.,  17  Surrey  Street,  Strand,  w.c. 
Solicitor — Mr.  W.  Wynn  Evans,  i  3  Clifford's  Inn,  e.c. 
Authors'  Club,  2  Whitehall  Court,  s.w.  Founded  in  1891  by  Sir 
Walter  Besant,  and  reconstructed  in  1008.  The  privileges  of 
a  residential  club  are  combined  with  the  advantages  of  a 
literary  society,  and,  although  entirely  independent  of  the 
Incorporated  Society  of  Authors,  the  Authors'  Club  is  fully  in 
sympathy  with  that  institution.  The  Monday  evening  House 
Dinners,  at  which  many  literary  celebrities  have  been  enter- 
tained, have  long  been  famous.  For  the  year  1909  the  President 
was  Mr.  Thomas  Hardy,  and  amongst  those  acting  with  him

Morley  Roberts  ;  Mr.  William  Senior ;  The  Ven.  Archdeacon

The  new  suite  of  rooms  overlooks  the  Victoria  Embankment

Authors,  Incorporated  Society  el,  39  Old  Queen  Street,  Storey's 
Gate,  s.w.  Founded  in  1883  by  Sir  Walter  Besant,  Sir 
Frederick  Pollock,  and  other  well-known  writers.  Incorporated 
in  1884  under  the  Presidency  of  Lord  Tennyson.  On  Lord 
Tennyson's  death  Mr.  George  Meredith  was  elected  President ; 
to  him  succeeded  Mr.  Thomas  Hardy  in  1909.  Its  main  objects 
are  the  maintenance,  definition,  and  defence  of  literary,  dra- 
matic, and  musical  property  and  the  work  of  black-and-white 
artists,  the  consolidation  of  the  laws  of  Domestic  Copyright, 
and  the  promotion  of  International  Copyright.  The  member- 
ship is  about  2,000,  and  continues  to  increase.  The  general 
work  of  the  Society,  with  much  information  on  technical 
subjects,  will  be  found  in  the  pages  of  The  Author,  published 
monthly,  and  now  in  its  sixteenth  volume.  (No  issue  m  August 
and  September.)  Full  details  of  its  works  are  contained  in 
the  Prospectus  issued  from  the  Society's  offices.  The  Society 
issues  also  from  time  to  time  pamphlets  bearing  on  literary 
production.  Among  these  may  be  mentioned  "  The  Methods 
of  Publishing,"  "  The  Cost  of  Production  "  (now  out  cf  print), 
"  Forms  of  Agreement  issued  by  the  Publishers'  Associa- 
tion, with  Comments  by  G.  Herbert  Thring,"  "  The  Addenda 
to  the  Methods  of  Publishing,"  and  a  few  others.  All  in- 
formation with  regard  to  the  standing  of  the  different  pub- 
lishing houses,  contracts  for  the  publication  of  books,  serial 
issue  in  magazines,  dramatic  productions,  and  the  marketing 
of  musical  compositions,  can  be  obtained  from  the  Secretary. 
Members  are  also  entitled  to  the  opinion  of  the  Society's 
Solicitor  in  addition  to  that  of  the  Secretary,  who  is  himself 
a   Solicitor.     The   rights    of    authors    are    supported    in    the

part  ii.]  SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS  871 

Courts  of  Law  at  the  expense  of  the  Society,  subject  to  the 
sanction  of  the  Committee,  and  counsel's  opinion  is  obtained 
(with  the  Committee's  consent)  without  any  extra  charge  to 
the  member.  These  are  a  few  of  the  many  matters  that  it 
takes  into  consideration.  One  of  the  most  interesting  develop- 
ments is  that  of  a  Pension  Scheme  for  the  benefit  of  authors 
who  are  members  of  the  Society.  The  Pension  Fund  Com- 
mittee have  already  assigned  three  pensions,  and  hope  to  grant 
another  shortly.  The  names  on  the  Council  include  many  of 
those  best  known  in  the  various  branches  of  literature.  The 
Society  is  managed  by  a  special  Committee,  with  Sub-Com- 
mittees on  Art,  Copyright,  and  the  Drama.  Subscription — 
One  guinea  per  annum,  or  ten  guineas  for  life-membership. 
Secretary — Mr.  G.  Herbert  Thring,  Solicitor  in  England  for  La 
Societe  des  Gens  de  Lettres.

Boi  01  ub.  The  Boz  Club  was  founded  in  the  year  1900  by  Percy 
Fitzgerald,  M.A.,  F.S.A.,  who  selected  as  the  original  members 
those  only  who  had  personally  known  Dickens,  and  belonged 
to  the  Athenaeum  Club — about  a  dozen  persons.  Later,  those 
who  had  written  on  the  subject  of  Dickens,  or  had  studied  his 
books,  were  admitted,  bringing  the  number  up  to  fifty  or  so. 
Lord  James  of  Hereford  is  President,  and  Mr.  H.  F.  Dickens, 
K.C.,  Chairman  of  the  Committee.  In  the  year  1903  the  Club 
was  remodelled  and  extended.  There  is  now  a  roll  of  about 
160  members.  Like  the  Walter  Scott  Club  at  Edinburgh,  it 
has  no  fixed  locale  or  habitation.  A  guinea  is  the  annual  sub- 
scription, which  includes  the  price  of  a  dinner,  at  which  guests 
are  admitted  by  an  invitation  of  the  Committee.  At  the 
dinner  there  is  an  address  by  the  Chairman,  and  a  discussion 
on  Dickensian  topics. 

British  and  Foreign  Bible  Society.    See  under  Publishers. 

British  Academy  for  the  Promotion  of  Historieal,  Philosophical, 
and  Philological  Studies,  The.  Burlington  House,  Piccadilly,  W. 
Founded  by  royal  charter,  August,  1902.  Objects — The  ad- 
vancement and  study  of  history,  philosophy,  law,  politics 
economics,  archaeology,  and  philology. 

British  Association  for  the  Advancement  of  Science,  Burlington 
House.  1 83 1.  Objects — To  give  a  stronger  impulse  and  a  more 
systematic  direction  to  scientific  inquiry  ;  to  promote  the  inter- 
course of  those  who  cultivate  science  in  different  parts  of  the 
British  Empire  with  one  another  and  with  foreign  philosophers  ; 
and  to  obtain  a  more  general  attention  to  the  objects  of  science, 
and  a  removal  of  any  disadvantages  of  a  public  kind  which 
impede  its  progress.  Meetings — One  week  in  each  year  ;  in 
1911  at  Portsmouth.  In  1910  the  Association  met  at  Sheffield. 
Publication — Annual  Report.  President  (1910) — Rev.  Prof. 
T.  G.  Bonney,  F.R.S.  General  Secretaries— Major  P.  A.  Mac- 
Mahon,  R.A.,  F.R.S.,  and  Prof.  W.  A.  Herdman,  F.R.S. 
Assistant  Secretary — O.  J.  R.  Howarth,  M.A.,  Burlington 
House,  w. 

British  Constitution  Association.  To  resist  Socialism :  to  uphold  the 
fundamental  principles  of  the  British  Constitution — personal 
liberty  and  personal  responsibility — and  limit  the  functions 
of  governing  bodies  accordingly.  President — Harold  Cox. 
Chairman  of  Council — C.  F.  Ryder.  Deputy-Chairman — 
S.  Hutchinson  Harris.  Chairman  of  Committee — Sir  William 
Chance,  Bt.  Deputy-Chairman — Mark  H.  Judge.  Hon.  Secre- 
tary—V/.  V.  Cooper.  Office — Broad  Sanctuary  Chambers, 
Tothill  Street,  s.w.  Annual  Subscription — Ten  shillings 
(minimum)  ;  Associates,  2s.  6d.  Publications — Constitution 
Papers,  Monthly,  id. ;  Annual  Reports,  6d. 

British  Record  Society,  Limited.  1886.  Object— The  publication 
of  Records  from  the  Public  Record  Office  and  Calendars  of 
Wills  from  the  various  Probate  Registries,  relating  to  Great 
Britain.  Yearly  subscription — £1  is.  Hon.  Secretary,  E.  A. 
Fry,  124  Chancery  Lane,  w.c.

part  ii.]  SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS  873 

British  School  at  Athens.— This  School  (founded  in  1886)  gives 
to  British  students  of  Greek  archaeology  and  art  the  oppor- 
tunity of  pursuing  their  researches  in  Greece  itself,  with  com- 
mand of  the  means  which  the  recent  great  advances  of  the 
science  have  rendered  indispensable. 

Athens  is  every  year  becoming  more  and  more  the  centre  of 
the  archaeological  world.  The  architecture  of  Greece  can 
nowhere  else  be  studied  to  such  advantage  ;  and  the  concentra- 
tion in  the  Athenian  museums  of  the  numerous  and  most 
important  discoveries  which  have  taken  place  on  Greek  soil  in 
the  last  few  years  has  made  a  personal  knowledge  of  them 
indispensable  to  a  proper  training  ;  in  particular,  they  may 
almost  be  said  to  hold  a  monopoly  of  the  materials  for  the 
investigation  of  prehistoric  and  early  archaic  art. 

The  student  requires  two  auxiliaries  when  working  in 
Athens — firstly,  the  command  of  an  adequate  library ;  and 
secondly,  the  advice  of  a  trained  archaeologist,  residing  on  the 
spot,  and  following  the  rapid  advances  of  the  science,  due 
partly  to  new  discovery  and  partly  to  the  rearrangement  of 
old  materials. 

These  advantages  are  now  provided  for  French,  German, 
Austrian,  American,  and  English  archaeologists,  through  the 
schools  which  they  have  established.  By  the  recent  addition 
of  the  Penrose  Memorial  Library,  the  British  School  is  now  in 
point  of  equipment  one  of  the  best  in  Athens.  It  is  also  by 
means  of  these  schools  that  most  excavations  on  Greek  soil 
have  been  carried  out ;  and  those  conducted  in  Cyprus,  in 
the  Peloponnese,  in  Melos,  and  in  Crete  by  the  British  School 
are  an  encouraging  proof  of  the  work  that  may  be  done  in  the 
future  if  the  School  is  adequately  supported. 

Any  persons  who  bring  satisfactory  testimonials  of  their 
qualifications  are  admitted  as  students  free  of  charge.     The 

Srincipal  conditions  imposed  are  that  they  shall  pursue  some 
efinite  course  of  Hellenic  study  or  research,  residing  for  the 
purpose  not  less  than  three  months  in  Greek  lands,  and  that 
they  shall  at  the  end  of  the  session  write  a  report  of  the  work 
which  they  have  done.  Applications  from  intending  students 
should  be  made  to  the  Secretary,  John  ff.  Baker-Penoyre, 
Esq.,  M.A.,  22  Albemarle  Street,  W.,  who  will  also  be  happy  to 
supply  any  further  information. 

The  Annual  of  the  School,  containing  the  official  reports  on 
the  School  Excavations,  is  supplied  free  to  subscribers  of  £1  and 
upwards.  Donations  or  annual  subscriptions  to  the  School 
are  greatly  needed,  and  will  be  gladly  received  and  acknow- 
ledged by  the  Hon.  Treasurer — V.  T.  York,  Farrington  Works, 
Shoe  Lane,  e.g.

have  been  issued,  of  which  one  ordinary  part  appeared  during

Browning  Union,  The,  North  London.  Founded  in  1889.  Objects —

Kingsland,  10  Tnornhill  Crescent,  Barnsbury,  n. 

Buddhist  Soeiety  of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland.  Founded  1907. 
Objects — To  promote  a  wider  knowledge  of  the  tenets  of

Catholic  Record  Soeiety,  The.  Founded  1904.  Objects — The 
objects  are  the  transcribing,  printing,  indexing  and  distributing 
to  its  members  the  Catholic  Registers  of  Baptisms,  Marriages, 
and  Deaths,  and  other  old  Records  of  the  Faith,  chiefly  personal 
and  genealogical,  since  the  Reformation  in  England  and  Wales. 
Management — The  affairs  of  the  Society  are  managed  by  a 
Council  consisting  of  twelve  members  and  four  honorary  officers. 
Subscription — The  subscription  is  one  guinea  per  annum,  which 
entitles  members  to  the  publications  for  the  year.  An  Annual 
Meeting  is  held  in  the  month  of  June  or  July,  of  which  at  least 
seven  days'  notice  is  sent  to  all  members.  The  Bursal  deals 
with  membership  and  subscriptions.  Membership — 372. 
Patron — The  Archbishop  of  Westminster.  President — The 
Duke  of  Norfolk,  K.G.,  P.C.  Hon.  Bursar — Leonard  C. 
Lindsay,  F.S.A.,  23  Belgrave  Road,  s.w.  Hon.  Sec. — Major 
F.  J.  A.  Skeet,  Hatfield  Regis  Grange,  Hatfield  Broad  Oak, 
Essex.

Chemical    Society,    Burlington    House,    w.     1841.     Incorporated 
1848.     Objects — The  general  advancement  of  chemistry  and 
of  those  branches  of  science  immediately  connected  with  it, 
by   the   reading,    discussion,   and   subsequent   publication   of 
original  communications  ;  and   the  formation  of  a  chemical 
library.     Membership — Fellows,    by   ballot,    and   subscription 
to  the  Obligation.     Meetings — Twice  a  month  from  November 
to  June,  except  January,  once,  and  Easter,   once,   or  as  the 
Council  sees  fit ;  and  the  annual  general  meeting.     The  Society 
celebrated  its   Jubilee   in    1891.     Publications — Transactions, 
Proceedings,  and  Annual  Reports  on  the  Progress  of  Chemistry, 
a  volume  of  Memorial  Lectures,  1 893-1 900.     President — Pro- 
fessor H.  B.  Dixon,  M.  A.,  F.R.S.  Treasurer — Alexander  Scott, 
D.Sc,  F.R.S.    Hon.  Secretaries — M.  O.  Forster,  D.Sc,  Ph.D., 
F.R.S.,  and  A.  W.  Crossley,  D.Sc,  Ph.D.,  F.R.S.     Foreign 
Secretary — Horace  T.  Brown,  LL.D.,  F.R.S.     Assistant  Secre- 
tary— Stanley  C.  Carr,  F.C.I.S.     Librarian — F.  W.  Clifford. 
Chemistry,  Institute  of,  of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland,  30  Bloomsbury 
Square,  w.c.     Founded  1877.     Incorporated  by  Royal  Charter, 
June  13,  1885.     Objects — To  promote  the  better  education  of 
persons  desirous  of  qualifying  themselves  to  be  professional 
consulting  analytical  and  technological  chemists  and  public 
analysts ;    to  examine  candidates,   and    to   grant  certificates 
of  competency  ;  and  to  elevate  the  profession  of  chemistry  by 
setting  up  a  high  standard  of  scientific  and   practical  pro- 
ficiency, and  by  requiring  on   the   part  of  the  members  the 
observance  of  strict  rules  in  regard  to  professional  conduct. 
Associateship — No  person  is  qualified  to  become  an  Associate 
of  the  Institute  until  he  has  attained  the  age  of  twenty-one 
years,  and  unless  he  has  passed  a  Preliminary  Examination  in 
subjects  of  general  education,  has  completed  a  course  of  at 
least  three  years'  study  in  Theoretical  and   Practical  Chem- 
istry,  Physics,   Mathematics,   and  one  other    subject    to    be 
chosen  from  the  list  given  in  the  Regulations,  and  has  passed 
the  Intermediate  and  Final  Examinations  of  the  Institute. 
Fellowship — In  order  to  become  eligible  for  the  Fellowship, 
an  Associate  is  required  to  have  been  continuously  engaged 
for  a  further  term  of  three  years  in  the  study  and  practice 
of  chemistry  in  a  manner  satisfactory  to  the  Council.    Students 
— Candidates  applying  for  registration  as  Students  must  pass 
an  approved  preliminary  examination,  and  must  be  not  less

Child-Study  Soeiety.  1894.  Objects — To  interest  parents,  teachers,

Cowper  Society.  Founded  25  th  April,  1900,  the  centenary  of

1004  at  Westminster  School ;  in  1905  at  East  Dereham  ;  in 
1 906  at  Berkhamsted  ;  in  1907  at  Olney  ;  in  1908  at  Edmonton  ; 
in  1909  at  Cambridge;  in  19 10  at  Lincoln's  Inn  (London). 
Objects — To  aid  in  the  publication  of  unpublished  manuscripts

Cymmrodorion,  The  Honourable  Society  of,  64  Chancery  Lane, 
w.c.  Founded  1751.  Object — To  encourage  literature,  history,

Dante  Soeiety,  38  Conduit  Street,  w.  Patroness— H.R.H.  Princess 
Christian.  Honorary  Secretary — Luigi  Ricci.  Object — The 
study  of  Dante's  works  and  of  Italian  literature.  The  Dante 
Society  was  founded  in  1881  by  Signor  Luigi  Ricci,  its  first 
members  being  students  of  his  Italian  classes  at  the  City  of 
London  College.  It  now  includes  not  only  all  students  of 
Dante,  but  also  all  friends  of  Italy.  At  some  of  the  monthly 
meetings  over  500  members  and  their  friends  are  assembled. 
Conduit  Street  proving  too  small  for  such  large  audiences,  the 
lectures  are  often  given  at  the  Pfeiffer  Hall  in  Harley  Street. 
The  Dante  Library  is  now  open  at  38  Conduit  Street,  w. 
Entrance  Fee — Two  guineas.  Subscription — One  guinea  a  year. 
Life  Membership — Ten  guineas. 

Designers,  The  Soeiety  of.  Founded,  1896.  Hon.  Secretary  and 
Treasurer — J.  Scarratt  Rigby,  153  Cheapside,  e.c.  Objects — 
To  advance  the  arts  of  design,  and  to  safeguard  the  interests 
of  the  profession  of  Design  for  Applied  Art.  Membership 
is  open  to  professional  designers  for  Applied  Art. 

Devon  and  Cornwall  Reoord  Soeiety.  Objects — The  transcription 
and  printing  of  records  concerning  the  counties  of  Devon  and 
Cornwall.  President — The  Earl  of  Mount  Edgcumbe, 
G.C.V.O.  Chairman — The  Rev.  Sub-Dean  Granville.  Hon.

Lloyd's  Bank,  Exeter. 

Dlekens  Fellowship,  The.  Founded  1902,  to  encourage  good  feeling 
on  Dickensian  principles  among  the  members,  and  to  take  such 
measures  as  may  be  expedient  to  remedy  or  ameliorate  those 
social  evils  which  would  have  appealed  to  the  heart  of  Charles 
Dickens.  There  are  over  fourteen  thousand  members,  who  pay 
a  minimum  subscription  of  one  shilling  a  year.  Branches  are 
established  in  chief  towns  of  the  United  Kingdom,  America, 
and  the  Colonies.  The  Fellowship  has  its  own  organ — a 
monthly  magazine,  entitled  The  Dickensian,  edited  by  B.  W. 
Matz.     President — Mr.  Henry  F.  Dickens,  K.C.     Hon.  Gen.

Dorset  Natural  History  and  Antiquarian  Field  Club.  1875.  Object — 
To  promote  and  encourage  an  interest  in  the  study  of  the 
physical  sciences  generally  and  archaeology,  especially  the 
natural  history  of  the  county  of  Dorset  and  its  antiquities, 
prehistoric  records,  and  ethnology.  Its  influence  is  used  to 
prevent,  as  far  as  possible,  the  extirpation  of  rare  plants  and 
animals,  and  to  promote  the  preservation  of  the  antiquities 
of  the  county,  it  aims  at  effecting  its  objects  by  a  series  of 
summer  meetings  held  in  different  localities  each  year,  and  by 
a  series  of  winter  meetings  held  at  the  Dorset  County  Museum. 
An  illustrated  volume  of  the  Club's  Proceedings  is  issued  annually. 
Membership — 400.  Subscription — 10s.  a  year.  There  is  also 
an  entrance-fee  of  10s.  Hon.  Secretary — Rev.  Herbert 
Pentin,  M.A.  Milton  Abbey  Vicarage,  Dorset. 

Dorset  Reeords.  1894.  Object — The  publication  of  records  from 
the  Public  Record  Office,  parish  registers,  calendars,  &c, 
relating  to  the  county  of  Dorset.  Yearly  Subscription — 10s.  6d. 
Editor — E.  A.  Fry,  124  Chancery  Lane,  w.c. 

Dublin  Society,  Royal,  Leinster  House,  Dublin.  173 1.  Incorpor- 
ated 1749.  Objects — The  advancement  of  agriculture  and 
other  branches  of  industry,  and  of  science  and  art.  It  also 
carries  on  much  scientific  work  ;  lectures  on  various  topics  are 
delivered  annually  in  the  theatre  ;  and  there  are  musical  recitals 
during  the  Session.  The  Society  takes  rank  as  the  oldest  of 
its  kind  in  the  kingdom.  At  the  Society's  agricultural  premises. 
Balls  Bridge,  Dublin,  shows  are  held  annually  in  the  months  of 
April,  August,  and  December.  Hon.  Secretaries — Prof.  John 
Joly,  D.Sc,  F.R.S.,  and  Capt.  J.  Lewis  Riall,  D.L.  Registrar 
— R.  J.  Moss,  F.I.C.  Librarian  and  Editor  of  scientific  publica- 
tions—A. H.  Foord,  Ph.D.,  F.G.S. 

Early  English  Text  Society.  1864.  Object—To  bring  the  mass  of 
Old  English  Literature  within  the  reach  of  the  ordinary  student, 
and  to  wipe  away  the  reproach  under  which  England  has  long 
rested,  of  having  felt  little  interest  in  the  monuments  of  her 
early  life  and  language.  The  Society  desires  to  print  in  its 
Original  Series  the  whole  of  our  unpnnted  MS.  literature,  and 
in  its  Extra  Series  to  reprint  in  careful  editions  all  that  is  most 
valuable  of  printed  MSS.  and  early  printed  books.  It  greatly 
needs  more  support.  The  Society  has  issued  to  its  subscribers 
239  Texts.  Subscription — One  guinea  per  annum,  and  one 
guinea  additional  for  the  Extra  Series,  payable  in  advance 
1st  January.  Honorary  Secretary — Wm.  A.  Dalziel,  Esq., 
67  Victoria  Road,  Finsbury  Park,  London,  n. 

Edinburgh,  Royal  Society  of.  1783.  Meetings— First  and  third 
Mondays  from  November  to  July.  Fellowship — Ordinary, 
unlimited  ;  Honorary,  limited  to  fifty-six,  of  whom  twenty 
may  be  British  subjects.    Publications — Transactions,  4to.,  and

Elizabethan  Literary  Society,  Tqynbee  Hail,  Commercial  Street,  e. 
1884.  Object — The  study  of  Elizabethan  literature.  The 
Society  meets  every  Wednesday.  On  the  first  Wednesday 
of  each  month  a  paper  is  read,  and  these  monthly  meetings  are 
open  free  to  the  public.  On  the  other  Wednesdays  of  the 
session,  Elizabethan  drama  and  prose  will  form  the  subject  of 
study.  Nominal  subscription.  President — Sidney  Lee.  Hon- 
orary Secretary — Otto  Sallmann,  4  Caithness  Road,  West 
Kensington,  w. 

Elizabethan  Stage  Society,  The,  90  College  Street,  Chelsea,  s.w.  In 
July,  1 90 1,  the  old  English  Morality  Play  Everyman  was  revived 
in  the  Master's  Court  of  the  Charterhouse,  London,  and  the 
performance  repeated  to  the  University  Extension  Students  in 
the  quadrangle  of  University  College,  Oxford.  Henry  the  Fifth, 
Shakespeare's  historical  play,  was  acted  in  the  Elizabethan 
manner,  in  November,  1 901,  in  the  Lecture  Theatre,  Burlington 
Gardens,  w.  Various  performances  have  since  been  success- 
fully given,  and  in  August,  1906,  Goldsmith's  Comedy,  The 
Good  Natur'd  Man,  was  revived  before  the  University  Extension 
Students  at  the  Theatre  Royal,  Cambridge. 

English  Association,  The.  Founded  1906.  Objects — (a)  To  afford 
opportunities  for  intercourse  and  co-operation  amongst  all  those 
interested  in  English  language  and  Literature ;  (b)  to  help  to 
maintain  the  correct  use  of  English,  spoken  and  written  : 
(c)  to  promote  the  due  recognition  of  English  as  an  essential 
element  in  the  national  education  ;  (d)  to  discuss  methods  of 
teaching  English,  and  the  correlation  of  School  and  University 
work  ;  (e)  to  encourage  and  facilitate  advanced  study  in  English 
Language  and  Literature.  Publications — Papers  and  leaflets. 
Constitution — Central  committee  elected  annually,  and  local 
branches.  Subscription — 5s.  Hon,  General  Secretary-*-Pro- 
fessor  F.  S.  Boas.  Secretary  and  Assistant  Treasurer — Miss 
Elizabeth  Lee,  8  Mornington  Avenue  Mansions,  w. 

English  Goethe  Society.  Objects — The  English  Goethe  Society  was 
founded  on  February  26,  1886,  for  the  purpose  of  promoting 
the  study  of  Goethe's  work  and  thought ;  and  in  1891  its  scope 
was  extended,  so  that,  while  always  keeping  Goethe  as  the 
central  figure,  the  attention  of  the  members  might  also  be 
directed  to  other  fields  of  German  literature,  art,  and  science. 
The  Society  pursues  its  aims  by  means  of  meetings,  discussions, 
the  publication  of  transactions,  and  in  any  other  mode  which 
may  from  time  to  time  seem  advisable  to  the  governing  body. 
Meetings — Five  each  year.  Publications — Transactions,  and 
other  original  papers.  Subscription — Half  a  guinea  per  annum. 
One  guinea  per  annum  includes  membership  of  the  German 
Goethe  Society  at  Weimar.  The  Society  is  flourishing,  but 
would  welcome  new  members.  Secretary — Dr.  Eugene  Oswald, 
129  Adelaide  Road,  n.w.

Entente  Cordiale,  L*  (Anglo-French  Association).  1897.  Objects-— 
To  develop  and  maintain  friendly  social  and  commercial 
relations  between  the  French  and  English  peoples  ;  to  encourage 
a  more  general  knowledge  of  the  two  languages ;  and  to  make 
the  two  nations  better  acquainted  with  the  respective  feelings 
and  opinions  of  each  other.  Publications — Pamphlets  con- 
taining information  as  to  parts  of  France  worth  visiting  by 
English  people,  or  on  subjects  of  public  interest  to  both  nations 
and  corrections  of  erroneous  statements  made  in  the  press 
Subscription  —  Not  less  than  half  a  guinea  yearly,  due 
1st  January.  Entrance  fee — 10s.  6d.  Chairman  of  Council — 
A.  Barton  Kent,  Esq.  Treasurer — H.  S.  A.  Foy,  Esq. 
Honorary  Secretary — W.  H.  Sands,  Esq.,  6  Fig-Tree  Court, 
Temple,  e.c. 

Entomological  Society  of  London,  11  Chandos  Street,  Cavendish 
Square,  w.  1833.  Incorporated  1885.  Object — The  improve- 
ment and  diffusion  of  entomological  science.  Membership — 
International,  500.  Publications — Transactions  (quarterly), 
and  Proceedings.  Secretaries — H.  Rowland-Brown,  M.A., 
and  Commander  J.  J.  Walker,  M.A.,  R.N.  Librarian — 
George  Bethell,  *F.R.Hist.S. 

Ethologleal  Society  lor  the  Systematic  Study  of  Human  Character. 
Founded  1 004.  The  obj  ec t  of  the  Society  is  the  study  of  Human 
Nature,  not  through  any  one  department  of  science,  but  taking 
from  all  its  different  branches  the  most  practical  and  useful  to 
arrive  at  a  knowledge  of  the  intellect  and  character  of  man,  and 
the  laws  which  govern  their  manifestation  ;  and  not  limited  to 
any  one  method  of  research,  but  collecting  all  material  that  is 
available,  and  applying  it  to  the  preservation  of  the  mental 
health  of  the  individual,  to  education,  moral  reform,  and  the 
solution  of  various  sociological  problems.  Extract  from  Rules 
— "  The  Society  shall  hold  a  series  of  '  Conversazioni  '  from 
November  till  July,  to  which  eminent  authorities  shall  be 
invited  to  deliver  lectures  on  the  relation  of  their  branch  of 
science  or  philosophy  to  Human  Nature.  The  Annual  Sub- 
scription shall  be  for  Members  one  guinea,  and  for  Associates 
half  a  guinea.  A  Journal  shall  be  published  containing  the 
proceedings  of  the  Society  and  original  contributions."  Presi- 
dent— Bernard  Hollander,  Esq.,  M.D.  Treasurer — 
Geo.  Flindell  Brady,  Jun.,  Esq.  Hon.  Secretary — Mr. 
Edgar  Gardner,  57  Wimpole  Street,  w. 

Eugenics  Education  Society.  Objects — To  set  forth  the  national 
importance  of  Eugenics  in  order  to  modify  public  opinion, 
and  to  create  a  sense  of  responsibility  in  the  respect  of  bringing 
all  matters  pertaining  to  human  parentage  under  the  domina- 
tion of  Eugenic  ideals.  (2)  To  spread  a  knowledge  of  the 
laws  of  heredity  so  far  as  they  are  surely  known,  and  so  far 
that  knowledge  might  affect  the  improvement  of  the  races 
(3)  To  further  Eugenic  teaching  at  home,  in  schools,  and  else 
where.  Subscription — Membership,  one  guinea  per  annum 
Associate    Membership,   5s.  per  annum.     Privileges — Member

Fabian  Society .  Founded  1883  for  the  study  of  Socialism  and  its 
propaganda  mainly  by  educational  methods.  Membership — 
2,500,  largely  in  London,  but  also  in  the  provinces,  is  confined 
to  Socialists,  but  associates  paying  10s.  and  subscribers  of  5  s. 
receive  publications,  and  can  attend  the  meetings.  These, 
usually  lectures,  are  held  fortnightly  at  Essex  Hall,  Essex 
Street,  Strand,  except  in  the  holiday  season.  The  lectures  and 
publications  are  chiefly  devoted  to  the  consideration  of  political 
and  social  problems,  with  a  view  to  the  reconstruction  of 
society  on  Socialistic  lines.  The  Society  has  a  circulating 
library  of  over  4,000  books  on  Political  and  Economic  subjects, 
which  are  supplied  in  boxes  to  Trade  Unions,  Co-operative 
Societies,  and  other  organizations  at  a  charge  of  10s.  per  year, 
and  the  books  are  also  lent  to  members  free  of  charge. 
It  publishes  Fabian  Tracts  usually  at  id.,  which  explains  its 
views  of  Socialism  and  its  proposals  for  social  and  political 
reform.  Amongst  these  is  included  a  6d.  bibliography.  What  to 
Read  on  Social  and  Economic  Subjects  (fourth  edition),  1901, 
a  supplement,  More  Books  to  Read  (1906),  id.,  and  a  Guide  to 
Books  for  Socialists  (1907),  id.  Its  organ  is  the  Fabian  News, 
devoted  to  reports  of  the  Society's  Meetings,  reviews  of  books, 
and  a  monthly  list  of  new  books.  Secretary — Edward  R. 
Pease.     Office — 3  Clement's  Inn,  Strand,  w.c. 

Folk-Lore  Society.  1878.  Object — To  collect  and  preserve  the 
relics  of  Folk-Lore.  Publications — The  quarterly  journal, 
Folk-Lore,  volumes  and  Transactions  :  free  to  Members.  An 
additional  volume  is  also  issued  free  to  members  every  year. 
Subscription — One  guinea  per  annum.  Secretary — F.  A.  Milne, 
22  Albemarle  Street,  w. 

Folk-Song  Soeiety. — This  Society  was  founded  in  1898  for  the  pur- 
pose of  collecting  and  publishing  folk-songs,  ballads  and  tunes. 
It  publishes  in  its  journal  such  contributions  of  traditional 
songs  as  may  be  chosen  by  a  committee  of  musical  experts, 
and  may  from  time-  to  time  hold  meetings  at  which  these  songs 
are  introduced,  and  form  the  subjects  of  performance,  lecture, 
and  discussion.  Eleven  numbers  of  the  journal,  containing 
several  hundreds  of  songs  and  tunes,  copiously  annotated  by 
experts,  never  before  printed,  have  appeared  (up  to  December, 
1907).  The  subscription  has  been  fixed  at  10s.  6d.  annually 
(payable  on  June  1  in  each  year),  on  payment  of  which  members 
will  be  entitled  to  receive  all  publications  for  the  current  year, 
and  to  attend  all  meetings,  &c,  organized  by  the  Society. 
President — The  Right  Hon.  Lord  Tennyson,  G.C.M.G.  Vice- 
Presidents — Sir  Alexander  C.  Mackenzie,  Mus.Doc, 
F.R.A.M.  ;  Sir  C.  Hubert  Parry,  Bart.,  Mus.Bac,  D.C.L.; 
Sir   Charles   Villiers   Stanford,   Mus,Doc,    D.C.L.    Hon,

Friends'  Historical  Society. — Founded  1903.  Objects — To  encourage 
the  study  of  the  history  of  the  Society  of  Friends  (Quakers), 
and  to  publish  historical  data  relating  to  Friends.  Member- 
ship— Members  of  the  Society  of  Friends  and  others  introduced 
by  members.  Subscription — Five  shillings  per  annum.  Publi- 
cations— Quarterly  Journal,  with  occasional  Supplements,  free 
to  members.  Editor — Norman  Penney,  F.S.A.,  F.R.Hist.S. 
Office,  Devonshire  House,  Bishopsgate,  e.c. 

Friends  of  Russian  Freedom,  Society  of.  Founded  1889.  Objects — 
To  further  the  cause  of  political  and  religious  liberty  in  Russia 
through  the  awakening  of  English  public  opinion  on  the  subject, 
and  to  give  material  help  to  the  victims  of  political  and  religious 
intolerance  in  Russia,  as  well  as  assistance  to  Russian  workmen 
in  their  just  struggles  for  the  right  of  combination  and  the 
improvement  of  the  conditions  of  their  life.  The  Society 
publishes  Free  Russia,  a  quarterly  journal ;  and  arranges 
lectures  to  treat  and  discuss  questions  affecting  Russian  liberty. 
Minimum  Annual  Subscription — 5  s.  Funds  are  urgently 
needed  ;  and  full  particulars  of  the  aims  of  the  Society  and 
the  important  work  it  has  already  done  may  be  obtained  on 
application  to  Mr.  J.  Frederick  Green,  40  Outer  Temple, 
Strand,  w.c. 

Froebel  Society  of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland,  4  Bloomsbury  Square, 
w.c.  1874.  Incorporated  1891.  Objects — To  promote  co- 
operation among  those  engaged  in  Kindergarten  work  ;  to 
spread  the  knowledge  and  practice  of  the  system ;  and  to  main- 
tain a  high  standard  of  efficiency  among  Kindergarten  and 
Elementary  teachers.  The  work  of  the  Society  is  carried  on 
chiefly  by  lectures,  classes  for  teachers,  agency  for  teachers  and 
governesses,  properly  catalogued  lending  library,  Ac.  The  organ 
of  the  Society  is  the  monthly  magazine,  Child  Life.  For  further 
particulars  apply  to  the  Secretary,  Miss  Temple  Ormb,  LL.D.

Genealogical  and  Biographical  Society,  The,  38  Conduit  Street,  w. 
1902.  Objects — The  discovery,  publication,  and  preservation 
of  whatever  may  relate  to  genealogy,  biography,  and  local 
history.  To  carry  out  the  object  for  which  the  Society  is 
founded  there  will  be  a  periodical  publication  ;  meetings  of  the 
Society,  at  which  addresses  of  historical  interest  will  be

Geological  Field  Glass  (London).  Founded  1886.  Object — To 
impart  practical  knowledge  of  the  physical  geography  and 
geology  of  the  London  district  by  teaching  in  quarries  and 
by  cliffs,  given  by  the  same  Professor.  Excursions  are  made 
on  Saturday  afternoons,  between  the  end  of  April  and  middle 
of  July ;  are  generally  ten  in  number,  the  fee  for  which  is  one 
guinea.  Application  is  made  to  the  Secretary,  J.  W.  Jarvis, 
F.G.S.,  St.  Mark's  College,  Chelsea.  The  Director,  Professor 
Seeley,  King's  College,  w.c,  will  issue  his  report  on  She 
London  Excursions  in  the  autumn.

Glasgow  Athensum  (incorporated),  St.  George's  Place,  Glasgow,

Historical  Association. — Founded  1906.  Objects — (a)  The  collec- 
tion of  information  as  to  existing  systems  of  historical  teaching 
at  home  and  abroad,  by  getting  together  printed  books, 
pamphlets,  and  other  materials,  and  by  correspondence. 
(b)  The  distribution  of  information  amongst  the  members  of 
the  Association  as  to  methods  of  teaching  and  aids  to  teach- 
ing— viz.,  maps,  illustrations,  text-books,  etc.  (c)  The  en- 
couragement of  local  centres  for  the  discussion  of  questions 
relative  to  the  study  and  the  teaching  of  history,  (d)  The 
representation  of  the  needs  and  interests  of  the  study  of  history, 
and  of  the  opinion  of  its  teachers,  to  governing  bodies,  Govern- 
ment departments,  and  other  authorities  having  control  over 
education,  (e)  Co-operation  for  common  objects  with  the 
English  Association,  the  Geographical  Association,  the  Modern 
Language  Association,  and  the  Classical  Association.  All 
persons  engaged  or  interested  in  history  are  eligible  for  member- 
ship. The  annual  subscription,  payable  on  July  I,  is  five 
shillings.  An  annual  general  meeting  is  held  on  some  day 
during  the  first  fortnight  in  January.  President — T.  F.  Tour, 
M.A.,  Professor  of  Mediaeval  and  Modern  History,  Manchester. 
Univ.  Secretary — Miss  M.  B.  Curran,  6  South  Square,  Gray's 
Inn,  w.c.  Treasurer — J.  E.  Morris,  D.Litt.,  Oaklands, 
Bedford.

Imperial   Institute,  South   Kensington.     1887.    The  Institute  in- 
cludes collections  of  economic  products  and  manufactures  illus-

Colonies ;    the    Scientific    and    Technical    Department,    with 
extensive  laboratories  for  the  investigation  of  new  or  little- 
known   natural   products   with   a  view  to   their  commercial 
utilization  ;  and  a  Reference  Library  and  Reading  Room  con- 
taining a  large  collection  of  Indian  and  Colonial  works  of  refer- 
ence, and  the  principal  newspapers  and  official  publications  of

India  Soelety.     Founded  April,  1910.     Objects — This  Society  is  non- 
political,  and  aims  at  tne  study  and  appreciation  of  the  aesthetic 
aspects  of  Indian  culture,  as  displayed  in  architecture,  sculpture, 
painting,  literature;  and  music.     It  hopes  to  co-operate  with 
all  those  who  have  it  as  their  aim  to  keep  alive  the  traditional 
arts  and  handicrafts  of  India,  and  to  assist  in  the  development 
of  Indian  art  education  on  indigenous  and  traditional  lines. 
Works  showing  the  best  examples  of  Indian  art,  both  ancient 
and  modern,  are  issued  either  free  or  at  a  low  price  to  members. 
Subscription  — One  guinea  per  annum  ;  or  twelve  guineas  for 
life   membership.     Hon.    Secretary — Mr.    T.    W.    Rolleston, 
Ardeevin,  Christchurch  Road,  Hampstead,  n.w. 
Indian  Institute,  Broad  Street,  Oxford.     Founded  by  Sir  Monier 
Williams,    1883.     Objects — To    arouse    interest    in    Oriental 
literature  and  in  India  generally.     Particularly  to  draw  together 
the  successful  candidates  in  the  I.C.S.  open  competition,   to 
teach  Indian  languages,  and  to  hold  lectures  on  Indian  subjects. 
Library  open  from  10  a.m.  to  1  p.m.  during  term  and  vacation, 
and  Museum  from  10  a.m.  to  6  p.m.  during  Easter  and  Trinity 
terms  ;  at  other  times  10  a.m.  to  4  p.m.     The  Institute  is  closed 
from  August  16  to  September  14,  and  from  Christmas  Eve  to 
January  2,  and  on  Good  Friday  and  the  following  Saturday  and 
Monday.     Keeper — The  Boden  Professor  of  Sanskrit,  A.   A. 
Macdonell,    M.A.     Assistant    Keeper    and    Librarian,    Don 

M.  DE  Z.  WlCKREMASINGHE. 

International  Exchange  Service  of  the  Smithsonian  Institution. 
Since  its  foundation  in  1846  the  Smithsonian  Institution  has 
conducted  a  system  of  the  exchange  of  literary  and  scientific 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  ii.]  SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS  887 

publications  voluntarily  presented  by  institutions  or  indi- 
viduals in  the  United  States  to  correspondents  throughout  the 
world,  and  reciprocally. 

These  exchanges  are  in  no  sense  of  a  commercial  nature,  for 
no  publications  for  sale  are  allowed  transmission.  More  than 
one-half  of  the  exchanges  consist  of  Government  documents, 
while  the  remainder  are  publications  exchanged  between  learned 
societies  and  savants  throughout  the  world.  The  International 
Exchange  Service  has  salaried  resident  agents  in  London, 
Leipzig,  and  Budapest,  and  a  number  of  agents  in  various  parts 
of  the  world  lend  their  services  gratuitously.  The  correspon- 
dents who  more  or  less  regularly  exchange  publications  through 
the  Smithsonian  Institution  now  aggregate  60,123,  of  which 
1 1,783  are  in  the  United  States  and  the  rest  in  foreign  lands,  ex- 
tending even  to  the  remotest  corners  of  the  world. 

Further  particulars  with  reference  to  the  above  can  be  ob- 
tained on  application  to  Messrs.  W.  Wesley  &  Son,  28  Essex 
Street,  Strand,  w.c,  who  are  the  London  agents  for  the 
Smithsonian  Institution.     (See  p.  829.) 

International  Musical  Society.  1899.  Object — To  unite  in  one  body 
those  who  prosecute  musical  research,  in  whatever  country,  by 
means  of  {a)  an  organized  system  of  local  meetings  and  general 
conferences,  and  (6)  a  published  monthly  journal  (about  600  pp. 
yearly)  and  quarterly  magazine  (about  750  pp.  yearly).  The 
language  of  publications  is  alternatively  German,  English, 
French,  or  Italian  ;  but  on  an  average  one-fourth  of  space  is 
given  to  English,  and  the  same  to  French.  The  Society  has 
thirty-five  organized  branches  in  America,  Austria,  Belgium, 
Denmark,  Finland,  France,  Germany,  Great  Britain  and 
Ireland,  Holland,  Italy,  Russia,  Spain,  Sweden,  and  Switzer- 
land. Membership — By  paying  a  subscription  (in  return  for 
which  the  journal  and  magazine  are  received  post  free)  of  £1  ; 
or,  if  in  combination  with,  and  addition  to,  the  (British) 
Musical  Association,  £1  us.  Publications — As  above;  also 
certain  book  literature  at  reduced  rates.  President  —  (for 
Great  Britain  and  Ireland)  Sir  Alexander  Mackenzie, 
Principal  of  the  Royal  Academy  of  Music.  Hon.  Secretaries — 
Dr.  Ch.  Maclean,  '61  Drayton  Gardens,  South  Kensington, 
s.w.  ;  and  Dr.  W.  G.  McN aught,  Annandale,  Woodside  Park, 
North  Finchley,  n. 

Irish  Academy,  Royal,  Dublin.  Incorporated  1786.  Its  objects  are 
to  "  promote  the  study  of  science,  polite  literature,  and  antiqui- 
ties." Its  library  includes  many  invaluable  Irish  MSS.,  the 
Ordnance  Survey  papers,  &c.  Its  important  collection  of 
Irish  Antiquities  is  now  exhibited  in  the  National  Museum, 
Dublin,  under  the  Science  and  Art  Department.  Secretary — 
Prof.  John  A.  McClelland,  M.A.,  D.Sc,  19  Dawson  Street, 
Dublin.

Irish  Literary  Society,  20  Hanover  Square,  w.  1892.  Objects — To 
afford  a  centre  of  social  and  literary  intercourse  for  persons  of 
Irish  nationality,  and  to  promote  the  study  of  the  Irish  language, 
Irish  history,  literature,  music,  and  art.  Membership — 
Members  (town  and  country)  of  Irish  nationality ;  Associates 
of  any  other  nationality.  Subscription — Town  members,  £1  is. ; 
country  members,  12s.  6d.  Lectures — Monthly.  The  History 
and  "  Original  Nights  "  have  been  successfully  continued,  and 
the  classes  for  the  study  of  Irish  language  and  history  are  well 
attended.  The  number  of  books  on  the  library  shelves  is  over 
700.    Hon.  Secretary — Mr.  Alfred  Percival  Graves. 

Irish  Texts  Society.  1898.  Objects — The  publication  of  Irish  texts, 
with  translation  and  notes.  The  volume  for  190 1  was  Keating's 
History  of  Ireland,  Part  I.,  edited  by  Mr.  David  Comyn.  The 
volume  for  1902  was  a  romantic  tale  entitled  Caithreim 
Conghail  Clairighnigh,  edited  by  P.  M.  MacSweeney,  M.A.,  from 
a  seventeenth  -  century  MS.  Five  volumes  have  now  been 
issued  by  the  Society.  The  Society's  Irish-English  Dictionary, 
compiled  and  edited  by  Rev.  P.  S.  Dinneen,  M.A.,  is  now  ready, 
price  7s.  6d.  Subscription — 7s.  6d.  per  annum  ;  American  sub- 
scribers, $2.  Honorary  Secretary — Miss  Hull,  20  Hanover 
Square,  w. 

Japan  Society,  20  Hanover  Square,  w.  1892.  Objects — The  encour 
agement  of  the  study  of  the  Japanese  language,  literature, 
history,  folk-lore,  and  of  all  Japanese  matters.  Membership — 
Ordinary  Members,  Corresponding  Members,  and  Honorary 
Members.  Publications  —  Transactions  and  Proceedings,  free 
to  Members.  Exhibitions — Periodically.  Meetings — Monthly 
from  November  to  June.  Many  of  the  valuable  papers  read 
before  the  Society  have  appeared,  with  illustrations,  in  the 
Transactions,  of  which  several  volumes  have  recently  been 
issued.  Honorary  Secretaries — J.  P.  Reid  and  K.  Nojima. 
Assistant  Secretary — A.  E.  Brice. 

Jewish  Historical  Society  of  England.  Founded  1893  as  tne  out- 
come of  the  Anglo-Jewish  Historical  Exhibition,  Royal  Albert 
Hall.  Objects— The  study  of  Anglo- Jewish  History,  and  the

of  Transactions  have  been  issued.  Lectures  on  Jewish  history 
are  promoted,  and  the«Society  has  published  several  volumes

Jewish  Plea  Rolls  of  the  Exchequer.  Treasurer — Frank  Haes, 
28  Bassett  Road,  w.

Jewish  Literary  Societies,  Union  of.  1902.  Objects — The  diffusion 
of  a  knowledge  of  Jewish  literature,  history,  and  sociology  ;  the 
co-ordination  of  the  work  of  Jewish  literary  societies,  and 
^formation  of  new  societies  ;  the  promotion  of  popular  Jewish 
publications,  and  the  establishment  of  a  circulating  library 
containing  works  on  Jewish  History  and  Literature.  A  large 
number  of  Jewish  literary  and  allied  societies  in  Great 
Britain  are  associated  in  the  Union,  which  publishes  in  its 
Annual  Report  selected  papers  of  great  interest  and  value  to 
the  student  of  Jewish  history  and  thought.  President — Elkan 
N.  Adlbr.     Secretary — Miss  Arj*,  38  Fitzroy  Square,  w. 

Johnson  Society.  Founded  at  Lichfield,  September  17,  1 910,  the 
anniversary  of  Dr.  Johnson's  birth.  Objects — (a)  The  spread 
of  knowledge  of  the  life,  works,  character,  and  times  of  Samuel 
Johnson,  LL.D. ;  (b)  the  preservation  of  houses,  memorials, 
and  places  associated  with  Dr.  Johnson  or  any  of  his  friends 
or  contemporaries  ;  (c)  the  acquisition  for  exhibition  in  the 
Johnson  birthplace,  Lichfield,  by  purchase  or  loan,  of  books, 
letters,  manuscripts,  furniture,  or  other  objects  relating  to 
Dr.  Johnson,  his  contemporaries,  friends,  and  associates,  or 
otherwise  illustrative  of  his  life  and  times  ;  (d)  the  publication 
of  transactions  consisting  of  literary  matter  and  essays  relating 
to  Dr.  Johnson  and  his  times,  of  which  all  members  shall  be 
entitled  to  receive  a  copy ;  (e)  the  visiting  from  time  to  time 
of  places  connected  with  Dr.  Johnson  and  his  contemporaries  ; 
(/)  the  association  and  intercommunication  of  all  persons  in 
the  English-speaking  world  interested  in  Dr.  Johnson.  It  is 
hoped  to  establish  a  link  between  Johnsonians  scattered  all 
over  the  world. 

Journalists,  Institute  of,  Hall  of  the  Institute,  Tudor  Street,  e.c. 
Founded  in  1889,  by  conversion  of  the  National  Association  of 
Journalists  (1884).  Incorporated  1890.  Objects — The  pro- 
motion of  whatever  may  tend  to  the  elevation  of  the  status,  and 
the  improvement  of  the  qualifications  of  all  members  of  the 
journalistic  profession  ;  ascertainment  and,  where  necessary, 
amendment  of  the  law  and  practice  affecting  the  discharge  by 
journalists  of  their  professional  duties;  the  promotion  by  all 
reasonable  means  of  the  interests  of  journalists  and  journalism, 
&c.  Membership — International ;  at  the  date  of  its  con- 
version into  the  Institute,  about  1,200 ;  at  present,  2,500. 
Council  Meetings  and  Annual  Conference — Movable,  and  held 
in  the  chief  towns  throughout  the  country.  President,  191 1 — 
John  Mitchell,  J. P.,  F.J.I.  Secretary — Herbert  Cornish, 
F.J.I. 

Kyrle  Society.  This  Society  owes  its  origin  to  a  letter  written  in 
1 876  by  Miss  Miranda  Hill,  calling  attention  to  the  dull,  common- 
place lives  of  the  poor,  and  suggesting  that  means  taken  to 
enliven  and  beautify  those  lives  would  be  labour  well  spent. 
Accordingly,  in  1877,  a  society  was  started  with  this  object, 
and  the  name  "  Kyrle  "  was  chosen,  the  idea  being  taken  from 


Digitized  by  CjOOQIC 


I 


890  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

Pope's  lines  eulogizing  John  Kyrle,  the  "  Man  of  Ross." 
Objects — (a)  To  decorate  workmen's  clubs,  hospital  wards,  parish 
rooms,  or  any  room  used  for  social  gatherings,  without  dis- 
tinction of  creed,  (b)  To  secure  and  assist  in  securing  any  open 
spaces  in  or  near  the  metropolis,  and  to  prevent  spaces  being 
illegally  built  upon  ;  to  render  available  as  public  gardens 
disused  burial-grounds  and  other  waste  spaces,  and  to  provide 
seats,  plants,  &c,  for  them,  (c)  To  distribute  cut  flowers, 
plants,  ferns,  and  bulbs  to  hospitals,  workhouses,  and  other 
institutions,  and  amongst  the  homes  of  the  poor,  (d)  To 
organize  a  voluntary  choir  of  singers  to  perform  oratorios  for 
the  poor,  (e)  To  distribute  books,  magazines,  and  periodicals 
to  hospitals,  infirmaries,  workhouses,  clubs,  and  libraries  for 
the  benefit  of  the  poor.  President — H.R.H.  Princess  Louise, 
Duchess  of  Argyll.  Chairman — Lord  Monkswbll.  Hon. 
Secretary — Miss  Lilian  James.     Office — 2  Manchester  Street,  w. 

Lancashire  and  Cheshire  Antiquarian  Society.  1883.  Objects— To 
examine,  preserve,  and  illustrate  ancient  monuments  and 
records,  and  to  promote  the  study  of  history,  literature,  arts, 
customs,  and  traditions,  with  particular  reference  to  the 
antiquities  of  Lancashire  and  Cheshire.  Publications — Trans- 
actions, issued  annually.  Subscription — Half  a  guinea  annually. 
Entrance  fee,  half  a  guinea.  Secretary — George  C.  Yates, 
F.S.A.,  Urmston,  Manchester. 

Lancashire  and  Cheshire,  Historic  Society  of.  1848.  Objects— To 
collect,  arrange,  preserve,  and  publish  information  relating  to 
the  two  counties.  The  subscription  is  one  guinea  per  annum. 
Publications — Documents,  &c.  Meetings — From  October  to 
April.  Honorary  Secretary — Wm.  Fergusson  Irvine,  M.A., 
F.S.A.,  34  Castle  Street,  Liverpool. 

Lancashire  and  Cheshire,  Record  Society  of.  1878.  Object— The 
transcription  and  publication  of  original  documents  relating  to 
these  counties.  The  subscription  is  £1  is.  per  annum.  Publi- 
cations— Records,  &c,  free  to  Members.  Honorary  Secretary — 
Wm.  Fergusson  Irvine,  M.A.,  F.S.A.,  56  Park  Road  South, 
Birkenhead. 

Library  Assistants'  Association.    1895.    Objects — To  promote  the 

grofessional,  educational,  and  social  interests  of  its  members, 
y  discussions,  by  meetings  of  a  social  character,  and  in  such 
other  ways  as  may  be  suggested  from  time  to  time.  Confined 
to  persons,  other  than  chief  librarians,  actually  engaged  in  the 
administration  of  libraries  in  the  United  Kingdom  and  the 
Colonies.  Meetings — At  various  libraries  and  other  public 
institutions  monthly  from  October  to  May ;  Annual  Meeting 
and  Conference  in  June,  and  excursions  during  the  summer 
months.  Has  branches  in  Yorkshire,  the  North-Eastern  and 
Midland  Counties,  Ireland,  and  South  Wales.  Publications — 
The  Library  Assistant,  monthly,  free  to  members  and  hon. 
members  ;  and  Annual  Report.  President — W.  C.  Berwick 
Sayers.    Hon.  Treasurer — W.  Geo.  Chambers,  Public  Library,
