Library  Association,  24,  Whitcomb  Street,  London,  w.c.  October  5, 
1877.  (Incorporated  by  Royal  Charter,  1898.)  Objects — To 
unite  all  persons  engaged  or  interested  in  library  work,  for  the 
purpose  of  securing  the  best  administration  of  libraries ;  to 
endeavour  to  obtain  better  legislation  for  librarjps ;  to  aid  and 
encourage  the  establishment  of  new  libraries ;  and  to  encourage 
bibliographical  study  and  research.  Meetings — Monthly  from 
October  to  June  ;  and  an  Annual  General.  Between  the  years 
1877-1907  General  Meetings  have  been  held  in  many  of  the 
principal  towns  in  the  United  Kingdom ;  and  on  one  occasion, 
in  1 892,  in  Paris.  Publications — The  Library  A  ssociation  Record, 
monthly,  free  to  Fellows  and  Members ;  and  a  Year-Book.  Presi- 
dent— Dr.  F.  G.  Kenyon,  M.A.,  D.Litt.,  Principal  Librarian, 
British  Museum.  Hon,  Treasurer — Henry  R.  Tedder.  Hon. 
Secretary — L.  Stanley  J  ast.   A  ssistant  Secretary — Mrs.  Reilly. 

Lincoln  Record  Society.  Founded,  Oct.,  1910.  Objects — To  print 
inedited  documents  relating  to  the  diocese  and  county  of 
Lincoln.  Subscription — One  guinea  per  annum,  which  entitles 
members  to  copies  of  the  publications.  President — The  Bishop 
of  Lincoln.  Editor — Canon  Foster.  Secretary — Canon  Bell, 
Newark. 

Linguists,  Institute  of.  Founded  19 10.  Objects — To  promote  the 
knowledge  and  use  of  modern  languages.  The  Institute  con- 
sists of  (a)  Honorary  Members  nominated  by  the  Council  ; 
(b)  Associate  Members  approved  by  the  Council  after  satisfying 
the  examinations  or  other  tests  which  may  be  imposed. 
Applicants  for  admission  to  membership  in  Class  (a)  shall  be 
elected  at  the  Council's  discretion  without  examination.  It 
is  hoped  that  merchants,  bankers,  educationalists,  and  all 
interested  in  the  advancement  of  international  commerce, 
will  enrol  themselves  in  this  class.  The  object  of  the  examina- 
tions will  be  to  test  a  practical  knowledge  of  the  spoken 
language,  the  ability  of  the  candidate  to  read  at  sight  and 
understand  the  language  offered,  whether  written  or  spoken, 
and  to  express  himself  or  herself  both  orally  and  in  writing. 
Candidates  for  the  Associateship  examinations  shall  be 
examined  in  any  two  or  more  languages,  one  being  English. 
The  literary  examination  is  framed  to  test  a  knowledge  of 
the  current  language  and  literature,  and  will  not  include 
philology  or  (directly)  grammar.  Business  letters,  to  be 
written  and  answered,  form  an  integral  part  of  the  commercial 
section  examination.  In  the  list  of  Members,  Associates  shall 
be  distinguished  by  the  letters  A.I.L.  (Litt.)  or  A. IX.  (Com.), 
according  to  the  examination  passed.  The  examinations  of 
the  Institute  will  in  no  way  seek  to  rival  or  supersede  the 
examinations  of  the  Universities  or  other  learned  bodies. 
Secretary — George  Blount,  120  London  Wall,  London,  ex. 

Llnnean? Society  of  London,  Burlington  House,  w.  This  Society 
was^founded  in  February  1788  by  Sir  James  Edward  Smith, 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


I 


892  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

who,  on  the  recommendation  of  Sir  Joseph  Banks,  purchased 
the  library,  manuscripts,  and  famous  natural  history  collections 
of  Linnaeus  from  his  son's  executrix,  by  whom  they  were  offered 
for  sale.  In  March,  1802,  the  Society  was  incorporated  by  Royal 
Charter,  and  a  supplemental  charter  was  granted  in  1904 
for  the  admission  of  women.  The  meetings  were  held  first  at 
the  house  of  the  founder,  afterwards  in  Soho  Square,  and  even- 
tually at  Burlington  House,  whither  the  library  and  collections 
(with  the  exception  of  the  Linnean  minerals,  which  were  sold 
by  Sir  J.  E.  Smith)  were  removed  in  1857,  and  where  they  are 
still  carefully  preserved.  The  centenary  anniversary  of  the 
Society  was  celebrated  on  the  24th  May,  1888,  when  a  Linnean 
gold  medal  was  founded  in  commemoration  of  the  event ;  this  is 
annually  awarded  at  the  anniversary  meeting,  alternately  to  a 
botanist  and  to  a  zoologist.  The  Society,  under  the  patronage 
of  His  Majesty  the  King,  is  governed  by  a  President  and  Council, 
with  Treasurer  and  three  Secretaries,  annually  elected  at  the 
anniversary  meeting.  Fortnightly  evening  meetings  are  held 
throughout  the  session  from  November  to  June,  and  the 
botanical  and  zoological  papers  read  at  these  meetings  are 
subsequently  published  in  the  shape  of  quarto  Transactions  and 
octavo  Journals  extensively  illustrated.  General  Secretary — 
B.  Daydon  Jackson,  Ph.D. 

Literary  Fund,  Royal,  40  Denison  House,  296  Vauxhall  Bridge  Road, 
s.w.  1790.  Object — The  assistance  of  necessitous  authors, 
their  widows  and  orphans,  or  mothers  or  sisters.  The  income 
of  this  fund  is  about  £3,000.  The  number  of  grants  to  authors 
and  relatives  of  authors  since  the  foundation  of  the  fund  in 
1790  totals  4,840,  to  the  amount  of  £149,862.  The  average 
number  of  grants  m  recent  years  has  been  about  thirty-five, 
the  amount  disbursed  in  each  year  being  on  an  average  about 
£2,700.  The  fund  holds  an  anniversary  festival  each  year. 
Subscription — One  guinea  per  annum,  or  Life  Donation  of  ten 
guineas  paid  in  one  sum.  Secretary — A.  Llewelyn  Roberts, 
B.A. 

Literature,  Royal  Soeiety  of,  of  the  United  Kingdom,  20  Hanover

London  Institution,  Finsbury?  Circus,  e.c.  An  Association  of  950 
Proprietors  (incorporated  1807),  for  the  advancement  of 
literature  and  diffusion  of  useful  knowledge.  Library — A 
circulating  library,  a  large  news  room,  and  a  reference  library 
containing  nearly  100,000  volumes.  Evening  Lectures  are  given 
on  Mondays  and  Thursdays  between  November  and  March. 

London  School  of  Economics  and  Political  Seienee  (University  of 
London).  President — The  Right  Hon.  Lord  Rothschild. 
Chairman — Sidney  Webb.  Director — The  Hon.  W.  Pember- 
Reeves.  The  London  School  of  Economics  and  Political 
Science  was  founded  in  1895,  with  the  intention  of  providing 
a  system  of  higher  education  which  should  stand  in  the  same 
relation  to  the  life  and  calling  of  the  public  administrator  and 
the  merchant  as  do  the  medical  schools  of  the  Universities  to 
those  of  the  doctor.  Under  the  new  constitution  of  the  University 
of  London,  a  "  Faculty  of  Economics  and  Political  Science  (in- 
cluding Commerce  and  Industry)  "  was  established  in  1900,  and 
the  School  of  Economics  was  constituted  by  Act  of  Parliament  a 
"  School  of  the  University"  in  that  Faculty.  Students  of  the 
School  who  have  matriculated  at  the  University  of  London  are 
registered  as  internal  students  of  the  University,  and  may  pro- 
ceed to  degrees  in  Science  (B.Sc.  and  D.Sc.)  in  the  Faculty  of 
Economics.  All  the  lectures  and  classes  at  the  School  are,  how- 
ever, open  without  any  distinction  whatever  of  age,  or  sex,  or 
nationality  to  those  who  have  not  matriculated  and  do  not 
wish  to  pursue  a  full  University  course.  Special  instruction 
is  arranged  for  those  engaged  in  commerce,  banking,  insurance, 
accounting  and  railway  administration,  for  civil  and  municipal 
officials,  and  for  librarians.  Much  is  done  by  the  School  for 
the  encouragement  of  research  by  the  provision  of  courses  of 
lectures  on  methods  of  investigation,  by  the  award  of  student- 
ships varying  in  value  from  £25  to  ^200,  and  by  the  co-ordina- 
tion of  different  agencies  for  providing  the  student  engaged  in 
research  with  the  help  he  needs.  The  list  of  the  School  publica- 
tions bears  witness  to  results  achieved  in  this  direction.  The 
success  of  the  School  has  from  the  first  proved  steady  and  con- 
tinuous. Twice  it  has  had  to  remove  to  more  commodious 
premises  from  its  original  home  at  9  John  Street,  Adelphi,  and 
already  considerable  pressure  is  being  felt  upon  the  space  pro- 
vided in  the  new  building  erected  for  it  in  1902.  Since  the 
School  was  opened  in  1895  more  than  7,200  students  have  been 
entered  on  its  books,  of  whom  about  10  per  cent,  have  been 
foreigners. 

London  Topographical  Society.  The  London  Topographical  Society 
was  founded  for  the  publication  of  material  illustrating  the 
history  and  topography  of  the  City. and  County  of  London  from 
the  earliest  times  to  the  present  day.  This  object  is  effected 
by — (a)  The  reproduction  of  maps,  views,  and  plans  of  the 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


i 


894  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  11. 

capital  as  a  whole,  and  of  localities  within  its  area  at  different 
periods,  (b)  The  publication  of  documents  and  data  of  every 
description,  (c)  A  yearly  record  of  demolitions  and  topo- 
graphical changes.  The  London  Topographical  Society  is 
engaged  in  the  publication  of  a  complete  set  of  London  maps, 
views,  and  plans  in  facsimile,  so  that  every  period,  every  change 
of  importance,  may  receive  illustration  from  the  issues  of  the 
Society.  Several  maps  and  plans,  the  earliest  dating  from  1 550, 
have  already  been  published.  The  London  Topographical 
Record,  being  the  yearly  volume  of  the  Society,  is  also  pub- 
lished. This  includes  the  Reports  of  the  Annual  Meetings,  as 
well  as  notes  of  excavations,  demolitions,  and  changes  in  the 
Metropolis,  illustrated  by  plans  and  maps.  Annual  Subscrip- 
tion— One  guinea.  Secretary — Bernard  Gomme,  32  George 
Street,  Hanover  Square,  w. 
Lyceum  Club  (Ladies).  Founded,  1904,  by  Miss  Constance  Smedley. 
Literature,  science,  and  arts.  Entrance  Fee — Two  guineas. 
Subscription — Three  and  four  guineas.  Address — The  Secre- 
tary, 128  Piccadilly,  w. 

Malone  Society.  Founded  1906.  Objects — To  make  accessible 
materials  for  the  study  of  the  early  English  drama.  The  Pub- 
lications of  the  Society,  issued  to  members  only,  will  consist  of 
faithful  reprints  of  old  plays,  mostly  Tudor,  and  of  documents 
illustrative  of  the  history  of  the  drama  and  the  stage.  The 
following  have  been  published  by  the  Society  to  date :  St.  Johan 
the  Evangelist,  n.d. ;  Wealth  and  Health,  n.d.  ;  The  Battle  of 
Alcazar  (by  George  Peele),  1594  ;  Orlando  Futioso  (by  Robert 
Greene),  1594  ;  King  Leir,  1605  ;  Collections  (containing  frag- 
ments, documents,  notes  on  the  Publications,  etc.) ;  Sir  John 
Oldcastle,  1600;  Selimus,  1594;  Locrine,  1595;  Calisto  and 
Melebea,  n.d.  ;  The  Bugbears  (Lansd.  MS.,  807)  ;  and  a  further 
volume  of  Collections.  Hon.  Treasurer — A.  W.  Pollard. 
Hon.  Secretary — A.  Esdailb.  Hon.  General  Editor — W.  W. 
Greg.  Subscription — £1  is.  annually ;  no  entrance  fee. 
Applications  for  membership  should  be  addressed  to  the  Hon. 
Secretary,  166  Holland  Road,  w. 

Manx  Language  Society,  Douglas.  1899.  Object — The  preservation 
and  study  of  that  form  of  Gaelic  spoken  in  the  Isle  of  Man. 
In  association  with  the  Celtic  Association  (q.v.)  a  First  Lesson 
Book  is  now  published  at  sixpence,  Metrical  Psalms  in  Manx, 
price  sixpence,  cloth  one  shilling,  Manx  Proverbs  and  Sayings, 
price  threepence,  and  other  prints  in  the  language  are  issued 
from  the  press  of  the  Isle  of  Man  Examiner,  Douglas.  A  reprint 
of  Cregeen's  Manx  Dictionary  will  shortly  be  published.  Sub- 
scription— Half  a  crown  per  annum.  President — The  Lord 
Bishop  of  Sodor  and  Man.  Secretary — Miss  S.  Morrison, 
Peel,  Isle  of  Man.

1909.  Objects — (a)  To  bring  together  those  engaged  in,  or 
interested  in,  medical  libraries  and  medical  literature  for  the

discussion  of  matters  associated  with  their  fostering  and  care ;

Medico-Psychological  Association  of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland, 
11  Chandos  Street,  Cavendish  Square,  w.  1841.  Objects — 
The  promotion  and  cultivation  of  science  in  relation  to  mental 
disorder;  the  improvement  of  the  treatment  of  the  insane. 
Membership — By  oallot :  (a)  ordinary  members,  who  must  be 
registered  medical  practitioners ;  (b)  honorary ;  and  (c)  corre- 
sponding members.  The  number  in  1907  exceeded  680.  Meet- 
ings — The  Annual  Meeting  is  held  in  July,  either  in  London  or 
in  some  provincial  town  or  city.  General  Meetings  are  held 
in  February  in  the  provinces,  in  May  aad  November  in  London  ; 
and  Divisional  Meetings  in  Scotland,  Ireland,  and  the  provinces. 
See  announcements  in  the  weekly  Medical  Journals.  Publica- 
tion— The  Journal  of  Medical  Science.  Subscription — One 
guinea  per  annum.  Honorary  General  Secretary — C.  Hubert 
Bond,  M.D.,  Long-Grove  House,  Epsom.  Librarian — George 
Bethell,  F.R.Hist.S.

by  eminent  preachers ;  (5)  to  make  grants  of  the  Scriptures, 
tracts,  sermons,  books,  etc.,  for  free  distribution ;  (6)  to  en J

Modern  Language  Association,  The.  This  Association  has  been 
established  for  the  following  objects  : — {a)  To  raise  the  standard 
of  efficiency  in  the  teaching  of  Modern  Languages,  and  pro- 
mote their  study  in  our  schools  and  the  country  generally ; 
(6)  to  obtain  for  Modern  Languages  the  status  to  which  their 
intrinsic  value,  as  instruments  of  mental  discipline,  entitles 
them — apart  from  their  acknowledged  commercial  and  utili- 
tarian importance  ;  (c)  to  provide  means  of  communication 
for  students  and  teachers  of  foreign  languages.  The  Associa- 
tion has  formed  a  travelling  exhibition  of  books  and  apparatus 
for  use  in  modern  language  teaching.  It  also  co-operates 
with  the  Societe  d'Echange  International  des  Enfants  et  des 
Jeunes  Gens  at  Paris  in  promoting  exchanges  of  children 
between  families  of  different  nationality.  The  annual  sub- 
scription is  7s.  6d.  The  organ  of  the  Association  is  Modern 
Language  Teaching  (eight  times  a  year).  The  Association  also 
supports  the  Modern  Language  Review  (quarterly).  President 
— Professor  Karl  Breul,  Ph.D.,  Litt.D.  Chairman  of 
Committees — A.  A.  Somerville,  M.A.,  Eton  College.  Hon. 
Secretary — G.  F.  Bridge,  M.A.,  45  South  Hill  Park,  Hamp- 
stead,  n.w. 

National  Home-Reading  Union.  1889.  Objects — To  guide  readers 
in  the  use  of  books,  to  direct  self-education,  and  to  unite 
readers  in  circles  for  mutual  stimulus  and  help.  Publications —

Miss  Read,  12  York  Buildings,  Adelphi,  w.c. 

National  Literary  Society  of  Ireland,  6  St.  Stephen's  Green,  Dublin.

music,  art,  and  archaeology  of  Ireland.  Lectures — Weekly 
during  session  from  November  till  June.  Journals  issued

National  Service  League  (with  which  is  incorporated  The  Lads' 
Drill  Association).  Objects — (1)  To  insure  peace  and  security 
for  the  British  Empire  by  organizing  our  Land  Forces  in  such 
a  manner  that  we  may  not  only  be  able  to  defend  successfully 
any  portion  of  the  Empire  against  attack,  but  also  that  the 
strength  of  our  defensive  arrangements  may  render  any  attack 
improbable.  (2)  To  improve  the  moral  and  physical  condition 
of  the  nation,  and  thereby  increase  its  industrial  efficiency.

With  a  view  to  attaining  these  two  objects,  the  League  advo- 
cates that,  subject  to  certain  exemptions  to  be  denned  by  law, 
including  those  necessary  to  provide  for  the  requirements  of 
the  Navy  and  the  Mercantile  Marine — Every  man  of  sound 
physique,  without  distinction  of  class,  shall  be  legally  liable 
during  certain  years  of  his  life  to  be  called  upon  for  service 
in  the  United  Kingdom  in  case  of  emergency ;  in  order  to  fit 
him  for  this  duty  he  shall  be  legally  obliged  to  undergo  a  pre- 
liminary training  of  three  or  four  months,  between  the  ages  of 
eighteen  and  twenty-one,  followed  by  short  annual  camp 
trainings  for  the  next  three  or  four  years.  Publishes  a  journal 
— The  Nation  in  Arms — monthly,  free  to  members.  Sub- 
scriptions— (Life  Payments)  Hon.  Vice-Presidents,  £2$  ;  Mem- 
bers, £  10  ios.  (Annual  Payments)  Hon.  Vice-Presidents,  £$  ; 
Members,  £1  is. ;  Associates  (with  literature  and  journal), 
5s. ;  Student  Associates  (with  literature  and  journal),  2s.  6d.  ; 
Associates  (with  monthly  leaflet),  is.  President — Earl  Roberts, 
V.C.,  K.G.     Offices— 72  Victoria  Street,  s.w. 

National  Union  of  Conservative  and  Constitutional  Associations. 
Founded  in  1867.  Objects — (1)  To  form  a  centre  of  united 
action  and  communication  between  Conservative  and  Consti- 
tutional Associations  and  Clubs,  and  to  accomplish  all  objects 
in  accordance  with  Conservative  principles  and  policy  which 
can  be  most  effectually  ensured  by  co-operation.  (2)  To  pro- 
mote the  formation  and  organization  of  Conservative  and  Con- 
stitutional Associations  and  Clubs.  (3)  To  disseminate  Con- 
stitutional principles  by  means  of  the  newspaper  press  and 
other  publications,  and  by  addresses,  lectures,  etc.,  on  subjects 
of  national  and  political  interest.  (4)  To  maintain  such  rela- 
tions with  Conservative  and  Constitutional  Associations  and 
Clubs  as  will  enable  them  at  any  time  to  give  speedy  and 
united  expression  to  the  Conservative  feeling  of  the  country, 
by  petition,  resolutions,  or  otherwise.  Secretary — Thomas 
Cox,  Esq.,  St.  Stephen's  Chambers,  Westminster,  s.w.  Librarian 
and  Editor — Mr.  J.  Marshall,  St.  Stephen's  Chambers,  West- 
minster, s.w.  (For  publications  issued  by  the  Association,  see 
list  of  publications,  pp.  694  to  768.)

Newspaper  Press  Fund,  11  Garrick  Street,  w.c.  1864.  Incorpo- 
rated 1890.  Object — The  assistance  of  necessitous  members  of 
the  literary  departments  of  the  Press  who  have  become  members 
of  the  Fund,  their  widows  and  orphans,  The  invested  funds 
in  December,  1907,  amounted  to  ^54,500 ;  annual  income 
and  expenditure  about  365,000.  President — Lord  Glenesk. 
Treasurer — Lord  Burnham.     Secretary — J.  P.  C.  Coast. 

Newspaper  Society,  The.  Founded  in  1836  as  "  The  Provincial 
Newspaper  Society, "  when  it  drew  its  members  exclusively 
from  the  Provinces.  In  1889  it  was  enlarged  so  as  to  include 
both  the  London  and  Provincial  Press,  and  its  title  changed 
to  "  The  Newspaper  Society."  Main  Objects — (a)  To  promote 
and  safeguard  newspaper  interests  in  Parliament ;  (b)  to  watch 
the  operation  of  the  law  of  libel,  to  initiate  such  reforms 
therein  as  may  from  time  to  time  appear  necessary,  and  to 
provide  a  central  machinery  by  means  of  which  its  members, 
when  proceeded  against  for  libel,  may  enter  into  combination 
for  their  common  defence  ;  (c)  to  promote  co-operation  amongst 
its  members  in  all  matters  affecting  their  common  interests ; 
(d)  to  afford  opportunities  of  personal  intercourse  by  means  of 
occasional  conferences,  and  an  annual  meeting  and  dinner. 
Secretary — Mr.  H.  Whorlow,  Morning  Post  Buildings,  346 
Strand,  w.c. 

Ophthalmologieal  Soeiety  of  the  United  Kingdom,  1 1  Chandos  Street, 
Cavendish  Square.  1880.  6,000  volumes  all  relating  to 
ophthalmology.  Open  to  Members  9  to  6,  and  9  to  2  Satur- 
days. Closed  September.  Printed  catalogue,  subjects  and 
authors.     Librarian — George  Bethell,  F.R.Hist.S. 

Oriental  Translation  Fund,  New  Series.  Under  this  name  the  work 
of  the  Old  Oriental  Translation  Fund,  founded  in  1828,  is 
carried  on.  For  translations,  circulars,  &c,  apply  to  The 
Royal  Asiatic  Society,  22  Albemarle  Street,  w. 

Palestine  Exploration  Fund,  The,  38  Conduit  Street,  w.  Founded 
1865.  Objects — A  Society  for  the  accurate  and  systematic 
investigation  of  the  Archaeology,  the  Topography,  the  Geology 
and  Physical  Geography,  the  Manners  and  Customs  of  the 
Holy  Land,  for  Biblical  Illustration.  Publications — Many 
books,  maps,  and  plans,  invaluable  to  the  student  of  Semitic 
archaeology,  have  been  published.  The  Quarterly  Statement 
is  a  periodical  report  of  the  Society's  work,  which  is  sent  post 
free  to  subscribers  of  at  least  half  a  guinea  per  annum.  Hon. 
Secretary — J.  D,  Crace.

Philosophical  Society  of  Glasgow  (Royal),  207  Bath  Street.  1802. 
Principal  Objects — To  absorb  and  continue  the  Association

moral  sciences,  and  the  arts  of  design,  with  their  applications,

Freeland  Fergus,  M.D.  Secretary — Professor  Peter  Ben- 
nett.

particulars  may  be  obtained  from  "the  Secretary,  52  Holborn 
viaduct,  b.c.

Presbyterian  Historical  Society  of  Ireland.  Objects—To  collect  and 
preserve  the  materials  for,  and  promote  the  knowledge  of,  the 
history  of  Presbyterianism  in  Ireland.  It  is  desirable  that  a 
Register  should  be  made  of  all  records  (session  books,  baptismal 
and  marriage  registers,  MSS.,  orginal  documents,  rare  books 
and  pamphlets,  sermons,  congregational  histories — manuscript 
or  printed),  portraits,  pictures  of  churches,  communion  plate, 
tokens,  curious  relics,  etc.  Contributions  of  such,  either  as 
gifts  or  on  loan,  are  invited,  and  arrangements  have  been  made 
for  their  preservation  in  the  Assembly  Buildings,  where  they  will 
be  easily  accessible.  All  communications  should  be  addressed 
to  The  Secretaries,  Historical  Society,  Presbyterian  Church 
House,  Belfast.

Proportional  Representation  Society.  President— The  Right  Hon. 
Lord  Avebury.  Chairman  of  Committee  (composed  of  mem- 
bers of  all  parties) — The  Right  Hon.  Lord  Courtney  of 
Penwith.  Objects — (1)  To  reproduce  the  opinions  of  the 
electors  in  Parliament  and  other  public  bodies  in  their  true  pro- 
portions. (2)  To  secure  that  the  majority  of  electors  shall  rule 
and  all  considerable  minorities  shall  be  heard.  (3)  To  give 
electors  a  wider  freedom  in  the  choice  of  representatives. 
Methods — By  holding  public  meetings,  disseminating  literature,

Psychical   Research,   Society   tor,   20   Hanover  Square,   w.     1882. 
Objects — To  examine  the  nature  and  extent  of  any  influence 
which  may  be  exerted  by  one  mind  upon  another,  otherwise 
than  through  the  recognised  sensory  channels  ;  to  study  hypno- 
tism and  mesmerism,  and  to  inquire  into  the  alleged  phenomena 
of  clairvoyance  ;  to  investigate  any  reports,  resting  on  testi- 
mony sufficiently  strong  and  not  too  remote,  of  apparitions 
coinciding  with  some  external  event  (as,  for  instance,  a  death), 
or  giving  information  previously  unknown  to  the  percipient, 
or  being  seen  by  two  or  more  persons  independently  of  each 
other  ;  to  inquire  into  various  alleged  phenomena  apparently 
inexplicable  by  known  laws  of  nature,  and  commonly  referred  by 
spiritualists  to  the  agency  of  extra-human  intelligences  ;  to 
collect  and  collate  existing  materials  bearing  on  the  history  of 
these  subjects.     The  aim  of  the  Society  is  to  approach  these 
various  problems  without  prejudice  or  prepossession  of  any 
kind,  and  in  the  same  spirit  of  exact  and  unimpassioned  inquiry 
which  has  enabled  science  to  solve  so  many  problems  once  not 
less   obscure.     Publications — Proceedings    and   Journal.     Sub- 
scription— Members,   £2   2s.   per   annum ;  Associates,   £1    is. 
Secretary — Mr.  R.  A.  H.  Bickford-Smith,  20  Hanover  Square,  w. 
Publishers'  Association,  Stationers'  Hall  Court.     Objects — To  pro- 
mote and  protect  by  all  lawful  means  the  interests  of  the  Pub- 
lishers of  Great  Britain  and  Ireland.     Membership — About  90  ; 
those  only  eligible  who  have  carried  on  a  bond-fide  book-pub- 
lishing business  for  at  least  one  year.    President — Mr.  William 
Heinbmann.     Secretary — W.  Poulten,  Stationers'  Hall  Court, 
London,  ex. 
Publishers'  Congress,  Permanent  Office  of  International.   Up  to  June, 
1 901,  the  administrative  work  of  the  Publishers'  Congress  was 
carried  on  by  the  Association  in  whose  country  the  last  congress 
had  been  held ;  but  as  many  inconveniences  arose  in  consequence, 
it  was  resolved  at  the  last  meeting  of  the  Congress  that  a  per- 
manent office  should  be  established,  supported  by  the  contribu- 
tions of  the  principal  associations.     Berne  was  chosen  as  the 
most  central  and  neutral  locality.     The  Office  is  charged  with 
the  collection  of  information  concerning  postal  rates,  custom 
duties,  international  copyright  questions,  and  all  other  matters 
affecting  the  book  trade,  and  with  carrying  into  effect  so  far

part  n.]  SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS  903 

as  possible  the  resolutions  of  the  congresses.  The  Secretary's 
report  of  the  first  year's  working  shows  not  only  the  great 
value  of  the  Permanent  Office  to  all  interested  in  publishing, 
but  indicates  also  the  good  and  useful  work  which  it  is  already 
doing.  The  Executive  Committee  charged  with  the  direction 
of  the  Permanent  Office  consists  of  Mr.  W.  P.  Van  Stockum,

Pare  Literature  Society.  11  Buckingham  Street,  w.c.  1854.  Object 
— The  diffusion  of  already  existing  pure  literature  among  the 
poorer  classes,  chiefly  by  supplying  books  at  low  rates.  9,970 
libraries  have  been  supplied  to  working  men,  schools,  &c,  to 
the  value  of  £78,591.  The  Secretary  and  other  agents  of  the 
Society  have  visited  4,210  booksellers'  shops.  Secretary — 
N.  H.  Spurdens. 

Quill  Club,  The.  Founded  1898.  Objects— To  afford  assistance  to 
those  desirous  of  entering  the  ranks  of  literature.  To  supply 
aspirants  with  criticism  of  their  work,  free  advice  concerning 
the  placing  of  MSS.  with  magazines,  etc.,  and  suggestions  with 
regard  to  transactions  with  publishers,  agents,  and  the  like. 
To  protect  its  members  against  tricksters  trading  on  literary 
beginners,  and  to  maintain  a  record  of  concerns  which,  after 
investigation,  are  considered  to  be  dishonestly  conducted. 
To  provide  members  with  a  postal  lending  library  of  selected 
volumes  containing  instruction  in  literary  and  journalistic 
matters.  To  afford  aspirants  facilities  for  social  intercourse  by 
meetings  in  London  and  other  centres.  The  Club  supplies 
wants  of  the  unfledged  author  and  literary  student  that  are  not 
met  by  any  other  society.  A  series  of  literary  competitions  is 
conducted  for  the  benefit  of  members.  The  club  is  managed 
by  honorary  officers,  who  are  elected  by  the  members  yearly. 
The  income  is  entirely  devoted  to  the  work  of  the  Club,  and 
audited  balance-sheets  are  published  annually.  Subscription — 
Ten  shillings  yearly.  No  entrance  fee.  Meetings — In  London, 
on  the  first  Saturday  of  each  month,  and  in  other  centres  where 
the  local  membership  is  of  sufficient  strength.  Publication — 
The  Quill  Club  Circular  (monthly).  President — Max  Pember- 
ton.  Vice-Presidents— T.  P.  O'Connor,  M.P.,  W.  W.  Jacobs, 
H.  B.  Marriott  Watson,  A.  St.  John  Adcock,  A.  E.  W. 
Mason,  Hilaire  Belloc,  M.P.,  G.  K.  Chesterton.  Chair- 
man— Juan  W.  P.  Chamberlin,  23  Nassington  Road,  Hamp- 
stead,  n.w.  Corresponding  Secretary-~-Mis9  Gertrude  Garratt, 
9  Burma  Road,  Clissold  Park,  n.  '

Rationalist  Press  Association,  Limited.  Founded  1899.  Objects — 
"  To  stimulate  freedom  of  thought  and  inquiry  in  reference  to 
ethics,  theology,  philosophy,  and  kindred  subjects.  To  pro- 
mote a  secular  system  of  education.  To  publish  and  to  dis- 
tribute books,  pamphlets,  and  periodicals  designed  to  .  .  . 
assist  in  the  spread  of  Rationalist  principles.  Rationalism 
may  be  defined  as  the  mental  attitude  which  unreservedly  accepts 
the  supremacy  of  reason,  and  aims  at  establishing  a  system  of 
philosophy  and  ethics  verifiable  by  experience  and  independent 
of  all  arbitrary  assumptions  or  authority."  Among  the  j  cheap 
reprints  already  published  are  Darwin's  Origin  of  Species, 
Renan's  Life  of  Jesus,  Herbert  Spencer's  Education,  Sir  Alfred 
C.  Lyall's  Asiatic  Studies,  and  works  by  J.  S.  Mill,  Haeckel, 
Huxley,  Tyndall,  Clodd,  and  M.  Arnold.  The  more  expensive 
publications  include  various  works  by  John  M.  Robertson, 
Joseph  McCabe,  and  others,  a  popular  edition  of  Supernatural 
Religion,  and  a  two-guinea  edition  of  Professor  Haeckel's  The 
Evolution  of  Man,  now  reissued  in  cheap  reprint  form  (384  pages 
with  408  illustrations)  at  one  shilling  and  two  shillings.  Under 
the  auspices  of  the  Association  many  lectures  have  been  de- 
livered m  London  and  the  provinces,  including  special  courses 
on  evolution,  illustrated  by  oxyhydrogen  lantern.  At  the 
offices  of  the  Association  a  reading-room  and  library  are 
available  for  the  use  of  Members.  Chairman — Edward  Clodd. 
Among  the  Honorary  Associates  are  George  Brandes,  A.  W. 
Benn,  Dr.  F.  J.  Furnivall,  Professor  Ernst  Haeckel,  Leonard 
Huxley,  Eden  Phillpotts,  Professor  Edward  Westermarck. 
Subscription — Not  less  than  five  shillings  per  annum.  Secre- 
tary— Charles  E.  Hooper,  5  and  6  Johnson's  Court,  Fleet 
Street,  e.c. 

Ray  Society.  Founded  1844.  Objects — To  produce  for  its  sub- 
scribers such  works  on  Natural  History,  especially  that  of  the 
British  Isles,  as,  from  the  expense  of  their  illustration  or  other 
causes,  could  not  be  profitably  brought  out  by  a  publisher. 
Besides  personal  subscribers,  there  are  on  tne  list  of  the  Society 
about  150  public  libraries,  museums,  scientific  societies,  Uni- 
versities, and  kindred  institutions  in  this  country  and  abroad. 
In  addition  to  receiving  the  annual  volume  or  volumes,  sub- 
scribers have  the  privilege  of  purchasing  works  already  issued 
at  a  reduction  from  the  published  price.  The  annual  subscrip- 
tion is  one  guinea.  Membership  about  300.  President — The 
Right  Hon.  Lord  Avebury,  D.C.L.,  F.R.S.,  etc.  Treasurer — 
F.  DuCane  Godman,  D.C.L.,  F.R.S.,  etc.  Secretary — John 
Hopkinson,  F.L.S.,  F.Z.S.,  etc.,  Weetwood,  Watford.

Roman  world.  Annual  Subscription — One  guinea.  Acting

Rome,  British  School  at  This  school,  projected  in  1899,  was  opened 
in  the  spring  of  1901,  and  has  been  modelled,  in  many  respects, 
on  the  existing  British  School  at  Athens.  Objects — The  School 
is  intended,  first,  as  a  training-ground  for  students  fresh  from 
the  universities  and  other  educational  institutions  ;  secondly, 
as  a  centre  round  which  more  mature  students  may  group 
themselves  for  purposes  of  systematic  research  ;  and,  thirdly, 
as  a  source  of  mformation  and  advice  for  visitors  desiring  to 
pursue  serious  studies  in  Rome  and  Italy.  The  province  of 
the  School  is  not  purely  archaeological,  but  includes  all  periods 
of  Roman  and  Italian  history,  art,  antiquities,  and  literature. 
The  excavation  of  ancient  sites,  which  has  formed  an  important 

Eart  of  the  work  of  the  School  at  Athens,  is  excluded  in  Italy 
y  the  rules  of  the  Italian  Government.  But  in  other  respects 
the  work  of  the  Roman  School  is  more  varied  than  that  of  the 
Athenian,  since  the  range  of  studies  which  centre  in  Rome  is 
wider,  including,  for  example,  palaeography,  Christian  as  well 
as  classical  antiquities,  and  the  art  and  architecture  of  the 
Renaissance. 

Vol.  I.  of  the  Papers  of  the  school,  published  in  August,  1902, 
contains  papers  by  the  Director  (Mr.  Rushforth)  on  the  "  Church 
of  S.  Maria  Antigua,"  and  by  Dr.  Ashby  on  the  "  Classical 
Topography  of  the  Roman  Campagna."  Vol.  II.,  published 
October,  1904,  consists  of  reproductions  of  the  drawings  of 
Andreas  Koner,  with  text  by  Dr.  Ashby.  With  the  sanction 
and  co-operation  of  the  Italian  authorities  the  school  has 
undertaken  a  work  of  lasting  importance  —  the  cataloguing 
of  the  works  of  ancient  art  in  the  municipal  museums  in 
Rome.  Vol.  III.,  published  1906,  contains  a  further  paper 
on  the  Roman  Campagna  by  Dr.  Ashby,  and  articles  on 
historical  sculptures  by  Mr.  H.  Stuart  Jones  and  Mr.  A.  J.  B. 
Wace.  Vol.  IV.  contains  further  instalments  of  the  articles 
on  the  Campagna  and  on  historical  reliefs,  and  papers  on  the 
goldsmiths  of  Rome  by  Mr.  S.  J.  A.  Churchill ;  an  ivory  statuette 
in  the  British  Museum,  by  Mr.  A.  H.  S.  Yeames  ;  and  an  im- 
portant study  of  the  Iron  Age  in  South  Italy,  by  Mr.  T.  E.  Peel. 
At  present  the  annual  income  of  the  School  is  about  £1,000,  of 
which  /500  is  received  from  H.M.  Government.  The  continu- 
ance of  this  grant  mainly  depends  on  the  amount  of  support 
accorded  by  the  public.  The  Director  of  the  School  is  Dr. 
Ashby.  Further  information  will  be  supplied  by  the  Secretary 
John  ff.  Baker  Pbnoyrb,  Esq.,  M.A.,  22  Albemarle  Street,  w., 
or  by  any  other  member  of  the  committee.  Subscriptions  and 
donations  are  urgently  needed.  They  should  be  paid  to  the 
credit  of  the  "  British  School  at  Rome,"  at  the  Condon  and 
Westminster  Bank,  Lothbury,  B.C. 
Romilly  Society,  The.  Founded  1897.  Objects. — To  amend  the 
criminal  law  and  its  administration,  reform  prison  regulations, 
reclaim  offenders,  abolish  cruel  punishments,  and  to  prevent

and  redress  the  miscarriage  of  justice.  This  Society  is  named 
after  Sir  Samuel  Romilly,  the  most  distinguished  and  the 
earliest  of  those  who  advocated  a  more  merciful  criminal  code. 
The  present  Society  was  founded  in  1897  by  Charles  H.  Hop- 
wood,  Recorder  of  Liverpool  in  1886.  The  Society  cordially 
invites  the  co-operation  of  those  who  are  willing  to  assist  in 
promoting  the  above  objects.  Hon.  Treasurer — H.  E.  Ormerod, 
Esq.,  K.C.  Secretary — C.  Phillipson,  M.A.,  LL.D.,  1  Mitre 
Court  Buildings,  Temple,  e.c.

Royal  Colonial  Institute,  Northumberland  Avenue,  w.c.  1868. 
Incorporated  1882.  Objects — To  provide  a  place  of  meeting 
for  all  gentlemen  connected  with  the  Colonies  and  British  India, 
and  others  taking  an  interest  in  Colonial  and  Indian  affairs  ;  to 
establish  a  reading-room  and  library,  in  which  recent  and 
authentic  intelligence  upon  Colonial  and  Indian  subjects  may 
be  constantly  available,  and  a  Museum  for  the  collection  and 
exhibition  of  Colonial  and  Indian  productions,  &c,  &c.  Meet- 
ings— Monthly,  and  occasional  afternoon  meetings  in  addition, 
at  which  papers  are  read  and  discussed.  Publications — Journal, 
monthly  for  eight  months  during  the  session ;  Proceedings, 
yearly.  The  present  annual  income  is  rather  over  .£8,000  ; 
and  at  the  close  of  the  year  the  library  contained  over  75,000 
volumes,  all  dealing  with  the  Colonies  and  India.  Acting- 
Secretary  and  Librarian — James  R.  Boosi.

Selborne  Society,  The.  Founded  1885.  Objects — The  promotion  of 
the  study  of  natural  history.  The  preservation  from  needless 
destruction  of  wild  birds,  animals,  and  plants.  The  protection 
of  places  and  objects  of  antiquarian  interest  or  natural  beauty. 
Membership — Those  who  subscribe  a  minimum  of  5s.  per 
annum  are  entitled  to  hold  office,  vote  at  meetings,  and  to 
receive  the  Society's  Magazine  (The  Selborne  Magazine,  pub- 
lished monthly).  The  subscriptions  of  Members  shall  be  due  on 
January  1  in  each  year,  and  shall  be  payable  in  advance.  The 
Subscription  of  those  admitted  during  the  last  quarter  of  the 
year  will  date  from  January  1  following,  but  wiU  be  payable 
at  the  time  of  election.  President — The  Rt.  Hon.  Lord 
Avebury,  D.C.L.,  F.R.S.  Honorary  Secretary — Wilfred 
Mark  Webb,  F.L.S.,  42  Bloomsbury  Square,  w.c. 

Seidell  Society.  1887.  Object — To  encourage  the  study  and  advance 
the  knowledge  of  the  history  of  English  law.  The  Publications 
of  the  Society  are  reprints  of  original  documents,  with  trans- 
lations and  commentaries,  free  to  Members.  Volume  XVII. 
(1903)  was  the  first  volume  of  a  reprint  and  translation  of  the 
Year-Books  of  Edward  II.,  by  Prof.  Maitland.  This  has  been 
continued  in  Volumes  XIX.  (1904)  and  XX.  (1905),  and  XXII. 
(1907).  Volumes  XVIII.  (1904)  and  XXI.  (1906)  form  a  new 
and  complete  digest  and  translation  of  Borough  Customs, 
arranged  and  edited  with  bibliographical  and  historical  intro- 
ductions by  Miss  Mary  Bateson,  late  Fell,  of  Newnham  College, 
Cambridge.  Volume  XXII.  (1908)  is  the  first  volume  of  Select 
Cases  on  the  Law  Merchant  in  Local  Courts,  1 270-1638,  with  an 
historical  introduction  by  Prof.  Gross,  late  of  Harvard,  U.S.A. 
This  is  to  be  continued  in  a  later  volume,  which  will  deal  with

the  King's  Courts.  Volume  XXIII.  (1909)  is  a  second  volume

Sette  of  Odd  Volumes,  The.  1878.  The  Sette  of  Odd  Volumes  is  a 
Society  of  book-lovers  and  especially  of  such  as  are  interested 
in  out-of-the-way  forms  of  literature,  art,  and  science,  the 
Membership  consisting  of  twice  twenty-one  brethren.  The 
motto  of  the  Society  is  "  Conviviality  and  Mutual  Admiration." 
The  "  Sette  "  dines  at  Oddenino's  Restaurant  on  the  fourth 
Tuesday  in  each  month,  when  papers  are  read  on  divers  subjects, 
being  afterwards  privately  issued  as  "  Opuscula."  The  "  Sette  " 
is  governed  by  odd  rules,  and  fresh  elections  of  President  and 
Officers  take  place  in  July.     Address  :  Imperial  Restaurant,  w. 

Shakespeare  League,  The  London.  Founded  23rd  April  ("  Shake- 
speare Day  "),  1902.  Objects — The  commemoration  of  Shake- 
speare in  connection  with  London,  where  his  works  were 
originally  produced  and  printed ;  and  the  organization  of  an 
annual  celebration  of  "  Shakespeare  Day  "  in  London.  Annual 
subscription — Five  shillings.  Secretary — Miss  Elspet  Keith, 
17  Park  Avenue,  Willesden  Green,  n.w. 

Shakespeare  Society.  Founded  1909.  In  1875  fifteen  of  Professor 
Henry  Morley's  students  started  a  Shakespeare  Reading 
Society,  which  steadily  grew  to  200  members  at  the  end  of 
fifteen  years.  For  the  next  fifteen  it  dwindled.  In  1905,  how- 
ever, it  was  again  revived,  and  over  300  joined  in  three  months. 
In  1909  150  separated  from  the  Shakespeare  Reading  Society, 
and  called  themselves  the  Shakespeare  Society  only.  The 
membership  now  numbers  230.  Mr.  H.  B.  Irving  is  President. 
Sir  Henry  Irving  was  for  thirty  years  President  of  the  S.R.S. 
Objects — The  study  of  elocution  and  literature,  and  the  render- 
ing the  works  of  Shakespeare  in  Elizabethan  style.  For 
further  particulars  and  information  apply  the  Hon.  Secretary — 
Allan  Gomme,  Esq.,  12  Dryden  Chambers,  119  Oxford  Street, 
w. 

Soeiety  for  the  Study  of  Inebriety.  Founded  1884.  Objects — The 
systematic  study  of  Inebrity  and  the  investigation  of  all  forms 
of  Alcoholism.  Meetings  for  discussion  are  held  in  the  rooms 
of  the  Medical  Society  of  London,  11  Chandos  Street,  Cavendish 
Square,  London,  w.,  on  the  afternoons  of  the  second  Tuesday 
in  January,  April,  July,  and  October.  Qualified  medical 
practitioners  are  admitted  to  the  Society  as  members,  and  other 
gentlemen  and  ladies  interested  in  the  work  of  the  Society  are

Sociological  Society.  Founded  1903.  Objects — The  aims  of  the 
Society  are  scientific,  educational,  and  practical.  It  seeks 
to  promote  investigation,  and  to  advance  education  in  the 
social  sciences  in  their  various  aspects  and  applications.  It 
aims  at  affording  a  common  ground  on  which  workers  from  all 
fields  and  schools  concerned  with  social  phenomena  may  meet 
•—economist  and  historian,  psychologist  and  moralist,  anthro- 
pologist and  archaeologist,  geographer  and  naturalist;  as  also 
physician  and  alienist,  criminologist  and  jurist,  hygienist  and 
educationist,  philanthropist  and  social  reformer,  politician  and 
cleric.  The  Sociological  Society  prosecutes  its  work  by  means 
customary  to  an  efficiently  organized  learned  society — namely, 
Meetings  for  Papers  and  Discussions,  the  collection  of  relevant 
periodical  and  book  literature,  and  by  publications.  The 
Society  is  endeavouring  to  raise  a  fund  of  about  £5,000  for  a 
Reference  Library  of  Sociology,  and  for  the  issue  of  sociological 

Sublications.  Meetings — Fortnightly  between  October  and 
[ay,  free  to  all  Members  of  the  Society.  Publication — Socio- 
logical Review,  quarterly,  free  to  Members.  The  foreign  corre- 
spondents of  the  Society  include  many  of  the  most  eminent 
Continental  and  American  sociologists.  Membership  is  open 
to  all  interested  in  sociological  studies.  Subscription — (annual) 
£1  is.  (26  francs ;  5  dollars  ;  21  marks),  payable  in  advance  on 
January  1  ;  payment  for  Life  Membership,  £10  10s.  (260  francs  ; 
50  dollars  ;  210  marks).  Cheques  in  payment  of  subscription 
and  of  donations  should  be  crossed  Clydesdale  Bank  and 
forwarded  to  the  Secretary,  from  whom  further  information 
can  be  had.  Libraries  and  other  corporate  bodies  are  admissible 
as  members.  Secretary — S.  K.  Ratcliffe,  21  Buckingham 
Street,  Strand,  w.c.

Statistical  Society,  Royal,  9  Adelphi  Terrace,  w.c.     March  15,  1834. 
Objects — The  collection,  arrangement,  discussion,  and  publication 
of  facts  bearing  on  and  illustrating  the  complex  relations  of 
modern  society  in  its  social,  economical,  and  political  aspects  ; 
the  formation  of  a  statistical  library.     Membership — Fellows 
and  honorary  Fellows,  about  500.     Library — 50,000  volumes. 
Publication — Journal,  quarterly,  free  to  Fellows.     President — 
Rt.  Hon.  Sir  Charles  Dilke,  Bt.,  M.P.     Hon.  Secretaries — 
Sir  J.  Athelstane  Baines,  C.S.I.  (Foreign),  R.  Henry  Rew, 
A.  Wilson  Fox,  C.B.,  G.  Udny  Yule.     Assistant  Secretary — 
J.  H.  Magee,  B.A.     Chief  Clerk  and  Librarian — John  A.  P. 
Mackenzie. 
Surrey  Parish  Register  Sooiety.     1903.    Object— The  publication  of 
the  older  Registers  of  Parishes  in  the  County  of  Surrey  which 
have  not  been  already  printed.     Council  Room — 140  Wardour 
Street,  London,  w.     Tne  publication  for  the  year  1903  was 
Richmond  Registers,   Part  I. ;   for  the  year    1904,   Godalming 
Registers,   Part   I.  ;   for   the   year    1905,   Richmond  Registers, 
Part  II.  ;  for  the  year  1906,  The  Registers  of  Wanborough,  Far- 
leigh,    Woldingham,    and   Tats  field ;   for   the   year    1907,    The 
Registers    of   Addington,    Chelsham,    and    Warlingham.     Hon. 
Secretary — W.  Bruce  Bannerman,  Esq.,  F.S.A.,  The  Lindens, 
Sydenham  Road,  Croydon,  to  whom  all  communications  should 
be  addressed. 
Surtees  Society,  The,  Whitehouse,  Northallerton.    Object — The  pub- 
lication of  inedited  MSS.  illustrative  of   the  intellectual,  the 
moral,  the  religious,  and  the  social  condition  of  those  parts 
of  England  and  Scotland  between  the  Humber  and  the  Firth 
of  Forth  on  the  east,  and  the  Mersey  and  Clyde  on  the  west,  a 
region  which  constituted  the  ancient  kingdom  of  Northumbria. 
Since  it  was  founded  in  1834  the  Society  has  published  108 
volumes.       Subscription  —  One    guinea ;     no    entrance    fee. 
Secretary — William  Brown,  F.S.A. 
Swedenberg  Sooiety,  1  Bloomsbnry  Street,  w.c.     18 10.    Objects — 
The  translation,  publication,  and  distribution  of  the  writings  of 
Emanuel  Swedenborg.     Subscription — Minimum,  ten  shillings 
annually.     Secretary — James  Speirs.

Toynbee  Natural  History  Society,  28  Commercial  Street,  b.  1886.

Victoria  Institute,  1  Adelphi  Terrace  House,  w.c.  1865.  Object — The

especially  those  which  are  alleged  to  bear  on  the  great  truths

Viking  Club,  The:  Society  for  Northern  Research.  Founded  1892. 
Objects — To  meet  for  the  discussion  of  papers  on  Northern 
history,  literature,  music,  art,  archaeology,  language,  folk- 
lore, and  anthropology;  and  to  encourage  the  transcription 
and  publication  of  original  documents  relating  to  Northern 
history,  etc.  Several  volumes  of  Proceedings  (Saga-Books)  have 
already  been  issued,  containing  papers  on  Norse  history  and 
antiquities.  Subscription — 10s.  per  annum,  payable  1st  Janu- 
ary. Old-lore  Series,  10s.  66.  extra.  Orkney  and  Shetland 
Old-lore  Series  of  tke  Viking  Club — Objects — The  aim  of  the  Old- 
lore  Series  is  to  bring  together  materials  for  the  history  of 
Orkney  and  Shetland,  as  well  as  of  the  Norse  race  in  the  North 
of  Scotland,  from  all  available  sources,  whether  printed  works, 
manuscripts,  archaeology,  folklore,  or  otherwise.  It  will  also 
be  a  medium  of  notes  and  queries  for  all  interested  in  these 
subjects.  Publication — Miscellany  and  Records,  issued  quar- 
terly. Annual  Subscription — 10s.  6d.,  or  five  annual  subscrip- 
tions, sos.,  due  in  advance.  Life  Subscription — Subscribers  of 
one  sum  of  ^10  10s.  or  more  shall  receive  all  the  issues  of  the 
Series  during  their  lifetime,  without  extra  charge,  and  shall 
have  their  names  and  subscriptions  enrolled  in  the  list  of 
Founders,  which  will  be  printed  once  a  year,  as  long  as  the 
Series  continues.  Editors — Mr.  and  Mrs.  A.  W.  Johnston. 
President — Professor  W.  P.  Ker,  M.A.  Hon.  Librarian — 
A.  W.  Johnston,  F.S.A.  Scot.,  59  Oakley  Street,  Chelsea,  s.w. 
Hon.  Treasurer — A.  Shaw  Mellor,  M.A.,  14  Westbourne 
Street,  Hyde  Park,  w.  Hon.  Secretary — Mrs.  Wintle  John- 
ston, 59  Oakley  Street,  Chelsea,  s.w. 

Villon  Society,  The:  This  Society  is  a  purely  private  one,  that 
does  not  invite  general  membership,  and  has  no  connection 
with  the  general  public,  except  at  the  time  of  its  occasional  issues

Zoological  Society  of  London*  1826,  by  Sir  Stamford  Raffles  and

—P.  Chalmers  Mitchell,  M.A.,  D.Sc.,  LL.D.,  F.R.S.

Australasian  Association  for  the  Advancement  of  Science.  1888. 
Congresses  are  held  at  different  places,  and  Reports  published 
in  alternate  years.  There  is  a  library.  President — Prof. 
Bragg,  M.A.,  F.R.S.  Permanent  Hon.  Secretary — Prof.  A. 
Liversidge,  LL.D.  (absent  in  Europe).  J.  H.  Maiden, 
Royal  Scoiety's  House,  Sydney,  acting. 

Royal  Anthropological  Society.  7  Lincoln's  Inn  Chambers,  Elizabeth 
Street,  Sydney.  Founded  1896.  Objects — The  investigation 
of  the  history  and  ethnology  of  mankind.  A  monthly  journal, 
The  Science  of  Man,  is  published.  Director — Dr.  Alan  Carroll, 
M.A.,  D.Litt..  Ph.D.,  &c.  Corresponding  Secretary — Isidore

Queensland 

Royal  Society  of  Queensland.  Founded  in  i860  as  the  Philosophical 
Society  ;  name  changed  in  1885.  The  Society  holds  monthly 
meetings,  and  issues  Transactions  at  certain  periods.  Small 
library.  Members — no.  Hon.  Secretary — E.  H.  Gurney, 
Technical  College,  Ann  Street,  Brisbane. 

South    Australia 

Royal  Society  of  South  Australia,  Incorporated.  Founded  1853. 
Objects — The  acquiring  and  recording  of  facts  new  to  science. 
Members — 88.  Thirty-one  volumes  of  Transactions  and  Proceed- 
ings and  vol.  i.,  parts  1,  2,  and  3,  and  vol.  ii,  part  1,  of  Memoirs 
have  appeared.  Small  library.  Exchanges  with  175  learned 
bodies.  Hon.  Secretary — G.  G.  Mayo,  C.E.,  Institute,  Ade- 
laide, South  Australia. 

Tasmania 

Royal  Society  of  Tasmania,  Hobart.  Founded  1844.  Objects — The 
scientific  study  of  the  country.  Members — 150.  It  has  a 
library,  and  annual  Papers  and  Proceedings  have  been  issued 
since  1850.  President — The  Governor.  Secretary — Robert 
Hall,  C.M.Z.S.

part  ii.]  SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS  915 

Victoria 

Royal  Geographical  Society  of  Australasia  (Victoria  Branch),  421 
Collins  Street,  Melbourne.  Founded  1883.  Objects — To  pro- 
mote the  study  of  all  branches  of  geographical  science,  more 
especially  in  its  relation  to  commerce  and  civilization.  Methods 
— (1)  By  Hie  study  of  official  and  scientific  documents,  by  com- 
munications with  learned  and  other  societies  ;  by  correspondence 
with  explorers,  travellers,  and  men  of  science  ;  and  by  the  en- 
couragement of  the  teaching  of  geography  in  schools  and  col- 
leges. (2)  To  hold  meetings,  at  which  papers  shall  be  read,  or 
lectures  given,  by  members  or  others.  (3)  To  promote  and 
encourage  the  exploration  of  the  less  known  regions  of  the  earth. 
(4)  To  publish  a  journal  of  the  proceedings  of  the  Society,  with 
a  summary  of  geographical  information.  (5)  To  form  a  collec- 
tion of  maps,  charts,  and  geographical  works  of  reference.  The 
Society  consists  of  members,  associates,  corresponding  and 
honorary  members.  Subscription — Members,  £1  is. ;  associates 
10s.  6d.  per  annum.  Hon,  Secretary — Thomas  Walker 
Fowler,  421  Collins  Street,  Melbourne. 

Royal  Society  of  Victoria.  Founded  1854.  Objects — The  advance- 
ment of  science.  Members — 83  ;  Associates — 60.  There  is  a 
library.  Transactions  or  Proceedings  have  been  issued  since  the 
foundation.  Hon.  Secretary — T.  S.  Hall,  M.A.,  The  University, 
Melbourne. 

CANADA 

Canadian  Institute,  Toronto.  Incorporated  185 1.  Objects—  The 
promotion  of  science,  the  study  of  the  history,  folk-lore,  and 
physical  conditions  of  the  country.  Has  a  good  library,  and 
publishes  Transactions  from  time  to  time.  Members — 250. 
Secretary — Prof.  J.  H.  Faull,  Ph.D. 

Canadian  Society  of  Authors.  Founded  February,  1899.  Objects^ 
To  promote  the  production  of  literature  in  Canada,  and  the 
interests  of  Canadian  authors  ;  to  discuss  and  circulate  informa- 
tion with  regard  to  public  questions  affecting  literary  workers  ; 
and  to  obtain  and  distribute  information  as  to  channels  of 
publication  open  to  Canadian  authors.  The  Society  exerted 
great  influence  in  connection  with  the  preparation  and  passing 
of  the  Canadian  Copyright  Act  of  1900,  and  has  since  been  most 
energetic  in  defending  the  interests  of  Imperial  copyright. 
Subscriptions — Full  Members,  two  dollars ;  Associates,  two 
dollars.  Secretary — Pelham  Edgar,  Ph.D.,  Victoria  College, 
Toronto. 

Literary  and  Scientific  Society,  Ottawa.  Founded  1870.  Objects— 
The  discussion  of  literary  and  scientific  questions.  The  library 
contains  over  4,000  volumes.  Membership,  about  400.  Trans- 
actions have  been  published  since  1897.  President — Dr.  C. 
Morse.     Secretary— -S.  J.  Jenkins.

Nova  Seotia  Historical  Society,  Halifax,  Nova  Scotia,  Canada. 
Founded  in  1878.  Objects — The  collection  and  preservation 
of  all  documents,  papers,  and  other  objects  of  interest  which 
may  serve  to  throw  light  upon  and  illustrate  the  history  of 
Nova  Scotia ;  the  reading  and  publication  of  documents  and 
papers  on  historical  subjects ;  and  the  formation  of  an  his- 
torical library.  Library — The  library  of  the  Society  is  incor- 
porated with  that  of  the  Legislature  of  Nova  Scotia.  Publi- 
cation— Collections  (12  vols,  published),  price  50  cents  to  non- 
members,  except  vol.  ii.,  wnich  is  75  cents.  Membership — 
About  112.  Subscription — Five  dollars  on  admission,  and  two 
dollars  annually  thereafter  ;  persons  not  resident  within  fifteen 
miles  of  Halifax,  two  dollars  on  admission,  and  one  dollar 
annually  thereafter.  Meetings — Monthly,  November  to  May. 
Corresponding  Secretary — Harry  Piers. 

Ontario  Historical  Society.  Founded  1888 ;  reorganized  1898. 
Objects — To  study  Canadian  history,  especially  as  it  relates  to 
Ontario ;  and  to  form  a  union  of  all  local  historical  organiza- 
tions. Many  societies  are  affiliated,  totalling  1,200  members. 
There  is  a  small  library.  The  annual  publication  is  Papers  and 
Records,  and  volumes  of  local  history  and  reprints  are  issued. 
Subscription  —  One  dollar  per  annum.  Acting  Secretary — 
Mrs.  E.  J.  Thompson,  Education  Department,  Toronto,  Ontario. 

Royal  Society  of  Canada,  Ottawa.  Founded,  1882,  by  the  Duke  of 
Argyll  for  the  advancement  of  science.  Most  of  the  historical 
and  scientific  societies  of  Canada  are  affiliated.  Has  sections  on 
French  and  English  literature,  and  on  mathematical  and 
biological  sciences.  Transactions  are  issued.  President — Rev. 
Prof.  George  Bryce.  Hon.  Secretary — Dr.  J.  Fletcher. 
Hon.  Treasurer — L.  M.  Lambe. 


INDIA 

Asiatic  Society  of  Bengal.  Founded  1784.  Objects — To  study  the 
history,  language,  literature,  and  social  conditions  of  the  present 
and  former  native  inhabitants  of  India.  Members — 407  in 
number.  A  Journal  is  published,  as  also  Memoirs.  Hon.  Gen.

Librarian — J.  H.  Elliott.     Office — 57  Park  Street,  Calcutta. 

Asiatic  Society  of  Ceylon.  Founded  1845.  Objects — To  institute 
and  promote  inquiries  into  the  history,  religions,  languages, 
literature,  arts,  and  social  conditions  of  the  native  inhabitants 
of  Ceylon ;  also  the  geology  and  zoology  of  the  island. 
Members — 209.  President — The  Hon.  J.  Ferguson,  C.M.G. 
Hon.  Secretaries — H.  C.  P.  Bell,  J.  Harward,  M.A.,  and 
Gerard  A.  Joseph,  Colombo  Museum. 

There  are  also  branches  of  the  Asiatic  Society  at  Singapore 
{Hon.  Secretary,  H.  N.  Ridley,  M.A.)  and  Shanghai  (Hon.

NEW  ZEALAND 

New  Zealand  Institute,  Wellington.  Founded  1867.  Objects— The

Gbo.  M.  Thomson,  F.L.C.,  F.C.S.     Secretary— Tuos.  King.

Royal  Society  of  South  Africa.  Founded  1908.  Objects— To

Institute  of  Jamaica,  Kingston.  Founded  1879.  Objects — The

of  the  Institute.  Has  a  museum.  Hon.  Secretary — T.  M. 
Kelshall.

9i8  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK     [part  ii. 


SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS:   FOREIGN 

AMERICA 

American  Library  Association.  Founded  1876.  Object — To  pro- 
mote the  welfare  of  libraries  in  America  by  interchange  of 
opinion  and  experience  and  by  co-operative  publication  of 
useful  library  reports.  Membership — Now  numbers  2,000. 
Organ — Bulletin  of  the  American  Library  Association  (bi- 
monthly). Publications — A.L.A.  Bulletin,  A.L.A.  Booklist, 
proceedings  of  annual  meeting,  annual  handbook,  and  many 
special  titles  listed  in  the  latter.  Executive  Offices — 34  New- 
bury Street,  Boston,  Mass.  President — C.  H.  Gould,  McGill 
University  Library,  Montreal.  Secretary — J.  I.  Wyer,  Jun., 
New  York  State  library,  Albany. 

Smithsonian  Institution,  Washington,  U.S.A.  Founded  by  the  muni- 
ficence of  James  Smithson,  "  for  the  increase  and  diffusion 
of  knowledge  among  men,"  who  left  an  endowment  of  $540,000, 
an  amount  since  increased  to  nearly  $1 ,000,000.  The  Act  of  Con- 
gress establishing  the  Institution  was  passed  on  August  10, 1846. 
Its  statutory  members  are  the  President,  the  Vice-President, 
the  Chief  Justice  of  the  United  States,  and  the  heads  of  the 
executive  departments.  The  Institution  is  controlled  and 
directed  by  a  Board  of  Regents,  through  an  Executive  Officer, 
who  is  also  the  Secretary  of  the  Board.  There  are  six  chief 
divisions  of  the  work  of  the  Institution,  which  is  mainly  original 
scientific  advancement  and  research.  These  are:  (1)  The 
National  Museum  (including  the  National  Gallery  of  Art)  ;  (2) 
the  International  Exchange  Service  (see  p.  886)  ;  (3)  the 
Bureau  of  American  Ethnology ;  (4)  the  National  Zoological 
Park ;  (5)  the  Astrophysical  Observatory ;  and  (6)  the  Regional 
Bureau  for  the  United  States,  International  Catalogue  of 
Scientific  Literature.  It  has  a  table  at  the  Naples  Zoological 
station,  undertakes  expeditions  and  explorations,  and  issues 
annual  reports  and  numerous  monographs  and  papers  covering 
all  branches  of  knowledge.  Secretary — Charles  D.  Walcott, 
Esq.,  Washington  (D.C.),  U.S.A. 

Soeiety  of  American  Authors,  Incorporated.  Founded  May  12, 1892, 
Objects — To  further  and  unify  the  interests  of  American  authors, 
and  to  better  the  conditions  of  the  profession  of  letters  in 
America.  Subscription — Five  dollars  per  annum.  Entrance 
Fee — Two  dollars.  The  organ  is  The  American  Author,  pub- 
lishe  monthly.  Secretary — Mr.  G.  Grosvenor  Dawe,  128 
Broadway,  New  York.

Association  de  la  press*  ttrangere.  Founded  1883.  Objects— To

DENMARK 

Society  of  Danish  Authors  (Dansk  Forfatterforening).  Founded  in 
1894.  Objects — To  look  after  the  practical  interests  of  Danish 
authors  and  maintain  their  professional  interests  with  regard  to 
legislature ;  and  to  establish  a  benefit  fund.  It  was  greatly 
due  to  the  efforts  of  the  Society  that  Denmark  has  lately  joined 
the  Berne  Convention.  Membership — 150,  comprising  poets, 
dramatists,  and  novelists ;  scientific,  philosophical,  and  historical 
writers.  President — Karl  Larsen.  Secretary — Louis  Bobe. 
Address — Kronprinsessegade  38,  Copenhagen. 

FRANCE 

Aeademie  Francaise.  The  Acad6mie  Francaise  was  founded  in  1635 
by  Cardinal  Richelieu,  and  is  the  highest  of  the  five  Academies 
constituting  the  Institut  de  France.  It  is  composed  of  forty 
members,  elected  for  lif e.  The  Academy  awards  prizes  annually 
in  all  branches  of  learning  and  literature,  and  others  at  longer 
periods  ;  it  is  the  official  guardian  of  the  French  language,  and 
represents  more  fully  than  any  other  institution  the  dignity  of 
letters  in  France.  Permanent  Secretary  and  Treasurer — 
M.  Gaston  Boissier.  Chef  de  Secretariat — M.  Pingard. 
Offices — Palais  de  PInstitut,  23  Qua!  Conti,  Paris. 

Association  des  Journalistes  parislens.  Founded  for  moral  and 
material  protection.  A  benevolent  fund  (GEuvre  du  Denier  des 
Veuves  et  des  Vieillards)  was  formed  in  1894,  since  which  time 
8,145  persons  (widows  of  deceased  journalists  having  four 
children,  or  members  over  seventy  years  of  age)  have  been 
assisted,  and  over  145,250  francs  distributed.  Secretary — 
Joseph  Denais,  14  Rue  de  la  Grange-Bateliere,  Paris.    %*l-

and  to  give  assistance  in  case  of  reverse  or  illness.     Each

Association  lltteralre  et  artistlque  Internationale.  Founded  by 
Victor  Hugo  in  1878  for  procuring  universal  protection  of  intel- 
lectual property.  Subscription — Twenty  francs  per  annum. 
Membership  exceeds  400  throughout  the  world.  A  Congress  is 
held  yearly  at  one  of  the  principal  cities.  In  1902  it  was  held  at 
Naples  ;  in  1903  at  Weimar.  A  Bulletin  is  published  monthly. 
Perpetual  Secretary — M.  J.  Lermina,  85  Boulevard  de  Port 
Royal,  Paris. 

Association  syndicate  de  la  presse  Strangers  a  Paris.  A  social

Address — 20  Rue  de  Ranelagh. 

Association  syndicate  professionnelle  des  Joornalistes  republieains 
francais.  Founded  1881.  Object — To  safeguard  and  defend 
the  interests  of  the  members  of  the  profession.  Members  must 
be  of  French  birth,  and  must  have  been  employed  continuously 
and  at  a  salary  as  a  journalist  for  at  least  three  years.  Total 
number  of  members  limited  to  350  ;  all  above  this  number  are 
associates.  Financial  assistance  is  given  to  sick  members  or 
those  out  of  work.  Entrance  Fee — Fifty  francs.  Annual  Sub- 
scription— Twenty-four  francs.  General  Secretary — L.  Victor- 
Meunier.    Address — 16  Rue  Vivienne,  Paris. 

Goneourt  Academy.  Founded  January,  1903.  Under  his  will, 
Edmond  de  Goneourt  left  his  entire  property  to  Alphonse 
Daudet  and  Leon  Hennique,  directing  them  to  solicit  official 
sanction  for  a  literary  society  comprising  ten  members.  To 
each  Academician  an  annuity  of  6,000  francs  was  bequeathed  ; 
and  a  prize  of  5,000  francs  was  to  be  awarded  every  year  to  the 
author  of  some  work  of  Action.  The  members  were  to  meet 
at  certain  intervals,  and  other  rules  were  laid  down  by  the 
will  as  to  the  organization  of  the  Academy.  Edmond  de 
Goneourt  died  July  16,  1896.  Legal  disputes  arose  relative 
to  the  validity  of  the  will,  and  other  delays  occurred  ;  and 
it  was  not  until  January  19,  1903,  that  official  sanction  was 
granted  to  the  foundation.  By  the  will  only  eight  members 
were  nominated,  as  follows:  Alphonse  Daudet,  J.  K.  Huys- 
mans,  Leon  Hennique,  Octave  Mirbeau,  the  brothers  J.  H. 
Rosny,  Gustave  Gefifroy,  and  Paul  Marguerite.  Alphonse 
Daudet  died  in  1898  ;  and  in  November,  1900,  M.  Leon 
Hennique,  as  empowered  by  the  will,  filled  the  vacancies  by 
electing  MM.  Elemir  Bourges,  Lucien  Descaves,  and  Leon 
Daudet.  As  finally  constituted  the  Academy  consists  of  the 
following  :     President — Leon     Henrique.       Vice-President —

Soctete*  des  Auteurs  et  Compositeurs  dramatlques.  Founded  in  1829 
to  protect  the  interests  of  its  members,  and  to  collect  the 
royalties  on  dramatic  pieces  belonging  to  those  members,  acted 
in  France  or  elsewhere.  The  successive  Presidents  of  the 
Society  since  its  foundation  have  been  MM.  Eugene  Scribe,  de 
Saint-Georges,  Auguste  Maquet,  Camille  Doucet,  Alexandre 
Dumas,  Victorien  Sardou,  and  Ludovic  Halevy.  Secretaries — 
MM.  George  Pellerin  and  Robert  Gagnat.  Office — 8  Rue 
Hippolyte  Lebas,  Paris. 

Sooitte*  des  Gens  de  Lettres.  Founded  December  10, 1837.  Objects — To 
defend  the  interests,  social,  personal,  and  pecuniary,  of  all  its 
members,  and  to  see  that  they  obtain  and  profit  by  their  rights. 
To  assure  them  an  adequate  return  for  their  literary  labours. 
To  improve  the  terms  under  which  these  labours  are  undertaken. 
To  make  as  profitable  a  use  as  possible  for  the  general  good  of 
the  Society  of  the  contributions  of  its  members,  notably  in  the 
matters  of  certain  "  rights  of  reproduction  "  over  their  work. 
The  latter  object  refers  to  the  practice  prevailing  in  France  of 
selling  or  leasing  the  serial  rights  of  stories  after  they  have  ap- 
peared in  volume  form.  Members  must  yield  these  rights  to  the 
Society,  which  pays  the  member  a  fixed  price,  and  gets  what  it 
can  from  publishers  and  editors  in  excess  of  this  for  the  benefit 
of  the  Society.  The  Society  has  three  funds  :  (1)  A  fund  from 
which  advances  can  be  made  to  assist  members  in  their  literary 
work ;  (2)  a  fund  upon  which  sick  members  can  draw ;  and 
(3)  a  pension  fund.  The  Society  also  has  the  direction  of 
other  funds  for  annual  Uterary  prizes,  such  as  those  of  the 
Fondation  Chauchard,  Le  Prix  Balzac,  Le  Prix  Petit  Bourg, 
which  together  amount  to  10,000  francs ;  there  are  several 
smaller  prizes  of  from  200  to  600  francs  each.  Information  as 
to  the  work  of  the  Society  can  be  obtained  from  the  little 
book  published  by  the  Incorporated  Society  of  Authors  for 
Mr.  S.  Squire  Sprigge.  Entrance  Fee — Eighty  francs.  Annual 
Subscription — Twenty  francs.  Office — 10  Cite  Rougemont, 
Paris. 

Syndicat  de  la  Presse  e'trangere.  An  Association  for  representatives 
of  foreign  newspapers  in  Paris.  President — J.  Janzon  (Stock- 
holms  Dagblad).  vice-President — T.  Steinherz  (Pariser  Kurier) . 
Address — 19  Av.  Wagram.

authority.  The  Society  comprises  the  editors  of  political 
journals  of  Paris  without  distinction  of  opinion.     A  benevolent

Sehffler  Society  {Schiller  Verein).  1895.  Objects — The  archive  and

Exceeds  600.  Address — Langenscheidt'sche  Verlagsbuchhand- 
lung  (Prof.  G.  Langenscheidt),  Berlin-Schoeneberg. 

Union  for  the  Protection  of  Literary  Property  {Litterarischer  Rechts- 
schutz- Verein).  Founded  April,  1900.  Object — The  defence 
of  the  authors  rights  in  literary  property  as  between  publisher 
and  author,  and  the  furthering  of  all  legal  rights  in  intellectual 
property.  The  organ  of  the  Society  is  Recht  der  Feder.  Presi- 
dent— Herr  M.  Hildebrandt.  Address — Passauerstrasse  38, 
Berlin,  W.  50. 

Union  of  German  Women  Writers  (Deutscher  Schriftstellerinnenbund). 
Founded  in  1896  by  Fraulein  Wernicke.  Object — To  advance 
the  common  interests,  and  to  afford  opportunities  for  social 
acquaintance  and  mutual  knowledge  of  writings.  Membership 
— Exceeds  100.  Organ — Das  Recht  der  Feder.  Transactions 
are  issued  irregularly.  Fortnightly  meetings  are  held. 
Directress — Frau  L.  Braun,  N.  Bayreutherstrasse  13,  Berlin. 

Union  of  German  Writers  {Deutscher  Schriftsteller  Verband).  Founded 
October,  1887.  Objects — To  further  the  interests  of  German 
authors,  and  to  provide  for  such  of  its  members  as  are  inca- 
pacitated by  reason  of  age.  Legal  advice  is  given  gratis,  as 
ateo  information  concerning  channels  for  work.  The  Society 
possesses  a  house  for  retired  writers,  Demmins  Hort,  at 
Wiesbaden.  The  organ  of  the  Society  is  Litterarische  Praxis. 
Subscription — Eight  marks  per  annum.  Secretary — Heinrich 
Abrahms.     Office — Charlottenstrasse  37,  Berlin,  W. 

Wagner  Endowment  Fund  {R.  Wagner  Stipendien-Stiftung).  Founded 
by  Richard  Wagner  in  1882  to  assist  friends  and  students  of 
music  of  small  means  to  attend  the  Bayreuth  festivals.  Grants, 
as  a  rule,  are  only  made  to  members  of  the  Wagner  Society 
(see  below).     Director — Max  Grosz,  Bayreuth. 

Wagner  Soeiety  {Der  allgemeine  R.  Wagner-Verein).  1883.  Object 
— To  foster  the  works  of  Richard  Wagner  by  giving  assistance 
to  duly  accredited  students  to  visit  the  festival  at  Bayreuth. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


I 


926  THE  LITERARY  YEAR-BOOK      [part  ii. 

There  are  fifty-eight  representatives  of  the  Society  throughout 
the  world,  and  twenty-seven  branches  have  been  formed. 
Yearly  Subscription — Four  marks.  Forty  per  cent,  of  the 
annual  income  of  the  Society  is  contributed  to  the  R.  Wagner 
Stipendien-Stiftung  (see  supra) ;  forty  per  cent,  is  applied  in 
the  purchase  of  free  tickets  for  the  members  of  the  Society ; 
and  the  remaining  twenty  per  cent,  is  carried  to  the  Festival 
Endowment  Fund,  the  interest  of  which  is  applied  in  support 
of  the  Bayreuther  Blatter.  Treasurer — Herr  Thelen.  Central 
Office — Eichhornstrasse  2,  Berlin,  W. 


HOLLAND 

In  almost  all  provinces  of  the  Netherlands  there  is  a  provincial 
Society  which  chiefly  has  for  object  the  history,  especially  of 
customs  and  habits,  of  former  times.  Also  in  several  towns 
an  archaeological  Society  for  local  history,  etc.  Many  of  these 
societies  have  a  library  and  a  museum. 

There  are  also  an  Historical  Society  {Secretary — Dr.  S.  Muller, 
Utrecht)  and  a  Nederl.  Oudheidkundige  Bond  (Secretary — 
Dr.  Ovbrvoordb,  Leiden). 

In  Friesland  there  is  a  special  Frisian  Society  entirely  devoted  to  the 
study,  maintenance,  and  promotion  of  the  Frisian  language. 

In  Leiden  exists  the  most  famous  Maatsehappy  van  Nederlandsehe 
Letterkunde.  This  society  possesses  a  splendid  library  (c/.Leiden), 
and  annually  elects  twenty  members  from  Holland  and  eight 
from  abroad,  and  publishes  yearly  an  Annual  and  biographies 
of  deceased  members  ;  also  a  Tydschrift  (periodical  for  philology 
and  literary  history),  philological  and  historical  tracts  (to-day 
inter  alia  a  repertory  of  Dutch  History  and  Archaeology), 
gives  critical  editions  of  medieval  texts  and  reprints  of  rare 
books  (cf.  prospectus). 

International  Genealogical  Institute,  Groningen,  Holland.  Founded 
1906.  Objects — The  discovery,  publication,  and  preservation 
of  whatever  may  relate  to  genealogy  and  heraldry.  Contains 
a  genealogical  and  heraldic  library.  Director — Count  Wladimir 
de  Saint-Obin. 


ITALY 

Society  of  Italian  Authors  (Societd  Italiana  degli  Autori).  Founded 
1882.  Objects — To  safeguard  the  rights  and  interests  of  authors  ; 
to  give  assistance  in  case  of  illness  or  inability  to  work.  In 
general  the  constitution  of  the  Society  resembles  that  of  the 
French  Society  of  Authors.  Publication — /  Diritti  d'Autore, 
a  monthly  journal.  Subscription — Entrance  fee,  ten  lire ; 
annual  subscription,  twenty  lire.  Secretary  —  Giuseppe 
Borghi.     Address — Corso  Venezia,  4  Milan. 


Digitized  by  VjOOQIC 


part  n.]  SOCIETIES  AND  CLUBS  927 

NORWAY 

The  Authors'  Union  (Forfatterforening).  This  is  an  old  and  well- 
established  association  of  Norwegian  authors  for  the  furthering 
and  protection  of  literary  rights.  The  Society  has  no  pension 
fund.     Office — Christiania. 

SWEDEN 

The  Publicist  Club  (Publicist  klubben).  A  well  organized  Society 
for  union  and  protection  of  journalists  of  all  parties,  and 
the  assistance  of  members  and  their  families.  At  the  same 
time  a  social  centre  for  the  literary  life  of  Stockholm.  The 
Society  is  connected  with  press  societies  throughout  the  country. 
President — Adolf  Hallgren.  Address — Stockholm.  Member 
in  London — Mr.  Fritz  Henriksson,  33  Cloudesdale  Road, 
s.w.,  National  Liberal  Club,  and  The  Press  Club. 

The  Swedish  Authors'  Union  (Sveriges  Fdrfattareforening),  Objects — 
To  further  the  interests  of  Swedish  authors  in  every  direction, 
but  especially  in  regard  to  legislature.  The  society  has  a 
pension  fund.  Most  Swedish  authors  are  members.  Secretary 
— Dr.  Axel  Raphael,  Stockholm.

TABULATED  LIST  OF  THE  CONTENTS 
OF  CHEAP  SERIES  OF  REPRINTS 


NOTE. 

Series  of  Cheap  Reprints  of  Books  (dictionaries  and  similar  reference- 
books  not  included)  likely  to  be  of  interest  to  the  general  reader  are 
analysed  in  the  following  Tables,  and  the  books  they  contain  are  indexed 
under  the  surnames  of  their  Authors.     The  price  rarely  exceeds  5s. 

The  Lists  are  taken  from  the  Catalogues  of  the  Publishers  concerned, 
and  each  series  is  indicated  by  an  initial  letter  (A  to  Zd),  The  key  to 
this  alphabet  will  be  found  on  pages  iv  to  x,  where  the  following 
particulars  of  each  series  are  given  in  tabular  form  :  (1)  Reference-letter; 
(2)  number  of  volumes  published  (to  November,  1910);  (3)  Publisher's 
name  and  address ;  (4)  size  of  the  books  in  inches ;  (5)  prices  and  styles 
of  binding. 


In  the  Lists  themselves  (pages  xi  to  xlii) — 


A  numeral  (2,  3,  etc.)  after  the  reference-letter  indicates  the  number 
of  volumes  in  which  the  book  is  issued.  Where  no  numeral  is 
added,  the  book  is  issued  in  one  volume. 

An  asterisk  (*)  after  the  reference-letter  indicates  that  the  book  is 
incomplete  (Selections,  Extracts,  etc.). 

A  dagger  (f)  after  the  reference-letter  indicates  that  more  than  one 
book  is  bound  up  in  the  same  volume. 

Mere  cheap  reprints  of  copyright  boohs  are  not  included,  as  com- 
petitive editions  of  such  boohs  do  not  exist;  but  in  all  cases 
where  non-copyright  editions  differ  from  the  copyright  edition, 
care  has  been  taken  to  indicate  this  fact  by  adding  at  length 
the  Publisher's  name  and  the  price  of  the  copyright  edition 
or  editions. 

All  modern  fiction  and  all  juvenile  literature  are  also  excluded. 

Information  as  to  omissions  or  mistakes  will  be  extremely  welcome,  and 
should  be  addressed  to  the  Editor  of  The  Literary  Year-Booh,  In  the 
meantime  it  is  hoped  that  these  Lists  will  continue  to  merit  the  apprecia- 
tion which  they  have  hitherto  received  at  the  hands  of  book-buyers  among 
the  public  and  in  the  trade.

New  Dictionary  of  Statistics.  A  Supplement  to  "Mulhall," 
bringing  the  work  down  to  the  latest  possible  dates  in  each  department. 
By  A.  D.  Webb,  B.Sc,  F.S.S.     Super  royal  8vo.     About  21 S.  net. 

This  work  is  compiled  on  the  basis  of  arrangement  adopted  by  the  late  Dr.  Michael 
G.  Mulhall  in  his  famous  "Dictionary  of Statistics ,"  {sup.  royal  Zvo*,  aw.  net\  and 
the  information  contained  in  that  work  is  carried  down  to  the  latest  dates  for  which 
the  figures  are  available.  At  the  same  time,  proportionate  space  is  devoted  to  the 
many  important  departments  of  inquiry  which  have  attained  to  public  interest  in  the 
United  Kingdom  and  other  countries  stnee  MuUuUVs  day.

Advertisements  xlix 


ENTIRELY  NEW  AND  UP-TO-DATE  EDITION  OF 

SONNENSCHEIN'S 

THE  BEST  BOOKS : 

A  READER'S  GUIDE. 

lirTHE  BEST  BOOKS"  (first  published  in  September,  1887,  revised 
•*•  and  reset  in  March,  1 89 1,  four  further  impressions  having  been 
printed),  supplemented  by  "THE  READER'S  GUIDE"  (first  pub- 
lished in  May,  1895,  with  a  reiinpression  in  September,  1901),  have  for 
many  years  past  formed  the  Universal  Reference- Book  as  a  guide  to 
literature,  having  been  in  daily  use  by  librarians,  students,  general  readers, 
booklovers,  and,  to  some  extent,  booksellers.  The  work  has  been  out  of 
print  for  several  years  past,  and  a  new  edition  has  been  urgently  demanded 
from  all  quarters  of  the  globe. 

All  the  matter  in  THE  BEST  BOOKS  and  THE  READER'S 
GUIDE  that  remain  still  of  value  has  been  retained,  though  many  new 
characterizations  have  been  made  and  numerous  books  which  appeared  in 
the  previous  issues  without  annotations  are  now  characterized  for  the  first 
time.  The  additional  titles  and*  characterizations  deal  with  all  books 
published  since  1895,  bringing  the  literature  down  to  1910.  The  whole 
contents  of  the  book  will  probably  exceed  the  large  total  of  150,000  books, 
and  it  is  estimated  that  the  complete  work  will  be  in  the  hands  of  the 
public  before  June  30, 191 1.  Publication  is  being  effected  in  Three  Parts, 
of  which  Part  I.  is  ready. 

Part  1.  (Classes  A— C) :    THEOLOGY,  MYTHOLOGY,  FOLK- 
LORE, and  PHILOSOPHY. 

THEOLOGY  contains  134  Sections,  with  hundreds  of  Paragraphs  and  Sob- 
paragraphs. 

MYTHOLOGY  AND  FOLKLORE  contains  39  Sections,  with  hundreds  of 
Paragraphs  and  Sub-paragraphs. 

PHILOSOPHY  contains  75  Sections,  with  Hundreds  of  Paragraphs  and  Sub- 
paragraphs. 

Part  11.  (Classes  D— G):  SOCIOLOGY,  GEOGRAPHY,  HISTORY 

and  ANTIQUITIES. 
Part  111.  (Classes   H— K) :    SCIENCE,  MEDICINE,   ARTS   and 
TRADES,    LITERATURE,   and   PHILOLOGY,  will,   the 
Prefaces,  Index  of  Authors  and  of  Subjects,  etc. 
The  Work  will  be  published  at   14s.  net  each  per  Part,  and  at 
42s.  net  for  the  Complete  Work. 

The  Parts  are  being  issued  in  boards,  uncut,  for  binding  (if  desired)  as 
soon  as  complete:  the  complete  book  will  be  very  strongly  bound  in  buck- 
ram, with  an  edition  interleaved  with  thin  but  very  opaque  writing-paper,  in 
half-morocco,  to  meet  the  request  of  a  large  number  of  Librarians  and 
others  who  desire  to  keep  the  work  up-to-date. 


LONDON:  GEORGE  ROUTLEDGE  &  SONS,  LIMITED.
