Publishers  and  Editors  recommend  this  Agency  to  Authors,  and  ask  us  for  contributions- 

On  the  same  day  we  have  placed  three  novels— at  publisher's  risk — and  a  short 
story  with  the  St  rami  Magazine. 

We  have  obtained  for  the  serial  use  of  a  50,000  word  story,  by  an  unknown 
writer,  £qs  more  than  was  first  offered. 

At  far  better  terms  than  first  offered,  we  have  placed  with  a  well-known  firm  of  pub- 
lishers a  novel  which  the  author's  own  agent,  seeing  no  hope  of  success,  refused 
to  deal  with. 

For  the  serial  use  of  a  65,000  word  novel  by  a  well-known  author,  we  have  succeeded 
in  obtaining  an  offer  £90  in  advance  of  the  price  previously  paid  by  the  same 
editor  to  the  author  direct. 


OUR  8UCCE88  is  due  largely  to  the  fact  that  we  submit  only  carefully 
selected  work — we  decline  to  deal  with  over  a  thousand  MSS.  annually !  Editors 
know  this,  and  READ  the  MSS.  we  send  them.  A  story  which  a  client  admits 
previously  "  trotting  round  for  four  yoaro,"  has  been  placed  by  us,  within 
ten  days  of  receipt,  with  a  magazine  which  had  returned  it  when  submitted  by 
the  author !

PLACING  M8S.— Frequently  capable  authors  are  discouraged,  not  because  their 
work  is  inferior,  but  because  they  constantly  submit  it  to  the  wrong  editors.  Even 
should  the  author  be  able  to  submit  his  own  MSS.,  with  as  good  a  prospect  or  success  as 
his  literary  agent,  it  is  a  wearisome  business,  and  he  would  generally  prefer  to  be  left  free 
to  continue  his  writing.  Beyond  the  actual  postage  incurred  on  his  behalf  and. the  small 
reading  fee  mentioned  below,  we  make  no  charge  for  our  efforts  to  place  an  author's 
work.     If  a  manuscript  is  accepted  we  deduct  10  per  cent,  from  the  amount  received. 

CRITICISING  AND  REVI8ING.— Very  often  a  manuscript's  chance  of  acceptance  is 
spoilt  by  a  bad  ending  or  beginning,  by  little  inaccuracies,  or  by  a  poor  title.  Any  MS. 
you  may  submit  to  us  for  criticism  will  be  carefully  read.  If  there  seems  to  be  no  reason- 
able prospect  of  success,  it  will  be  returned.  Should  any  change  seem  advisable  such 
alterations  will  be  suggested  as  may  increase  the  value  of  the  author's  work.  For  this 
our  charge  is  as.  for  reading  a  manuscript  up  to  5,000  words,  3s.  6d.  up  to  10,000,  and 
3s.  for  every  additional  10,000  words.  The  reading  fee  for  Verse  is  3d.  per  20  lines  up  to 
200,  and  2d.  for  every  additional  20  lines;  minimum  is.  Fees  for  revision  are  quoted 
after  perusal  of  the  work. 

TYPEWRITING  AND  TRANSLATIONS. -We  also  have  a  branch  of  this  business 
which  undertakes  every  description  of  Typewriting  and  European  Translations.  Among 
our  clients  are  Messrs.  J.  &  E.  Buntpus,  Ltd.,  Oxford  Street,  W.,  and  Messrs.  J.  M. 
Dent  &  Sons,  Publishers,  London,  and  other  well-known  firms. 

All  communications  should  be  addressed  to  The  Secretary, 

THE  GARDEN  CITY  LITERARY  AGENCY  AND  TYPEWRITING 
AND  TRANSLATION  BUREAU, 
LETCH  WORTH,  HERTS. 

Chief  Critic  and  Reviser:  g.  f.  leatherdale,  m.A.  (Oxon).

SOHOOL  OF  ART,  JOURNALISM.  AND 
SECRETARIAL    TRAINING    FOR    WOMEN. 

Principal— Miss  CARTWRIGHT  {Editress,  "  The  Literary  and  Musical 

Rkvihw,"  etc.). 

Under  the  patronage  of  Baroness  de  Bert  ouch,  Helen  Mathers,  "  Madge  "  of  Truth, 

Winifred  Graham,  Mrs.  T.  P.  O'Connor,  and  several  other  well-known  journalists. 

Individual  Tuition.  Lessons  also  given  tar  Correspondence. 
MSB.  Revised  and  Orltiolsel 
The  Curriculum  includes  :  Story  and  Article  Writing,  Interviewing,  Fashion  Drawing, 
Art,  Musical  and  Dramatic  Criticism,  Society  Reporting,  Book  Reviewing,  Sub-Editing, 
Decorative  Designing,  English  Literature,  General  Knowledge,  Proof  Correcting, 
Sscretarial  Dudes,  Shorthand  and  Typewriting,  Office  Routine,  French  and  German, 
Research  Work,  Indexing,  Advertisement  Writing,  Studies  in  Style. 

Lady  Journalists  in  thb  Making. 

"  Madge  "  of  Truth  writes :  "  You  ask  me  to  advise  you  about  your  cousin's  little  girl  taking  us 
journalism.  Oddly  enough,  only  the  other  day  J  came  across  an  instance  of  singular  success  in  thlp 
direciton.  A  girl  who  had  had  four  months'  tuition  at  Miss  Cartwright's  School  of  Journalism  is  now 
enjoying  an  income  of  two  hundred  a  year  as  contributor  to  a  daily  paper.  This  school  also  teaches 
secretarial  work  and  indexing.  Write  to  Miss  Cartwright,  she  will  send  you  all  particulars.  She  is 
most  successful  in  planting  out  her  girls  in  the  world."— /rw/A,  June  17, 1906.    - 

"  So  many  of  my  readers  want  to  know  just  how  and  when  and  where  to  plunge  into  the  sea  of 
journalism  that  I  have  no  hesitation  in  mentioning  this  school  as  a  centre  inwhich  there  is  a  genuine  quid 
*ro  fuor—W&K  T.  P.'s  Weekly,  September  «,  1904.  • 

"  In  the  keen  competition  of  modem  life  It  is  a  duty  to  give  our  daughters  a  profession  which  will 
develop  their  minds,  and  enable  them  to  fend  for  themselves  if  necessary.  Journalism  is  singularly  well 
suited  to  women,  as  it  gives  scope  for  their  natural  talents  and  the  range  of  their  imagination.  The 
question  of  training  is  an  all -important  one.  Miss  Cartwright  knows  the  value  of  this  met,  and  her 
pupils  are  carefully  studied,  and  launched  in  the  direction  that  best  befits  their  talents.  The  corre- 
spondence course  includes  correction  of  MSS.,  criticism  of  the  work  by  post,  etc.,  while  those  in  London 
have  the  advantage  of  tickets  for  theatres,  concerts,  art  exhibitions,  and  social  functions,  and  this  is  an 
education  itself."— Vide  The  American  Register,  June  a,  1906.

THE    NA* 


The  Most  Vigorous  and  Original 
of  the   critical   Weekly  Journals. 


>1pHE  NATION  keeps  its  readers  in  touch  with 
*  Liberal  thought  and  with  new  or  interesting 
movements  in  politics  and  social  affairs,  furnishing 
them  at  the  same  time  with  serious,  independent,  and 
authoritative  criticism  in  its  articles  on  Religion,  Art, 
the  Drama,  Finance,  and  Literature.  It  is  bright  in 
style,  varied  in  subject,  and  commands  a  most  brilliant 
staff  of  writers.  It  makes  a  special  feature  of  domestic 
and  external  politics  of  foreign  nations,  while  its  reviews, 
contributed  by  writers  of  unequalled  authority  in  their 
special  subjects,  are  always  fresh  and  up  to  date.
